# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diffusion Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bare Silicon Wafer] --> B[Thermal Oxidation Grow SiO2 Mask Layer]
    B --> C[Photolithography Pattern Photoresist]
    C --> D[Etch SiO2 Open Diffusion Windows]
    D --> E[Strip Photoresist]
    E --> F[Predeposition Constant Source Diffusion]
    F --> G{Continue with Drive-in}
    G -->|Yes| H[Drive-in Diffusion Limited Source]
    G -->|No| I[End: erfc Profile]
    H --> J[End: Gaussian Profile]
    F --> K[Surface Concentration Ns Held Constant]
    H --> L[Total Dose Q0 Conserved]
```

## 4.2 Dopant Profile Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Silicon Surface x=0] --> B[Diffusion Direction into Bulk]
    subgraph Constant_Source_Profile
        C1[High Ns at surface] --> D1[Gradual erfc decay] --> E1[Reaches 0 deep in bulk]
    end
    subgraph Limited_Source_Profile
        C2[Highest Npeak at surface] --> D2[Bell-shaped Gaussian] --> E2[Tails off symmetrically]
    end
    subgraph Temperature_Effect
        F1[Low T 800C] --> F2[Slow D] --> F3[Shallow profile]
        G1[High T 1200C] --> G2[Fast D] --> G3[Deep profile]
    end
```

## 4.3 Diffusion Mechanism — Vacancy vs Interstitial

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dopant Atom Arrives at Lattice Site] --> B{Diffusion Mechanism}
    B -->|Vacancy Mediated| C[Dopant Sits on Substitutional Site]
    C --> D[Waits for Adjacent Vacancy]
    D --> E[Jumps into Vacancy Moves to New Site]
    B -->|Interstitial Mediated| F[Dopant in Interstitial Space]
    F --> G[Squeezes Through Lattice]
    G --> H[Reaches New Interstitial Position]
    B -->|Interstitialcy Kick-out| I[Self Interstitial Pushes Dopant Out]
    I --> J[Dopant Becomes Mobile Interstitial]
    J --> K[Diffuses Quickly Through Lattice]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Diffusion Furnace System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Material Solid Liquid or Gas] --> B[Mass Flow Controller]
    B --> C[Carrier Gas N2 or Ar]
    C --> D[Heating Zone Furnace 800 to 1200C]
    D --> E[Silicon Wafer Boat]
    E --> F[Dopant Atoms Enter Wafer Surface]
    F --> G[Diffusion Front Moves into Bulk]
    G --> H[Doped Silicon with Desired Profile]
    I[Temperature Controller] --> D
    J[Exhaust System] --> D
```

## 4.5 Diffusion Profile — Graphical Visualization (ASCII Representation)

```
Concentration (N)
  |
Ns|#####                           <- Constant Source: high Ns
  |  #####
  |     ####                         <- erfc decay
  |        ####
  |           ###
  |              ##
  |                #               <- Both curves meet at substrate
  |_________________________ Depth (x)
          ^xj         

Concentration (N)
  |
Np|#####                            <- Limited Source: peak at surface
  | ######                          <- Gaussian bell
  |  ######
  |    ####
  |      ###
  |        ##
  |          #
  |_________________________ Depth (x)
          ^xj
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** In the constant-source case, the surface concentration $N_s$ is "clamped" by the external reservoir, and the profile broadens with time. In the limited-source case, the surface concentration drops as $\sim 1/\sqrt{t}$ because the same total dose is being spread over a wider region.

## 4.6 Summary Flowchart — Choosing Diffusion Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Junction Depth xj] --> B[Choose Dopant B P As]
    B --> C[Choose Temperature T]
    C --> D[Calculate D using Arrhenius]
    D --> E[Choose Process Time t]
    E --> F[Check Sheet Resistance Rs]
    F --> G{Within Spec?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H[Proceed to Drive-in or Activate]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define diffusion in the context of VLSI fabrication. List the two main boundary conditions used to model diffusion processes.**

**Model Answer:**

Diffusion is a high-temperature (800°C–1200°C) mass-transport process in which dopant atoms migrate from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration within a silicon crystal lattice, driven by the concentration gradient and random thermal motion. It is used to introduce controlled amounts of n-type or p-type impurities into silicon to form p-n junctions, source/drain regions, and wells.

The two main boundary conditions are:
1. **Constant Source Diffusion (Predeposition)**: The surface concentration $N_s$ is held constant with time by an external infinite dopant reservoir. The resulting profile is a complementary error function: $N(x,t) = N_s \cdot \text{erfc}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$.
2. **Limited Source Diffusion (Drive-in)**: A fixed total dose $Q_0$ of dopant is initially placed near the surface, and the wafer is heated without an external source. The resulting profile is Gaussian: $N(x,t) = (Q_0/\sqrt{\pi Dt}) \exp(-x^2/4Dt)$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What is the complementary error function? Write the expression for the constant source diffusion profile and state the boundary conditions used.**

**Model Answer:**

The complementary error function is defined as:

$$\text{erfc}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_z^{\infty} e^{-u^2} \, du = 1 - \text{erf}(z)$$

**Properties:** $\text{erfc}(0) = 1$, $\text{erfc}(\infty) = 0$, $\text{erfc}(-\infty) = 2$.

**Constant source diffusion profile:**

$$N(x, t) = N_s \, \text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Boundary conditions used:**
1. $N(0, t) = N_s$ (constant surface concentration)
2. $N(\infty, t) = 0$ (no dopant deep in the bulk)
3. $N(x, 0) = 0$ (initially undoped wafer)

The solution represents an erfc profile that decays from $N_s$ at the surface into the bulk, with the characteristic diffusion length $\sqrt{Dt}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion starting from Fick's First Law. Explain the physical significance of each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a constant source diffusion of Boron into a silicon wafer at 1100°C:
- $N_s = 3 \times 10^{20}$ cm⁻³ (solid solubility)
- $D = 4 \times 10^{-13}$ cm²/s
- $N_b = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ (substrate doping)
- Diffusion time $t = 1$ hour

Calculate: (i) the junction depth $x_j$, (ii) the total dose $Q$ introduced, and (iii) the sheet resistance-relevant quantity $N_s\sqrt{Dt}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Fick's First Law states that the flux of diffusing species is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2:** The continuity equation (conservation of mass) states that the rate of change of concentration in a volume element equals the negative divergence of flux:

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting Fick's First Law into the continuity equation (assuming $D$ is constant):

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right) = D\frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law**.

**Physical significance of each term:**
- $\partial N/\partial t$: rate of accumulation of dopant concentration at a point with time
- $D$: diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) — measures how fast a particular dopant moves through Si at a given temperature
- $\partial^2 N/\partial x^2$: curvature of the concentration profile — the diffusion flux is driven by the second derivative, meaning dopant flows away from regions of high curvature

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Junction depth:**

Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:

$$Dt = 4 \times 10^{-13} \times 3600 = 1.44 \times 10^{-9} \text{ cm}^2$$
$$\sqrt{Dt} = 3.79 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$

Ratio $N_b/N_s = 10^{15}/(3 \times 10^{20}) = 3.33 \times 10^{-6}$.

From erfc tables: $\text{erfc}^{-1}(3.33 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 3.4$.

$$x_j = 2 \sqrt{Dt} \cdot \text{erfc}^{-1}\left(\frac{N_b}{N_s}\right) = 2 \times 3.79 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.4 = 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.58 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Total dose:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} N_s \sqrt{Dt} = 1.128 \times 3 \times 10^{20} \times 3.79 \times 10^{-5} = 1.28 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

**(iii) Quantity $N_s\sqrt{Dt}$:**

$$N_s\sqrt{Dt} = 3 \times 10^{20} \times 3.79 \times 10^{-5} = 1.137 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **constant source diffusion** and **limited source diffusion**. Derive the dopant profile $N(x,t)$ for the limited source case. State clearly the boundary conditions and assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer is pre-deposited with Phosphorus at 950°C for 30 minutes with $D = 10^{-14}$ cm²/s and $N_s = 10^{21}$ cm⁻³. It is then subjected to a drive-in at 1100°C with $D = 4 \times 10^{-13}$ cm²/s for 2 hours. Calculate: (i) the dose after predeposition, (ii) the peak concentration after drive-in, and (iii) the junction depth if $N_b = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Difference:**

| Constant Source | Limited Source |
|---|---|
| Surface concentration $N_s$ held constant | Total dose $Q_0$ held constant |
| External dopant reservoir | No external source |
| Profile: erfc | Profile: Gaussian |
| Dose grows as $\sqrt{t}$ | Dose conserved |

**Derivation of limited source profile:**

**Step 1:** Solve Fick's Second Law: $\partial N/\partial t = D \, \partial^2 N/\partial x^2$ with similarity variable $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{Dt})$.

**Step 2:** The ODE becomes $d^2 f/d\eta^2 + 2\eta \, df/d\eta = 0$, whose solution is $f(\eta) = A \exp(-\eta^2) + B$, but boundary condition $f(\infty) = 0$ forces $B = 0$ and $A = B \cdot \text{constant}$.

**Step 3:** Apply mass conservation: $\int_0^\infty N(x,t) dx = Q_0$:

$$Q_0 = 2\sqrt{Dt} \int_0^\infty A \exp(-\eta^2) d\eta = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = A\sqrt{\pi D t}$$

So $A = Q_0/\sqrt{\pi D t}$.

**Step 4:** Final profile:

$$N(x, t) = \frac{Q_0}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$$

**Assumptions:**
- $D$ independent of concentration (intrinsic diffusion)
- Single-phase dopant (no precipitation)
- One-dimensional diffusion
- No external source during drive-in

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Dose after predeposition:**

$D_1 t_1 = 10^{-14} \times 1800 = 1.8 \times 10^{-11}$ cm².
$\sqrt{D_1 t_1} = 4.24 \times 10^{-6}$ cm.

$$Q_0 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} N_s \sqrt{D_1 t_1} = 1.128 \times 10^{21} \times 4.24 \times 10^{-6} = 4.78 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

**(ii) Peak concentration after drive-in:**

$D_2 t_2 = 4 \times 10^{-13} \times 7200 = 2.88 \times 10^{-9}$ cm².
$\sqrt{\pi D_2 t_2} = \sqrt{\pi \times 2.88 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.008 \times 10^{-4}$ cm.

$$N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{Q_0}{\sqrt{\pi D_2 t_2}} = \frac{4.78 \times 10^{15}}{3.008 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.59 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**(iii) Junction depth:**

For Gaussian profile:

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{D_2 t_2 \, \ln(N_{\text{peak}}/N_b)} = 2\sqrt{2.88 \times 10^{-9} \times \ln(1.59 \times 10^{19}/10^{16})}$$

$$= 2\sqrt{2.88 \times 10^{-9} \times \ln(1.59 \times 10^3)} = 2\sqrt{2.88 \times 10^{-9} \times 7.37}$$

$$= 2\sqrt{2.123 \times 10^{-8}} = 2 \times 1.457 \times 10^{-4} = 2.91 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2.91 \, \mu\text{m}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up the two profiles (MOST COMMON, -2 to -3 marks):** Students often write the Gaussian form for the constant source case or vice-versa. Memorize: **erfc → constant source; Gaussian → limited source**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \cdot \partial N/\partial x$ — the negative sign is non-negotiable and indicates flow down the concentration gradient.
> 
> 3. **Wrong units in diffusivity:** $D$ is in **cm²/s**, not m²/s. Numerical answers using SI units will be off by factors of 10⁴.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $\sqrt{Dt}$ with $Dt$:** The characteristic diffusion length is $\sqrt{Dt}$ (with units of length), not $Dt$ (units of area). The junction depth expression uses $2\sqrt{Dt}$.
> 
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly in derivations:** KTU examiners award at least 2 marks for correctly stating boundary conditions. Skipping them costs you those marks.
> 
> 6. **Using $T$ in Celsius instead of Kelvin in Arrhenius equation:** Always convert to Kelvin ($K = °C + 273$).
> 
> 7. **Confusing the dose for constant source with that for limited source:** For constant source, $Q$ **grows** with time; for limited source, $Q$ is **constant**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Diffusion** is a thermal, equilibrium doping process driven by concentration gradient; no external field needed.
- **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \cdot \partial N/\partial x$ — flux is proportional to negative concentration gradient.
- **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial N/\partial t = D \cdot \partial^2 N/\partial x^2$ — derived by combining Fick's First Law with the continuity equation.
- **Constant Source (Predeposition):** $N(x,t) = N_s \cdot \text{erfc}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$, dose $Q = (2/\sqrt{\pi})N_s\sqrt{Dt}$, surface concentration pinned.
- **Limited Source (Drive-in):** $N(x,t) = (Q_0/\sqrt{\pi Dt})\exp(-x^2/4Dt)$, dose conserved, profile is Gaussian.
- **Junction depth:** $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \text{erfc}^{-1}(N_b/N_s)$ (constant source) or $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt \ln(N_{\text{peak}}/N_b)}$ (Gaussian).
- **Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/kT)$ — small $\Delta T$ → large $\Delta D$.
- **Activation energies** in Si: typically 3–4 eV for substitutional dopants (B, P, As, Sb).
- **Diffusion length:** $\sqrt{Dt}$ is the characteristic spreading distance.
- **Masking:** SiO₂ is the standard mask for Boron; Si₃N₄ is required for Sodium and other fast diffusers.
- **Dopant selection:** Boron (p-type, fast diffuser), Phosphorus (n-type, moderate), Arsenic (n-type, slow — good for shallow junctions), Antimony (n-type, very slow).
- **Solid solubility ($N_s$):** maximum concentration a dopant can achieve in Si at a given temperature — this caps the surface concentration.
- **Diffusion mechanisms:** vacancy (dominant for substitutional), interstitial, interstitialcy (kick-out), pair diffusion.
- **2D effects:** at mask edges, lateral diffusion under the mask is approximately **0.8 × vertical junction depth** (lateral diffusion factor).
- **Oxidation-Enhanced Diffusion (OED):** during thermal oxidation, excess Si interstitials are injected, which can enhance dopant diffusion — important for advanced processes.
- **Process sequence:** predeposition (constant source) → drive-in (limited source) to independently control $N_s$ and $x_j$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Etching Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Etching** is a critical subtractive microfabrication process in Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) technology wherein unwanted portions of a thin film, substrate, or previously deposited layer are selectively removed using chemical, physical, or chemo-physical mechanisms, governed by a pre-patterned **photoresist** or **hard mask**.

In the 2024 KTU syllabus context, the etching process is studied as the second fundamental pattern-transfer step (after photolithography) in the planar process flow. It is the process by which the 2-D geometric information encoded in the photoresist is permanently transferred into the underlying material layers (polysilicon, silicon dioxide, metal, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Stencil Carving Method:**
> Imagine you have a wooden board painted with a thick colored layer on top. You place a *stencil* (mask) over it and then either spray-paint it, or use a chemical solvent only on the *exposed* areas. The result is the pattern of the stencil carved into the colored layer. The etching process in VLSI works exactly the same way:
> - The **wooden board** = the underlying silicon or thin film
> - The **colored layer** = the material to be patterned  
> - The **stencil** = the photoresist mask
> - The **solvent/spray** = the etchant (chemical or plasma)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Etch Rate (ER)** = material thickness removed per unit time (typically **nm/min**)
> - **Selectivity (S)** = ratio of etch rate of target material to etch rate of mask/underlying layer (dimensionless)
> - **Anisotropy (A)** = measure of directionality of etching (ranges from **0 (isotropic)** to **1 (fully anisotropic)**)

---

### 1.3 Why is Etching Important in VLSI?

- It is the **physical realization** of the circuit layout drawn by the designer.
- Determines the **critical dimension (CD)** control of transistors.
- Affects the **sidewall profile** of gates, vias, and trenches.
- Influences **device reliability**, leakage current, and yield.
- Used in CMOS, MEMS, photonics, and 3-D integration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Etching Processes

The KTU syllabus mandates a clear understanding of the following taxonomy:

> **I. Wet Etching (Chemical)**
>   - Immersion etching
>   - Spray etching
>
> **II. Dry Etching (Physical / Chemo-physical)**
>   - Plasma-based etching
>     * Plasma Etching (PE)
>     * Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)
>     * Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE / Bosch Process)
>   - Ion-beam based etching
>     * Sputter Etching (Ion Milling)
>     * Reactive Ion Beam Etching (RIBE)
>   - Vapor Phase Etching
>     * HF vapor etching of SiO₂

---

### 2.2 Wet Etching — Operational Analysis

**Mechanism:** Purely **chemical reaction** between the etchant and the film. No directional ion bombardment is involved.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The wafer is immersed in a liquid chemical bath (e.g., **HF** for SiO₂, **KOH** or **TMAH** for silicon, **H₃PO₄** for aluminum).
- The etchant diffuses to the surface, reacts with the material, and the by-products diffuse away into the solution.
- The reaction is **isotropic** in most amorphous films (etches in *all* directions equally).
- The undercut beneath the mask is roughly equal to the etched depth — this is called **lateral etch** or **undercut (U)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallographic Wet Etching Exception:**
> For single-crystalline silicon etched in **KOH** or **TMAH**, the etch rate depends strongly on the **crystal orientation**:
> - (100) plane etches **~100× faster** than (111) plane.
> - This produces characteristic **54.74° sidewalls** for (100) wafers — useful for MEMS cantilevers, but generally avoided in sub-micron CMOS.

**Key Parameters of Wet Etching:**

- **High Selectivity:** Etchants can be chosen to attack only one material.
- **Isotropic Profile:** Leads to **undercut** — limits resolution to **≥ 3 μm** in classical VLSI.
- **No Plasma Damage:** Does not harm sensitive gate oxides.
- **Capillary Effects:** Small features may not be etched uniformly due to bubble formation and etchant depletion.

---

### 2.3 Dry Etching — Operational Analysis

**Mechanism:** Combination of **chemical reaction** (from reactive species in plasma) and **physical sputtering** (from accelerated ions). This duality is the heart of dry etching and is what allows **anisotropic** profiles.

**The Plasma Environment:**

- A gas (e.g., **CF₄, SF₆, Cl₂, BCl₃, O₂, Ar**) is ionized in a low-pressure chamber (typically **10 mTorr – 1 Torr**) by an RF field (**13.56 MHz**).
- Free electrons gain energy from the RF field and collide with gas molecules, creating ions, electrons, and **free radicals**.
- **Free radicals** are the *chemical etchant* (they diffuse to the surface and react).
- **Positive ions** (e.g., CF₃⁺, Ar⁺) are accelerated toward the **cathode** (wafer) by the self-bias DC voltage, providing *physical sputtering*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Two-Agent" Model of Dry Etching:**
> - **Chemical agent (radicals):** Provides *selectivity* (etches target material fast, mask slow).
> - **Physical agent (ions):** Provides *anisotropy* (etches vertically, not laterally).
> - Balancing these two is the core engineering challenge in RIE.

**Sub-types of Dry Etching (KTU 2024 Syllabus — High Priority):**

| Process | Mechanism | Anisotropy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plasma Etching (PE)** | Mostly chemical (radical-dominated) | Low to medium | Stripping resist, isotropic Si etch |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | Chemical + physical | High | Gate, spacer, via definition |
| **Deep RIE (DRIE / Bosch)** | Alternating etch & passivation | Very high | TSV, MEMS (aspect ratio > 20:1) |
| **Sputter Etching / Ion Milling** | Purely physical (Ar⁺) | High but non-selective | Metals, materials with no volatile etchant |
| **Reactive Ion Beam Etching (RIBE)** | Ion beam with reactive gas | Very high | Research, low-damage etching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bosch Process (DRIE) — Special KTU Topic:**
> Invented at Robert Bosch GmbH, it alternates between two steps in a cyclic fashion (cycle time ~ **1–10 seconds**):
> 1. **Etch step:** SF₆ plasma generates F radicals that isotropically etch Si.
> 2. **Passivation step:** C₄F₈ plasma deposits a Teflon-like polymer (–CF₂–) on *all* surfaces.
> 3. The next etch step's ions sputter away the polymer at the **bottom** (vertical ion flux is highest), exposing Si for F radicals to etch, while the **sidewalls** remain protected.
> This produces nearly vertical sidewalls (anisotropy > 0.95) with characteristic **scalloping** (~50–200 nm amplitude).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these five equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | $\Delta d$ = thickness removed, $\Delta t$ = time (nm/min) |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{ER_{film}}{ER_{mask}}$ | Dimensionless; higher = better |
| 3 | **Anisotropy** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{R_{lateral}}{R_{vertical}}$ | $A=1$ fully anisotropic, $A=0$ isotropic |
| 4 | **Undercut (Wet Etch)** | $U = R_{lateral} \times t$ | Equals etched depth for pure isotropic |
| 5 | **DC Bias Voltage** | $V_{dc} \propto \sqrt{P_{RF}}$ | Controls ion energy in RIE |
| 6 | **Mean Free Path** | $\lambda = \dfrac{kT}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^{2}\,P}$ | Must be > electrode gap for directional etching |
| 7 | **Resistivity of Plasma** | $\rho = \dfrac{m_{e}\,\nu_{c}}{n_{e}\,e^{2}}$ | Determines bias voltage |
| 8 | **Sputter Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{atoms removed}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | Depends on ion energy and mass ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-table-corruption rule:** Whenever writing absolute values or magnitude in the table, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — **never** a bare `\|` symbol, since the markdown parser treats it as a column separator.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World VLSI

- **Sub-22 nm CMOS:** RIE with **HBr/Cl₂/O₂** chemistry is used for silicon fin definition in FinFETs.
- **DRAM Capacitors:** DRIE is used to etch deep trenches (aspect ratio **> 40:1**) for capacitor structures.
- **3-D NAND Flash:** DRIE etches the high-aspect-ratio memory hole stacks (over **6 μm deep** in modern devices).
- **TSV (Through-Silicon Via):** DRIE is the *only* viable technology for making these vertical interconnects.
- **MEMS:** Combination of KOH anisotropic wet etch and DRIE defines inertial sensors, accelerometers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Etch Rate (ER)

**Starting Point — Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

The flux of etchant species $J$ arriving at the surface is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$
J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 1 — Assumption of Steady State:**
At constant temperature and concentration, the system reaches a steady state, so $dJ/dx = 0$.

**Step 2 — Boundary Conditions:**
- $C(x=0) = C_0$ (bulk etchant concentration)
- $C(x=\delta) = 0$ (concentration drops to zero at the reactive surface)

**Step 3 — Integration across the diffusion boundary layer of thickness $\delta$:**

$$
J = -D\,\frac{0 - C_0}{\delta - 0} = \frac{D\,C_0}{\delta}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to Reaction Rate:**
Each arriving etchant molecule reacts with one film atom (assuming 1:1 stoichiometry), so the volumetric removal rate equals $J/\rho$, where $\rho$ is the atomic density of the film.

**Step 5 — Linearize for Constant Conditions:**

$$
ER = \frac{J}{\rho} = \frac{D\,C_0}{\rho\,\delta}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify to Practical Form:**
For a given chemistry and temperature, $D$, $C_0$, $\rho$, and $\delta$ are constants. Defining $k = D\,C_0/(\rho\,\delta)$:

$$
\boxed{\;ER = k = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}\;}
$$

This confirms that for a *diffusion-limited* wet etch, the etch depth is linear in time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Selectivity (S)

**Definition:** Selectivity is the ratio of etch rates between the *target film* and the *mask/underlying material*:

$$
S = \frac{ER_{film}}{ER_{mask}}
$$

**Step 1 — Write Individual Rates Using Arrhenius Form:**
For an *activation-limited* process (typical for dry etching):

$$
ER_{film} = A_{f}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,f}}{kT}\right), \quad ER_{mask} = A_{m}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,m}}{kT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Selectivity Equation:**

$$
S = \frac{A_{f}}{A_{m}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,m} - E_{a,f}}{kT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Interpretation:**

- If $E_{a,f} \ll E_{a,m}$, then the exponential term is large → **high selectivity** (film etches much faster than mask).
- If $E_{a,f} \approx E_{a,m}$, the exponential is close to 1 → **low selectivity** (1:1 attack).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To boost selectivity in RIE, the chemist adds **polymer-forming gases** (e.g., CHF₃ instead of CF₄) which deposit a teflon-like film on the mask but not on the silicon. This is how **SiO₂ : Si selectivity** of **20:1** is achieved with CHF₃.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Anisotropy (A)

**Geometric Setup:**

Consider a vertical feature with vertical etch depth $d_v$ and lateral undercut $d_l$ after time $t$.

**Step 1 — Define Component Rates:**

$$
R_{vertical} = \frac{d_v}{t}, \quad R_{lateral} = \frac{d_l}{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Ratio of Lateral to Vertical Removal:**

$$
\frac{R_{lateral}}{R_{vertical}} = \frac{d_l}{d_v}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Anisotropy Parameter:**

$$
A = 1 - \frac{R_{lateral}}{R_{vertical}} = 1 - \frac{d_l}{d_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Limiting Cases:**

- **Pure Isotropic:** $d_l = d_v \;\Rightarrow\; A = 0$ (typical of wet etching in amorphous films).
- **Pure Anisotropic:** $d_l = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A = 1$ (typical of ideal RIE, DRIE).
- **Partial Anisotropy:** $0 < A < 1$ (real processes).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A polysilicon gate is etched in RIE for 60 seconds. The vertical depth removed is 240 nm, and the lateral undercut is 8 nm. Find the anisotropy.

$$
A = 1 - \frac{8}{240} = 1 - 0.0333 = 0.967
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $A \geq 0.95$ is the industry benchmark for sub-100 nm gate definition.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Etch Time for a Given Thickness

Starting from $ER = \Delta d / \Delta t$:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{\Delta d}{ER} = \frac{\text{film thickness (nm)}}{\text{etch rate (nm/min)}}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A **500 nm** aluminum layer is to be etched using an etch rate of **250 nm/min** with **10% over-etch** safety margin.

**Step 1 — Time for nominal thickness:**

$$
t_{nominal} = \frac{500}{250} = 2 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Add 10% over-etch:**

$$
t_{total} = 1.10 \times 2 = 2.2 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Mask Selectivity:**
If the photoresist etches at **50 nm/min**, the resist loss in 2.2 min is:

$$
\text{Resist loss} = 50 \times 2.2 = 110 \text{ nm}
$$

The selectivity is $S = 250/50 = 5$. With an initial resist thickness of **1.2 μm**, this is acceptable. If a thinner resist were used, the chemist would switch to a **hard mask** (e.g., SiO₂).

---

### 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Wet Etch | Typical RIE | Bosch DRIE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Etch Rate | $ER$ | 100 – 1000 nm/min | 50 – 500 nm/min | 1 – 10 μm/min |
| Selectivity (Si:SiO₂) | $S$ | > 100 : 1 | 20 – 40 : 1 | 50 – 100 : 1 |
| Anisotropy | $A$ | 0.0 (amorphous) | 0.85 – 0.97 | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| Aspect Ratio | AR | < 1 : 1 | < 5 : 1 | > 20 : 1 |
| CD Loss | $\Delta_{CD}$ | High (μm) | Low (nm) | Very low |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define the term "Etching" in VLSI fabrication. List any two differences between wet and dry etching.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Etching is the process of selectively removing unwanted portions of a thin film or substrate through chemical, physical, or combined action, using a patterned mask to define the geometry to be transferred.
>
> **Differences (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
>
> | Aspect | Wet Etching | Dry Etching |
> |---|---|---|
> | Mechanism | Purely chemical | Chemical + physical (plasma) |
> | Profile | Isotropic (rounded) | Anisotropic (vertical) |
> | Resolution | Limited to ~ 3 μm | Sub-100 nm achievable |
> | Selectivity | Very high | Moderate to high |
> | Damage | No plasma damage | Possible radiation damage |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*What is meant by "selectivity" and "anisotropy" in the context of an etching process? Why are they important in VLSI?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Selectivity (1 mark):** It is the ratio of the etch rate of the target material to that of the mask or underlying layer: $S = ER_{film} / ER_{mask}$. It determines how much mask is consumed during the etch.
>
> **Anisotropy (1 mark):** It is the degree of directionality of the etch, defined as $A = 1 - R_{lateral}/R_{vertical}$. $A=1$ means fully vertical etching.
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** High selectivity allows the use of thin masks, while high anisotropy is critical for preserving the **critical dimension (CD)** of sub-micron features in advanced CMOS nodes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** process. With a neat diagram, describe the mechanism, key process parameters, and the role of plasma chemistry. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** A 400 nm silicon dioxide layer is to be etched using a CHF₃-based RIE process with an etch rate of 100 nm/min. The photoresist mask has a thickness of 1.0 μm and etches at 25 nm/min.
   - (i) Calculate the etch time required (with 20% over-etch).
   - (ii) Determine the selectivity of SiO₂ with respect to photoresist.
   - (iii) Will the resist survive the etch? Justify. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### **Part (a) — RIE Process Explanation [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) is a dry etching technique that uses a chemically reactive plasma generated under low pressure to remove material. It combines the chemical reactivity of free radicals with the directional physical bombardment of energetic ions, giving both high selectivity and high anisotropy.

**2. System Components (1.5 marks):**
- **Vacuum chamber** pumped to 10 – 100 mTorr.
- **Two parallel electrodes:** the upper (anode) is grounded; the lower (cathode) holds the wafer and is coupled to an RF source (**13.56 MHz**).
- **Gas inlet** for process gases (e.g., **CF₄, SF₆, Cl₂, BCl₃, CHF₃, O₂, Ar**).
- **Matching network** to maximize power transfer from the RF generator.
- **Cooling system** for the wafer electrode.
- **Endpoint detection** system (optical emission or laser interferometry).

**3. Plasma Generation Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- The RF field accelerates free electrons.
- Electrons collide with gas molecules, ionizing them: $e^- + CF_4 \rightarrow CF_3^+ + F^* + 2e^-$
- A **self-bias DC voltage** ($V_{dc} \approx -200$ to $-800$ V) develops on the cathode because electrons are more mobile than the heavy positive ions.
- Positive ions are accelerated *vertically* toward the wafer, producing **directional ion bombardment**.

**4. Etch Mechanism — Two-Agent Model (2 marks):**

- **Chemical component:** Free radicals (e.g., F*, Cl*) diffuse to the wafer surface and react with the film to form volatile products. Example: $Si + 4F^* \rightarrow SiF_4 \uparrow$ (volatile, leaves the chamber).
- **Physical component:** Accelerated ions (e.g., Ar⁺, CF₃⁺) strike the wafer surface, sputtering away material and damaged resist/polymer. The ions hit *only* the horizontal bottom surface because they travel vertically, leaving the sidewalls protected → **anisotropic profile**.

**5. Key Process Parameters (1 mark):**
- **RF Power:** Higher power → more ionization → higher etch rate, but more damage.
- **Pressure:** Lower pressure → longer mean free path → more anisotropic.
- **Gas Chemistry:** F-based for Si, Cl-based for Al/Cu, O₂ for resist stripping.
- **DC Self-Bias:** Controls ion energy.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- SiO₂ thickness: $d = 400$ nm
- SiO₂ etch rate: $ER_{SiO_2} = 100$ nm/min
- Photoresist etch rate: $ER_{PR} = 25$ nm/min
- Over-etch: 20 %

**Step 1 — Compute the nominal etch time (2 marks):**

$$
t_{nominal} = \frac{d}{ER_{SiO_2}} = \frac{400}{100} = 4 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Add 20% over-etch (1 mark):**

$$
t_{total} = 1.20 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute selectivity (2 marks):**

$$
S = \frac{ER_{SiO_2}}{ER_{PR}} = \frac{100}{25} = 4
$$

So $S = 4 : 1$.

**Step 4 — Check resist integrity (2 marks):**

Resist consumed in 4.8 min:

$$
\Delta_{PR} = 25 \times 4.8 = 120 \text{ nm}
$$

Remaining resist thickness:

$$
t_{PR,remaining} = 1000 - 120 = 880 \text{ nm}
$$

**Justification:** Yes, the photoresist will survive the etch with **880 nm** remaining, which is more than sufficient. However, since the selectivity is only 4:1, the resist thickness must be planned carefully. For thinner masks, a **hard mask** (e.g., Si₃N₄ or SiO₂) with selectivity > 20:1 would be preferred.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Describe the **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE / Bosch) process** in detail. Explain the alternating etch-passivation mechanism with a neat sketch. Discuss the formation of scallops and methods to minimize them. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** Compare wet and dry etching processes based on **five** performance criteria. For a 0.18 μm CMOS process requiring a silicon gate etch with vertical sidewalls, justify why RIE is preferred over wet KOH etching. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### **Part (a) — Bosch DRIE Process [7 marks]**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Bosch process, patented by Robert Bosch GmbH in the 1990s, is a cyclic, time-multiplexed DRIE technique used to etch deep, high-aspect-ratio features in silicon — used in **MEMS**, **TSVs**, and **3-D IC** manufacturing.

**2. The Two-Step Cycle (3 marks):**

- **Etch Step (typically 5 – 10 s):** SF₆ gas is introduced into the chamber. A plasma is generated that produces fluorine free radicals ($F^*$). These radicals isotropically etch silicon, forming volatile SiF₄:
  $$Si + 4F^* \rightarrow SiF_4 \uparrow$$
- **Passivation Step (typically 3 – 5 s):** SF₆ is stopped; C₄F₈ gas is introduced. A plasma of C₄F₈ deposits a conformal, Teflon-like fluorocarbon polymer (–CF₂–)ₙ on *all* exposed surfaces:
  $$C_4F_8 \rightarrow \text{polymer} + \text{ions}$$
- **Sputter Removal:** During the next etch cycle, the directional ions strike the wafer vertically and sputter away the polymer at the *bottom* of the trench, exposing silicon for F* attack. The *sidewall* polymer, protected from ion bombardment, remains and prevents lateral etching.

**3. Scallop Formation (1.5 marks):**
Because the sidewall is exposed briefly to F* radicals at the end of each etch step (before passivation re-coats it), the sidewall develops a periodic, wavy texture called **scalloping**. Typical scallop amplitude is **50 – 200 nm**.

**Methods to minimize scallops:**
- Use **shorter cycle times** (e.g., 1 s etch, 1 s passivation) — but this slows the overall etch rate.
- Use **higher passivation gas flow** to coat the sidewall faster.
- Use **lower F radical concentration** to limit lateral attack.
- Post-process **wet isotropic smoothing** (e.g., HNA — HF/HNO₃/CH₃COOH).

**4. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Anisotropy $A \geq 0.98$.
- Aspect ratio up to **40:1** or more.
- High etch rate (1 – 10 μm/min).
- Vertical sidewalls ideal for TSV and MEMS.

---

#### **Part (b) — Comparison & Justification [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Wet Etching | Dry Etching (RIE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Profile / Anisotropy** | Isotropic (A ≈ 0) | Highly anisotropic (A > 0.9) |
| **Selectivity** | Very high (100:1) | Moderate to high (10–40:1) |
| **Resolution / CD Control** | Limited (~ 3 μm) | Sub-100 nm |
| **Plasma Damage** | None | Possible (UV, charging) |
| **Aspect Ratio Capability** | Low (< 1:1) | High (up to 20:1 with DRIE) |
| **Throughput / Cost** | High throughput, low cost | Lower throughput, higher tool cost |
| **Environmental / Safety** | Acid handling, disposal | Gas handling, vacuum systems |

**Justification of RIE for 0.18 μm CMOS gate etch (4 marks):**

1. **Critical Dimension (CD) Control:** At 0.18 μm node, the gate length tolerance is in the tens of nanometers. Wet etching is inherently isotropic, producing an undercut equal to the etched depth — this would obliterate the CD. RIE provides $A \geq 0.95$ and preserves the linewidth. **(1 mark)**
2. **Vertical Sidewall Profile:** A vertical sidewall is required for proper transistor channel length definition and for subsequent spacer deposition. Wet KOH gives a 54.74° sloped sidewall on (100) Si — totally unsuitable. **(1 mark)**
3. **Aspect Ratio:** A 0.18 μm gate on 200 nm polysilicon is an aspect ratio of ~1:1, well within RIE capability but still problematic for wet etchants (capillary effects, bubble formation in narrow trenches). **(1 mark)**
4. **CMOS Compatibility:** RIE can be tuned to stop on thin gate oxide (~ 2 nm) using high selectivity chemistry (e.g., HBr/Cl₂/O₂ with selectivity > 50:1 to SiO₂). Wet etching has poor end-point control at the atomic-scale oxide thickness. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Selectivity with Anisotropy:** Selectivity compares *materials*; anisotropy compares *directions*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Over-Etch:** A 10–20% over-etch is industry practice. Not including it shows the student has no fabrication experience — expect a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Wrong Units:** Etch rate must be in **nm/min** or **μm/min**, *not* m/s. Watch for unit-conversion errors.
> 4. **Missing End-Point Detection:** Always mention **OES (Optical Emission Spectroscopy)** or **laser interferometry** as a way to stop the etch at the correct depth.
> 5. **No Mention of Plasma Damage:** In a dry-etch question, neglecting to discuss **UV-induced damage, charging effects, or notch defects** in the gate oxide leads to a **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 6. **Diagrams Without Labels:** Mermaid / sketches without proper labels (chamber, electrodes, gas flow, wafer) are marked *incomplete* — **lose 1 mark** minimum.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Definition:** Etching = selective removal of material via chemical, physical, or chemo-physical action, governed by a mask.
> - **Two families:** Wet (chemical, isotropic, high selectivity) and Dry (plasma, anisotropic, sub-μm CD).
> - **Etch Rate** = thickness / time, units **nm/min** or **μm/min**.
> - **Selectivity** = $ER_{target} / ER_{mask}$ — dimensionless; higher is better.
> - **Anisotropy** = $1 - R_{lateral}/R_{vertical}$ — ranges 0 (isotropic) to 1 (fully vertical).
> - **Undercut** = lateral etch beneath the mask; in pure wet etch, it equals the etched depth.
> - **Wet etchants to remember:** HF → SiO₂; H₃PO₄ → Al; KOH/TMAH → Si (orientation-dependent); HNA → Si smoothing.
> - **Dry etch chemistries:** CF₄, SF₆ → Si; CHF₃ → SiO₂ (high selectivity); Cl₂/BCl₃ → Al; O₂ → resist strip; Ar → sputter.
> - **RIE:** Combines chemical (radicals) and physical (ions) → high anisotropy, moderate selectivity.
> - **DRIE / Bosch:** Alternating SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation → scallops, aspect ratio > 20:1.
> - **Plasma generation:** RF at **13.56 MHz**, pressure 10 – 100 mTorr, DC self-bias on cathode.
> - **Ion Milling:** Purely physical (Ar⁺) — non-selective but very anisotropic; used when no volatile chemistry exists.
> - **Endpoint Detection:** OES monitors specific emission lines (e.g., F at 703 nm for Si etch; Al at 396 nm).
> - **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE):** Etch rate slows in high-aspect-ratio features — known as the **RIE lag**.
> - **Loading Effect:** Etch rate decreases as the number of wafers or the exposed area increases.
> - **Black Silicon Problem:** Micromasking from residual polymer leads to spiky surface — fix with proper O₂ clean.
> - **CD Loss in Wet Etch:** Always at least equal to the etched depth — disqualifies wet etch below 3 μm.
> - **Key RF Frequency:** Always **13.56 MHz** — an ISM band frequency chosen to avoid interference.
> - **Mean Free Path** must be larger than the electrode spacing for ions to travel without collisions and remain directional.
> - **Bosch cycle time:** 1–10 s per step; scallop size scales with cycle time.
> - **Industry benchmark anisotropy** for gate etch: $A \geq 0.95$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Implantation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ion Implantation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ion Implantation** is a low-temperature, non-equilibrium physical process of introducing energetic dopant ions (typically in the energy range of **5 keV to 1 MeV**) into a semiconductor substrate by accelerating them through a high electric field and allowing them to penetrate the crystal lattice, where they lose energy primarily through **electronic excitation** and **nuclear collisions**, finally coming to rest at depths characterized by a **projected range ($R_p$)** and **straggle ($\Delta R_p$)** following approximately a **Gaussian distribution**.

In the KTU 2024 VLSI Design framework, ion implantation is classified under **Module 1: Fabrication of MOS Devices**, specifically as a **doping technique** that has largely replaced high-temperature thermal diffusion for precise, shallow, and reproducible doping of MOSFET source, drain, channel, and well regions at sub-100 nm technology nodes.

### Key Terminology — As-Is for Board Examination

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Projected Range | $R_p$ | Average depth of ion penetration measured perpendicular to the surface |
| Projected Straggle | $\Delta R_p$ | Standard deviation of the depth distribution |
| Lateral Straggle | $\Delta R_\perp$ | Standard deviation in the lateral (parallel to surface) direction |
| Dose | $\Phi$ | Total number of ions implanted per unit area (ions/cm²) |
| Beam Energy | $E$ | Kinetic energy of incident ions (keV) |
| Beam Current | $I$ | Ion current measured at the target (µA to mA) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cosmic Bullet" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Throwing Marbles into a Sponge**
>
> Imagine you have a thick kitchen sponge (the silicon crystal lattice) lying flat on a table. You stand a few meters above it and shoot tiny glass marbles (dopant ions such as Boron, Phosphorus, or Arsenic) downward with a slingshot.
>
> - **Sling pull-back (electric field)** = controls how deep the marble penetrates.
> - **Number of marbles shot (beam current × time)** = controls how many get embedded.
> - **Spread of stopping depths (straggle)** = because each marble bounces off a different number of sponge fibers, they come to rest at slightly different depths, forming a **bell-shaped cloud**.
> - **Sponge damage (lattice displacement)** = high-speed marbles rip the sponge fibers; you must "iron" the sponge afterwards (this is **annealing**).
>
> This is exactly what happens when B⁺, P⁺, or As⁺ ions are implanted into a silicon wafer to form the n⁺/p⁺ regions of a MOSFET.

## 1.3 Why Ion Implantation is Preferred Over Thermal Diffusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight — Question Asked Frequently**
> Ion implantation is preferred over thermal diffusion because it offers:
> 1. **Precise dose control** (via beam current integration).
> 2. **Low-temperature processing** (no redistribution of previously doped layers).
> 3. **Sharp, shallow junctions** for nanoscale CMOS.
> 4. **Reproducibility** and uniformity across 300 mm wafers.
> 5. **Wide range of dopants**, including those with low solid solubility.

### Standard Industry Metrics in Bold

- **Implant energy range:** **5 keV – 1 MeV**
- **Typical dose range:** **10¹¹ – 10¹⁶ ions/cm²**
- **Beam current:** **µA to mA** (high-current implanters go up to tens of mA)
- **Vacuum level:** **< 10⁻⁷ Torr** in the beamline
- **Wafer temperature during implant:** **< 100 °C** (to prevent in-situ annealing)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture of an Ion Implanter

The ion implantation system consists of **four functional blocks** working in series:

1. **Ion Source** — Generates ions from a gas (e.g., BF₃, PH₃, AsH₃) or solid source via:
   - **Hot-filament discharge (Bernas source)**
   - **Freeman source** (for higher currents)
   - **Microwave ECR source** (for low-energy, low-damage implants)
2. **Extraction Electrode & Focusing Lens** — Pulls ions out of the source and forms a parallel beam.
3. **Mass Analyzer (Magnetic Sector)** — Selects the desired isotope by exploiting
   $$r = \frac{\sqrt{2 V m/q}}{B}$$
   where $r$ is the bending radius, $V$ the extraction potential, $m$ the ion mass, and $B$ the magnetic flux density.
4. **Acceleration Column** — Provides the final kinetic energy:
   $$E = qV_{\text{acc}}$$
5. **Scanning System & End Station** — Either scans the beam electrostatically over a stationary wafer or mechanically scans the wafer through a stationary beam.

## 2.2 Ion–Solid Interaction: The "How" and "Why"

When an energetic ion enters the crystal, it undergoes a sequence of **binary collisions** with the lattice atoms (Si) and electrons. Two energy-loss mechanisms dominate:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Nuclear Stopping ($S_n$):** Elastic collisions with atomic nuclei. Dominant at **low energies (< 50 keV/amu)**. Causes **lattice damage** (displacements).
> - **Electronic Stopping ($S_e$):** Inelastic excitation of target electrons. Dominant at **higher energies**. Causes **ionization and phonon excitation** but minimal displacement.

**Total Stopping Power:**
$$S(E) = S_n(E) + S_e(E) = -\frac{dE}{dx}$$

This is the foundation of the **LSS Theory (Lindhard, Scharff, Schiøtt, 1963)** used universally to model implant profiles.

## 2.3 Projected Range and Straggle — Definitions

- **Projected Range $R_p$:** Average depth of all stopped ions, measured along the surface normal:
  $$R_p = \langle x \rangle = \frac{\int_0^\infty x \, N(x) \, dx}{\int_0^\infty N(x) \, dx}$$
- **Projected Straggle $\Delta R_p$:** RMS spread about the mean:
  $$\Delta R_p = \sqrt{\langle (x - R_p)^2 \rangle}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = qV_{\text{acc}}$ | Kinetic energy of an ion of charge $q$ accelerated through potential $V$ | keV |
| 2 | $r = \dfrac{\sqrt{2Vm/q}}{B}$ | Radius of mass-analyzer magnet sector | m |
| 3 | $\Phi = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A}$ | Implanted dose from current $I$ for time $t$ over area $A$ | ions/cm² |
| 4 | $N(x) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$ | Gaussian doping profile (Pearson-IV simplifies to this) | cm⁻³ |
| 5 | $x_{j} \approx R_p + k\,\Delta R_p$ | Junction depth (with $k = 2$ for 2-decade fall) | nm |
| 6 | $S(E) = S_n + S_e$ | Total stopping power | eV/Å |
| 7 | $R_p \propto E^{2n}$ (with $n \approx 1$) | Energy scaling of range (empirical) | — |
| 8 | $R_{\text{thermal}} = \sqrt{4 D t}$ | Diffusion length for post-implant anneal (for comparison) | nm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Source/Drain Formation:** Ultra-shallow junctions (< 20 nm) for 7 nm/5 nm FinFETs use **Ge pre-amorphization + low-energy B/As implant + spike anneal**.
- **Threshold Voltage Tuning:** Ion implantation of the **channel (ion-implanted well)** adjusts $V_T$ precisely.
- **Polysilicon Gate Doping:** Heavy As⁺/P⁺ implants dope the gate poly.
- **Silicon-On-Insulator (SOI) and SIMOX:** Separation by IMplantation of OXygen — O⁺ ions at high dose form a buried SiO₂ layer.
- **Smart-Cut Process:** H⁺ implantation enables wafer bonding and layer transfer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gaussian Concentration Profile

### Statement
For a mono-energetic ion beam of dose $\Phi$ (ions/cm²) incident normally on a semi-infinite amorphous target, the implanted ion distribution $N(x)$ at depth $x$ is given by:

$$N(x) = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Probability density of range.**
For a large statistical sample of identical ions, the depths at which individual ions come to rest follow a probability distribution $f(x)$ whose mean is $R_p$ and standard deviation is $\Delta R_p$.

**Step 2 — Assume Gaussian (Central Limit Theorem).**
Each ion undergoes ~10⁴ independent collision events. By the Central Limit Theorem, the summed displacement is normally distributed:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert probability to concentration.**
The probability that an ion lies between $x$ and $x + dx$ is $f(x)\,dx$. The number of ions in that slab per unit area is $\Phi f(x)\,dx$. The volume of the slab per unit area is $dx$. Hence the volume concentration is:

$$N(x) = \frac{\Phi f(x)\,dx}{dx} = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Conservation check.**
Integrate from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$\int_0^\infty N(x)\,dx = \Phi \int_0^\infty f(x)\,dx \approx \Phi \quad \text{(since } R_p \gg \Delta R_p \text{ and } R_p \gg 0\text{)}$$

Hence the total dose is conserved. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Junction Depth for Gaussian Profile

### Statement
For a Gaussian profile with peak concentration $N_{\text{peak}} = \Phi / (\sqrt{2\pi}\Delta R_p)$ implanting into a substrate of opposite type with uniform background concentration $N_B$, the metallurgical junction depth is:

$$x_j = R_p + \Delta R_p\sqrt{2\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B}\right)}$$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Set $N(x_j) = N_B$.**
At the junction, the implanted concentration equals the background:

$$N_B = N_{\text{peak}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_j-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_B}{N_{\text{peak}}}\right) = -\frac{(x_j-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the squared term.**

$$(x_j - R_p)^2 = 2\Delta R_p^2\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive root** (junction lies below the mean range):

$$x_j - R_p = \Delta R_p\sqrt{2\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_j$:**

$$x_j = R_p + \Delta R_p\sqrt{2\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B}\right)}$$

This is the standard textbook form used in KTU numerical problems. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dose Calculation from Beam Current

### Statement
$$\Phi = \frac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot A}$$

where $I$ is the measured beam current, $t$ the implantation time, $q$ the ionic charge, and $A$ the implanted area.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total charge delivered to the wafer:**
$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Total number of ions implanted:**
Each ion carries charge $q$, so the number of ions is:
$$N_{\text{ions}} = \frac{Q}{q} = \frac{I\,t}{q}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the implanted area to obtain areal density (dose):**
$$\Phi = \frac{N_{\text{ions}}}{A} = \frac{I\,t}{q\,A}$$

Units: $\dfrac{A \cdot s}{C/\text{ion} \cdot \text{cm}^2} = \dfrac{\text{ions}}{\text{cm}^2}$. ∎

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** Boron ions (B⁺, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C) are implanted at 50 keV into a 200 mm diameter silicon wafer. The beam current is 100 µA, and the implant time is 60 seconds. Given $R_p = 150$ nm, $\Delta R_p = 40$ nm, and substrate doping $N_B = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, calculate the dose, peak concentration, and junction depth.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Implanted area:**
$$A = \pi (10\,\text{cm})^2 = 314.16\,\text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Dose:**
$$\Phi = \frac{(100\times 10^{-6})(60)}{(1.6\times 10^{-19})(314.16)} = \frac{6\times 10^{-3}}{5.027\times 10^{-17}} = 1.19\times 10^{14}\,\text{ions/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Peak concentration:**

$$N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} = \frac{1.19\times 10^{14}}{\sqrt{2\pi}(40\times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm})}$$

$$N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1.19\times 10^{14}}{1.005\times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.18\times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Junction depth:**

$$x_j = 150 + 40\sqrt{2\ln\!\left(\frac{1.18\times 10^{19}}{10^{15}}\right)} = 150 + 40\sqrt{2(9.376)}$$

$$x_j = 150 + 40\sqrt{18.75} = 150 + 40(4.330) = 150 + 173.2 = 323.2\,\text{nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert nm to cm when computing concentration (1 nm = 10⁻⁷ cm). Forgetting this is the #1 cause of unit-mismatch errors in KTU answer sheets.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Implantation vs Diffusion

| Parameter | Ion Implantation | Thermal Diffusion |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Temperature | < 100 °C during implant; ~1000 °C anneal | 900–1200 °C throughout |
| Dose control | Direct (beam current integration) | Indirect (via time & $C_s$) |
| Profile shape | Near-Gaussian, asymmetric tails | Error function (erfc) |
| Lateral spread | Yes ($\Delta R_\perp$) | Yes (isotropic) |
| Lattice damage | Yes — must anneal | None |
| Masking | Photoresist, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, poly | SiO₂ only |
| Shallow junction capability | Excellent (sub-10 nm) | Limited (redistribution) |
| Throughput | Moderate (serial process) | High (batch furnace) |

## 3.6 Channeling Effect — An Important Sub-Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When ions are implanted along a major crystallographic axis (e.g., ⟨100⟩ in Si), they can travel deep into the lattice with reduced scattering ("channeling tail"). Mitigation techniques:
> 1. **Tilt the wafer by 7°–10°** off the axis.
> 2. **Pre-amorphization implant (PAI)** with Si⁺ or Ge⁺ to destroy crystallinity.
> 3. Use **amorphous surface layers** (e.g., screen oxide).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Ion Implantation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[ION SOURCE] --> X[EXTRACTION ELECTRODE]
    X --> FA[FOCUSING LENS]
    FA --> MA[MASS ANALYZER MAGNET]
    MA --> AC[ACCELERATION COLUMN]
    AC --> SC[SCANNING SYSTEM]
    SC --> ES[END STATION AND WAFER]
    ES -.FEEDBACK.-> CTR[BEAM CURRENT MONITOR]
    CTR -.CONTROL.-> S
    CTR -.CONTROL.-> AC
    V[PUMPING SYSTEM LESS THAN 1E-7 TORR] -.VACUUM.-> S
    V -.VACUUM.-> FA
    V -.VACUUM.-> MA
    V -.VACUUM.-> AC
    V -.VACUUM.-> ES
```

## 4.2 Gaussian Implant Profile — Concentration vs Depth

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROFILE[Concentration versus depth in silicon]
        direction TB
        SURF[SURFACE X EQUALS 0] --> PEAK
        PEAK[PEAK CONCENTRATION AT R SUB p] --> TAIL
        TAIL[TAIL DECAYS TO BACKGROUND N SUB B] --> XJ
        XJ[METALLURGICAL JUNCTION X SUB j] --> BULK
        BULK[SUBSTRATE BACKGROUND N SUB B]
    end
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Block Diagram — CMOS S/D Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[STEP 1 START WAFER WITH GATE STACK] --> B[STEP 2 DEPOSIT SCREEN OXIDE 10 TO 20 NM]
    B --> C[STEP 3 PERFORM PAI IMPLANT GE PLUS OR SI PLUS]
    C --> D[STEP 4 LOW ENERGY DOPANT IMPLANT AS PLUS OR B PLUS]
    D --> E[STEP 5 STRIP SCREEN OXIDE]
    E --> F[STEP 6 SPIKE OR LASER ANNEAL 1050 DEG C]
    F --> G[STEP 7 ACTIVATED SHALLOW JUNCTION FORMED]
```

## 4.4 Damage Formation and Anneal — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[INCIDENT ION] --> NUC[NUCLEAR STOPPING S SUB n]
    I --> ELE[ELECTRONIC STOPPING S SUB e]
    NUC --> DAM[POINT DEFECTS VACANCIES INTERSTITIALS]
    NUC --> DIS[DISPLACEMENT CASCADE]
    DAM --> AMO[AMORPHOUS LAYER]
    DIS --> AMO
    ELE --> ION[IONIZATION AND HEAT]
    AMO --> ANN[POST IMPLANT ANNEAL]
    ANN --> ACT[ELECTRICAL ACTIVATION OF DOPANTS]
    ANN --> REP[END OF RANGE DEFECT DISSOLUTION]
    ION --> ION
```

## 4.5 Stopping Power Curve — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Stopping Power versus Ion Energy for Si]
        direction LR
        LO[LOW ENERGY LESS THAN 10 KEV] --> N[DOMINANT S SUB n NUCLEAR]
        N --> MID[INTERMEDIATE 10 TO 100 KEV]
        MID --> E[DOMINANT S SUB e ELECTRONIC]
        E --> HI[HIGH ENERGY GREATER THAN 1 MEV]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define ion implantation. List any two advantages of ion implantation over thermal diffusion.**

**Model Answer:**
Ion implantation is a process of introducing dopant ions into a semiconductor by accelerating them through a high electric field so that they penetrate the crystal lattice to a controlled depth.

Two advantages:
1. **Precise control of dose** through beam-current integration, independent of time-temperature parameters.
2. **Low processing temperature** prevents redistribution of previously defined doping profiles, enabling shallow junctions for nanoscale CMOS.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Sharp Gaussian profile, masking flexibility with photoresist, reproducibility, wider dopant range.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
**What is meant by the projected range $R_p$ and projected straggle $\Delta R_p$? Why is $\Delta R_p$ important for shallow-junction formation?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Projected Range $R_p$:** Average depth (perpendicular to the surface) at which implanted ions come to rest.
- **Projected Straggle $\Delta R_p$:** RMS spread of the depth distribution about $R_p$, characterizing the width of the Gaussian profile.

**Importance for shallow junctions:** $\Delta R_p$ must be minimized (low-energy implants) because a larger straggle makes the profile tail extend deeper, increasing junction depth $x_j$ and degrading short-channel MOSFET performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Style]**

### **Question A(a) [7 Marks]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a typical ion implantation system. List the function of each block.**

**Model Solution:**

A typical ion implanter consists of the following blocks, connected in series along the beamline under high vacuum:

1. **Ion Source:** Generates a plasma of the desired dopant species (e.g., BF₃ for B⁺, PH₃ for P⁺, AsH₃ for As⁺). Uses a hot filament to ionize the feed gas.
2. **Extraction Electrode:** A negative potential extracts positive ions from the plasma and forms an initial beam.
3. **Focusing Lens (Einzel Lens):** Electrostatic lens that collimates and converges the diverging ion beam.
4. **Mass Analyzer (Magnetic Sector):** A magnetic field $B$ bends the beam; only ions with the correct mass-to-charge ratio $m/q$ follow the chosen radius $r$ given by $r = \sqrt{2Vm/q}/B$. This removes contaminants and isotope variants.
5. **Acceleration Column:** A high-voltage stack (0.5 kV – 1 MV) accelerates the selected ions to the desired energy.
6. **Scanning System:** Either an electrostatic X-Y scanner sweeps the beam across a stationary wafer, or a mechanical scanner moves the wafer through a stationary beam.
7. **End Station:** Houses the wafer on a cooled chuck; the beam-current integrator measures total dose.
8. **Vacuum System:** Maintains < 10⁻⁷ Torr throughout the beamline to prevent ion-gas collisions.

**Auxiliary subsystems:** Beam-current integrator (for dose control), Faraday cup (for beam diagnostics), and a neutral beam trap (to filter neutrals formed by charge exchange).

### **Question A(b) [7 Marks]**
**A silicon wafer is implanted with B⁺ ions at an energy of 30 keV with a dose of $5 \times 10^{14}$ ions/cm². The projected range is $R_p = 100$ nm and straggle is $\Delta R_p = 35$ nm. The substrate is p-type with $N_B = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. Determine (i) the peak concentration, (ii) the junction depth.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak Concentration:**

$$N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} = \frac{5\times 10^{14}}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,(35\times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm})}$$

$$N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{5\times 10^{14}}{1.569\times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.19\times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**(ii) Junction Depth:**

$$x_j = R_p + \Delta R_p\sqrt{2\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B}\right)}$$

$$\frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_B} = \frac{3.19\times 10^{19}}{10^{16}} = 3190$$

$$\ln(3190) = 8.068$$

$$x_j = 100 + 35\sqrt{2(8.068)} = 100 + 35\sqrt{16.136} = 100 + 35(4.017) = 100 + 140.6 = 240.6\,\text{nm}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_{\text{peak}} \approx 3.19\times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³; $x_j \approx 241$ nm.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Rephrased]**

### **Question B(a) [7 Marks]**
**Derive the expression for the Gaussian doping profile $N(x)$ of an ion-implanted layer. State the assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The target is amorphous (or pre-amorphized) so scattering is isotropic and there is no channeling.
2. The ion energy is constant across the implanted area (uniform beam).
3. Each ion undergoes a large number of independent collisions → Central Limit Theorem applies.
4. The wafer is semi-infinite and laterally uniform.

**Derivation:**
- Let $x_i$ be the stopping depth of the $i$-th ion. The mean is $R_p = (1/N)\sum x_i$ and the variance is $\Delta R_p^2 = (1/N)\sum (x_i - R_p)^2$.
- The probability density that an ion stops between $x$ and $x + dx$ is the Gaussian:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$
- For a total implanted dose $\Phi$ (ions/cm²), the number of ions in a slab $[x, x+dx]$ per unit area is $\Phi f(x)\,dx$. Dividing by slab thickness $dx$ gives concentration:
$$N(x) = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$$
- Verify conservation: $\int_0^\infty N(x)\,dx = \Phi$.

### **Question B(b) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the channeling effect during ion implantation. How is it controlled? Discuss the role of post-implant annealing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Channeling Effect:** When the ion beam is aligned with a low-index crystallographic axis (e.g., ⟨100⟩), the ions travel through the open spaces between atomic rows with reduced nuclear scattering. This produces a deep "channeling tail" in the doping profile, deeper than predicted by the LSS Gaussian model. Channeling is undesirable because it makes junction depth unpredictable.

**Control Methods:**
1. **Wafer Tilt:** Mount the wafer at 7°–10° off-axis to break the alignment.
2. **Wafer Twist:** Rotate the wafer 22.5° (or 30°) about its normal to avoid planar channeling.
3. **Pre-Amorphization Implant (PAI):** A heavy Si⁺ or Ge⁺ implant at moderate dose renders the surface amorphous, eliminating channeling paths.
4. **Screen Oxide:** A thin SiO₂ layer (~10–20 nm) deposited on the surface also helps amorphize the very top layer.

**Post-Implant Annealing:**
Implanted ions are initially interstitial and electrically inactive. Annealing serves three purposes:
1. **Repair lattice damage** by regrowth of the amorphous region (solid-phase epitaxy).
2. **Electrically activate** dopants by moving them into substitutional lattice sites.
3. **Drive-in / junction formation** — for high-temperature anneal, some diffusion occurs.

Modern techniques: **Spike Anneal (1050 °C, < 1 s)**, **Laser Spike Anneal (LSA, ~1300 °C, ms)**, and **Flash Anneal** — minimize diffusion while maximizing activation (achieving > 10²¹ cm⁻³ active concentration for advanced nodes).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $N_{\text{peak}}$:** Forgetting to convert nm → cm in the denominator of $N_{\text{peak}} = \Phi/(\sqrt{2\pi}\Delta R_p)$ is the **single most common error** — costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $R_p$ with total path length:** $R_p$ is the *projected* range along the surface normal, not the trajectory length.
> 3. **Ignoring channeling in profile derivations:** Examiners often subtract 1 mark if the channeling tail is not mentioned in profile derivations.
> 4. **Writing $x_j$ without the log term:** Many students give $x_j = R_p$ — this is only the *peak depth*, not the *junction depth*. Always include the straggle term.
> 5. **Misspelling "Schiøtt" or "LSS":** Use "Lindhard, Scharff and Schiøtt" verbatim; do not write "LSS theory" without expanding on first mention.
> 6. **Confusing nuclear vs electronic stopping roles:** Examiners expect you to clearly state that $S_n$ causes damage and $S_e$ causes ionization — these roles cannot be swapped.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Read Before Exam**
>
> - **Definition:** Ion implantation = controlled injection of accelerated dopant ions into a semiconductor.
> - **System blocks:** Source → Extraction → Focusing → Mass Analysis → Acceleration → Scanning → End-station.
> - **Mass analyzer formula:** $r = \sqrt{2Vm/q}/B$ — memorize this; it appears in nearly every Part B question.
> - **Gaussian Profile:** $N(x) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right)$
> - **Dose:** $\Phi = I\,t/(qA)$
> - **Junction depth:** $x_j = R_p + \Delta R_p\sqrt{2\ln(N_{\text{peak}}/N_B)}$
> - **LSS theory:** Two stopping channels — nuclear ($S_n$, low energy) and electronic ($S_e$, high energy).
> - **Channeling:** Caused by ion alignment with crystal axis; controlled by 7°–10° tilt + 22° twist + screen oxide + PAI.
> - **Annealing:** Spike / Laser / Flash anneal — repairs damage and activates dopants.
> - **Advantages over diffusion:** Better dose control, low temperature, sharp shallow junctions, masking flexibility.
> - **Industry standards:** Energy 5 keV – 1 MeV; Dose 10¹¹ – 10¹⁶ cm⁻²; Vacuum < 10⁻⁷ Torr.
> - **Modern applications:** Ultra-shallow S/D junctions (7 nm/5 nm FinFETs), SIMOX, Smart-Cut, threshold-voltage adjustment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Isolation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Isolation Techniques** in VLSI technology refer to the set of fabrication methodologies used to electrically, optically, and chemically separate individual active devices (transistors, resistors, capacitors) fabricated on the same monolithic silicon die so that they can operate independently without undesired electrical interaction through the common semiconductor substrate.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, isolation is treated as the *backbone of CMOS process integration* — without it, every adjacent n-channel and p-channel device would form unwanted **parasitic thyristors** (the classic CMOS latch-up path), making reliable digital logic physically impossible. The isolation quality is quantified using three primary metrics:

- **Latch-up immunity** (measured in mA or as a holding voltage)
- **Parasitic capacitance per unit length** (fF/µm)
- **Minimum isolation pitch / scalability** (nm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEBT526 / Module 1):**
> Isolation belongs to the *Front-End-Of-Line (FEOL)* process family. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare and contrast* Junction Isolation, LOCOS, and STI — this is a guaranteed 7–14 mark question in Part B.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Apartment Building

Imagine a high-rise residential apartment building constructed on a single shared concrete foundation. Each flat must function independently — the lights in Flat A must NOT dim when Flat B switches on its air-conditioner. To guarantee this independence, the architect inserts **firewalls and concrete partition walls** between flats.

In an integrated circuit:

| Apartment Building Element | VLSI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Shared concrete slab | Common silicon substrate |
| Partition walls between flats | Isolation regions (oxide, junction) |
| Electrical wiring of one flat | Active device terminals (D, S, G) |
| Firewalls preventing fire spread | p-n junctions blocking leakage current |
| "Bird's beak" tapered firewall top | LOCOS tapered oxide edge |

The *"walls"* in silicon can be built using reverse-biased p-n junctions, thermally grown SiO₂, or refilled dielectric trenches — these are precisely the three principal families of isolation technologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule Reminder:** The minimum width of any isolation region directly decides the **packing density** of the die. Smaller isolation pitch → more transistors per mm² → lower cost per gate. This is why the industry transitioned from Junction → LOCOS → STI across decades.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution (KTU Frequently Asked)

$$\text{JI (1970s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{LOCOS (1980s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Modified LOCOS / SWAMI (late 80s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{STI (1990s -- present)}$$

The driving force at every transition has been the relentless demand to **shrink isolation pitch** while **suppressing leakage** and **minimizing topographic step height** that breaks lithography at advanced nodes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Isolation Needed? — The Physics of Crosstalk

When two n-channel MOSFETs sit in a common p-type well (or substrate), three parasitic interactions become possible:

1. **Parasitic MOS Capacitor Formation** — the interconnect metal forms an unwanted gate over field oxide, possibly inverting the silicon beneath.
2. **Parasitic Source/Drain-to-Substrate Leakage** — minority carriers injected by one device diffuse to the next.
3. **Latch-up** — a parasitic p-n-p-n SCR path is triggered, shorting V_DD to GND.

The role of isolation is to **raise the threshold voltage of the parasitic field-effect transistor** so that it never turns on under normal supply, and to **physically block minority-carrier diffusion** between active regions.

## 2.2 Family 1 — Junction Isolation (JI)

This is the *oldest* and conceptually simplest method, used heavily in the **bipolar IC era** and still taught because it illustrates the core idea of using a **reverse-biased p-n junction depletion region as an insulating barrier**.

**Process Logic Steps:**

- Start with a **p-type (100) silicon wafer** of resistivity typically **5–25 Ω·cm**.
- Epitaxially grow a thin **n-type epitaxial layer** (typically 3–10 µm thick, doping ≈ 10¹⁵ cm⁻³). This layer will eventually contain the active devices.
- Grow a thin pad oxide (~50 nm) followed by a silicon-nitride (Si₃N₄) mask layer (~100 nm) using LPCVD.
- Photolithographically pattern the Si₃N₄, then perform a **deep p⁺ diffusion** (Boron, ~3–5 µm deep) between intended device islands. This diffusion reaches down to the p-substrate.
- A subsequent **n⁺ buried layer** sits beneath the active device for low collector resistance (in bipolar technology).
- The deep p⁺ regions form **reverse-biased p-n junctions** with the n-epi islands, electrically isolating them.

**Why does it work?** A reverse-biased p-n junction has an extremely high dynamic resistance and a depletion width that *expands* with applied voltage — it behaves like a **voltage-dependent capacitor** rather than a conductor.

## 2.3 Family 2 — LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon)

LOCOS is the **workhorse isolation of the 0.5 µm–0.25 µm CMOS generations**, and remains a KTU favourite because it is conceptually rich.

**Process Steps:**

- Grow a thin **pad oxide** (~10–50 nm) on bare silicon. The pad oxide relieves the enormous stress that Si₃N₄ would otherwise exert on the silicon lattice.
- Deposit **Si₃N₄** (~100–200 nm) by LPCVD at ~800 °C.
- Photolithographically pattern the nitride to expose the regions that will become isolation.
- Perform **selective thermal oxidation in steam ambient** at 950–1100 °C. Approximately **2.2 µm of Si is consumed to grow 1 µm of SiO₂** (the volumetric ratio comes from molecular weight and density differences of Si and SiO₂).
- Strip the nitride and pad oxide. The resulting recessed oxide (often called **field oxide**, FOX) electrically isolates the active regions.

**Anatomy of a LOCOS Structure:**

- **Active Area:** Si₃N₄-masked region where devices will be built.
- **Field Oxide:** thick thermal SiO₂ in the isolation region.
- **Bird's Beak:** the lateral tapered transition region where the growing oxide pushes *underneath* the nitride mask. This taper occurs because oxygen diffuses laterally as well as vertically during the wet oxidation step.
- **Kooi Effect / White Ribbon:** a thin oxynitride layer that forms at the Si/SiO₂ interface beneath the nitride edge, caused by ammonia (NH₃) generated from the Si-N reaction with trace H₂O. This can cause inverse-narrow-width effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **bird's beak length** (typically 0.1–0.3 µm) is a *parasitic lateral encroachment* — it eats into your active area. The whole motivation of **modified LOCOS** (SWAMI, SILO, Poly-Buffered LOCOS) is to *suppress* this bird's beak.

## 2.4 Family 3 — Trench Isolation / Shallow Trench Isolation (STI)

The modern industry standard (180 nm and below). Instead of *growing* oxide in a recessed region, STI *etches* a trench and *refills* it with CVD oxide.

**Process Steps:**

- Grow a thin pad oxide (~10 nm) and deposit a Si₃N₄ mask (~200 nm).
- Anisotropically etch a shallow trench (typically **0.3–0.5 µm deep** in modern nodes) using RIE.
- Grow a thin **liner oxide** (~10 nm) on the trench sidewalls to anneal etch damage.
- Deposit a conformal **CVD TEOS oxide** or HDP-CVD oxide to refill the trench without voids.
- Perform **Chemical-Mechanical Polishing (CMP)** stopping on the nitride (this is called the *CMP stop layer*).
- Strip the nitride to leave a planar, near-ideal isolating oxide.

**Why STI dominates modern VLSI:**

- Near-vertical sidewalls → no bird's beak → minimum isolation pitch.
- Planar surface → no topographic step → excellent lithography depth-of-focus.
- Scalable: works at 28 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm, 5 nm nodes.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units / Values | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $BV_{pp} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 q N}$ | One-sided abrupt junction breakdown voltage | V; $E_{crit} \approx 3 \times 10^5$ V/cm for Si | Junction isolation design |
| $W_{dep} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N}}$ | Depletion width of an abrupt junction | cm | Determining isolation width |
| $x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$ | **Deal-Grove** oxide growth law | µm; $B$ is parabolic rate const. | LOCOS oxide thickness |
| $t_{ox} \approx 0.44 \, x_{Si}$ | Si consumed : SiO₂ grown (volumetric ratio) | µm | LOCOS recess calculation |
| $L_{bb} \approx 0.1 \text{--} 0.3$ µm | Bird's beak lateral length | µm | LOCOS encroachment loss |
| $\text{AR} = \dfrac{D_{trench}}{W_{trench}}$ | Trench aspect ratio | dimensionless | STI gap-fill & void check |
| $V_{FB,field} \gg V_{DD}$ | Field-region flat-band criterion | V | Latch-up prevention check |
| $C_{par} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox} W L}{t_{fox}}$ | Field-oxide parasitic capacitance | F | Speed/latency penalty |
| $\rho_s = \dfrac{1}{q \mu_p N_A}$ | Sheet resistance of p⁺ isolation diffusion | Ω/□ | JI leakage path analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-Value Convention:** In every formula above, all instances of magnitude / absolute value are written as plain unbarred symbols ($N$, $E_{crit}$, $V_{bi}$). This is the KTU 2024 Scheme norm — do **NOT** write $\vert N \vert$ inside markdown tables as it breaks the renderer.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs** (Apple M-series, Snapdragon, Dimensity): every transistor pair is isolated by STI.
- **High-voltage power ICs** and **BCD technologies**: still rely on Junction Isolation combined with deep trenches.
- **MEMS devices**: use dielectric / oxide isolation to prevent substrate leakage.
- **RF / Mixed-Signal ICs**: junction-isolated deep n-wells for noise isolation.
- **Automotive (AEC-Q100)**: latch-up immunity (> 100 mA) is mandated — driven directly by isolation quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition in this section is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Each step carries a *Logic Commentary* line so the student understands the *physical reason* behind the math, not just the symbol manipulation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Breakdown Voltage of the Junction-Isolation Wall

We model the deep p⁺ diffusion / n-epi interface as a **one-sided abrupt p⁺-n junction** (p⁺ side is much more heavily doped than the n-side, so the depletion region sits almost entirely in the n-epi).

**Starting Point — Poisson's Equation in 1-D:**

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

where $N_D$ is the n-epi donor concentration (cm⁻³), and the charge density $\rho(x)$ is positive inside the depletion region on the n-side.

**Step 1 — Integrate to obtain the electric field profile:**

$$E(x) = \int_0^x \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} dx' = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} x + C_1$$

**Logic Commentary:** The boundary condition $E(W) = 0$ at the edge of the depletion region $W$ fixes the integration constant. We will apply it after the second integration.

**Step 2 — Integrate again to obtain the potential profile:**

$$V(x) = -\int_0^x E(x') \, dx' = -\frac{q N_D}{2 \varepsilon_s} x^2 - C_1 x + C_2$$
