# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the formation of a thermal SiO₂ layer on a silicon wafer. State the two rate constants of the Deal-Grove model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thermal oxidation is performed by exposing a cleaned silicon wafer to either **dry oxygen** (O₂) at ~1000 °C or **wet oxygen** (H₂O vapor) at ~800–900 °C. The oxygen reacts with the silicon at the Si/SiO₂ interface to form SiO₂:

$$\text{Si (solid)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (solid)}$$

The growth follows the **Deal-Grove model**:

$$x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$$

- **A** = linear rate constant (surface-reaction limited, dominant for thin oxides)
- **B** = parabolic rate constant (diffusion-limited, dominant for thick oxides)

Dry oxidation gives a higher-quality, denser oxide; wet oxidation grows faster but produces a more porous oxide. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Differentiate between NMOS and PMOS transistors. Give two advantages of CMOS over NMOS technology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | NMOS | PMOS |
|---|---|---|
| Channel carriers | Electrons | Holes |
| Source/Drain type | n⁺ in p-substrate | p⁺ in n-substrate |
| Threshold voltage | Positive (typically +0.5 V) | Negative (typically –0.5 V) |
| Carrier mobility | Higher (~1350 cm²/V·s) | Lower (~480 cm²/V·s) |
| Current drive | Higher | Lower |

**Two advantages of CMOS over NMOS:**
1. **Near-zero static power dissipation** (no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state). *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Higher noise margin and superior scalability** to deep-submicron nodes, enabling billions of transistors on a single chip. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2, Q11a]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A (a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, describe the **complete CMOS twin-tub process flow** for fabricating an integrated circuit. List all masks used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CMOS twin-tub process is the most widely used process for fabricating CMOS integrated circuits. The process steps are:

1. **Wafer preparation:** p-type <100> silicon wafer is cleaned using RCA cleaning.
2. **Pad oxide (~10 nm) + Si₃N₄ (~100 nm) deposition** by LPCVD.
3. **Mask 1 (n-well mask):** Photoresist is patterned to open the n-well regions. The nitride is plasma-etched, and **phosphorus is ion-implanted** (high energy, ~150 keV) to form the n-well.
4. **Mask 2 (p-well mask):** The photoresist is re-patterned to expose the p-well regions, and **boron is ion-implanted** to form the p-well.
5. **Well drive-in:** High-temperature (~1100 °C, several hours) anneal diffuses dopants to required depth (~2–4 µm) and heals implant damage.
6. **Mask 3 (Active area mask):** Nitride is patterned; **LOCOS** grows the field oxide (~500 nm) in the field regions for device isolation.
7. **Nitride and pad oxide are stripped**; a **sacrificial oxide** is grown and threshold-adjust implants (B for NMOS, P for PMOS) are performed.
8. **Sacrificial oxide is stripped**, and the **gate oxide** (~5–10 nm) is grown by dry oxidation.
9. **Polysilicon** (~200 nm) is deposited by LPCVD and heavily doped n-type (POCl₃).
10. **Mask 4 (Poly gate mask):** Polysilicon is patterned by anisotropic RIE to form the gate electrodes.
11. **Mask 5 (NMOS S/D mask):** Photoresist protects the n-well (PMOS) region; **n⁺ S/D implant** (As, high dose) is performed using the poly gate as a self-aligned mask.
12. **Spacer formation:** SiO₂ is deposited and anisotropically etched to form sidewall spacers; **LDD n⁻ implant** (low-dose As) precedes the spacer in advanced processes.
13. **Mask 6 (PMOS S/D mask):** Photoresist protects the p-well region; **p⁺ S/D implant** (BF₂) is performed.
14. **Dopant activation anneal** (rapid thermal anneal, ~1000 °C, 5 s).
15. **ILD deposition** (BPSG), CMP planarization, **Mask 7 (Contact mask)** + etch, **tungsten plug** fill, W-CMP.
16. **Mask 8 (Metal-1 mask):** Al (or Cu damascene) interconnect patterning.
17. Repeat for M2, M3… and finally **passivation + Mask N (Bond pad mask)**.
18. **Wafer test, dicing, packaging**.

**Total masks used: ~8 to ~15** depending on process complexity (active, n-well, p-well, poly, n⁺S/D, p⁺S/D, contact, M1, M2, … bond pad). **(7 marks)**

---

**A (b)** Derive the expression for the **long-channel threshold voltage** of an NMOS transistor. Explain the **body effect** and the role of **threshold-adjustment implant**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of $V_T$:**

The threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor on a p-type substrate is the gate voltage at which a strong inversion layer forms at the Si/SiO₂ interface. The derivation proceeds in four steps:

(i) **Fermi potential** of the p-substrate:
$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

(ii) **Flat-band voltage**:
$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}}, \quad C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

(iii) **Maximum depletion width** at the onset of strong inversion (surface potential = $2\phi_F$):
$$x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{Si}(2\phi_F)}{qN_A}}$$

(iv) **Total threshold voltage** (sum of flat-band correction, surface band bending, and oxide voltage drop):
$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{qN_A x_{d,\max}}{C_{ox}} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Body effect:** When a reverse substrate bias $V_{BS} < 0$ is applied, the depletion region widens, requiring a larger gate voltage to achieve inversion. The threshold voltage increases by:
$$\Delta V_T = \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{BS}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right), \quad \gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Threshold-adjustment implant:** A shallow boron (or phosphorus for PMOS) implant is performed before gate oxidation. It raises (or lowers) the channel doping near the Si/SiO₂ interface, allowing precise control of $V_T$ without altering the bulk well doping. This is essential for tuning NMOS and PMOS threshold voltages independently in CMOS technology. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice – 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 1, Q11b]**

**B (a)** Explain the **unit processes** used in MOS IC fabrication with a focus on **oxidation, diffusion, ion implantation, lithography, and etching**. Compare **dry vs wet etching**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Oxidation:** A thermal SiO₂ layer is grown on a Si wafer at 800–1200 °C in either **dry O₂** (high-quality thin oxides used for gate dielectric) or **wet O₂ (H₂O steam)** (faster growth, used for thick field oxides). The Deal-Grove model describes the growth:
$$x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$$

**Diffusion:** Dopant atoms (B, P, As) are introduced from a solid, liquid, or gaseous source at high temperature (~1000 °C). Diffusion follows Fick's laws. It is used for deep well formation and drive-in, but has limited control for shallow junctions.

**Ion Implantation:** Dopant ions are ionized, accelerated (10–200 keV), and physically driven into the silicon. It offers **precise dose control, shallow junction capability, and lateral uniformity**. A subsequent **anneal** (RTA) is required to activate the dopants and repair lattice damage. Key parameters: **projected range $R_p$** and **straggle $\Delta R_p$**.

**Photolithography:** A photoresist is spin-coated, soft-baked, exposed to UV (or EUV) through a mask, and developed. It defines the pattern for subsequent etching or implantation. **Positive resist** becomes soluble upon exposure; **negative resist** becomes insoluble.

**Etching:** Removes material not protected by resist (or hard mask).
- **Wet etching** uses liquid chemicals (e.g., HF for SiO₂, KOH for Si). It is **isotropic**, low-cost, and selective, but cannot define sub-micron features.
- **Dry etching (RIE — Reactive Ion Etching)** uses a plasma of reactive gases. It is **anisotropic** (vertical sidewalls), enabling fine line-width control. Used for poly gate definition, contact hole etching, and modern sub-10 nm nodes.

| Property | Wet Etching | Dry Etching (RIE) |
|---|---|---|
| Directionality | Isotropic | Anisotropic |
| Line-width control | Poor (undercutting) | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Selectivity | High | Moderate |
| Throughput | High | Moderate |
| Use case | Field oxide, bulk Si | Poly gate, contact, vias |

**(7 marks)**

---

**B (b)** A p-type silicon wafer with $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³ has a 10 nm thick SiO₂ gate oxide. An n⁺ polysilicon gate is used. Calculate the threshold voltage. Take $Q_{ss}/q = 5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻², $n_i = 1.45 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $\phi_{MS} = -0.95$ V, $T = 300$ K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fermi potential:**
$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$
$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times \ln(6.897 \times 10^5) = 0.02585 \times 13.446 = 0.3476 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Oxide capacitance per unit area:**
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F/cm}}{10 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Flat-band voltage:**
$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}} = -0.95 - \frac{(5 \times 10^{10})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$V_{FB} = -0.95 - \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-9}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = -0.95 - 0.0232 = -0.9732 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold voltage expression:**
$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

Compute the square-root term:
$$2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A(2\phi_F) = 2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times (2 \times 0.3476)$$
$$= 2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times 0.6952$$
$$= 2.316 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$\sqrt{2.316 \times 10^{-15}} = 4.812 \times 10^{-8}$$

Divide by $C_{ox}$:
$$\frac{4.812 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.1395 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Final threshold voltage:**
$$V_T = -0.9732 + 2(0.3476) + 0.1395 = -0.9732 + 0.6952 + 0.1395$$
$$\boxed{V_T = -0.1385 \text{ V}}$$

**Note:** The negative threshold voltage indicates a **depletion-mode** device (channel is already present at $V_{GS} = 0$). In practice, the threshold-adjustment implant and work-function engineering are tuned to give a positive $V_T \approx +0.5$ V for an enhancement-mode NMOS. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up A and B constants in the Deal-Grove model.** Memorize: A is *linear* (reaction-limited), B is *parabolic* (diffusion-limited).
> 2. **Sign errors in $V_{FB}$ and $V_T$.** For an n⁺ poly gate on p-substrate, $\phi_{MS}$ is *negative*; ensure you don't accidentally flip its sign.
> 3. **Confusing "source" and "drain" in MOSFETS.** Convention: the source is the terminal at lower potential for NMOS, higher for PMOS. The doping type is the same (both n⁺ or both p⁺).
> 4. **Skipping the self-aligned nature of the gate.** Examiners specifically test whether you understand why MOSFETs can be scaled to nanometer dimensions while bipolar transistors cannot.
> 5. **In CMOS, the body of NMOS is p-well and PMOS is n-well — don't mix them up.** The two well regions must be properly isolated using field oxide or shallow-trench isolation (STI).
> 6. **Forgetting the threshold-adjustment implant.** Examiners often ask why a separate implant is used when the well itself could be tuned. The answer: tuning the well changes the body factor $\gamma$ and degrades the body effect; the surface implant controls $V_T$ while keeping $\gamma$ and junction capacitance low.
> 7. **Units trap:** Resistivity is in Ω·cm, doping in cm⁻³, oxide thickness in cm (for CGS) or nm. Convert consistently before substituting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MOS = Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor** structure; modern "metal" gate is actually heavily doped polysilicon or a metal stack (TiN, TaN, W) over a high-$\kappa$ dielectric (HfO₂, ZrO₂).
- **NMOS = n-channel device on p-substrate**; current carried by electrons.
- **PMOS = p-channel device on n-substrate**; current carried by holes; mobility is ~2.8× lower.
- **CMOS = NMOS + PMOS on the same chip**; the foundation of modern VLSI.
- **Six unit processes** of planar technology: **Oxidation, Diffusion, Ion Implantation, Lithography, Etching, Deposition/Metallization**.
- **Self-aligned gate** is the key innovation enabling MOSFET scaling; the gate acts as its own mask for S/D implantation.
- **LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon)** is the traditional isolation technique; **STI (Shallow Trench Isolation)** is used in modern sub-micron nodes for better planarity and density.
- **Threshold voltage equation:** $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$
- **Body effect:** $\Delta V_T = \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{BS}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ with $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$.
- **Deal-Grove oxidation law:** $x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$. **A** = linear constant; **B** = parabolic constant.
- **Twin-tub CMOS process** allows independent optimization of NMOS (p-well) and PMOS (n-well) — the industry-standard process since the late 1980s.
- **Typical mask count:** ~8–15 for a modern CMOS process; EUV lithography steps add to cost but enable sub-7 nm features.
- **LDD (Lightly Doped Drain)** and **Halo implants** are used to mitigate **hot-carrier effects** and **short-channel effects** in deep-submicron devices.
- **Al interconnect** is being replaced by **Cu (copper) damascene** with a Ta/TaN barrier; Cu has ~40% lower resistivity and superior electromigration lifetime.
- **Static power ≈ 0** for CMOS in steady state → the reason CMOS dominates battery-powered and high-density digital design.
- **Key constants:** $n_i = 1.45 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³; $kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K; $\varepsilon_{ox}/\varepsilon_0 = 3.9$; $\varepsilon_{Si}/\varepsilon_0 = 11.7$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU board exam:** Be prepared to draw the **CMOS cross-section** showing both NMOS and PMOS, label the two wells, the gate oxide, the poly gate, the n⁺/p⁺ S/D regions, the field oxide, and the metal contacts. Examiners award 2–3 marks for a clean, fully labeled cross-sectional diagram alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS n-Well and p-Well Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS n-Well and p-Well Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CMOS (Complementary Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor) technology** is the dominant VLSI fabrication paradigm in which both **n-channel (NMOS)** and **p-channel (PMOS)** transistors are fabricated on the same silicon die. Because NMOS requires a **p-type body** and PMOS requires an **n-type body**, the fabrication process must create two oppositely doped regions (called *wells* or *tubs*) on a single substrate.

The two classical single-well processes are:

- **n-Well Process:** NMOS transistors are fabricated **directly in the p-type substrate**, while PMOS transistors are placed inside a separately diffused **n-Well**. The p-substrate is biased at **V_SS (0 V)**, and the n-Well is biased at **V_DD**.
- **p-Well Process:** PMOS transistors sit in the **n-type substrate**, and NMOS transistors are built inside a **p-Well** diffused into it. The n-substrate is biased at **V_DD**, and the p-Well is biased at **V_SS (0 V)**.

A modern extension, the **Twin-Well (Dual-Well) Process**, simultaneously forms both n-Well and p-Well in a lightly doped epitaxial layer, allowing independent optimization of transistor characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course *PEEBT526 – VLSI Design* requires students to clearly distinguish between **n-Well**, **p-Well**, and **Twin-Well (Bi-CMOS compatible)** processes and to identify the **cross-section, biasing, and process step sequence** for each. A typical 14-mark question asks for a labelled cross-section *and* a process flow.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a large two-storey apartment building sitting on a **flat concrete slab (the silicon substrate)**. The slab itself is the **p-type floor** in an n-Well process — strong enough to host ground-floor families (NMOS devices) without modification.

To accommodate the **second family (PMOS devices)** on the same slab, the architects first **pour a raised platform made of opposite-coloured concrete (the n-Well)** on top of the slab. Now, the **first family lives directly on the slab**, while the **second family lives on the raised platform**, both sharing the same building but on materially different foundations. The plumbing (metal interconnects) connects both families through staircases (vias) — analogous to the **metal layers and contact cuts** in CMOS.

In a **p-Well process**, the slab is *n-type*, and the special platform built on top is *p-type* — the roles are simply inverted. In a **Twin-Well process**, two platforms (one of each type) are poured side-by-side on a *neutral* lightweight foundation (the epitaxial layer), giving the architects the freedom to design each family’s home optimally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The well is essentially a **region of opposite doping** carved into the substrate so that both transistor polarities can coexist on a single silicon chip. The choice of which type of well to form dictates the biasing scheme and the threshold-voltage tuning for both devices.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following parameters must be memorised for the KTU board exam (with standard values at **T = 300 K**):

- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ (≈ 26 mV at 300 K)
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F/cm}$
- **Relative permittivity of Si:** $\varepsilon_{Si} = \mathbf{11.7}$
- **Relative permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = \mathbf{3.9}$
- **Typical SiO₂ thickness:** $t_{ox} \approx 2\text{–}10\ \text{nm}$ (modern nodes)
- **Typical well doping concentration:** $N_{well} \approx 10^{16}\text{–}10^{17}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- **Typical substrate doping:** $N_{sub} \approx 10^{14}\text{–}10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The n-Well CMOS Process — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The n-Well process is the **industry-standard** flow because NMOS carriers (electrons) have a mobility **μ_n ≈ 2.5 × μ_p** higher than PMOS carriers (holes). Placing NMOS directly in the *low-doped, low-defect p-substrate* yields superior carrier mobility. The PMOS, with intrinsically lower mobility, is relegated to the more heavily doped n-Well where its slower performance is acceptable.

**Why n-Well?**
- The p-substrate is tied to **V_SS = 0 V**, which automatically **eliminates the body effect** for NMOS transistors when their sources are also at 0 V.
- The n-Well is tied to **V_DD**, so the body-to-source voltage of the PMOS is fixed at 0 V (no body effect).
- Process complexity is lower: **only one well-implant mask** is required.

**Why NMOS in the substrate and not in a well?**
Because electrons are the faster carriers, NMOS must sit in the **highest-quality, lowest-doped region** to maximise mobility and minimise junction capacitance. The starting p-substrate (or p-epitaxial layer) satisfies this requirement inherently.

## 2.2 The p-Well CMOS Process — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The p-Well process was historically the **first CMOS process** developed (by RCA in the 1960s). The roles are inverted:

- The **starting wafer is n-type** (resistivity ≈ 5 Ω·cm).
- An **NMOS transistor is built inside a p-Well** diffused into the n-substrate.
- The **PMOS transistor is built directly in the n-substrate**.

**Disadvantages of the p-Well process:**
- The **NMOS body** is now inside a diffused well, which is **more heavily doped** than the substrate — this **increases body-effect coefficient γ** and degrades NMOS performance.
- The PMOS sits in the n-substrate, which is typically of moderate doping, but **junction capacitance to the body is higher** for PMOS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Why is the n-Well process preferred over the p-Well process?"* — answer with **three points**: (1) better NMOS performance due to placement in the lightly doped substrate, (2) elimination of body effect for NMOS, and (3) lower junction capacitance on the NMOS side.

## 2.3 Process Step Sequence (Common CMOS Fab Flow)

Regardless of the well type, every CMOS process follows the **Seven Universal Module Sequence**:

1. **Substrate Preparation** — Starting wafer (p-type for n-Well process, n-type for p-Well process); optional epitaxial layer growth.
2. **Well Formation** — Mask, photolithography, ion implantation, and high-temperature drive-in to form the well region.
3. **Isolation** — Either **LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon)** with a *pad oxide + nitride mask*, or **STI (Shallow Trench Isolation)** in sub-0.25 μm nodes.
4. **Active Area Definition** — Patterning the regions where transistors will be built.
5. **Gate Oxide Growth and Gate Electrode Formation** — Thermal oxidation of SiO₂, followed by poly-Si deposition, doping, and patterning.
6. **Source/Drain Formation** — n⁺ and p⁺ implants (with LDD spacers in short-channel devices).
7. **Contact, Metallisation, and Passivation** — Silicidation, contact cuts, Al/Cu metallisation, and final Si₃N₄ or SiO₂ passivation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential of p-type bulk (use $N_D$ for n-type with a minus sign) | V |
| 2 | $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold voltage with body effect (NMOS in p-substrate or p-Well) | V |
| 3 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient — quantifies sensitivity of $V_{TH}$ to $V_{SB}$ | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate-oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9\,\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of SiO₂ (constant) | F/cm |
| 6 | $R_{sheet} = \dfrac{1}{q\,\mu\,N_A\,t}$ | Sheet resistance of a uniformly doped layer (well, poly, diffusion) | Ω/□ |
| 7 | $C_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}{2(V_{bi} - V)}}$ | Bottom-wall junction capacitance per unit area (zero-bias form) | F/cm² |
| 8 | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | Built-in potential of a p–n junction | V |
| 9 | $x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\varepsilon_{Si}\phi_F}{qN_A}}$ | Maximum depletion width in p-substrate (one-sided junction) | cm |
| 10 | $\mu_n \approx \mathbf{1350\ cm^2/V\cdot s}$ ; $\mu_p \approx \mathbf{480\ cm^2/V\cdot s}$ | Bulk mobilities in lightly doped Si at 300 K | cm²/V·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline rule for KTU derivations:** When asked to derive the *body-effect coefficient*, always start from Poisson’s equation, integrate twice across the depletion region, and apply Gauss’s law at the oxide–silicon interface. **Do not skip the boundary condition** $V(0)=0$ at the neutral bulk — that is a common board-exam trap.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The choice between n-Well, p-Well, and Twin-Well is **not merely academic** — it directly impacts:

- **Circuit Performance:** Drive current $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ depends on the **mobility μ of the carrier**, which is highest in the lowest-doped body. This is why NMOS-in-substrate is faster.
- **Latch-up Immunity:** Twin-Well processes with a heavily doped buried layer and retrograde well profiles offer the best **latch-up robustness** by reducing the gain of the parasitic SCR (thyristor) inherent in CMOS.
- **Mixed-Signal / Bi-CMOS Integration:** Twin-Well flows are mandatory for integrating high-performance NPN and PNP bipolar transistors alongside CMOS, since each BJT polarity requires its own well of opposite type.
- **Scaling and Short-Channel Control:** Retrograde well profiles (peak doping below the surface) suppress punch-through and improve DIBL — a feature available only with ion-implanted twin wells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Body-Effect Coefficient γ

**Objective:** Derive an expression for the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ for an NMOS transistor formed in a p-type substrate (or p-Well) of uniform doping $N_A$, given that the source is tied to bulk and a reverse bias $V_{SB} \geq 0$ is applied between source and body.

### Step 1 — Set up Poisson’s Equation in the Depletion Region

The one-dimensional Poisson equation in the depleted p-region (charge density $\rho = -qN_A$) is

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_{Si}} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Explanation:** The depletion region is ionised, so each acceptor contributes a fixed negative charge; Poisson’s equation converts that charge density into the curvature of the electrostatic potential.

### Step 2 — Integrate Once to Obtain the Electric Field

Applying the boundary condition that the electric field vanishes at the edge of the depletion region ($x = x_d$),

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}(x_d - x)$$

**Explanation:** The constant of integration is fixed by the physical requirement that the field goes to zero where mobile carriers reappear at the neutral bulk.

### Step 3 — Integrate Again to Obtain the Potential

Integrating from the surface ($x=0$) to the neutral bulk ($x = x_d$),

$$V(x_d) - V(0) = -\int_{0}^{x_d}\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}(x_d - x)\,dx = -\frac{qN_A x_d^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Explanation:** The total band-bending across the depletion region is negative because the surface is in depletion. We now identify the surface potential with $\phi_s$ and the bulk potential as the reference (0 V).

### Step 4 — Express the Surface Potential in Terms of Depletion Width

The surface potential $\phi_s$ at the onset of strong inversion equals $2\phi_F$. For an applied reverse bias $V_{SB}$ between source and body, the total potential drop becomes $2\phi_F + V_{SB}$:

$$2\phi_F + V_{SB} = \frac{qN_A x_d^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Explanation:** The added term $V_{SB}$ widens the depletion region because the source-to-body junction becomes more reverse-biased, sweeping more majority carriers away from the surface.

### Step 5 — Solve for the Depletion Charge per Unit Area

The depletion charge magnitude per unit area is $Q_B = qN_A x_d$. Substituting the expression for $x_d$ from Step 4,

$$Q_B = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **total ionised acceptor charge** per unit area, a key quantity that the gate must balance through its own induced charge.

### Step 6 — Define the Body-Effect Coefficient

The body-effect coefficient is defined as the constant of proportionality between $Q_B$ and the square-root factor:

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

such that

$$Q_B = \gamma C_{ox}\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$

**Explanation:** Dividing $Q_B$ by $C_{ox}$ gives the equivalent voltage that must be added to the gate voltage to compensate for the body charge — this extra voltage is precisely the **threshold-voltage shift** $\Delta V_{TH}$.

### Step 7 — Final Threshold Voltage with Body Effect

Combining the flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$, the surface potential $2\phi_F$, and the body-effect term,

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

which can be compactly written as

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

**Explanation:** The first three terms $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F}$ are collected into the zero-bias threshold $V_{TH0}$. The last term is the additional shift when the body is reverse-biased with respect to the source.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — n-Well vs p-Well vs Twin-Well

| Parameter | n-Well Process | p-Well Process | Twin-Well (Dual-Well) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Starting substrate** | p-type (≈ 10¹⁵ cm⁻³) | n-type (≈ 10¹⁵ cm⁻³) | p⁻ epitaxial on p⁺ substrate |
| **NMOS body** | p-substrate (direct) | p-Well (diffused) | p-Well (independently doped) |
| **PMOS body** | n-Well (diffused) | n-substrate (direct) | n-Well (independently doped) |
| **NMOS body bias** | V_SS = 0 V (no body effect) | V_SS = 0 V via p-Well contact | V_SS = 0 V via p-Well contact |
| **PMOS body bias** | V_DD via n-Well contact | V_DD (no body effect) | V_DD via n-Well contact |
| **NMOS mobility** | **High** (low-doped substrate) | **Lower** (doped p-Well) | **High** (optimised p-Well) |
| **PMOS mobility** | Moderate (doped n-Well) | Moderate (n-substrate) | Moderate (optimised n-Well) |
| **Body-effect coefficient γ (NMOS)** | **Small** (≈ 0.3 V$^{1/2}$) | **Larger** (≈ 0.5 V$^{1/2}$) | Tunable per device |
| **Junction capacitance (NMOS)** | **Low** | Higher | Low (retrograde well) |
| **Latch-up immunity** | Moderate | Moderate | **High** (with buried layer) |
| **Mask count** | Lower | Lower | **Higher** (extra well mask) |
| **Process complexity** | Simple | Simple | **Complex** |
| **Bi-CMOS compatibility** | Partial | Partial | **Full** |
| **Industry preference** | **Industry standard since 1980s** | Legacy | Modern sub-100 nm nodes |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Threshold Voltage Calculation

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor is fabricated in a p-Well of doping $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$. The gate oxide is SiO₂ with thickness $t_{ox} = 10\ \text{nm}$. The source is at 0 V and the body is at $V_{SB} = 1.5\ \text{V}$. Find $\gamma$ and the threshold voltage shift $\Delta V_{TH}$. Take $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ and $V_{TH0} = 0.7\ \text{V}$ at zero body bias.

**Step 1 — Compute the Fermi potential**

$$\phi_F = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)\ \text{V}$$

Calculating the natural log: $\ln(3.33 \times 10^{6}) = \ln(3.33) + 6\ln(10) = 1.204 + 13.816 = 15.02$

Therefore $\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 15.02 \approx 0.388\ \text{V}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the gate-oxide capacitance**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F/cm}}{10 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the body-effect coefficient**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{Si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \times 5 \times 10^{16}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-8}}$$

The numerator inside the square root: $2 \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 5\times10^{16} = 2 \times 1.6 \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 5 \times 10^{-17} \approx 16.58 \times 10^{-15}$

Numerator: $\sqrt{1.658 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.288 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C/cm}^{3/2}$

Therefore $\gamma = 1.288 \times 10^{-7} / 3.45 \times 10^{-8} \approx 3.73 \times 10^{0} \cdot \text{V}^{1/2}\cdot\text{—}$ — *wait, recompute the units*:

$\gamma$ has units of V$^{1/2}$ since $C_{ox}$ has units of F/cm² = C/(V·cm²) and the numerator has units of C/cm$^{3/2}$. The ratio gives V$^{1/2}$/cm$^{1/2}$… but per unit area the cm² cancels, so $\gamma$ is in V$^{1/2}$ — that is the standard convention.

$$\gamma \approx \frac{1.288 \times 10^{-7}}{3.45 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.73\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the threshold voltage shift**

$$\Delta V_{TH} = \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right) = 3.73 \cdot \left(\sqrt{2(0.388) + 1.5} - \sqrt{2(0.388)}\right)$$

$$= 3.73 \cdot \left(\sqrt{2.276} - \sqrt{0.776}\right) = 3.73 \cdot (1.509 - 0.881) = 3.73 \times 0.628 \approx 2.34\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Final threshold voltage**

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \Delta V_{TH} = 0.7 + 2.34 = 3.04\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The very large $\Delta V_{TH}$ illustrates why **minimising $V_{SB}$** and keeping $N_A$ small is critical for low-power, low-voltage CMOS design. In sub-100 nm nodes, retrograde wells and halo implants are used to control short-channel effects while keeping the **surface** doping low.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Section of an n-Well CMOS Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nwell_cross_section["n-WELL CMOS CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction TB
        vdd["VDD Metal Rail - Top"]
        metal_pmos["Metal Contact - PMOS Drain - Source"]
        metal_nmos["Metal Contact - NMOS Drain - Source"]
        poly_pmos["Poly-Si Gate - PMOS"]
        poly_nmos["Poly-Si Gate - NMOS"]
        oxide_pmos["Gate Oxide SiO2 - PMOS"]
        oxide_nmos["Gate Oxide SiO2 - NMOS"]
        pplus_s["p+ Source-Drain - PMOS"]
        nplus_s["n+ Source-Drain - NMOS"]
        nwell_region["n-Well Region - PMOS Body - Doped 1e16 cm-3"]
        psub_region["p-Substrate - NMOS Body - Doped 1e15 cm-3"]
        nwell_contact["n+ Well Contact - Tied to VDD"]
        psub_contact["p+ Substrate Contact - Tied to VSS"]
        sti_left["STI Oxide Isolation"]
        sti_right["STI Oxide Isolation"]
        vss["VSS Metal Rail - Bottom"]
    end

    vdd --> metal_pmos
    vdd --> nwell_contact
    metal_pmos --> pplus_s
    pplus_s --> nwell_region
    nwell_region --> nsub_side["n+ Source Drain p+ - Right Side"]
    nwell_region --> poly_pmos
    poly_pmos --> oxide_pmos
    oxide_pmos --> psub_top["Surface Channel Interface"]

    psub_region --> nplus_s
    nplus_s --> metal_nmos
    psub_region --> psub_contact
    psub_contact --> vss
    nplus_s --> poly_nmos
    poly_nmos --> oxide_nmos
    oxide_nmos --> psub_region

    sti_left -.-> nwell_region
    sti_right -.-> psub_region
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *left half* of the chip contains the **PMOS** transistor — note the p⁺ source/drain diffusions sitting inside the **n-Well** that is contacted from the top to **V_DD**. The *right half* contains the **NMOS** — its n⁺ source/drain sits directly in the **p-Substrate** that is grounded via the substrate contact. A thick field oxide (STI) electrically isolates the two halves.

## 4.2 Cross-Section of a p-Well CMOS Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pwell_cross_section["p-WELL CMOS CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction TB
        vdd_top["VDD Metal Rail - Top"]
        metal_pmos_left["Metal Contact - PMOS Drain - Source"]
        metal_nmos_right["Metal Contact - NMOS Drain - Source"]
        poly_pmos_left["Poly-Si Gate - PMOS"]
        poly_nmos_right["Poly-Si Gate - NMOS"]
        oxide_pmos_left["Gate Oxide - PMOS"]
        oxide_nmos_right["Gate Oxide - NMOS"]
        pplus_sd["p+ Source-Drain - PMOS"]
        nplus_sd["n+ Source-Drain - NMOS"]
        nsub_main["n-Substrate - PMOS Body - Doped 1e15 cm-3"]
        pwell_inner["p-Well Region - NMOS Body - Doped 1e16 cm-3"]
        nsub_contact["n+ Substrate Contact - Tied to VDD"]
        pwell_contact["p+ Well Contact - Tied to VSS"]
        sti_a["STI Oxide Isolation"]
        sti_b["STI Oxide Isolation"]
        vss_btm["VSS Metal Rail - Bottom"]
    end

    vdd_top --> metal_pmos_left
    vdd_top --> nsub_contact
    metal_pmos_left --> pplus_sd
    pplus_sd --> nsub_main
    nsub_main --> poly_pmos_left
    poly_pmos_left --> oxide_pmos_left
    oxide_pmos_left --> pplus_sd

    pwell_inner --> nplus_sd
    nplus_sd --> metal_nmos_right
    pwell_inner --> pwell_contact
    pwell_contact --> vss_btm
    nplus_sd --> poly_nmos_right
    poly_nmos_right --> oxide_nmos_right
    oxide_nmos_right --> pwell_inner

    nsub_main -.-> sti_a
    pwell_inner -.-> sti_b
```

**Reading the diagram:** The polarity is **inverted** compared to the n-Well cross-section. The PMOS sits in the *n-type substrate* (left), and the NMOS sits in a *p-Well* diffused into the substrate (right). The p-Well is heavily doped relative to the substrate, which is the root cause of the higher body-effect coefficient for NMOS in this process.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Twin-Well (Dual-Well) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Step 1 - p- Substrate Start"] --> a2["Step 2 - Grow p- Epitaxial Layer"]
    a2 --> a3["Step 3 - Buried n+ Layer Implant"]
    a3 --> a4["Step 4 - Pattern n-Well Mask"]
    a4 --> a5["Step 5 - Phosphorus Implant for n-Well"]
    a5 --> a6["Step 6 - Pattern p-Well Mask"]
    a6 --> a7["Step 7 - Boron Implant for p-Well"]
    a7 --> a8["Step 8 - High Temp Drive-In for Wells"]
    a8 --> a9["Step 9 - LOCOS or STI Isolation"]
    a9 --> a10["Step 10 - Active Area Patterning"]
    a10 --> a11["Step 11 - Gate Oxide Growth"]
    a11 --> a12["Step 12 - Poly-Si Deposition and Doping"]
    a12 --> a13["Step 13 - Gate Patterning"]
    a13 --> a14["Step 14 - LDD n- and p- Implants"]
    a14 --> a15["Step 15 - Spacer Formation"]
    a15 --> a16["Step 16 - n+ and p+ Source Drain Implants"]
    a16 --> a17["Step 17 - Silicidation"]
    a17 --> a18["Step 18 - Contact and Metallisation"]
    a18 --> a19["Step 19 - Passivation and Test"]
```

**Reading the flow:** Notice that Steps 4–8 form the **two wells sequentially**, while Steps 9–16 are common CMOS finishing steps. The buried n⁺ layer (Step 3) is the key latch-up suppression feature unique to twin-well CMOS.

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Which Process to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start - Choose CMOS Process"]
    q1{"Does circuit require Bi-CMOS integration?"}
    a1["Use Twin-Well Process"]
    a2["Continue evaluation"]
    q2{"Is NMOS performance the bottleneck?"}
    a3["Use n-Well Process - Industry Standard"]
    a4["Continue evaluation"]
    q3{"Is legacy p-substrate wafer inventory available?"}
    a5["Use p-Well Process"]
    a6["Default to n-Well Process"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- "Yes" --> a1
    q1 -- "No" --> q2
    q2 -- "Yes" --> a3
    q2 -- "No" --> q3
    q3 -- "Yes" --> a5
    q3 -- "No" --> a6
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> Differentiate between an n-Well and a p-Well CMOS process. Mention at least **three** distinguishing points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | n-Well Process | p-Well Process |
|---|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Starting wafer is **p-type substrate** | Starting wafer is **n-type substrate** |
| 2 | NMOS is built **directly in the p-substrate**; PMOS is in a separately diffused **n-Well** | PMOS is built **directly in the n-substrate**; NMOS is in a separately diffused **p-Well** |
| 3 | NMOS body is the lightly doped substrate → **high mobility, low body effect** | NMOS body is the heavily doped p-Well → **lower mobility, larger body effect** |
| 4 | Industry standard for sub-micron nodes | Used in early CMOS era; now legacy |

*(Each valid contrasting point = 1 mark; full credit for 3 contrasting points.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Retest]**
> What is the **body-effect coefficient γ**? Write the expression and explain the role of $C_{ox}$ and $N_A$ in determining it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ quantifies the **sensitivity of the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ to a reverse body-to-source bias $V_{SB}$** in a MOS transistor.

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

- $N_A$ appears **inside the square root**: doubling $N_A$ increases $\gamma$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$, which **worsens** body effect. (1 mark)
- $C_{ox}$ appears in the **denominator**: a thicker oxide (smaller $C_{ox}$) increases $\gamma$, again worsening body effect. (1 mark)
- A **small $\gamma$ is desired** for stable, predictable threshold voltage; this is achieved with a **thin gate oxide** and **low channel doping**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive n-Well Process Question

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *(a)* With the help of a **neat labelled cross-sectional diagram**, explain the **n-Well CMOS process**. Mention the biasing of the substrate and the well. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Derive the **threshold voltage equation** of an NMOS transistor including the **body effect**, starting from Poisson’s equation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Substrate choice:** A p-type silicon wafer of doping $N_A \approx 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ is taken as the starting material. An optional p⁻ epitaxial layer is grown to suppress latch-up. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — n-Well formation:** A layer of SiO₂ is thermally grown, photolithography is performed using the **n-Well mask**, and **phosphorus ions** are implanted at energies of 100–200 keV to a dose of $10^{12}\text{–}10^{13}\ \text{cm}^{-2}$. A high-temperature drive-in (1100 °C, several hours) diffuses the phosphorus to a depth of 1–3 μm. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — LOCOS/STI isolation:** A pad oxide + Si₃N₄ stack is deposited, patterned, and field oxide (≈ 500–800 nm) is grown in the exposed silicon regions to electrically isolate the active islands. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 4 — Active area and gate stack:** After stripping the nitride, a thin gate oxide (5–10 nm) is grown thermally, followed by LPCVD poly-Si deposition, n⁺ doping of the poly (for NMOS work-function tuning), and gate patterning. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — S/D formation:** LDD implants, sidewall spacer formation, then n⁺ (arsenic) and p⁺ (boron) source/drain implants. The n⁺ regions are masked during the p⁺ step and vice versa. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 6 — Contacts and metallisation:** Silicidation (TiSi₂ or CoSi₂), BPSG deposition, contact cuts, Al/Cu metal-1, inter-metal dielectric, and subsequent metal layers. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 7 — Cross-section description (use the diagram in Section 4.1 as reference):** Identify the **p-substrate** (NMOS body, biased to V_SS = 0 V), the **n-Well** (PMOS body, biased to V_DD), the **n⁺ source/drain** of the NMOS, the **p⁺ source/drain** of the PMOS, the **poly-Si gates**, the **gate oxide**, and the **STI/LOCOS field oxide**. Also label the **n⁺ well contact** and the **p⁺ substrate contact**. *(2 marks for the neat labelled diagram and the description)*

**Biasing summary (1 mark):**
- p-Substrate: V_SS = 0 V (through p⁺ substrate contact)
- n-Well: V_DD (through n⁺ well contact)
- This ensures both p–n junctions (substrate-to-nWell and source-to-body) are always reverse-biased, eliminating forward conduction and latch-up.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

*(This is a complete, board-ready derivation — every line earns credit.)*

**Step 1 — Poisson’s equation in the depletion region (1 mark):**

The 1-D Poisson equation in the depleted p-region (charge density $\rho = -qN_A$):

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_{Si}} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Step 2 — Integration for the electric field (1 mark):**

Integrating once and using the boundary condition $\mathcal{E}(x_d) = 0$:

$$\mathcal{E}(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}(x_d - x)$$

**Step 3 — Surface potential (1 mark):**

Integrating the field from the surface ($x=0$) to the depletion edge ($x = x_d$):

$$\phi_s = \frac{qN_A x_d^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

At strong inversion with source-to-body bias $V_{SB}$, $\phi_s = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$:

$$2\phi_F + V_{SB} = \frac{qN_A x_d^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_{Si}}$$

**Step 4 — Depletion charge (1 mark):**

$$Q_B = qN_A x_d = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Step 5 — Body-effect coefficient (1 mark):**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}, \quad \text{so} \quad Q_B = \gamma C_{ox}\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$

**Step 6 — Threshold voltage (1 mark):**

The gate must support the flat-band voltage, the surface potential at inversion, and the body charge:

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form (1 mark):**

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

where $V_{TH0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $\mathcal{E}(x_d) = 0$ — without it, the constant of integration is undefined and the whole derivation collapses. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Sign error** in Poisson’s equation: the charge density is *negative* in a p-region, so the second derivative of potential is *positive* (concave up). Mixing up the sign gives a negative $Q_B$ — *physically impossible*. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Conflating $V_{TH0}$ with $V_{TH}$** when a body bias is present — the question explicitly says *"including body effect"*, so the final answer **must** contain the square-root difference term.
> 4. **Drawing the cross-section without showing the well contact and substrate contact** — these are mandatory to score the full 2 marks for the diagram.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]**
> *(a)* Explain the **p-Well CMOS process** with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Why is the **n-Well process preferred** over the p-Well process in modern VLSI? *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A p-Well of doping $N_A = 8 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ is used as the body of an NMOS. The gate oxide is 8 nm thick, $V_{FB} = -0.9\ \text{V}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, and $V_{SB} = 1.2\ \text{V}$. Calculate the **zero-bias threshold voltage** $V_{TH0}$ and the **shifted threshold voltage** $V_{TH}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — p-Well process definition (1 mark):**
The p-Well CMOS process uses an **n-type silicon wafer** as the starting substrate. The **NMOS transistor is built inside a p-Well** that is diffused into the n-substrate, while the **PMOS transistor is built directly in the n-substrate**.

**Step 2 — Cross-section description (2 marks):**
Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2. Label: n-Substrate (PMOS body, biased to V_DD), p-Well (NMOS body, biased to V_SS = 0 V), n⁺ source/drain of NMOS inside the p-Well, p⁺ source/drain of PMOS in the n-substrate, n⁺ substrate contact (tied to V_DD), p⁺ well contact (tied to V_SS), STI/LOCOS field oxide, and poly-Si gates.

**Step 3 — Process flow highlights (1 mark):**
Steps: n-substrate start → p-Well mask + boron implant + drive-in → LOCOS isolation → gate oxide + poly-Si gate → n⁺ S/D (for NMOS in p-Well) → p⁺ S/D (for PMOS in n-sub) → contacts and metallisation.

**Step 4 — Why n-Well process is preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Better NMOS performance** — NMOS in n-Well process sits in the *low-doped p-substrate*, giving higher electron mobility (~1350 cm²/V·s) and lower junction capacitance. In p-Well process, NMOS sits in a *doped p-Well*, suffering reduced mobility and higher capacitance. *(1 mark)*
2. **Elimination of body effect for NMOS** — The p-substrate is tied to V_SS, and the NMOS source is also typically at V_SS, so $V_{SB} = 0$, giving no body-induced threshold shift. *(1 mark)*
3. **Process simplicity and scaling** — n-Well process requires only one well-mask and integrates well with retrograde well profiles and shallow trench isolation, making it scalable to deep submicron nodes. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Fermi potential (1 mark):**

$$\phi_F = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(5.33 \times 10^6)$$

$\ln(5.33 \times 10^6) = \ln(5.33) + 6\ln(10) = 1.674 + 13.816 = 15.490$

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 15.490 \approx 0.400\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Oxide capacitance (1 mark):**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{8 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-13}}{8 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.32 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Body-effect coefficient (1.5 marks):**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 8\times10^{16}}}{4.32 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Numerator inside the square root: $2 \times 1.6 \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 8 \times 10^{-17} = 26.53 \times 10^{-15}$

$\sqrt{2.653 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.629 \times 10^{-7}$

$$\gamma = \frac{1.629 \times 10^{-7}}{4.32 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.377\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-bias threshold voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{TH0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F} = -0.9 + 2(0.400) + 0.377 \times \sqrt{0.800}$$

$= -0.9 + 0.800 + 0.377 \times 0.894 = -0.9 + 0.800 + 0.337 = 0.237\ \text{V}$

**Step 5 — Shifted threshold voltage (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V_{TH} = \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right) = 0.377 \times \left(\sqrt{0.800 + 1.2} - \sqrt{0.800}\right)$$

$= 0.377 \times (\sqrt{2.0} - \sqrt{0.8}) = 0.377 \times (1.414 - 0.894) = 0.377 \times 0.520 = 0.196\ \text{V}$

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \Delta V_{TH} = 0.237 + 0.196 = 0.433\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Part (b)**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_{FB}$ and $V_{TH0}$**: many students write $V_{TH0} = V_{FB}$ — *incorrect*; the threshold includes the surface potential and the body term.
> 2. **Unit errors**: $C_{ox}$ must be in **F/cm²**; the formula $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}/C_{ox}$ will collapse dimensionally if you mix metres and centimetres. Convert oxide thickness to **cm** before substituting.
> 3. **Calculator slips** in $\ln(5.33 \times 10^6)$: the dominant contribution is $6 \times \ln(10) = 13.816$, not the *prefactor*; forgetting this gives a $\phi_F$ that is off by an order of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

- **Define CMOS in one line:** Complementary MOS using both NMOS and PMOS on the same die.
- **n-Well process:** p-substrate, NMOS in substrate, PMOS in n-Well, p-sub at V_SS, n-Well at V_DD — *industry standard*.
- **p-Well process:** n-substrate, PMOS in substrate, NMOS in p-Well, n-sub at V_DD, p-Well at V_SS — *legacy*.
- **Twin-Well process:** both wells independently doped in a p⁻ epi layer on p⁺ substrate; mandatory for Bi-CMOS and modern sub-100 nm nodes.
- **Well formation steps:** mask → ion implant (Phosphorus for n, Boron for p) → high-T drive-in.
- **Why n-Well wins:** better NMOS mobility, zero body effect for NMOS, lower junction capacitance, simpler biasing.
- **Threshold equation (must memorise):**

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

- **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{Si}N_A}/C_{ox}$ — *increases* with $N_A$ and with thicker oxide.
- **Fermi potential:** $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A/n_i)$ — use $N_D$ for n-type bulk.
- **Physical constants to memorise:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V_T = 25.85$ mV, $\varepsilon_{Si} = 11.7\varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9\varepsilon_0$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $\mu_n \approx 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p \approx 480$ cm²/V·s.
- **Common board traps:** wrong sign in Poisson’s equation, forgetting $\mathcal{E}(x_d) = 0$, using $N_A$ inside the well while $N_D$ is at the surface, mixing cm and m in $C_{ox}$, and omitting the well/substrate contact in cross-section diagrams.
- **Mobility ratio:** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$–$3$ — *this is the reason NMOS is placed in the best (lowest-doped) region of the silicon*.
- **Process sequence to recall (in order):** substrate → well → isolation → active → gate oxide + poly → S/D → silicidation → contact/metallisation → passivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of MOSFET

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is the fundamental building block of modern Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) circuits. It is a four-terminal, voltage-controlled semiconductor device whose source-to-drain conduction is regulated by an electric field established across a thin insulating oxide layer beneath a metal (or polysilicon) gate electrode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT526, Module 2):**
> The MOSFET is a unipolar field-effect transistor in which the conducting channel between the source and drain terminals is created, modulated, or eliminated by the voltage applied to the gate terminal, which is electrically isolated from the channel by a thin layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂). The fourth terminal, the *body* (or *substrate* or *bulk*), provides the reference potential for the channel.

## 1.2 The Four Terminals of a MOSFET

| Terminal | Symbol | Role |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Gate | G | Controls the channel conductivity through capacitive coupling |
| Drain | D | Terminal at which the carriers leave the channel |
| Source | S | Terminal through which carriers enter the channel |
| Body / Substrate / Bulk | B | Provides the semiconductor reference; tied to a fixed potential |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Hydraulic Tap Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of a MOSFET as a smart water tap.**
> - The **gate** is the *rotating knob* on the tap. You do not touch the water directly.
> - The **oxide layer** is the *rubber gasket* — it completely insulates the knob from the water, just as the gate oxide prevents any DC current from entering the gate.
> - The **channel** between source and drain is the *pipe* through which water (current) flows.
> - The **substrate (body)** is the *plumbing wall* that holds everything together.
> When you twist the knob harder (apply a stronger gate voltage $V_{GS}$), the pipe opens wider and more current flows from source to drain. When the knob is released ( $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ ), the pipe is shut and no current flows. This is precisely the switching behavior exploited in digital CMOS logic.

## 1.4 Why MOSFET Dominates VLSI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight — The MOSFET is the heart of VLSI for three reasons:**
> 1. **High input impedance** — the oxide insulation means virtually zero gate DC current ( $\sim 10^{-15}$ A ), enabling massive fan-out.
> 2. **Scalability** — Dennard scaling rules allow the physical dimensions (channel length $L$ and oxide thickness $t_{ox}$) to shrink generation after generation, from 10 μm (1970s) down to sub-3 nm (today).
> 3. **CMOS compatibility** — nMOS and pMOS devices share a single silicon substrate, enabling ultra-low static power complementary logic.

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module

> [!NOTE]
> The following values are used throughout KTU VLSI problems:
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm**
> - **Relative permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{r,ox} = 3.9$
> - **Relative permittivity of Silicon:** $\varepsilon_{r,Si} = 11.7$
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$
> - **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**
> - **Dielectric constant of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = \varepsilon_{r,ox}\,\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ F/cm}$
> - **Dielectric constant of Si:** $\varepsilon_{Si} = \varepsilon_{r,Si}\,\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12}\ F/cm}$

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Physical Structure of an nMOS Transistor

An **n-channel MOSFET (nMOS)** fabricated on a p-type silicon substrate consists of the following vertically-stacked physical regions, read from top to bottom:

1. **Polysilicon (or Metal) Gate Electrode** — A heavily-doped polycrystalline silicon layer (or modern replacement-metal gate) that receives the controlling voltage $V_{GS}$.
2. **Gate Oxide (SiO₂)** — A thermally-grown, ultra-thin insulating layer of silicon dioxide, typically with thickness $t_{ox} = 1\text{ to } 10\ \text{nm}$ in modern processes.
3. **Channel Region** — The region directly beneath the oxide where the conducting inversion layer is induced by the gate field.
4. **P-N Junctions** — Two n⁺ heavily-doped regions diffused (or implanted) into the p-type substrate, forming the **Source** and **Drain** terminals.
5. **P-type Substrate (Body)** — The mechanical and electrical foundation; typically connected to ground (0 V) in an nMOS-only technology or to $V_{SS}$ in CMOS.
6. **Substrate Contact (Body Tap)** — A p⁺ heavily-doped region providing a low-resistance ohmic contact to the body terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The MOS capacitor is the heart of the device.** The metal/polysilicon gate, the SiO₂ dielectric, and the silicon surface form a parallel-plate capacitor. The capacitance per unit area is the *gate oxide capacitance* $C_{ox}$, which controls the speed and current-driving capability of the transistor.

## 2.2 Symmetry Between nMOS and pMOS Structures

| Feature | nMOS | pMOS |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Substrate (well) type | p-type | n-type (n-well) |
| Source / Drain doping | n⁺ | p⁺ |
| Body terminal voltage | 0 V (ground) | $V_{DD}$ |
| Active carriers | Electrons | Holes |
| Channel induced when | $V_{GS} > V_{THn}$ | $V_{GS} < V_{THp}$ |

In a standard **twin-well (or n-well) CMOS process**, both transistors coexist on a single p-type wafer: the pMOS is built inside a separately-implanted n-well, while the nMOS sits directly in the p-substrate.

## 2.3 Geometric Parameters of the MOSFET Structure

The structural geometry of a MOSFET is defined by four key dimensions:

- **$L$ — Channel Length:** the lateral distance between the source and drain metallurgical junctions, measured along the direction of current flow. Modern values are sub-30 nm.
- **$W$ — Channel Width:** the lateral dimension perpendicular to current flow; it sets the current-drive strength of the device.
- **$t_{ox}$ — Gate Oxide Thickness:** the vertical thickness of the SiO₂ (or high-$\kappa$ dielectric) layer separating the gate from the channel.
- **$x_j$ — Source/Drain Junction Depth:** the vertical depth of the n⁺ or p⁺ diffused regions, typically 50–200 nm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Insight:** The **aspect ratio $W/L$** is the single most important geometric figure of merit. A large $W/L$ yields a high drive current (used for high-fan-out gates, clock buffers, or output drivers). A small $W/L$ yields a small device (used for low-power, high-density logic).

## 2.4 The Three Conduction States of the nMOS Structure

The structural arrangement enables three distinct operating regimes:

1. **Cut-off:** No channel exists. The two back-to-back p-n junctions (body-source and body-drain) block current. $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$.
2. **Triode (Linear):** A continuous conducting channel connects source to drain. The device behaves as a voltage-controlled resistor. $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$.
3. **Saturation (Active):** The channel is "pinched off" near the drain end. The drain current becomes (ideally) independent of $V_{DS}$. $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as the primary reference while solving Module 2 problems.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ | F/cm | Physical constant |
| 2 | Oxide permittivity | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | $\varepsilon_{r,ox}\,\varepsilon_0 = 3.9\,\varepsilon_0$ | F/cm | $\varepsilon_{r,ox}=3.9$ |
| 3 | Silicon permittivity | $\varepsilon_{Si}$ | $\varepsilon_{r,Si}\,\varepsilon_0 = 11.7\,\varepsilon_0$ | F/cm | $\varepsilon_{r,Si}=11.7$ |
| 4 | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox}$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | F/cm² | **Most important structural formula** |
| 5 | Total gate oxide capacitance | $C_{G}$ | $C_{ox}\,W\,L$ | F | Sets gate delay |
| 6 | Fermi potential (p-type substrate) | $\phi_F$ | $\dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | V | Positive for p-type |
| 7 | Fermi potential (n-type well) | $\phi_F$ | $\dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_D}{n_i}\right)$ | V | Positive for n-type |
| 8 | Bulk (depletion) potential | $\phi_{s0}$ | $2\,\phi_F$ | V | For balanced nMOS |
| 9 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | V$^{1/2}$ | Depends on substrate doping |
| 10 | Threshold voltage (nMOS, no body effect) | $V_{TH0}$ | $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A\,2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | V | Set by structure & doping |
| 11 | Threshold voltage with body effect | $V_{TH}$ | $V_{TH0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{BS}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | V | $V_{BS}$ is body-to-source bias |
| 12 | Flat-band voltage | $V_{FB}$ | $\phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}}$ | V | $\phi_{MS}$ = metal-semiconductor work-fn difference |
| 13 | Depletion width in substrate | $W_B$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}{q\,N_A}}$ | cm | Modulated by $V_{BS}$ |
| 14 | Inversion-layer charge density | $Q_n$ | $-C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ | C/cm² | Negative for electron channel |
| 15 | Sheet resistance of inversion layer | $R_{ch}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH})}$ | $\Omega/\square$ | Used in delay estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every quantity in the table above is *extracted directly from the physical structure* (oxide thickness, doping, channel dimensions). Therefore, the entire DC and transient behaviour of any CMOS gate can be predicted from a handful of process parameters and the layout-drawn $W/L$. This is the foundation of **device–circuit co-design** taught in the KTU 2024 VLSI syllabus.

## 2.6 Structural Comparison — Enhancement vs Depletion MOSFETs

| Feature | Enhancement (E-MOS) | Depletion (D-MOS) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Default channel at $V_{GS}=0$ | Absent | Physically present (implanted) |
| Conduction at $V_{GS}=0$ | OFF (no current) | ON (conducts) |
| Turn-on requirement | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ (nMOS) | Need $V_{GS} < V_{TH,p}$ to *deplete* and turn OFF |
| Threshold voltage type | Positive for nMOS | Can be negative for nMOS (depletion-mode) |
| KTU 2024 emphasis | **Primary device for digital CMOS** | Historically important; used in analog/E/D logic |

> [!NOTE]
> All devices discussed in the KTU 2024 PEEBT526 VLSI Design course are **n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFETs** unless otherwise stated, since these form the n-network of every CMOS logic gate.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gate Oxide Capacitance Per Unit Area, $C_{ox}$

The gate, oxide, and silicon surface constitute a parallel-plate capacitor. We apply the fundamental capacitance relation to derive the structural capacitance that controls the device.

**Step 1 — Identify the physical plates.**

The upper plate is the gate electrode (metal or polysilicon). The lower plate is the induced inversion-layer charge (or, in depletion, the depletion region) in the silicon surface. The dielectric is SiO₂ of thickness $t_{ox}$.

**Step 2 — Recall the parallel-plate capacitance formula.**

The capacitance per unit area of a parallel-plate structure is

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{dielectric}}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_{dielectric}$ is the absolute permittivity of the insulator and $d$ is its thickness.

**Step 3 — Substitute SiO₂ parameters.**

The dielectric constant of SiO₂ is $\varepsilon_{ox} = \varepsilon_{r,ox}\,\varepsilon_0 = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F/cm}$. The plate separation is $t_{ox}$.

**Step 4 — Write the final expression for $C_{ox}$.**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{t_{ox}(\text{in cm})}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Express in convenient units.**

A more practical unit for VLSI problems is fF/μm². Using the conversion $1\ \text{F/cm}^2 = 10^{7}\ \text{fF}/(10^{4}\,\mu\text{m})^2 = 10^{7}\,\text{fF}/10^{8}\,\mu\text{m}^2 = 0.1\ \text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$ :

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{t_{ox}(\text{cm})}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

Converting $t_{ox}$ from nm to cm ($1\ \text{nm} = 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}$) gives the practical design formula

$$C_{ox}\ (\text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2) = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^{7}\ \text{fF/cm}^2}{t_{ox}(\text{nm}) \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{7}}{t_{ox}(\text{nm})} = \dfrac{34.5}{t_{ox}(\text{nm})}\ \text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result explains why modern technologies keep shrinking $t_{ox}$: a thinner gate oxide directly produces a larger $C_{ox}$, which yields a higher drive current and a faster transistor.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Substrate Fermi Potential, $\phi_F$

The Fermi potential describes how far the Fermi level $E_F$ lies from the intrinsic level $E_i$ in a doped semiconductor.

**Step 1 — Start from the charge-neutrality condition at the substrate doping $N_A$.**

In a p-type substrate, the hole density $p \approx N_A$, and the electron density $n \approx n_i^2 / N_A$ (by mass-action law).

**Step 2 — Apply the Boltzmann relation for the hole density.**

$$p = n_i \, e^{(E_i - E_F)/kT} = N_A$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E_i - E_F$.**

$$E_i - E_F = kT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert energy to voltage using $q\phi = E$.**

$$\phi_F = \dfrac{E_i - E_F}{q} = \dfrac{kT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at 300 K with $N_A = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$.**

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(6.67 \times 10^4)$$

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 11.108 \approx 0.287\ \text{V}$$

This positive value confirms that for a p-type substrate, $E_F$ lies *below* $E_i$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Depletion Width, $W_B$

The depletion width below the gate is set by balancing the ionized acceptor charge in the silicon with the induced surface potential.

**Step 1 — Express the one-sided step-junction charge density.**

The ionized acceptor charge per unit area in the depletion region is

$$Q_B = q\,N_A\,W_B$$

**Step 2 — Relate $Q_B$ to the surface potential $2\phi_F - V_{BS}$ using Poisson's equation in 1-D.**

Integrating Poisson's equation across the depletion region with zero field at the edge of the depletion region:

$$Q_B = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $Q_B$ and solve for $W_B$.**

$$q\,N_A\,W_B = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}$$

$$W_B = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}{q\,N_A}}$$

**Step 4 — Discuss the dependence on $V_{BS}$.**

When the body is biased *negatively* with respect to the source ( $V_{BS} < 0$ ), the term $(2\phi_F - V_{BS})$ increases, which increases $W_B$, increases the depletion charge that must be balanced by the gate, and consequently **increases the threshold voltage** — this is the structural origin of the **body effect**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Threshold Voltage, $V_{TH}$

The threshold voltage is the gate voltage at which the surface of the p-type substrate becomes strongly inverted (i.e., electron density equals bulk hole density). We sum the three voltage drops across the MOS stack at the onset of strong inversion.

**Step 1 — Identify the three components of $V_{GS}$ at strong inversion.**

- Drop across the oxide: $V_{ox} = -Q_{total}/C_{ox}$
- Surface potential: $2\phi_F$
- Flat-band voltage offset: $V_{FB}$

**Step 2 — Write the charge neutrality at strong inversion.**

At the verge of strong inversion, the total semiconductor charge per unit area is the sum of the depletion charge and the inversion charge. At threshold, by definition, inversion charge $\rightarrow 0$, so

$$Q_{total} = -Q_B = -\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the three terms.**

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \dfrac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_B$ from Step 3.3.**

$$V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F - V_{BS})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$.**

Define $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{Si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$. Also, let $V_{TH0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F}$ (the threshold voltage when $V_{BS}=0$). Then

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{BS}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

This is the canonical expression universally used in KTU 2024 exam answers.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — $C_{ox}$ Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Calculate the gate oxide capacitance per unit area (in fF/μm²) for an nMOS transistor with $t_{ox} = 5$ nm, and the total gate capacitance for a device with $W = 1.2$ μm and $L = 0.18$ μm.

**Step 1 — Apply the design formula derived in §3.1.**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{34.5}{t_{ox}(\text{nm})} = \dfrac{34.5}{5} = 6.9\ \text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total gate capacitance.**

$$C_G = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L = 6.9 \times 1.2 \times 0.18 = 1.4904\ \text{fF}$$

**Step 3 — Report with appropriate significant figures.**

$$C_G \approx 1.49\ \text{fF}$$

This tiny capacitance (femtofarad range) is the reason MOSFETs can switch at gigahertz frequencies.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — $\phi_F$ and $V_{TH}$ Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A p-type substrate has $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$. Compute $\phi_F$ and the bulk potential $2\phi_F$. Assume $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, $V_{FB} = -0.9$ V, $V_{BS} = 0$ V, and $T = 300$ K.

**Step 1 — Compute $\phi_F$.**

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(3.33 \times 10^6)$$

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 15.02 = 0.388\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $2\phi_F$.**

$$2\phi_F = 0.776\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{ox}$.**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{34.5}{10} = 3.45\ \text{fF}/\mu\text{m}^2 = 3.45 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\gamma$.**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{16}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.664 \times 10^{-14}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{1.29 \times 10^{-7}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.374\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_{TH}$.**

$$V_{TH0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F} = -0.9 + 0.776 + 0.374 \times \sqrt{0.776}$$

$$V_{TH0} = -0.124 + 0.374 \times 0.881 = -0.124 + 0.329 = 0.205\ \text{V}$$

This is a typical sub-0.3 V threshold for a modern low-power nMOS device.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MOSFET Structure

The following Mermaid diagram decomposes the MOSFET into its functional regions and shows how the four terminals interact with the structural elements.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ext["EXTERNAL TERMINALS"]
        termG["TERMINAL G - Gate"]
        termS["TERMINAL S - Source"]
        termD["TERMINAL D - Drain"]
        termB["TERMINAL B - Body or Substrate"]
    end

    subgraph stack["VERTICAL STRUCTURAL STACK"]
        layerA["LAYER 1 - Polysilicon or Metal Gate"]
        layerB["LAYER 2 - Gate Oxide SiO2 with thickness tox"]
        layerC["LAYER 3 - Surface Channel Region of length L"]
        layerD["LAYER 4 - n+ Source Region doping ND greater than 10 to power 19"]
        layerE["LAYER 5 - n+ Drain Region doping ND greater than 10 to power 19"]
        layerF["LAYER 6 - p-type Substrate doping NA between 10 to power 15 and 10 to power 17"]
        layerG["LAYER 7 - p+ Body Contact for low resistance body tie"]
    end

    subgraph junc["INTERNAL P-N JUNCTIONS"]
        j1["JUNCTION J1 - Body to Source p-n junction normally reverse biased"]
        j2["JUNCTION J2 - Body to Drain p-n junction normally reverse biased"]
    end

    termG --> layerA
    termS --> layerD
    termD --> layerE
    termB --> layerF
    termB --> layerG

    layerA -.controls electric field.-> layerB
    layerB -.induces inversion layer.-> layerC
    layerC -.connects.-> layerD
    layerC -.connects.-> layerE
    layerD --- j1
    layerE --- j2
```

## 4.2 Layer-by-Layer Fabrication Flow of an nMOS Structure

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the chronological sequence in which the structural layers of a modern self-aligned nMOS device are built.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["STEP A - Start with p-type Silicon Wafer as the substrate"]
    stepB["STEP B - Grow Field Oxide for device isolation using LOCOS or STI"]
    stepC["STEP C - Grow thin Gate Oxide of thickness tox by thermal oxidation"]
    stepD["STEP D - Deposit and heavily dope Polysilicon Gate layer"]
    stepE["STEP E - Pattern Gate using photolithography and etch to define L"]
    stepF["STEP F - Form n+ Source and Drain by ion implantation of Arsenic or Phosphorus self aligned to gate edges"]
    stepG["STEP G - Anneal to activate dopants and repair lattice damage"]
    stepH["STEP H - Form p+ Body Contact by Boron implant"]
    stepI["STEP I - Deposit and pattern Metal Interconnects at S G D and B"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key structural insight:** Steps C through F are what *defines* the MOSFET. The thinness of $t_{ox}$ in Step C, the doping level of the substrate in Step A, the channel length $L$ set by the lithography in Step E, and the self-aligned source/drain implants in Step F collectively determine every electrical parameter in the KTU Formula Sheet (§2.5).
