# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the threshold voltage of a MOSFET. Explain the body effect with its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$)** of a MOSFET is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) required to create a strong inversion layer (a conducting channel) at the surface of the semiconductor beneath the oxide. Below $V_{TH}$, the channel does not exist and the drain current is essentially zero (apart from subthreshold leakage).

**Body Effect:** When a voltage $V_{SB}$ is applied between the source and the body (bulk) terminal, the source-bulk p-n junction becomes reverse-biased. This widens the depletion region under the channel, requiring a larger gate voltage to achieve the same inversion. As a result, $V_{TH}$ increases with $V_{SB}$ according to:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

**Significance:** The body effect is critical in **stacked CMOS logic gates** (such as NAND), **source followers**, and **cascode amplifiers** where the source terminal is not at ground potential. Ignoring it leads to incorrect delay and gain calculations. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is velocity saturation? How does it modify the long-channel saturation current equation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Velocity saturation** is a short-channel effect in which the drift velocity of charge carriers (electrons in NMOS, holes in PMOS) ceases to increase linearly with the lateral electric field and saturates at a maximum value of approximately $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s. It becomes dominant when the channel length $L$ is below approximately 0.25 $\mu$m.

**Modification of Saturation Current:**
The ideal square-law equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

is replaced by the **linear** relation:

$$I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) v_{sat}$$

This degradation of $I_D$ from quadratic to linear in $V_{OV}$ is one of the primary reasons for reduced transconductance efficiency in nanoscale CMOS. It is a key consideration in modern deep-submicron design. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ of an NMOS transistor operating in the **triode (linear) region**. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an NMOS transistor with the following parameters:
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $W = 10 \,\mu\text{m}$, $L = 1 \,\mu\text{m}$
- $V_{TH} = 0.7$ V
- $V_{GS} = 2.0$ V, $V_{DS} = 0.5$ V

Determine:
(i) The region of operation.
(ii) The drain current $I_D$.
(iii) The transconductance $g_m$.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Derivation of Triode-Region $I_D$ — [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Gradual channel approximation (GCA) holds — electric field along the channel is much smaller than the field across the oxide.
2. The transistor is in strong inversion ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$).
3. The channel charge is approximated by $Q_i = C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x))$, neglecting depletion charge.
4. Mobility $\mu_n$ is constant (no velocity saturation).
5. No channel length modulation.
6. The drain and source are symmetric.

**Derivation:**

Let $V(x)$ be the channel potential at position $x$ measured from the source. The inversion charge per unit area is:

$$Q_i(x) = C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)]$$

The drain current at any point in the channel is given by the drift current:

$$I_D = W Q_i(x) \mu_n \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

Substituting:

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)] \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel, we separate variables:

$$I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)] \, dV(x)$$

Integrating from $x=0, V=0$ to $x=L, V=V_{DS}$:

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} (V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V) \, dV$$

$$I_D L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]}$$

**[7 marks distributed as: 1 mark for stating assumptions; 1 mark for $Q_i(x)$; 1 mark for current equation; 2 marks for separation and integration; 1 mark for limits; 1 mark for final result]**

---

#### (b) Numerical Computation — [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V$^2$
- $W/L = 10/1 = 10$
- $V_{TH} = 0.7$ V
- $V_{GS} = 2.0$ V
- $V_{DS} = 0.5$ V

**(i) Region of operation — [2 marks]:**

Overdrive voltage: $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 2.0 - 0.7 = 1.3$ V
Saturation voltage: $V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV} = 1.3$ V

Since $V_{DS} = 0.5$ V $< V_{DS,sat} = 1.3$ V, the transistor is in the **triode (linear) region**.

**(ii) Drain Current — [3 marks]:**

Using the triode equation:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 10 \cdot \left[ (1.3)(0.5) - \frac{(0.5)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = 10^{-3} \cdot \left[ 0.65 - 0.125 \right]$$

$$I_D = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.525$$

$$I_D = 0.525 \text{ mA} = \mathbf{525 \,\mu\text{A}}$$

**(iii) Transconductance — [2 marks]:**

In the triode region, $g_m$ is:

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{DS}$$

$$g_m = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 10 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ mA/V} = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ mS}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch of the $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ characteristic curve, explain the three operating regions of an NMOS transistor: cutoff, triode, and saturation. Derive the expression for $I_D$ in the **saturation region**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An NMOS transistor in saturation has the following parameters:
- $I_D = 0.5$ mA
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 20$
- $V_{TH0} = 0.6$ V
- $\gamma = 0.4$ V$^{1/2}$
- $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V
- $V_{SB} = 1.0$ V

Calculate:
(i) The threshold voltage $V_{TH}$.
(ii) The overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$.
(iii) The transconductance $g_m$ and output resistance $r_o$ (assume $\lambda = 0.05$ V$^{-1}$).

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Three Operating Regions and Saturation $I_D$ Derivation — [7 Marks]

**Sketch description (textual representation of the $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ plot):**

For a fixed $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, the $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ curve rises linearly for small $V_{DS}$ (resistive region), curves over parabolically, and finally flattens at $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. As $V_{GS}$ increases, the curves shift upward, with the flat (saturation) current being proportional to $(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$.

**Three Regions:**

1. **Cutoff Region:** $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ — no channel, $I_D \approx 0$.
2. **Triode Region:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $0 \leq V_{DS} < V_{DS,sat}$ — continuous channel acts as a voltage-controlled resistor. $I_D$ is **quadratic in $V_{DS}$**.
3. **Saturation Region:** $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{DS,sat}$ — channel pinched off near the drain. $I_D$ is **constant** (ideal), acting as a **voltage-controlled current source**.

**Derivation of Saturation $I_D$:**

The boundary between triode and saturation occurs at $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Substituting this into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH})(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{2} \right]$$

$$= \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}$$

**[7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for sketch and region description; 5 marks for the derivation]**

---

#### (b) Numerical Computation — [7 Marks]

**Given:** $I_D = 0.5$ mA, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 20$, $V_{TH0} = 0.6$ V, $\gamma = 0.4$ V$^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V, $V_{SB} = 1.0$ V, $\lambda = 0.05$ V$^{-1}$.

**(i) Threshold Voltage — [3 marks]:**

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.6 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{0.7 + 1.0} - \sqrt{0.7} \right)$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.6 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{1.7} - \sqrt{0.7} \right)$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.6 + 0.4 \left( 1.3038 - 0.8367 \right)$$

$$V_{TH} = 0.6 + 0.4 \cdot 0.4671 = 0.6 + 0.1868$$

$$\boxed{V_{TH} \approx 0.787 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Overdrive Voltage — [2 marks]:**

From the saturation equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$

$$0.5 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 20 \cdot V_{OV}^2$$

$$0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot V_{OV}^2$$

$$V_{OV}^2 = 1.0 \implies V_{OV} = \mathbf{1.0 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Transconductance and Output Resistance — [2 marks]:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 20 \cdot 1.0 = 10^{-3} \text{ A/V} = \mathbf{1.0 \text{ mA/V}}$$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.05 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \mathbf{40 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to determine the operating region first.** Students often blindly plug into the saturation equation without verifying that $V_{DS} \geq V_{DS,sat}$. This leads to a **wrong answer in 1 out of 3 cases** — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{GS}$ with the overdrive voltage** ($V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$). The square-law equation is in terms of $V_{OV}$, not $V_{GS}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the body effect** when the source is not at ground (e.g., in stacked transistors or source followers). Always check whether $V_{SB} \neq 0$ before computing $V_{TH}$.
> 4. **In transconductance calculations in the triode region**, the correct formula is $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{DS}$, **not** the saturation formula $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.
> 5. **Sign of $\gamma$:** The body-effect coefficient is always taken as **positive** for an NMOS transistor when $V_{SB} > 0$. Some students incorrectly substitute it with a negative sign.
> 6. **Units:** Always convert $\mu\text{A/V}^2$ to $\text{A/V}^2$ before final numerical substitution. Leaving mixed units is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Threshold voltage** $V_{TH}$ = gate voltage at which strong inversion begins. Governed by $V_{FB}$, surface potential $2\phi_F$, and body-effect term.
- **Body effect** increases $V_{TH}$ when $V_{SB} > 0$ (NMOS). Equation: $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma (\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **Three regions** of operation: Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{TH}$), Triode ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$), Saturation ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$).
- **Triode $I_D$** is **quadratic in $V_{DS}$** and **linear in $V_{OV}$**: $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)[V_{OV} V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$.
- **Saturation $I_D$** is **quadratic in $V_{OV}$** and **independent of $V_{DS}$** (ideal): $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}^2$.
- **Channel length modulation** adds the factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ to the saturation current.
- **Velocity saturation** replaces the quadratic dependence with a linear one: $I_D = W C_{ox} V_{OV} v_{sat}$.
- **Transconductance $g_m$** in saturation: $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$.
- **Output resistance** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
- **Subthreshold swing** $S = n V_T \ln 10 \approx 70$ mV/decade (theoretical minimum at 300 K).
- **Aspect ratio $W/L$** is the **primary sizing parameter** in CMOS design.
- **Overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$** is the **key design variable**, not $V_{GS}$ directly.
- **Body-effect coefficient** $\gamma = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{Si} N_A}/C_{ox}$ — increases with substrate doping $N_A$ and decreases with oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$.
- **Always verify the region of operation** before applying any I-V equation in numerical problems.
- **Units must be consistent** — convert $\mu\text{A}$ to A, $\mu\text{m}$ to m, etc., before final computation.
- **PMOS equations** are identical in form to NMOS, except mobility becomes $\mu_p$ and all voltages are referenced to $|V_{GS}|$, $|V_{TH}|$, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Channel Length Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Channel Length Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** is a second-order short-channel effect in MOSFETs wherein the effective electrical length of the inversion layer (the conductive channel) is *modulated* (i.e., shortened) as the drain-to-source voltage ($V_{DS}$) increases, beyond the saturation pinchoff voltage. This phenomenon causes the drain current ($I_D$) in saturation to no longer remain constant, but instead rise linearly with $V_{DS}$, producing a finite, non-infinite output resistance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CLM is classified as a **short-channel effect arising from two-dimensional electrostatic field distribution near the drain**, and it is mathematically quantified by the **channel-length modulation parameter** $\lambda$ (with units of $\text{V}^{-1}$), also expressible via the **Early Voltage** $V_A$ (with units of V).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Channel length modulation forms the *theoretical bridge* between the ideal square-law MOSFET model (infinite output resistance) and real-device behavior (finite output resistance). It is the *foundational premise* for deriving small-signal parameters such as $r_o$, $g_{ds}$, and the intrinsic gain of single-stage amplifiers.

## 1.2 Physical Origin – The "Why" Behind CLM

When a MOSFET enters saturation, the channel is *pinched off* near the drain end. The conventional (long-channel) theory assumes that the pinch-off point remains **fixed** at the location where $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_{DS,sat}$. However, in real devices, the *depletion region* of the reverse-biased drain-body p-n junction extends laterally into the channel. As $V_{DS}$ is increased further:

- The drain-body depletion width grows $\propto \sqrt{V_{DS} + \Phi_{bi}}$.
- This depletion region **eats into the physical channel length** $L$.
- The *effective* channel length becomes $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$, where $\Delta L$ is the length of the depletion region encroaching under the gate.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Flexible Garden Hose":** Imagine water flowing through a flexible rubber hose (the inversion layer). When you press a thumb over the open end (mimicking $V_{DS}$), water sprays out faster because the exit nozzle is constricted. The *effective* length of hose still being squeezed is shorter than the original. The harder you press (higher $V_{DS}$), the shorter the effective length, and the more water (current) gushes out. CLM is the *electrical twin* of this hose-shortening effect.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The principal parameters associated with CLM are:

- **$\lambda$** — Channel-length modulation coefficient, typically in the range **$0.01$ to $0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$** for modern sub-micron CMOS.
- **$V_A$** — Early Voltage, typically **$10\ \text{V}$ to $100\ \text{V}$** for analog CMOS, **>$50\ \text{V}$** for well-designed long-channel devices.
- **$r_o$** — Small-signal output resistance, typically **$10\ \text{k}\Omega$ to $100\ \text{k}\Omega$**.
- **$L_{eff}$** — Effective channel length after depletion-region encroachment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Mechanism

The phenomenon of Channel Length Modulation unfolds in the following structured sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Channel Formation:** With $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, an inversion layer of length $L$ is induced under the gate oxide, connecting source and drain.
2. **Step 2 — Saturation Pinch-off:** When $V_{DS}$ reaches the saturation voltage $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, the channel *pinches off* at the drain end (classical long-channel assumption).
3. **Step 3 — Depletion-Region Expansion:** As $V_{DS}$ continues to rise above $V_{DS,sat}$, the reverse bias across the drain-body junction increases, causing the drain depletion region to widen laterally.
4. **Step 4 — Effective Length Reduction:** Part of the metallurgical channel length $L$ is now depleted. The new effective length is $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$, where $\Delta L$ is the length of the gate-controlled depletion region.
5. **Step 5 — Current Modulation:** Because $I_D \propto 1/L_{eff}$ (from the gradual-channel approximation), the drain current increases linearly as $L_{eff}$ shrinks. The slope of $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$ defines $\lambda$.

## 2.2 Why the Slope Matters

The *output resistance* $r_o$ — a critical small-signal parameter for analog CMOS design — is the reciprocal of the slope of $I_D$ in saturation:

$$r_o = \left( \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} \right)^{-1}$$

Since CLM makes $I_D$ depend on $V_{DS}$, $r_o$ becomes **finite**, fundamentally limiting the voltage gain of common-source amplifiers: $A_v = -g_m r_o$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the entire arsenal of CLM equations expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the table; understand the rows.**

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2 (1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | **Master CLM-corrected saturation equation** (NMOS; replace $\mu_n$ with $\mu_p$ for PMOS) | A |
| 2 | $I_{D0} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | Square-law current *without* CLM (ideal long-channel) | A |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{V_A}$ | Definition linking modulation parameter to Early voltage | $\text{V}^{-1}$ / V |
| 4 | $V_A = V_{DS,sat} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}}} - 1 \right)^{-1}$ | Closed-form Early voltage from depletion-length ratio | V |
| 5 | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$ | Small-signal output resistance in saturation | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D = \dfrac{1}{r_o}$ | Output conductance (slope of $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve) | S (Siemens) |
| 7 | $\Delta L \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_{si}}{qN_A}\left(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}\right)}$ | Approximate depletion-region encroachment (1-D abrupt junction) | m |
| 8 | $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$ | Effective channel length | m |
| 9 | $A_v = -g_m r_o = -\dfrac{2(V_{GS}-V_{TH})}{V_{DS,sat} + 1/\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda V_{DS}}$ | Intrinsic gain of common-source amplifier (approximate) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $I_D \approx I_{D0} \left(1 + \dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_A}\right)$ | Linearized CLM form (preferred for hand analysis) | A |

> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - Valid only in **saturation**: $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$.
> - $\Delta L \ll L$ (perturbation regime; otherwise square-law breaks down).
> - $\lambda$ is treated as a **constant** for a given $L$ in hand calculations; in reality it grows $\propto 1/L$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Channel length modulation is the *gatekeeper* parameter that defines analog CMOS performance:

- **Operational Transconductance Amplifiers (OTAs):** Finite $r_o$ sets the open-loop DC gain.
- **Current Mirrors:** CLM introduces *systematic gain error* between input and output branches; high-performance mirrors use **cascode** structures to suppress $\lambda$ effects.
- **A/D and D/A Converters:** Matching of $r_o$ across current-mirror arrays directly impacts DNL/INL.
- **RF CMOS Design:** Output conductance noise ($4kT\gamma g_{ds}$) sets the noise floor of LNA output stages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Effective Length to $I_D(V_{DS})$ Relationship

### Starting Premise

The long-channel saturation drain current is:

$$I_{D0} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

### Step 1 — Substitute Effective Length

Replace $L \rightarrow L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$ in the denominator:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L - \Delta L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

### Step 2 — Factor Out $L$ in the Denominator

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{L - \Delta L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Long-Channel Form

The first two terms combine to $I_{D0}$:

$$I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \frac{L}{L - \Delta L} = I_{D0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Delta L / L}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Binomial Approximation

Since CLM is a *small perturbation* ($\Delta L \ll L$), use $(1-x)^{-1} \approx 1 + x$:

$$I_D \approx I_{D0}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Express $\Delta L$ in Terms of $V_{DS}$

From the 1-D depletion approximation:

$$\Delta L = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_{si}}{qN_A}\left(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}\right)}$$

This is *not* linear in $V_{DS}$. However, for hand analysis we **linearize** by defining:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \lambda V_{DS}$$

This empirical linearization gives the **canonical KTU saturation equation**:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_{D0}(1 + \lambda V_{DS})}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Resistance $r_o$ from the Slope of $I_D$–$V_{DS}$

### Step 1 — Differentiate $I_D$ with Respect to $V_{DS}$

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = I_{D0} \cdot \lambda$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $I_{D0}$ at the Bias Point

At a given bias $V_{GS}$, the operating drain current is $I_D$ (including CLM). Note that $I_D \approx I_{D0}$ for small $\lambda V_{DS}$:

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \lambda I_D$$

### Step 3 — Invert to Get Output Resistance

$$\boxed{r_o = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that $r_o \propto 1/I_D$. Doubling the bias current **halves** the output resistance. This is why cascode biasing with *low currents* is preferred for high-gain analog stages.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Early Voltage $V_A$ from Depletion Geometry

Starting from $I_D = I_{D0} \cdot L/(L - \Delta L)$:

$$I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \Delta L / L}$$

Now substitute $\Delta L / L = \sqrt{(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})/V_P}$ where $V_P$ is the pinch-off voltage scale. After linearization at $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat}$:

$$I_D \approx I_{D0}\left[1 + \frac{V_{DS,sat}}{V_P}\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{V_{DS,sat}}} - 1\right)\right]$$

For small perturbations, this linearizes to:

$$I_D \approx I_{D0}\left(1 + \frac{V_{DS}}{V_A}\right)$$

where, for an abrupt drain junction:

$$\boxed{V_A = \frac{L}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_{si}}{qN_A V_{DS,sat}}}} \cdot V_{DS,sat} = L \sqrt{\dfrac{qN_A V_{DS,sat}}{2\epsilon_{si}}}$$

This shows $V_A \propto L$ and $V_A \propto \sqrt{V_{DS,sat}}$, confirming the empirical rule that **shorter channels → smaller $V_A$ → larger $\lambda$ → smaller $r_o$**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** NMOS transistor with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{TH} = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{GS} = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $\lambda = 0.05\ \text{V}^{-1}$, $V_{DS} = 3\ \text{V}$.
> **Find:** (a) $I_D$ with CLM, (b) $r_o$, (c) $V_A$.

### (a) Drain Current with CLM

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{V}$$

$$I_{D0} = \frac{1}{2}(100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2)(10)(1.0)^2 = 500\ \mu\text{A}$$

$$I_D = 500\ \mu\text{A} \cdot (1 + 0.05 \cdot 3) = 500 \cdot 1.15 = 575\ \mu\text{A}$$

### (b) Output Resistance

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.05 \cdot 575\ \mu\text{A}} = \frac{1}{28.75\ \mu\text{A/V}}$$

$$r_o \approx 34.78\ \text{k}\Omega$$

### (c) Early Voltage

$$V_A = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20\ \text{V}$$

**Verification:** $V_A / I_D = 20 / (575\ \mu\text{A}) = 34.78\ \text{k}\Omega$ ✓

## 3.5 Comprehensive Derivation Summary Table

| # | Symbol | Meaning | Derivation / Source Equation |
|---|--------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $I_D$ | Drain current (with CLM) | $I_{D0}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ |
| 2 | $I_{D0}$ | Ideal saturation current | $\tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ |
| 3 | $\lambda$ | Modulation parameter | $\Delta L / (L \cdot V_{DS})$ (linearized) |
| 4 | $V_A$ | Early Voltage | $1/\lambda$ |
| 5 | $r_o$ | Output resistance | $V_A / I_D$ |
| 6 | $g_{ds}$ | Output conductance | $\lambda I_D$ |
| 7 | $g_m$ | Transconductance | $\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$ |
| 8 | $A_v$ | Intrinsic CS gain | $-g_m r_o$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: CLM Effect on $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block visualizes the *system-level flow* of how CLM transforms the ideal MOSFET saturation curve into a real, sloped curve, and how the slope maps to $r_o$, $\lambda$, and $V_A$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MOSFET in Saturation Region"] --> B["Pinchoff at Drain End"]
    B --> C["Drain Depletion Region Expansion"]
    C --> D["Effective Channel Length Decreases"]
    D --> E["Leff = L minus Delta L"]
    E --> F["Drain Current Increases Linearly"]
    F --> G["Slope of ID vs VDS equals lambda times ID0"]
    G --> H["Finite Output Resistance ro = 1 over lambda ID"]
    H --> I["Intrinsic Gain Av = minus gm times ro"]
    I --> J["Limited Analog Amplifier Performance"]
    C --> K["Delta L proportional to sqrt VDS"]
    K --> L["Linearize: Delta L over L equals lambda VDS"]
    L --> M["Define Early Voltage VA = 1 over lambda"]
    M --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram: CLM Parameter Dependency Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Channel Length L"] --> P3["Lambda = 1 over VA"]
    P2["Doping Concentration NA"] --> P3
    P4["VDS minus VDSsat"] --> P5["Delta L"]
    P5 --> P3
    P3 --> P6["ro = 1 over lambda ID"]
    P7["Bias Current ID"] --> P6
    P6 --> P8["Intrinsic Gain Av"]
    P9["Transconductance gm"] --> P8
    P8 --> P10["Common Source Amplifier Performance"]
    P6 --> P11["Current Mirror Gain Error"]
    P11 --> P12["Cascode Topology Required"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram: I-V Curve Comparison (Ideal vs Real with CLM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IDEAL["Ideal Long-Channel Behavior"]
        I1["VDS less than VDSsat"] --> I2["Triode Region, ID rises quadratically"]
        I2 --> I3["VDS greater than VDSsat"]
        I3 --> I4["Saturation: ID constant with VDS"]
        I4 --> I5["ro = infinity"]
    end
    subgraph REAL["Real Device with CLM"]
        R1["VDS less than VDSsat"] --> R2["Triode Region unchanged"]
        R2 --> R3["VDS greater than VDSsat"]
        R3 --> R4["Saturation: ID = ID0 times 1 plus lambda VDS"]
        R4 --> R5["ro = VA over ID, finite"]
        R5 --> R6["Slope of ID VDS curve gives gds"]
    end
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic of Channel Length Modulation (Physical View)

```
              Gate (VGS)
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │  ◄───  L  ───►      │   ◄── ΔL ──►│
   Source│░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ Drain      │ N+
   N+    │░ INVERSION ░░PINCH░│             │ 
   ◄─────┤░░░ LAYER  ░░░OFF░░░├─────────────┤
        │   (channel)  ░░░░░░░│ Depletion   │
        └─────────────────────┘ Region       │
              │                           ◄──┤
           Substrate (Body)            Body Contact
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**

- The shaded inversion layer extends from **Source** to a *pinch-off* point, located at distance $L$ from the source.
- The region from the pinch-off point to the **Drain** contact contains the **depletion region** of length $\Delta L$.
- As $V_{DS}$ increases, $\Delta L$ grows, *eroding* the effective channel length.
- The drain current depends on the *remaining* conducting channel of length $L - \Delta L$.

## 4.5 Conceptual Signal-Flow Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Apply VGS greater than VTH"] --> form["Form Inversion Channel of Length L"]
    form --> vds["Increase VDS past VDSsat"]
    vds --> dep["Drain-Body Junction Reverse-Biased"]
    dep --> expand["Depletion Region Expands by Delta L"]
    expand --> short["Effective Channel Length = L minus Delta L"]
    short --> rise["Drain Current Rises by Factor 1 over 1 minus Delta L over L"]
    rise --> mod["Modulation Factor 1 plus lambda VDS"]
    mod --> ro["Finite Output Resistance ro"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** Define Channel Length Modulation in a MOSFET. How is it quantified in circuit analysis?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Channel Length Modulation (CLM) is a short-channel effect in which the effective length of the inversion layer in a MOSFET is reduced as the drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ increases beyond saturation. This is caused by the lateral expansion of the drain–body depletion region into the channel. In circuit analysis, it is quantified by the modulation parameter $\lambda$ (in $\text{V}^{-1}$) or equivalently by the Early voltage $V_A = 1/\lambda$. The drain current in saturation is modeled as:
> $$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$$
> The term $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ represents the modulation factor. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **Q2.** State and explain the significance of Early Voltage in MOSFET small-signal analysis.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Early Voltage $V_A$ is defined as the (negative) extrapolated $V_{DS}$ intercept of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ saturation characteristic. It is the reciprocal of the channel-length modulation parameter: $V_A = 1/\lambda$. **Significance:**
> 1. It determines the small-signal output resistance of the transistor in saturation: $r_o = V_A / I_D$.
> 2. A larger $V_A$ (i.e., smaller $\lambda$) implies a "stiffer" current source behavior and higher intrinsic voltage gain $A_v = -g_m r_o$.
> 3. For a fixed technology, $V_A \propto L$, so long-channel devices exhibit larger $V_A$ and are preferred in analog design. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 ESE, Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A.** (a) Derive the expression for drain current in saturation including channel length modulation, starting from the long-channel square-law equation. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b) An NMOS transistor has** $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $L = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, $V_{TH} = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{GS} = 1.5\ \text{V}$, $\lambda = 0.04\ \text{V}^{-1}$, and $V_{DS} = 2.5\ \text{V}$. Compute (i) $I_D$ with CLM, (ii) $r_o$, (iii) Early voltage $V_A$, and (iv) intrinsic gain $A_v = -g_m r_o$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Long-Channel Equation:**

$$I_{D0} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 2 — Effect of CLM:** Replace $L \rightarrow L - \Delta L$:

$$I_D = I_{D0}\cdot \frac{L}{L-\Delta L} = I_{D0}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\Delta L/L}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize for $\Delta L \ll L$:**

$$I_D \approx I_{D0}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define $\lambda$ such that $\Delta L/L = \lambda V_{DS}$:**

$$\boxed{I_D = I_{D0}(1 + \lambda V_{DS}) = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) $\Delta L \ll L$ (small perturbation); (ii) mobility is constant; (iii) channel-length modulation is the only short-channel effect considered; (iv) device is in saturation $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Overdrive voltage:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Ideal saturation current:**

$$I_{D0} = \frac{1}{2}(100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2)\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)(1.0)^2 = 500\ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Drain current with CLM:**

$$I_D = 500\ \mu\text{A} \cdot (1 + 0.04 \cdot 2.5) = 500 \cdot 1.10 = 550\ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Output resistance:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.04 \cdot 550\ \mu\text{A}} = \frac{1}{22\ \mu\text{S}} \approx 45.45\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Early voltage:**

$$V_A = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Transconductance:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \cdot 10 \cdot 1.0 = 1\ \text{mA/V}$$

**Step 7 — Intrinsic gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m r_o = -(1\ \text{mA/V})(45.45\ \text{k}\Omega) = -45.45\ \text{V/V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to multiply by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ and use $I_{D0}$ directly in the $r_o$ formula. This yields a 10% error and loses 2–3 marks. Always use the **actual** $I_D$ (with CLM) in $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.

---

### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B.** (a) Explain the physical origin of channel length modulation with the help of a depletion-region diagram. How does it lead to a finite output resistance? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Two NMOS transistors in the same technology have** $L_1 = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$, and identical $W$, $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$. If $\lambda_1 = 0.02\ \text{V}^{-1}$, calculate $\lambda_2$, the ratio of output resistances $r_{o2}/r_{o1}$, and the ratio of intrinsic gains $A_{v2}/A_{v1}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Physical Origin:**

When the MOSFET is biased in saturation ($V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$), the channel is pinched off near the drain. The drain–body p-n junction is reverse-biased, and its depletion region extends laterally into the channel. The penetration depth of this depletion region into the gate-controlled channel is:

$$\Delta L \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_{si}}{qN_A}(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}$$

As $V_{DS}$ increases, $\Delta L$ grows, reducing the effective channel length from $L$ to $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$. The drain current is inversely proportional to the channel length, so:

$$I_D \propto \frac{1}{L_{eff}} = \frac{1}{L - \Delta L}$$

This produces a positive slope $\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS} > 0$ in saturation, which is the small-signal output conductance $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D$. Its reciprocal $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ is the **finite output resistance** of the device. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — $\lambda$ scaling with $L$:**

Empirically, $\lambda \propto 1/L$ for short-channel devices. Thus:

$$\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} = \frac{L_1}{L_2} = \frac{1\ \mu\text{m}}{0.5\ \mu\text{m}} = 2$$

$$\lambda_2 = 2 \cdot \lambda_1 = 2 \cdot 0.02 = 0.04\ \text{V}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Output resistance ratio:**

Since $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$, and $W$ are identical, $I_{D0}$ is the same for both devices *only* if the $\lambda V_{DS}$ correction is small. The actual drain currents:

$$I_{D1} = I_{D0}(1 + 0.02 \cdot V_{DS}), \quad I_{D2} = I_{D0}(1 + 0.04 \cdot V_{DS})$$

Output resistances:

$$\frac{r_{o2}}{r_{o1}} = \frac{1/(\lambda_2 I_{D2})}{1/(\lambda_1 I_{D1})} = \frac{\lambda_1 I_{D1}}{\lambda_2 I_{D2}}$$

For small CLM ($I_D \approx I_{D0}$):

$$\frac{r_{o2}}{r_{o1}} \approx \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \frac{0.02}{0.04} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Intrinsic gain ratio:**

$$A_v = -g_m r_o, \quad g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$$

So $g_m$ is **inversely proportional to $L$**:

$$\frac{g_{m2}}{g_{m1}} = \frac{L_1}{L_2} = 2$$

$$\frac{A_{v2}}{A_{v1}} = \frac{g_{m2}}{g_{m1}} \cdot \frac{r_{o2}}{r_{o1}} = 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1$$

**Conclusion:** Halving the channel length **doubles** $g_m$ but **halves** $r_o$ (since $\lambda$ doubles), leaving the **intrinsic gain unchanged** in this first-order model. This is the famous analog-design trade-off that motivates **cascode** topologies and longer channel lengths in gain stages. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mistake in Q.B(b):** Students often write "intrinsic gain is independent of $L$" as a *qualitative* statement without showing the algebraic cancellation. Marks are awarded for the **explicit calculation** $A_{v2}/A_{v1} = (L_1/L_2)(\lambda_1/\lambda_2) = 1$. Skip the algebra and you lose 4 marks.

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Likely Short-Concept Questions (2-Mark Rapid Fire)

> **[KTU University Exam – Expected Questions, Module 2]**

1. **What is the unit of $\lambda$?** → $\text{V}^{-1}$ (reciprocal volts).
2. **What is the relationship between $V_A$ and $\lambda$?** → $V_A = 1/\lambda$.
3. **Does $\lambda$ increase or decrease with channel length $L$?** → Decreases; $\lambda \propto 1/L$.
4. **Name the small-signal parameter affected directly by CLM.** → Output resistance $r_o$ (or output conductance $g_{ds}$).
5. **Write the modified saturation current expression including CLM.** → $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** CLM = shortening of effective channel length as $V_{DS}$ rises beyond $V_{DS,sat}$, due to lateral drain-depletion expansion.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $I_D = I_{D0}(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$, valid in saturation only.
- [x] **Definition of $V_A$:** $V_A = 1/\lambda$; unit is Volts; typical range 10–100 V.
- [x] **Output Resistance:** $r_o = V_A / I_D = 1/(\lambda I_D)$; **finite**, not infinite.
- [x] **Output Conductance:** $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D$.
- [x] **Transconductance Unaffected by CLM:** $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.
- [x] **Intrinsic Gain:** $A_v = -g_m r_o = -2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})/(V_{DS,sat} \cdot \lambda + 1) \cdot (\text{form varies})$.
- [x] **Scaling Rule:** $\lambda \propto 1/L$; $V_A \propto L$.
- [x] **Depletion Length:** $\Delta L \approx \sqrt{(2\epsilon_{si}/qN_A)(V_{DS}-V_{DS,sat})}$.
- [x] **Condition for Validity:** $\Delta L \ll L$ (small-perturbation assumption).
- [x] **Design Implication:** Long-channel devices are preferred for high-gain analog stages; cascode structures are used to suppress CLM-induced gain error.
- [x] **PMOS Note:** Replace $\mu_n$ with $\mu_p$ and reverse voltage polarities; $\lambda_p$ is typically smaller than $\lambda_n$ because holes have lower mobility → often used in current-mirror biasing.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Using $I_{D0}$ (ideal) instead of $I_D$ (with CLM) in $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ — always use the **actual biased** $I_D$.
- [x] **Key Difference from BJT:** BJT Early effect and MOSFET CLM are mathematically analogous (both use $1 + V_{CE}/V_A$ form) but originate from physically different mechanisms — base-width modulation vs. channel-length modulation.
- [x] **Exam Hall Mantra:** *Saturation + CLM → $I_D = I_{D0}(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$; $r_o = V_A / I_D$; $\lambda$ in $\text{V}^{-1}$, $V_A$ in V.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Channel Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Channel Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Short Channel Effects in MOS Transistors

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Short Channel Effects (SCEs)** are the collective set of non-ideal, second-order electrical phenomena that manifest in a MOSFET when the **channel length (L)** of the device is aggressively scaled down to the sub-micrometer regime, typically when L approaches or becomes comparable to the **junction depth (r<sub>j</sub>)** of the source and drain, and the **depletion width** of the source/drain-body p-n junctions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Short Channel Effects refer to the deviations in the ideal long-channel MOSFET behavior (such as threshold voltage, drain current, and subthreshold slope) that arise due to the two-dimensional electrostatic coupling between the source, drain, and channel regions when the device dimensions enter the deep-submicron regime.*

The key short channel effects covered in the KTU PEEBT526 syllabus include:

- **Threshold Voltage Roll-off (V<sub>th</sub> roll-off)**
- **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)**
- **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)**
- **Velocity Saturation of Carriers**
- **Hot Carrier Injection (HCI) / Hot Carrier Effect (HCE)**
- **Subthreshold Conduction / Subthreshold Swing Degradation**
- **Punch-through Effect**
- **Surface Scattering and Mobility Degradation**
- **Reverse Short Channel Effect (RSCE)**
- **Narrow Width Effect**

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Leaky Faucet" Model:**
> Imagine a long water channel with a precisely controlled dam (the gate electrode) at its center. When the dam is closed, no water flows — this represents the MOSFET in the **off** (subthreshold) state. As the channel (the river) becomes shorter, the dam's authority weakens because the source reservoir on one side and the drain reservoir on the other side start "talking" to each other directly. Water leaks through even when the dam is fully closed. **Short Channel Effects are exactly this leakage** — the gate loses its supreme authority over the channel, and the device conducts even when it ideally should not.

### Why Short Channel Effects Occur — Physical Origins

1. **Two-Dimensional (2D) Potential Distribution:** In long-channel devices, the electrostatic potential along the channel is governed by the gate alone (1D Poisson's equation). In short-channel devices, the **source and drain depletion regions** intrude into the channel and modulate the channel potential — making the problem inherently 2D.
2. **Charge Sharing:** The depletion charge in the channel is no longer controlled solely by the gate; it is now shared between the **gate, source, and drain** electrodes.
3. **High Lateral Electric Fields:** As L shrinks while V<sub>DS</sub> remains high, the lateral field (E = V<sub>DS</sub>/L) skyrockets, driving carriers into velocity saturation and impact ionization regimes.
4. **Non-Equilibrium Transport:** Carriers traverse the channel in such short distances that they do not undergo enough scattering events to reach thermal equilibrium with the lattice.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric:** The transition to short-channel behavior typically begins when **L ≤ 0.25 μm** in older technologies and at **L ≤ 45 nm** in modern nodes (with heavily engineered junctions and ultra-thin gate oxides).

---

## 2. Classification of Short Channel Effects

Short channel effects are broadly classified as follows based on their impact on the I-V characteristics:

| Category | Specific Effect | Impact on Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold-Related** | V<sub>th</sub> Roll-off, DIBL, RSCE | Shift in turn-on voltage |
| **Current-Related** | Velocity Saturation, Mobility Degradation, CLM | Saturation current reduction/change |
| **Subthreshold-Related** | Subthreshold Swing (S) Increase | Higher leakage current |
| **Reliability-Related** | Hot Carrier Effect, NBTI, Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown | Device aging, lifetime reduction |
| **Geometry-Related** | Narrow Width Effect, Punch-through | Process-dependent V<sub>th</sub> shift |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 1. Threshold Voltage Roll-off (V<sub>th</sub> Roll-off)

### Physical Mechanism

In a **long-channel MOSFET**, the threshold voltage V<sub>th</sub> is independent of the channel length L and the drain voltage V<sub>DS</sub>. As L decreases, the source and drain depletion regions consume a larger fraction of the channel depletion charge. The gate must therefore support **less** depletion charge, so a **lower gate voltage** is required to form the inversion layer. Consequently, **V<sub>th</sub> decreases** as L decreases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charge Sharing Model (Yau, 1974):**
> The depletion charge in the channel is shared between the **gate**, the **source**, and the **drain**. As L shrinks, more of this charge is "sourced" from the S/D junctions rather than the gate, reducing the gate's required voltage.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** The bulk depletion region under the gate has a trapezoidal shape (in 2D) rather than a rectangular shape (in 1D long-channel).
- **Step 2:** The trapezoid has its two slanted edges controlled by the source and drain junctions.
- **Step 3:** The effective charge per unit area that the gate must induce is reduced by a factor that depends on the ratio of the **depletion width (W<sub>d</sub>)** to the **channel length (L)**.
- **Step 4:** This reduction translates directly into a V<sub>th</sub> shift.

### Simplified Charge Sharing Result

The fractional reduction in V<sub>th</sub> follows:

$$\Delta V_{th} \approx \frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} \cdot \left[ \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right) \right]$$

For deep submicron devices, this is often simplified to the empirical form:

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{th,\infty} - \Delta V_{th,rolloff} \cdot e^{-L/L_{crit}}$$

where:
- **V<sub>th,∞</sub>** = threshold voltage of long-channel (infinite L) device
- **L<sub>crit</sub>** = critical length below which SCEs become significant
- **ΔV<sub>th,rolloff</sub>** = magnitude of the roll-off (technology-dependent)

---

## 2. Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)

### Physical Mechanism

When a high **V<sub>DS</sub>** is applied, the drain depletion region expands and **lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier**. This is conceptually similar to the gate's action — but it happens "from the drain side". As V<sub>DS</sub> increases, the barrier is lowered, so V<sub>th</sub> decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **DIBL Effect:** As V<sub>DS</sub> ↑, the potential barrier at the source-channel junction ↓, so V<sub>th</sub> ↓, and the subthreshold leakage current ↑ exponentially.

### Defining Equations

The DIBL-induced threshold shift is:

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\sigma \cdot V_{DS}$$

where **σ** is the **DIBL coefficient** (typically 0.01 to 0.1 V/V).

In some textbooks, the DIBL effect is expressed as:

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta \cdot \left( V_{DS} - V_{DS,low} \right)$$

where:
- **η** = DIBL parameter (V/V)
- **V<sub>DS,low</sub>** = small V<sub>DS</sub> (typically 0.05 V) used in the reference measurement

---

## 3. Subthreshold Swing (S) and Subthreshold Conduction

### Physical Definition

The **subthreshold swing S** is the inverse slope of the log(I<sub>D</sub>) vs. V<sub>GS</sub> characteristic in the subthreshold (weak inversion) region:

$$S = \left( \frac{d(\log_{10} I_D)}{dV_{GS}} \right)^{-1} = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\ln I_D)}$$

### Closed-Form Expression

The theoretical minimum S at room temperature is:

$$S = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{C_{d}}{C_{ox}} \right)$$

where:
- **kT/q** = thermal voltage ≈ **25.85 mV** at 300 K
- **C<sub>d</sub>** = depletion-layer capacitance per unit area
- **C<sub>ox</sub>** = gate-oxide capacitance per unit area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Limit:** When C<sub>d</sub> → 0, S → 60 mV/decade at 300 K. This is the **Boltzmann tyranny limit** — it is the absolute lower bound for conventional MOSFETs at room temperature.

### S Degradation in Short Channels

In short-channel devices, the effective S increases beyond the long-channel value due to:
- Drain-induced barrier lowering
- Increased depletion capacitance (C<sub>d</sub>) due to short-channel charge sharing
- Surface potential coupling between source and drain

---

## 4. Channel Length Modulation (CLM)

### Physical Mechanism

In saturation, the pinch-off point should ideally be fixed at the drain end. However, as V<sub>DS</sub> increases, the depletion region near the drain widens, **pushing the pinch-off point slightly toward the source**. The effective channel length shortens.

### Mathematical Model

The effective channel length is:

$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$$

where **ΔL** is the length of the depletion region near the drain at the pinch-off point:

$$\Delta L = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_{si}}{q N_A} \left( V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat} \right)}$$

The drain current in saturation is then:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2 L_{eff}} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where **λ** is the **channel-length modulation coefficient**:

$$\lambda = \frac{\partial L_{eff} / \partial V_{DS}}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** λ is **inversely proportional** to L. As L decreases, λ increases, causing greater output conductance and reduced Early voltage. This is highly detrimental to analog circuit gain.

---

## 5. Hot Carrier Effect (HCE) / Hot Carrier Injection (HCI)

### Physical Mechanism

In a short-channel device, the lateral electric field near the drain is extremely high:

$$E_{lateral} = \frac{V_{DS}}{L}$$

When this field exceeds **10<sup>5</sup> V/cm**, carriers (electrons in nMOS) gain kinetic energy far exceeding the thermal equilibrium value — they become **"hot carriers"**. These energetic carriers can:

1. **Inject into the gate oxide** (trapped charges → V<sub>th</sub> drift)
2. **Generate interface traps** at the Si-SiO<sub>2</sub> interface (mobility degradation)
3. **Cause impact ionization** → substrate current I<sub>sub</sub> and snapback in I-V curves

### Empirical Lifetime Model

The device lifetime under HCI stress is given by:

$$\tau \propto \exp\left( \frac{\Phi_B}{q E_m \lambda_m} \right)$$

where:
- **Φ<sub>B</sub>** = Si-SiO<sub>2</sub> energy barrier (≈ 3.1 eV for electrons)
- **E<sub>m</sub>** = peak lateral electric field at the drain
- **λ<sub>m</sub>** = mean free path of the hot carrier (≈ 7.8 nm in Si)

---

## 6. Velocity Saturation

In long-channel devices, carrier drift velocity is linear with field: **v = μE**. In short channels with high lateral fields, the velocity **saturates** at **v<sub>sat</sub> ≈ 10<sup>7</sup> cm/s** for electrons in silicon.

The complete velocity-field relationship (Caughey-Thomas model):

$$v = \frac{\mu_{eff} E}{\left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\mu_{eff} E}{v_{sat}} \right)^{\beta} \right]^{1/\beta}}$$

For β = 1, this reduces to:

$$v = \frac{\mu_{eff} E}{1 + \frac{\mu_{eff} E}{v_{sat}}}$$

The drain current in the velocity-saturated regime:

$$I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) v_{sat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication:** In the velocity-saturated regime, I<sub>D</sub> becomes **linear** in V<sub>GS</sub> rather than quadratic — a fundamental departure from the square-law long-channel behavior.

---

## 7. Punch-Through Effect

When V<sub>DS</sub> is so high that the **source and drain depletion regions meet** within the channel, a continuous conducting path forms from source to drain, independent of V<sub>GS</sub>. The device loses its gate control entirely.

The punch-through voltage:

$$V_{PT} = \frac{q N_A}{2 \epsilon_{si}} \left( \frac{L}{2} \right)^2$$

For a **0.18 μm** technology with N<sub>A</sub> = 10<sup>17</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>, V<sub>PT</sub> can be as low as a few volts.

---

## 8. Reverse Short Channel Effect (RSCE)

Counter-intuitively, in some devices (especially with retrograde or halo implants), **V<sub>th</sub> increases** as L decreases, before the normal roll-off kicks in. This is due to the non-uniform channel doping induced by transient-enhanced diffusion (TED) of the S/D implant dopants.

---

## 9. Narrow Width Effect

When the device width W is small, the fringing fields from the **channel edges (LOCOS or STI isolation)** contribute additional depletion charge that the gate must support. This **increases V<sub>th</sub>** for narrow-W devices.

$$V_{th}(W) = V_{th,\infty} + \frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{\pi W_d}{2W}$$

---

## 10. KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the following formulas are essential for KTU 2024 ESE problems. Memorize the meaning of every symbol.**

| # | Formula | Description | Key Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th}(L) = V_{th,\infty} - \Delta V_{th,rolloff}$ | Threshold voltage roll-off | L: channel length |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$ | DIBL threshold shift | η: DIBL coefficient (V/V) |
| 3 | $S = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | Subthreshold swing | S in mV/decade |
| 4 | $S_{min} \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec}$ at 300 K | Boltzmann limit | T = 300 K |
| 5 | $I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2 L_{eff}} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | CLM-corrected saturation current | λ: CLM coefficient (V<sup>-1</sup>) |
| 6 | $\Delta L = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_{si}}{q N_A}(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}$ | Pinch-off depletion length | ε<sub>si</sub>: Si permittivity |
| 7 | $v = \frac{\mu_{eff} E}{1 + \mu_{eff} E / v_{sat}}$ | Velocity-field (β=1) | v<sub>sat</sub> ≈ 10<sup>7</sup> cm/s |
| 8 | $I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) v_{sat}$ | Velocity-saturated current | Linear in V<sub>GS</sub> |
| 9 | $V_{PT} = \frac{q N_A}{2\epsilon_{si}}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2$ | Punch-through voltage | N<sub>A</sub>: channel doping |
| 10 | $\tau \propto \exp(\Phi_B / (q E_m \lambda_m))$ | HCI lifetime | Φ<sub>B</sub>: barrier height |
| 11 | $E_{lat} = V_{DS} / L$ | Lateral electric field | High in short L |
| 12 | $V_{th}(W) = V_{th,\infty} + \frac{q N_A W_d \pi W_d}{2 C_{ox} W}$ | Narrow width V<sub>th</sub> shift | W: channel width |
| 13 | $r_{out} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Output resistance in saturation | For analog design |
| 14 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ | Long-channel transconductance | Linear in V<sub>GS</sub> |
| 15 | $g_{m,sat} = W C_{ox} v_{sat}$ | Velocity-saturated transconductance | Constant (saturated) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Modern CMOS design mitigates SCEs through **HALO (pocket) implants**, **shallow junction engineering**, **high-k gate dielectrics**, **strained silicon channels**, and **multi-gate architectures (FinFETs, Gate-All-Around)**. These are the "engineering responses" to the physical realities of short channel behavior.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage Roll-off Using the Charge Sharing Model

### Setup

Consider an nMOS transistor with:
- Channel length **L**
- Channel doping **N<sub>A</sub>**
- Source/Drain junction depth **r<sub>j</sub>**
- Bulk depletion width **W<sub>d</sub>**
- Gate oxide thickness **t<sub>ox</sub>**

The total depletion charge per unit area (long-channel, 1D):

$$Q_{B,1D} = q N_A W_d$$

### Long-Channel Threshold Voltage

The long-channel threshold voltage is:

$$V_{th,\infty} = V_{FB} + 2 \phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \epsilon_{si} q N_A (2 \phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where:
- **V<sub>FB</sub>** = flat-band voltage
- **φ<sub>F</sub>** = Fermi potential = (kT/q) ln(N<sub>A</sub>/n<sub>i</sub>)
- **C<sub>ox</sub>** = ε<sub>ox</sub>/t<sub>ox</sub>

The bulk charge term is:

$$\frac{\sqrt{2 \epsilon_{si} q N_A (2 \phi_F)}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}}$$

### 2D Charge Sharing Model (Yau)

In a short-channel device, the depletion region under the gate is a **trapezoid**. The bulk charge is shared among:
- The **gate** (charge controlled by V<sub>GS</sub>)
- The **source** and **drain** (charges supported by the junctions)

The fraction of charge controlled by the gate is approximately:

$$f_g = 1 - \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When L is very large, the bracket term is small, so f<sub>g</sub> → 1 (gate controls all charge). When L is small, f<sub>g</sub> < 1, meaning the gate has lost control.

### Effective Bulk Charge

The bulk charge that the gate must still support:

$$Q_{B,eff} = f_g \cdot Q_{B,1D} = q N_A W_d \left[ 1 - \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right) \right]$$

### Threshold Voltage Expression

Substituting back:

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{FB} + 2 \phi_F + \frac{Q_{B,eff}}{C_{ox}}$$

This gives the roll-off:

$$\Delta V_{th} = V_{th,\infty} - V_{th}(L) = \frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical insight:** ΔV<sub>th</sub> ∝ 1/L. As L decreases, the roll-off magnitude increases. This is the fundamental reason V<sub>th</sub> cannot be independently set in short-channel devices.

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:**
> Given: N<sub>A</sub> = 5 × 10<sup>17</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>, t<sub>ox</sub> = 5 nm, r<sub>j</sub> = 50 nm, L = 0.25 μm.
> Compute: W<sub>d</sub>, C<sub>ox</sub>, and ΔV<sub>th</sub>.

**Step 1 — Compute φ<sub>F</sub>:**

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$\phi_F = 0.0259 \cdot \ln(3.45 \times 10^7) = 0.0259 \cdot 17.36 \approx 0.45 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute W<sub>d</sub>:**

$$W_d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_{si} (2 \phi_F)}{q N_A}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (11.7 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-14}) \cdot 0.9}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{17})}}$$

$$W_d = \sqrt{\frac{1.864 \times 10^{-12}}{8.0 \times 10^{-2}}} = \sqrt{2.33 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm} = 48.3 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute C<sub>ox</sub>:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.91 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracket term:**

$$x = \frac{2 W_d}{r_j} = \frac{2 \cdot 48.3}{50} \approx 1.93$$

$$\sqrt{1 + 1.93} - 1 = \sqrt{2.93} - 1 = 1.712 - 1 = 0.712$$

**Step 5 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{17})(4.83 \times 10^{-5})}{6.91 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{3.864 \times 10^{-6}}{6.91 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.59 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute ΔV<sub>th</sub>:**

$$\Delta V_{th} = 5.59 \cdot \frac{50}{250} \cdot 0.712 = 5.59 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.712 \approx 0.796 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This roll-off (~0.8 V) is unacceptably large! It demonstrates why **junction engineering, halo implants, and high-k dielectrics** are essential in sub-100 nm CMOS.

---

## Derivation 2: DIBL-Induced Threshold Shift

### Physical Picture

The drain terminal acts as a "second gate" from the drain side. Using the superposition principle in the 2D Laplace equation, the source-to-channel barrier is lowered by an amount proportional to V<sub>DS</sub>.

### Derivation via Field Coupling

The surface potential at the source end of the channel is approximately:

$$\phi_s = \phi_{s,0} - \eta V_{DS}$$

where **φ<sub>s,0</sub>** is the surface potential at V<sub>DS</sub> = 0.

The threshold condition **φ<sub>s</sub> = 2φ<sub>F</sub>** shifts as:

$$2 \phi_F = \phi_{s,0}^{th} - \eta V_{DS} \implies \phi_{s,0}^{th} = 2 \phi_F + \eta V_{DS}$$

This means the **V<sub>GS</sub> needed to reach threshold is reduced by η·V<sub>DS</sub>**:

$$V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th}(V_{DS,low}) - \eta (V_{DS} - V_{DS,low})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A well-designed MOSFET has **η < 50 mV/V**. A η of 100 mV/V or more indicates severe DIBL and is unacceptable for low-power design.

---

## Derivation 3: Subthreshold Swing Degradation

### Starting Point

The subthreshold current is dominated by **diffusion**:

$$I_{D,sub} = I_0 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n k T / q}\right)$$

where **n** is the **subthreshold ideality factor**:

$$n = 1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}$$

### Derivation of S

Taking the logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(I_{D,sub}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{(kT/q)\ln(10) \cdot n}$$

Differentiating with respect to V<sub>GS</sub>:

$$\frac{d \log_{10}(I_{D,sub})}{dV_{GS}} = \frac{1}{n \cdot (kT/q) \ln(10)}$$

Inverting:

$$S = n \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) = \left(1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}\right) \cdot 60 \text{ mV/dec at 300 K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it Matters in Short Channel:** The total gate capacitance becomes the sum of C<sub>ox</sub>, C<sub>d</sub>, **and** the parasitic S/D-to-channel capacitances, so n increases above 1, and S exceeds 60 mV/dec. This is the primary cause of **exponential leakage increase** in deep submicron circuits.

### Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** For a device with C<sub>ox</sub> = 2 × 10<sup>-6</sup> F/cm² and C<sub>d</sub> = 4 × 10<sup>-7</sup> F/cm², compute S.

$$n = 1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}} = 1 + \frac{4 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.2$$

$$S = 1.2 \cdot 60 = 72 \text{ mV/decade}$$

The **leakage current increases 10× for every 72 mV** of V<sub>GS</sub> increase — a 20% degradation from the ideal 60 mV/dec.

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix: Short Channel vs. Long Channel

| Parameter | Long-Channel Behavior | Short-Channel Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| I<sub>D</sub> vs. V<sub>GS</sub> | Quadratic (saturation) | Linear (velocity-saturated) |
| V<sub>th</sub> vs. L | Constant | Decreases (roll-off) |
| V<sub>th</sub> vs. V<sub>DS</sub> | Independent | Decreases (DIBL) |
| S (mV/dec) | ≈ 60 + (C<sub>d</sub>/C<sub>ox</sub>)×60 | > 75 typically |
| Output resistance r<sub>o</sub> | Very high | Reduced (CLM) |
| Mobility model | Constant μ | Field-dependent μ(E) |
| g<sub>m</sub> | Linear in V<sub>GS</sub> | Saturates to W·C<sub>ox</sub>·v<sub>sat</sub> |
| Hot carrier effect | Negligible | Dominant reliability issue |
| Leakage current | Negligible | Significant (subthreshold + GIDL) |
| Channel potential | 1D Poisson | 2D Poisson |
