# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing V<sub>th</sub> roll-off with DIBL.** Roll-off is a function of **L** (geometry), while DIBL is a function of **V<sub>DS</sub>** (bias). Examiners will deduct marks for mixing them.
> 2. **Forgetting units in DIBL coefficient.** η must be in V/V or mV/V. The numerical value of the shift ΔV<sub>th</sub> must include both η and V<sub>DS</sub>.
> 3. **Quoting S = 60 mV/dec as the actual subthreshold swing.** This is the **theoretical minimum**, not the actual value. Always write S = (1 + C<sub>d</sub>/C<sub>ox</sub>) × 60 mV/dec.
> 4. **Skipping the punch-through vs. CLM distinction.** Punch-through = source and drain depletion regions **touch**; CLM = pinch-off point moves toward source. These are **different** mechanisms.
> 5. **Not mentioning halo/pocket implants in the mitigation answer.** Examiners expect modern-day process solutions to be cited for full marks.

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Short Channel Effects. List any four major short channel effects observed in submicron MOSFETs. (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> Short Channel Effects (SCEs) are the undesirable electrical phenomena that arise in a MOSFET when the channel length L is scaled down to dimensions comparable to the source/drain junction depth and the depletion-region width. They cause the device to deviate from the ideal long-channel I-V behavior.
>
> Four major short channel effects are:
> 1. **Threshold voltage (V<sub>th</sub>) roll-off** — V<sub>th</sub> decreases as L decreases.
> 2. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** — V<sub>th</sub> decreases as V<sub>DS</sub> increases.
> 3. **Hot Carrier Effect (HCE)** — High-energy carriers inject into the gate oxide, causing V<sub>th</sub> drift.
> 4. **Subthreshold swing degradation** — S exceeds 60 mV/decade, increasing leakage.
> 5. (Optional) **Channel Length Modulation, Punch-through, Velocity Saturation, Narrow Width Effect.**

---

### Question 2: What is DIBL? Write the mathematical expression for the DIBL-induced threshold voltage shift. (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering) is the phenomenon in which the application of a high drain-to-source voltage (V<sub>DS</sub>) lowers the potential energy barrier at the source-channel junction, thereby reducing the threshold voltage and increasing the subthreshold leakage current.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$$
>
> where **η** is the DIBL coefficient (in V/V), typically less than **50 mV/V** in well-engineered devices.
>
> The total threshold voltage including DIBL is:
>
> $$V_{th}(L, V_{DS}) = V_{th,\infty} - \Delta V_{th,rolloff} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE norms, Module 2 long questions carry **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — V<sub>th</sub> Roll-off and Charge Sharing

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **threshold voltage roll-off** in short-channel MOSFETs. Using the **charge sharing model**, derive the expression for ΔV<sub>th</sub> as a function of channel length L. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
In an ideal long-channel MOSFET, V<sub>th</sub> is determined solely by the gate, oxide, and bulk parameters — it is independent of L. As L decreases, the depletion regions of the source and drain extend further into the channel and **share** part of the bulk depletion charge. The gate now controls a smaller fraction of the depletion charge, so a **smaller V<sub>GS</sub>** is sufficient to invert the channel. Thus, V<sub>th</sub> **decreases** as L decreases.

**Assumptions of the Charge Sharing Model (1 Mark):**
- The source and drain junctions are **abrupt** with uniform doping.
- The depletion regions are **rectangular** in cross-section.
- The bulk is **uniformly doped**.
- The channel length is short enough that the source/drain depletion regions reach the channel but the junctions do not yet punch through.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

The total depletion charge per unit area in the long-channel case:

$$Q_{B,1D} = q N_A W_d$$

In the short-channel case (2D), the depletion region under the gate is a **trapezoid**. The fraction of charge controlled by the gate is:

$$f_g = 1 - \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right)$$

The effective bulk charge the gate must support:

$$Q_{B,eff} = f_g \cdot q N_A W_d = q N_A W_d \left[ 1 - \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right) \right]$$

Substituting into the threshold voltage expression:

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{FB} + 2 \phi_F + \frac{Q_{B,eff}}{C_{ox}}$$

The roll-off:

$$\Delta V_{th} = V_{th,\infty} - V_{th}(L) = \frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{r_j}{L} \left( \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 W_d}{r_j}} - 1 \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ΔV<sub>th</sub> ∝ 1/L — confirming that roll-off becomes catastrophic in deep-submicron devices.

---

**(b)** For an nMOS transistor with the following parameters, compute the **threshold voltage roll-off** when the channel length is reduced from 1 μm to 0.25 μm.
- N<sub>A</sub> = 10<sup>17</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>
- t<sub>ox</sub> = 10 nm
- r<sub>j</sub> = 80 nm
- n<sub>i</sub> = 1.45 × 10<sup>10</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>
- T = 300 K (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fermi potential (1 Mark):**

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10^{17}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$= 0.0259 \cdot \ln(6.9 \times 10^6) = 0.0259 \cdot 15.75 \approx 0.408 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Depletion width (1 Mark):**

$$W_d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_{si} (2 \phi_F)}{q N_A}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (11.7 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-14}) \cdot 0.816}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot 10^{17}}}$$

$$W_d = \sqrt{\frac{1.691 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-2}}} = \sqrt{1.057 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.028 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 102.8 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — Oxide capacitance (1 Mark):**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-6}} = 3.453 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Prefactor (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{q N_A W_d}{C_{ox}} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{17})(1.028 \times 10^{-5})}{3.453 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{1.645 \times 10^{-7}}{3.453 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.4765 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Bracket term (1 Mark):**

$$x = \frac{2 W_d}{r_j} = \frac{2 \cdot 102.8}{80} = 2.57$$

$$\sqrt{1 + 2.57} - 1 = \sqrt{3.57} - 1 = 1.889 - 1 = 0.889$$

**Step 6 — Roll-off at L = 1 μm (1 Mark):**

$$\Delta V_{th}(1 \mu m) = 0.4765 \cdot \frac{80}{1000} \cdot 0.889 = 0.4765 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 0.889 \approx 0.0339 \text{ V} = 33.9 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 7 — Roll-off at L = 0.25 μm (1 Mark):**

$$\Delta V_{th}(0.25 \mu m) = 0.4765 \cdot \frac{80}{250} \cdot 0.889 = 0.4765 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 0.889 \approx 0.1355 \text{ V} = 135.5 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Reducing L from 1 μm to 0.25 μm increases ΔV<sub>th</sub> from **34 mV** to **136 mV** — a **4× increase**. This illustrates the severity of short-channel roll-off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — DIBL, Subthreshold Swing, and Hot Carrier Effect

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the physical mechanism of **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** with a neat energy-band diagram description. Derive the expression for the **subthreshold swing S** and state the theoretical limit at 300 K. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**DIBL Physical Mechanism (3 Marks):**
In a short-channel MOSFET, the drain is in close proximity to the source. When V<sub>DS</sub> is applied, the drain depletion region expands and creates a strong lateral field that **penetrates into the channel region near the source**. This penetration effectively reduces the height of the source-to-channel potential energy barrier that the gate must overcome to form the inversion layer. As a result, a smaller V<sub>GS</sub> is sufficient to reach threshold — V<sub>th</sub> decreases as V<sub>DS</sub> increases.

The energy band diagram shows the **source-side barrier** being lowered as V<sub>DS</sub> rises. This is mathematically captured by:

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

**Subthreshold Swing Derivation (3 Marks):**

The subthreshold current is dominated by carrier diffusion:

$$I_{D,sub} = I_0 \exp\left[\frac{q (V_{GS} - V_{th})}{n k T}\right]$$

where the ideality factor:

$$n = 1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}$$

Taking the log-base-10:

$$\log_{10}(I_{D,sub}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n (kT/q) \ln(10)}$$

The subthreshold swing is the inverse slope:

$$S = n \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10)$$

Substituting n:

$$S = \left(1 + \frac{C_d}{C_{ox}}\right) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10)$$

**Theoretical Limit (1 Mark):**
At T = 300 K, with C<sub>d</sub> → 0 (infinitely thin depletion region):

$$S_{min} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) = 25.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.303 \approx 59.6 \text{ mV/decade} \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec}$$

This is the famous **Boltzmann tyranny limit**.

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Hot Carrier Effect** in short-channel MOSFETs. List **three** process-level techniques to mitigate HCE. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hot Carrier Effect Explanation (4 Marks):**
In a short-channel MOSFET, the lateral electric field near the drain end is extremely high:

$$E_{lateral} = \frac{V_{DS}}{L}$$

When this field exceeds **10<sup>5</sup> V/cm**, electrons (in nMOS) gain substantial kinetic energy from the field and become "hot" — i.e., their energy far exceeds the thermal energy kT. The energy gained is acquired over a distance of the **mean free path λ<sub>m</sub>**.

**Consequences of HCE:**
- **Impact ionization** near the drain generates electron-hole pairs → **substrate current I<sub>sub</sub>**
- Hot electrons **inject into the gate oxide** and become trapped → permanent V<sub>th</sub> shift
- **Interface state generation** at Si-SiO<sub>2</sub> → reduced mobility, transconductance degradation
- Over time, the device performance degrades — this is a **reliability issue** (not a one-time failure)

**Mitigation Techniques (3 Marks):**
1. **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD) structure** — A lightly doped n<sup>-</sup> region is placed between the channel and the heavy n<sup>+</sup> drain to **grade the lateral field** and reduce E<sub>max</sub>.
2. **Shallow junction engineering** — Reducing r<sub>j</sub> and using **silicon-on-insulator (SOI)** or **fin-shaped channels (FinFETs)** to confine carriers and reduce the field.
3. **Bias optimization** — Reducing the supply voltage V<sub>DD</sub> in critical reliability-limited circuits to keep E<sub>lateral</sub> below the impact-ionization threshold.
4. (Optional) **Drain-engineering with pocket/halo implants** to reduce peak field at the drain junction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Short Channel Effects**

- **Definition (Must Know):** SCEs = deviations from ideal long-channel MOSFET behavior when L approaches the depletion-region width and junction depth.

- **V<sub>th</sub> Roll-off (Charge Sharing):** ΔV<sub>th</sub> ∝ 1/L. Caused by S/D depletion regions sharing bulk charge with the gate.

- **DIBL:** ΔV<sub>th</sub> = −η·V<sub>DS</sub>. DIBL coefficient η < 50 mV/V is desirable.

- **Subthreshold Swing Limit:** S<sub>min</sub> = 60 mV/decade at 300 K. Real devices exceed this due to C<sub>d</sub>/C<sub>ox</sub> ratio.

- **Channel Length Modulation:** Effective L shrinks with V<sub>DS</sub> → output resistance r<sub>o</sub> = 1/(λI<sub>D</sub>) reduces → analog gain degrades.

- **Velocity Saturation:** v = μE/(1 + μE/v<sub>sat</sub>). v<sub>sat</sub> ≈ 10<sup>7</sup> cm/s for Si. Short-channel I<sub>D</sub> becomes linear in V<sub>GS</sub>.

- **Hot Carrier Effect:** Triggered when E<sub>lateral</sub> > 10<sup>5</sup> V/cm. Mitigated by LDD, shallow junctions, and pocket implants.

- **Punch-Through:** S/D depletion regions touch → gate loses control. V<sub>PT</sub> ∝ L².

- **Narrow Width Effect:** V<sub>th</sub> increases for small W due to fringing fields at the LOCOS/STI edges.

- **Reverse SCE (RSCE):** V<sub>th</sub> **increases** as L decreases (counter-intuitive) due to non-uniform doping from S/D implant diffusion.

- **Mitigation Engineering Solutions:** High-k dielectrics, FinFETs/GAA, halo/pocket implants, retrograde wells, strained-Si channels, SOI.

- **Key Numerical Constants:** q = 1.6 × 10<sup>-19</sup> C; ε<sub>si</sub> = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10<sup>-14</sup> F/cm; ε<sub>ox</sub> = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10<sup>-14</sup> F/cm; kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K; v<sub>sat</sub> ≈ 10<sup>7</sup> cm/s; n<sub>i</sub> = 1.45 × 10<sup>10</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup> (Si at 300 K).

- **Exam Tip:** When asked about a single SCE, always state (i) the physical cause, (ii) the mathematical expression, (iii) the engineering impact, and (iv) at least one mitigation technique.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Narrow Channel Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Narrow Channel Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Narrow Channel Effect (NCE)** is a short-channel phenomenon observed in MOSFETs when the channel width (W) is scaled down to dimensions comparable to the depletion layer widths of the source/drain junctions and the isolation sidewalls. As a result, the **threshold voltage (V_TH)** becomes strongly dependent on the channel width, violating the assumption of a one-dimensional (1-D) electrostatic model.

Mathematically, the narrow channel effect is described by a **threshold voltage roll-up** that can be expressed as:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{TH,\infty} + \Delta V_{TH}^{NCE}(W)
$$

where $V_{TH,\infty}$ is the threshold voltage of a wide-channel (1-D reference) device, and $\Delta V_{TH}^{NCE}$ is a positive increment that grows rapidly as W decreases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The narrow channel effect is classified as a *second-order short-channel effect* and is critical for analog and SRAM design where minimum-width transistors are extensively used. It arises primarily due to **fringing electric fields** from the gate to the isolation sidewalls (LOCOS bird's beak or STI corners) and the **additional depletion charge** that must be supported by the gate.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **rectangular swimming pool** filled with water. The water level represents the threshold voltage needed to "fill" the channel with inversion charge. Now suppose you add **thick concrete walls (the STI/LOCOS isolation)** on the two short sides of the pool. As the pool width shrinks:

- The **concrete walls** cast a "shadow" of additional electric field into the pool.
- You now need to pour **more water (higher gate voltage)** to cover the entire floor area because the walls "soak up" some of the field lines.
- The narrower the pool, the larger the proportion of wall influence, and the higher the threshold voltage becomes.

This "wall effect" is exactly what happens in a real MOSFET: the **isolation sidewalls** introduce fringing fields that deplete the channel edges, demanding a higher gate voltage to achieve inversion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard physical parameters govern the magnitude of the narrow channel effect:

- **Silicon permittivity ($\varepsilon_{si}$) = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm = 1.035 × 10⁻¹² F/cm**
- **Oxide permittivity ($\varepsilon_{ox}$) = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm = 3.453 × 10⁻¹³ F/cm**
- **Elementary charge (q) = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Built-in potential of the p-n junction ($\phi_{bi}$) ≈ 0.7 V** (for typical Si doping)
- **Thermal voltage (V_T = kT/q) ≈ 25.85 mV** at 300 K
- **Substrate doping ($N_A$) ≈ 10¹⁵ – 10¹⁷ cm⁻³** (typical range for CMOS)

> [!NOTE]
> The narrow channel effect is **distinguished** from the **narrow width effect (NWE)** and the **inverse narrow channel effect (INCE)**. The NCE is the classical roll-up behavior due to isolation fringing; the INCE refers to an initial *decrease* in $V_{TH}$ at moderate widths due to halo implants, followed by an increase at very small widths. Both are KTU-relevant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Narrow Channel Effect

The 1-D long-channel threshold voltage of a MOSFET is given by:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

This 1-D expression assumes the channel is **infinitely wide**, so depletion charge appears only beneath the gate. In a real device with finite width, two additional depletion regions exist:

1. **Source-body and drain-body lateral depletion regions** that extend sideways into the channel.
2. **Bottom-of-the-isolation (STI) or bird's beak (LOCOS) depletion regions** that extend upward from the field-oxide edge.

Both effects force the gate to support extra charge to invert the channel.

## 2.2 Two Main Mechanisms

### 2.2.1 Fringing Field Effect (Geometric Component)

The gate electrode terminates at the channel edges. The electric field lines from the gate fringe outward and terminate on the **sidewall depletion region** of the isolation. This fringing:

- Reduces the effective gate-controlled charge.
- Increases the threshold voltage.
- Becomes dominant when the channel width is on the order of the **depletion width** ($x_d$).

### 2.2.2 Sidewall Depletion Charge (Electrostatic Component)

The depletion region from the **channel-stop implant** (the heavier p+ doping beneath the field oxide) and the isolation sidewall adds charge that must be balanced by additional gate charge. The incremental threshold voltage shift is proportional to this extra charge per unit gate area.

## 2.3 The LOCOS Bird's Beak Contribution

For LOCOS isolation, the gradual oxide taper ("bird's beak") creates a **thin-oxide region at the channel edge**. This thin-oxide zone:

- Has a higher oxide capacitance.
- Acts as a low-threshold parasitic transistor in parallel with the main channel.
- **Lowers** the effective threshold (in contrast, the STI geometry *raises* it).

The classical "bird's beak" length $L_B$ governs this effect.

## 2.4 The STI Corner Effect

For Shallow Trench Isolation (STI), the **sharp corner** at the active-area edge creates a high field concentration. Combined with the channel-stop implant doping, this leads to a strong **roll-up** of $V_{TH}$ as W shrinks.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $V_{TH} = V_{TH,\infty} + \Delta V_{TN}$ | Threshold voltage including NCE | V |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{TN} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}$ | LOCOS bird's beak roll-up (classical) | V |
| 3 | $\Delta V_{TN}^{STI} \approx \dfrac{q N_A t_{si}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{j}}{W}$ | STI sidewall charge roll-up (simplified) | V |
| 4 | $x_{dm} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}$ | Maximum depletion width (1-D) | cm |
| 5 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 6 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{N_A}{n_i} \right)$ | Fermi potential of p-substrate | V |
| 7 | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ss}}{C_{ox}}$ | Flat-band voltage | V |
| 8 | $L_B \approx 0.1 \cdot x_j$ | Bird's beak length (LOCOS empirical) | cm |
| 9 | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_{eff}}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS}$ | Linear-region drain current (with $W_{eff}$) | A |
| 10 | $W_{eff} = W_{drawn} - \Delta W$ | Effective channel width after NCE correction | cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** In KTU problems, the term *narrow width effect* is often used synonymously with *narrow channel effect*. Both refer to the **threshold voltage dependence on W** when W is scaled. Do not confuse it with the **reverse short channel effect (RSCE)** which is length-dependent.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The narrow channel effect has direct consequences in:

- **6T SRAM cell design** — minimum-width pull-down and access transistors suffer NCE roll-up, increasing the read static noise margin (SNM) but reducing the write margin.
- **Analog CMOS design** — narrow-width differential pair transistors exhibit mismatch due to NCE-induced $V_{TH}$ variation.
- **Standard cell libraries** — width quantization must account for the NCE roll-up at the minimum width.
- **DRAM and FLASH memory** — narrow channel devices in memory arrays are heavily impacted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage of a 1-D (Wide-Channel) MOSFET

We begin from Poisson's equation in the depletion approximation. In the direction perpendicular to the channel (y-direction), the charge density is $\rho(y) = -q N_A$. The 1-D Poisson equation is:

$$
\frac{d^2 \phi}{dy^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}
$$

Integrating once with respect to y, applying the boundary condition that the field goes to zero at the depletion edge ($y = x_d$):

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dy} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} (y - x_d)
$$

Integrating a second time from y = 0 (oxide-silicon interface) to y = $x_d$:

$$
\phi(y) = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} \left( \frac{y^2}{2} - x_d y \right) + \phi(0)
$$

The surface potential at the onset of strong inversion is $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$. The bulk potential is taken as zero. Therefore:

$$
\phi(x_d) - \phi(0) = -2\phi_F = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} \left( \frac{x_d^2}{2} - x_d^2 \right) = -\frac{q N_A x_d^2}{2 \varepsilon_{si}}
$$

Solving for the depletion width $x_d$:

$$
x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_{si} (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}
$$

The total depletion charge per unit area is:

$$
Q_B = q N_A x_d = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

At threshold, the gate voltage equals the flat-band voltage plus the voltage drop across the oxide plus the surface potential:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

This is the classical long-channel, wide-channel threshold voltage $V_{TH,\infty}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Threshold Voltage with Narrow Channel Effect (LOCOS Bird's Beak Model)

The narrow channel effect adds an extra depletion charge $Q_{SW}$ from the sidewall region. The threshold voltage becomes:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_B + Q_{SW}}{C_{ox}}
$$

For the LOCOS bird's beak geometry, the additional sidewall depletion charge per unit gate area is approximately:

$$
Q_{SW} = q N_A \cdot x_{dm} \cdot \frac{L_B}{W}
$$

where $L_B$ is the bird's beak length, $x_{dm}$ is the maximum depletion width, and W is the drawn channel width.

**Step 1:** Write the gate charge balance at threshold:

$$
V_{TH} C_{ox} = V_{FB} C_{ox} + 2\phi_F C_{ox} + Q_B + Q_{SW}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Q_{SW}$:

$$
V_{TH} C_{ox} = V_{FB} C_{ox} + 2\phi_F C_{ox} + Q_B + q N_A x_{dm} \frac{L_B}{W}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_{TH}$:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}} + \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the 1-D threshold voltage:

$$
V_{TH,\infty} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}
$$

**Step 5:** The narrow channel correction is therefore:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

**Step 6 (Refinement):** The classical Akers model for the bird's beak introduces a factor of $\pi/2$ accounting for the curved depletion boundary at the bird's beak:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

The complete narrow channel threshold voltage is:

$$
V_{TH}^{NC} = V_{TH,\infty} + \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

**Numerical example for KTU practice:**

Given:
- $N_A = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³
- $t_{ox} = 20$ nm $= 20 \times 10^{-7}$ cm
- $L_B = 0.2$ µm $= 0.2 \times 10^{-4}$ cm
- $V_{SB} = 0$ V
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
- W = 1 µm $= 10^{-4}$ cm

Compute $\phi_F$:

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot \ln \left( \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} \right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(3.33 \times 10^5)
$$

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot 12.717 = 0.329 \text{ V}
$$

Compute $x_{dm}$:

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2 \cdot 0.329}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 5 \times 10^{15}}}
$$

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{1.361 \times 10^{-12}}{8.01 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{1.699 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.12 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.412 \text{ µm}
$$

Compute $C_{ox}$:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-13}}{20 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.727 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2
$$

Compute $\Delta V_{TN}$:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 5 \times 10^{15} \cdot 4.12 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.727 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 10^{-4}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 5 \times 10^{15} = 8.01 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
8.01 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4.12 \times 10^{-5} = 3.30 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
3.30 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.2 \times 10^{-4} = 6.60 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 6.60 \times 10^{-13} = 1.037 \times 10^{-12}
$$

Denominator:

$$
1.727 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 10^{-4} = 1.727 \times 10^{-11}
$$

Result:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{1.037 \times 10^{-12}}{1.727 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.060 \text{ V} = 60 \text{ mV}
$$

**Interpretation:** At W = 1 µm, the threshold voltage is increased by **60 mV** due to the narrow channel effect. As W decreases to 0.5 µm, $\Delta V_{TN}$ doubles to 120 mV, demonstrating the strong inverse proportionality to W.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse Narrow Channel Effect (INCE)

In modern CMOS with **halo (pocket) implants** at the source/drain edges, the surface doping concentration $N_{S,eff}(y)$ becomes a function of position along the channel. The effective substrate doping is highest at the source and drain ends.

The threshold voltage can be approximated as an average over the channel:

$$
V_{TH} \approx V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q \overline{N_A} (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

For narrow channels, the halo regions from the **two sidewalls overlap** in the middle of the channel. This overlap effectively **raises** the average channel doping, which initially **lowers** the threshold voltage (because higher $N_A$ may paradoxically *decrease* the surface potential term depending on $V_{SB}$ and the specific doping profile). At very small W, however, the STI corner fringing dominates, and $V_{TH}$ rises sharply.

The net behavior is a **V-shaped** $V_{TH}$ vs. W curve:

$$
V_{TH}(W) = V_{TH,\infty} - \Delta V_{INCE}(W) + \Delta V_{NCE}^{fringe}(W)
$$

where the first correction is negative (decreases $V_{TH}$) and the second is positive (increases $V_{TH}$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of NCE on Device Performance

| Device Parameter | Effect of NCE | Direction of Change |
|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$) | Increases with decreasing W | ↑ (roll-up) |
| Subthreshold swing (S) | Slightly increases | ↑ |
| Drain current ($I_D$) | Decreases (lower overdrive) | ↓ |
| Transconductance ($g_m$) | Decreases | ↓ |
| Output resistance ($r_o$) | Modest increase | ↑ |
| Mobility ($\mu_{eff}$) | Slightly degraded near edges | ↓ |
| $V_{TH}$ matching ($\sigma V_{TH}$) | Worsens (Pelgrom: $A_{VT}/\sqrt{WL}$) | ↑ mismatch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Section of an MOS Transistor Showing Narrow Channel Geometry

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a narrow-channel MOSFET**, showing the gate, isolation sidewalls, depletion regions, and the fringing field lines that cause the narrow channel effect.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Source Region n+] --> B[Channel Region p-type]
    B --> C[Drain Region n+]
    D[Gate Oxide SiO2 Layer] --> E[Polysilicon Gate Electrode]
    F[STI Left Sidewall Isolation] --> G[Field Oxide Region]
    H[STI Right Sidewall Isolation] --> I[Field Oxide Region]
    J[Channel-Stop Implant p+ Beneath Field Oxide] --> K[Increases Sidewall Depletion Charge]
    L[Fringing Field Lines from Gate to Sidewall] --> M[Threshold Voltage Roll-up]
    N[Effective Channel Width Weff] --> O[Smaller Than Drawn Width W]
    P[Birds Beak Region LOCOS] --> Q[Thin Oxide Taper at Channel Edge]

    E --> L
    L --> M
    F --> K
    H --> K
    K --> M
    B --> N
    N --> O
    P --> Q
    Q --> M
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: NCE Threshold Voltage Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Long Channel Wide VTH infinite] --> B[Add Fringing Field Contribution delta V fringe]
    C[Add Sidewall Depletion Charge delta V sw] --> B
    D[Add LOCOS Birds Beak delta V bb] --> B
    B --> E[Total Narrow Channel Threshold Voltage VTH NC]
    F[Subtract Halo Implant Effect delta V INCE] --> E
    E --> G[Final VTH versus W Curve V shaped for INCE]
    E --> H[Final VTH versus W Curve Monotonic Roll up for Classical NCE]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: V_TH vs. Channel Width Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASSIC_NCE[Classical Narrow Channel Effect]
        A1[Wide Channel VTH constant] --> A2[Narrow Channel VTH increases]
        A2 --> A3[Asymptotic VTH infinity as W to infinity]
    end

    subgraph INCE[Inverse Narrow Channel Effect]
        B1[Wide Channel VTH constant] --> B2[Moderate Width VTH decreases]
        B2 --> B3[Very Narrow Width VTH increases sharply]
    end

    subgraph NCE_IMPACT[Engineering Impact]
        C1[SRAM Stability Improvement] --> C2[Analog Mismatch Degradation]
        C2 --> C3[Increased Standby Leakage at Narrow Widths]
    end
```

## 4.4 Physical Mechanism: Fringing Field Lines

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Gate Polysilicon at VGS] --> B[Main Vertical Field to Channel]
    A --> C[Fringing Field Line 1 to Left Sidewall]
    A --> D[Fringing Field Line 2 to Right Sidewall]
    C --> E[Terminates on Depletion Charge at Sidewall]
    D --> F[Terminates on Depletion Charge at Sidewall]
    E --> G[Extra Charge Required from Gate]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Threshold Voltage Increases]
    B --> I[Main Channel Inversion Charge]
    H --> J[Reduced Effective Channel Width Weff]
    I --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above represent **functional flow** rather than literal physical cross-sections due to the limitations of Mermaid rendering for semiconductor structures. The actual physical cross-section would show a rectangular active area flanked by vertical STI trenches with a polysilicon gate on top.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define the narrow channel effect in a MOSFET. How does it influence the threshold voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The narrow channel effect (NCE) is a second-order short-channel phenomenon in which the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ of a MOSFET becomes a function of the channel width W when W is comparable to the depletion region widths. It arises primarily due to the **fringing electric fields** from the gate to the isolation sidewalls and the **additional depletion charge** that must be supported at the channel edges (STI corners or LOCOS bird's beak). As the channel width is reduced, the proportion of edge depletion to channel area increases, causing the threshold voltage to **roll up** (increase). The effect is mathematically expressed as $V_{TH} = V_{TH,\infty} + \Delta V_{TN}(W)$, where $\Delta V_{TN} \propto 1/W$.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark physical cause, 1 mark mathematical relation and explanation)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between the narrow channel effect (NCE) and the inverse narrow channel effect (INCE).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical NCE | Inverse NCE (INCE) |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Physical Origin** | Fringing field from gate to sidewall; sidewall depletion charge | Overlap of halo (pocket) implants from the two source/drain ends |
| **Trend in $V_{TH}$ vs. W** | Monotonic increase as W decreases | Initial *decrease* followed by sharp *increase* at very small W |
| **Dominant Regime** | Older LOCOS and STI processes without halo implants | Modern sub-100 nm CMOS with halo implants |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\Delta V_{TN} \propto 1/W$ | $\Delta V_{INCE}$ first increases then $\Delta V_{fringe}$ dominates |

*(1 mark for each row in the table; 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**Q(a)** With the help of a suitable cross-section, explain the physical origin of the narrow channel effect in a short-channel MOSFET. Discuss the role of the LOCOS bird's beak and the STI corner. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage shift $\Delta V_{TN}$ due to the narrow channel effect in a MOSFET with LOCOS isolation. A p-MOSFET has the following parameters: $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 15$ nm, $L_B = 0.15$ µm, $V_{SB} = 0$ V, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, and W = 0.8 µm. Compute $\Delta V_{TN}$ in mV. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 marks):

1. **Cross-section description (2 marks):** Draw a cross-section of a MOSFET with LOCOS isolation showing the gate oxide, polysilicon gate, channel, source/drain regions, and the tapered bird's beak region. For STI, show the sharp trench corners and channel-stop implant.

2. **Physical origin of NCE (3 marks):** When W is comparable to the depletion width, two effects occur:
   - The gate field fringes laterally to the sidewall depletion region, reducing the effective gate control.
   - The sidewall depletion charge from the channel-stop implant must be balanced by additional gate charge.
   - These together increase the threshold voltage as W shrinks.

3. **Role of bird's beak (LOCOS) (1 mark):** The bird's beak provides a tapered thin-oxide region that effectively acts as a low-$V_{TH}$ parasitic transistor in parallel, but the classical NCE roll-up is dominated by the sidewall depletion charge contribution.

4. **Role of STI corner (1 mark):** The sharp STI corner creates high field concentration; combined with the channel-stop implant, it produces a strong roll-up of $V_{TH}$ as W decreases.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 marks):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The threshold voltage shift due to LOCOS bird's beak is:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the Fermi potential $\phi_F$:

$$
\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln \left( \frac{N_A}{n_i} \right) = 0.02585 \ln \left( \frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} \right)
$$

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(6.67 \times 10^5) = 0.02585 \cdot 13.41 = 0.347 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum depletion width $x_{dm}$:

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2 \cdot 0.347}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{16}}}
$$

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{1.437 \times 10^{-12}}{1.602 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{8.97 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.99 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.299 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the oxide capacitance:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-13}}{15 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.302 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute all values:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{16} \cdot 2.99 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.15 \times 10^{-4}}{2.302 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{16} = 1.602 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
1.602 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.99 \times 10^{-5} = 4.79 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
4.79 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.15 \times 10^{-4} = 7.19 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 7.19 \times 10^{-13} = 1.129 \times 10^{-12}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2.302 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{-4} = 1.842 \times 10^{-11}
$$

Result:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{1.129 \times 10^{-12}}{1.842 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.0613 \text{ V} \approx 61.3 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Interpretation and final statement:
- The threshold voltage of the p-MOSFET is increased by **61.3 mV** due to the narrow channel effect at W = 0.8 µm.
- If W is reduced to 0.4 µm, $\Delta V_{TN}$ doubles to **122.6 mV**, demonstrating the **inverse proportionality** to channel width.
- This roll-up must be considered in narrow-width analog and SRAM cell design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q(a)** Explain the inverse narrow channel effect (INCE) in modern CMOS devices. How does it differ from the classical NCE, and what is its physical origin? Discuss its impact on SRAM cell stability. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** A narrow n-MOSFET has the following parameters: $V_{TH,\infty} = 0.5$ V, $N_A = 2 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, $L_B = 0.1$ µm, W = 0.5 µm, $V_{SB} = 1$ V, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. Calculate the narrow channel threshold voltage $V_{TH}^{NC}$ including the bird's beak correction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 marks):

1. **Definition of INCE (2 marks):** The inverse narrow channel effect (INCE) is observed in modern sub-100 nm CMOS devices using **halo (pocket) implants** at the source and drain ends. As W decreases, the halo regions from the two ends **overlap**, raising the average channel doping, which initially **reduces** the threshold voltage. At very small W, the STI corner fringing dominates, and $V_{TH}$ rises sharply, producing a **V-shaped** $V_{TH}$-W curve.

2. **Difference from classical NCE (2 marks):**
   - Classical NCE: Monotonic roll-up of $V_{TH}$ with decreasing W, due to fringing field and sidewall depletion.
   - INCE: V-shaped curve; initial roll-down then roll-up, due to halo overlap followed by fringing dominance.

3. **Physical origin (2 marks):** Halo implants are tilted-angle implants designed to suppress short-channel effects. They create a non-uniform lateral doping profile with peak doping near the source and drain. When W is comparable to twice the lateral straggle of the halo, the two halos overlap, raising the average channel doping $N_{A,eff}$.

4. **Impact on SRAM (1 mark):** In a 6T SRAM cell, the pull-down NMOS transistors are typically minimum-width devices. The V-shaped $V_{TH}(W)$ curve means that any process variation in W can produce either higher or lower $V_{TH}$, impacting both the read SNM (Static Noise Margin) and the write margin. Designers must carefully size the cell to avoid the INCE-sensitive region.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 marks):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the Fermi potential $\phi_F$:

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot \ln \left( \frac{2 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}} \right) = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(1.333 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot 14.103 = 0.365 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum depletion width $x_{dm}$ with $V_{SB} = 1$ V:

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (2 \cdot 0.365 + 1)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 2 \times 10^{16}}}
$$

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{\frac{2.07 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.73}{3.204 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.581 \times 10^{-12}}{3.204 \times 10^{-3}}}
$$

$$
x_{dm} = \sqrt{1.118 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.344 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.3344 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the oxide capacitance:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-13}}{10 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.453 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the body-effect coefficient (1-D threshold shift due to $V_{SB}$):

$$
\Delta V_{TH,\infty} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{TH,\infty} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 2 \times 10^{16} \cdot 1.73}}{3.453 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

Inside the square root:

$$
2 \cdot 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 2 \times 10^{16} = 6.634 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
6.634 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.73 = 1.148 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.148 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.071 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{TH,\infty} = \frac{1.071 \times 10^{-7}}{3.453 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.310 \text{ V}
$$

Verify that $V_{TH,\infty} = 0.5$ V already includes this term (assumed given).

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute the narrow channel correction $\Delta V_{TN}$:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{q N_A x_{dm} L_B}{C_{ox} W}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 2 \times 10^{16} = 3.204 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
3.204 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.344 \times 10^{-5} = 1.071 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
1.071 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-4} = 1.071 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 1.071 \times 10^{-12} = 1.683 \times 10^{-12}
$$

Denominator:

$$
3.453 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.727 \times 10^{-11}
$$

Result:

$$
\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{1.683 \times 10^{-12}}{1.727 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.0975 \text{ V} \approx 97.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final narrow channel threshold voltage:

$$
V_{TH}^{NC} = V_{TH,\infty} + \Delta V_{TN} = 0.500 + 0.0975 = 0.5975 \text{ V} \approx 0.60 \text{ V}
$$

**Interpretation:** At W = 0.5 µm, the narrow channel effect adds **97.5 mV** to the threshold voltage, raising it from 0.5 V to nearly 0.6 V. This is significant and must be accounted for in low-power analog design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error on $\Delta V_{TN}$:** Students often forget that NCE **increases** $V_{TH}$ and accidentally subtract it. NCE always **rolls up** the threshold voltage in the classical case.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in C_ox:** $t_{ox}$ must be in **cm** (not nm) when using CGS units. A common mistake is mixing SI and CGS.
> 3. **Confusion with halo overlap:** The inverse narrow channel effect (INCE) is *not* a "narrow width effect" – it is a halo-overlap phenomenon. Do not interchange the two in short answers.
> 4. **Forgetting $\pi/2$ factor:** The classical Akers model includes a $\pi/2$ factor for the bird's beak curvature. Omitting it gives a 36% error.
> 5. **Not converting µm to cm:** All computations in CGS must have W, $L_B$, and $t_{ox}$ converted to **cm** to match $\varepsilon_{si}$ and $\varepsilon_{ox}$ units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Narrow Channel Effect (NCE)** = threshold voltage dependence on channel width W when W is comparable to depletion width.
- [x] **Primary cause:** Fringing electric field from the gate to the isolation sidewall + additional sidewall depletion charge.
- [x] **Threshold voltage roll-up:** $V_{TH}$ *increases* as W *decreases*, opposite to short-channel $V_{TH}$ roll-off.
- [x] **Classical formula (LOCOS):** $\Delta V_{TN} = (\pi/2) \cdot q N_A x_{dm} L_B / (C_{ox} W)$.
- [x] **STI corner effect:** Sharp trench corners enhance the fringing field, producing a stronger roll-up than LOCOS.
- [x] **LOCOS bird's beak:** Tapered thin-oxide region of length $L_B \approx 0.1 x_j$.
- [x] **Inverse NCE (INCE):** V-shaped $V_{TH}$ vs. W curve due to halo implant overlap in advanced CMOS.
- [x] **Body bias sensitivity:** $x_{dm}$ depends on $V_{SB}$; NCE becomes more pronounced at higher body bias.
- [x] **Doping dependence:** Higher $N_A$ → larger $x_{dm}$ → larger $\Delta V_{TN}$ (more pronounced NCE).
- [x] **Oxide thickness dependence:** Thinner $t_{ox}$ → larger $C_{ox}$ → *smaller* $\Delta V_{TN}$ (NCE suppressed).
- [x] **Width dependence:** $\Delta V_{TN} \propto 1/W$ — halving W doubles the roll-up.
- [x] **SRAM impact:** NCE improves read stability (higher $V_{TH}$ on pull-down) but degrades write margin.
- [x] **Analog impact:** NCE increases $V_{TH}$ mismatch in narrow differential pair transistors.
- [x] **Pelgrom mismatch law:** $\sigma V_{TH} = A_{VT} / \sqrt{WL}$ — NCE degrades the $A_{VT}$ parameter.
- [x] **Distinguish from RSCE:** RSCE is length-dependent; NCE is width-dependent. Both can co-exist in short, narrow devices.
- [x] **Standard cell sizing:** Width quantization in standard cell libraries must avoid the strong roll-up region.
- [x] **Key exam formula to memorize:** $V_{TH} = V_{TH,\infty} + (\pi/2) \cdot q N_A x_{dm} L_B / (C_{ox} W)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling Factors for Device Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Scaling Factors for Device Parameters # 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scaling of MOS Transistors** is the systematic and geometric reduction of the physical dimensions of a MOSFET (channel length **L**, channel width **W**, gate oxide thickness **tₒₓ**, and junction depths) together with a coordinated adjustment of **doping concentration (Nₐ)** and **supply voltage (V_DD)**, governed by a dimensionless **scaling factor S > 1**, in order to achieve higher transistor density, faster switching speed, and lower power dissipation per function. The objective is to preserve the **internal electric field distribution** of the device so that the physical behavior (mobility, velocity saturation profile, hot-carrier effects) remains identical to the pre-scaled (long-channel) reference, only compressed in space and time. ## 1.2 The Three Canonical Scaling Theories | # | Scaling Model | What is Held Constant? | Voltage Adjustment | Key Advantage / Drawback | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Constant Electric Field (CE) Scaling** — *Dennard, 1974* | Electric field **E** | V_DD and V_t both scaled by 1/S | Ideal field behavior, but requires new low-voltage designs | | 2 | **Constant Voltage (CV) Scaling** | Supply voltage **V_DD** | Voltages **not** scaled | Backward compatible with older designs, but field increases by S | | 3 | **Quasi-Constant Voltage / Lateral Scaling** | Field is partially scaled | V scaled by 1/√S, tₒₓ by 1/S | Practical compromise used in industry | ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The City-Block Model 🏙️ > [!NOTE] > **Think of a MOS transistor as a "city block":** > - **L** = length of the street, **W** = width of the street, **tₒₓ** = thickness of the road foundation, **V_DD** = water pressure in the pipes. > > **Constant Field Scaling** is like *rebuilding the entire city at 1/S the size, with proportionally thinner roads and proportionally lower water pressure* — everything looks and behaves the same, just miniaturized. The traffic (current) drops, but the rush-hour cycle (delay) speeds up. The electric "wind" (field) blowing through the streets is unchanged. > > **Constant Voltage Scaling** is like *shrinking the streets but keeping the water pressure the same* — the wind inside the pipes becomes dangerously strong, blowing electrons out of the channel (hot-carrier effects) and burning the pavement (oxide breakdown). ## 1.4 The Scaling Factor S The scaling factor **S > 1** represents the *ratio of the original dimension to the scaled dimension*: $$S = \frac{L_{\text{original}}}{L_{\text{scaled}}} = \frac{W_{\text{original}}}{W_{\text{scaled}}} = \frac{t_{ox,\text{original}}}{t_{ox,\text{scaled}}}$$ For example, moving from a **180 nm** node to a **90 nm** node corresponds to **S = 2**. ## 1.5 Why Scaling is Performed — The Three Pillars of Moore's Law > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **Density (D) ↑** → More transistors per unit silicon area → lower cost per function. > 2. **Speed (f) ↑** → Intrinsic gate delay ↓ → faster clock frequencies. > 3. **Power per function (P·τ) ↓** → Energy-efficient computation. All three are simultaneously achieved **only** by **Constant Electric Field Scaling**, which is why CE scaling is the *theoretical gold standard* taught in KTU Module 2. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Master Scaling Rules — Operational Logic The starting point is the **Poisson equation** for the surface potential of a MOS structure: $$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \frac{q N_a}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$ For the scaled device, all **physical dimensions shrink by S**, all **voltages shrink by S**, and **doping rises by S**. This is the only combination that simultaneously satisfies: 1. Poisson's equation (charge neutrality preserved), 2. Gauss's law (boundary fields preserved), and 3. The current continuity equation (carrier velocity profile preserved). ## 2.2 Why Each Parameter Scales The Way It Does | Parameter | Reasoning Behind Its Scaling | |---|---| | **L, W, tₒₓ, xⱼ** | Geometric reduction — the definition of scaling. | | **Nₐ ↑ by S** | To preserve the depletion-region aspect ratio: depletion width scales as 1/√S, so to maintain charge per unit area, Nₐ must rise by S. | | **V_DD, V_t ↓ by 1/S** | To keep the **vertical and lateral electric fields** identical to the long-channel reference. | | **Cₒₓ ↑ by S** (per unit area) | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \Rightarrow C_{ox}' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} = S \cdot C_{ox}$ | | **Total Gate Capacitance C_g ↓ by 1/S** | $C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L \Rightarrow C_g' = (S \cdot C_{ox}) \cdot (W/S) \cdot (L/S) = C_g/S$ | | **Channel Resistance R_ch** | $R_{ch} = \dfrac{L}{\mu C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_t)}$. Numerator ↓ by 1/S, denominator ↓ by 1/S²·S = 1/S. **Ratio → constant.** | | **Drain Current I_D ↓ by 1/S** | $I_D = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot f(V)$. μ unchanged, Cₒₓ × S, W/L unchanged, f(V) ↓ by 1/S². **Net: I_D ↓ by 1/S.** | | **Power P ↓ by 1/S²** | $P = V_{DD} \cdot I_D \Rightarrow P' = (V_{DD}/S) \cdot (I_D/S) = P/S^2$ | | **Power Density (P/Area) = constant** | Area ↓ by 1/S², so P/Area unchanged — **no thermal runaway.** | | **Intrinsic Delay τ ↓ by 1/S** | $\tau = \dfrac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_D} \Rightarrow \tau' = \dfrac{(C_g/S)(V_{DD}/S)}{I_D/S} = \tau/S$ | | **Power-Delay Product PDP ↓ by 1/S³** | $PDP = P \cdot \tau \Rightarrow PDP' = (P/S^2)(\tau/S) = PDP/S^3$ | ## 2.3 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exhaustive for Module 2 derivations** and is the single most important reference for ESE preparation. Memorize the **CE Scaling** column. Note: vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Device / Circuit Parameter | Relation to Physical Quantity | **CE Scaling (Dennard)** | **CV Scaling** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Channel length | L | $L' = L/S$ | $L' = L/S$ | | 2 | Channel width | W | $W' = W/S$ | $W' = W/S$ | | 3 | Gate oxide thickness | tₒₓ | $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/S$ | $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/S$ | | 4 | Junction depth | xⱼ | $x_j' = x_j/S$ | $x_j' = x_j/S$ | | 5 | Substrate doping | Nₐ | $N_a' = S \cdot N_a$ | $N_a' = S \cdot N_a$ | | 6 | Supply voltage | V_DD | $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}/S$ | $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$ | | 7 | Threshold voltage | V_t | $V_t' = V_t/S$ | $V_t' = V_t$ | | 8 | Oxide capacitance / unit area | Cₒₓ = εₒₓ/tₒₓ | $C_{ox}' = S \cdot C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox}' = S \cdot C_{ox}$ | | 9 | Total gate capacitance | C_g = CₒₓWL | $C_g' = C_g/S$ | $C_g' = C_g/S$ | | 10 | Drain current | I_D | $I_D' = I_D/S$ | $I_D' = S \cdot I_D$ | | 11 | Channel resistance | R_ch | $R_{ch}' = R_{ch}$ | $R_{ch}' = R_{ch}/S$ | | 12 | Transconductance | g_m | $g_m' = g_m$ | $g_m' = S \cdot g_m$ | | 13 | DC Power dissipation | P = V·I | $P' = P/S^2$ | $P' = S \cdot P$ | | 14 | Power density | P / Area | $P'/\text{Area}' = P/\text{Area}$ (constant) | $P'/\text{Area}' = S^2 \cdot (P/\text{Area})$ | | 15 | Intrinsic gate delay | τ = C_g V / I_D | $\tau' = \tau/S$ | $\tau' = \tau/S$ | | 16 | Power-delay product | PDP = P·τ | $PDP' = PDP/S^3$ | $PDP' = PDP$ (constant) | | 17 | Current density | J = I/A | $J' = J$ (constant) | $J' = S \cdot J$ | | 18 | Electric field | E = V/L | $E' = E$ (constant) | $E' = S \cdot E$ | | 19 | Body-effect coefficient | γ = √(2qεₛᵢNₐ)/Cₒₓ | $\gamma' = \gamma \cdot \sqrt{S}$ | $\gamma' = \gamma \cdot \sqrt{S}$ | | 20 | Switching energy | E_sw = C_g V² | $E_{sw}' = E_{sw}/S^3$ | $E_{sw}' = E_{sw}/S$ | | 21 | Chip area | A | $A' = A/S^2$ | $A' = A/S^2$ | | 22 | Transistor density | n (per unit area) | $n' = S^2 \cdot n$ | $n' = S^2 \cdot n$ | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > This scaling theory is the **mathematical backbone of Moore's Law**. Every technology node transition — 180 nm → 130 nm → 90 nm → 65 nm → 45 nm → 32 nm → 22 nm → 14 nm → 7 nm → 5 nm → 3 nm — is an empirical approximation to one of the three scaling models. Modern FinFET and GAA (Gate-All-Around) transistors use **CE-like scaling in the channel** combined with **CV-like scaling at the system level** to manage leakage currents that Dennard himself did not anticipate in 1974. The breakdown of pure CE scaling below ~90 nm is called the **"end of Dennard scaling"** and is the reason multi-core processors replaced single-core frequency scaling around 2005. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Below are the **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** that KTU expects a student to reproduce in the ESE answer book. **No algebraic step is skipped.** --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drain Current I_D under CE Scaling Starting from the long-channel saturation current expression: $$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$$ **Step 1 — Mobility μ_n:** Mobility is a function of the vertical field, which is preserved. Therefore: $$\mu_n' = \mu_n$$ **Step 2 — Oxide capacitance per unit area Cₒₓ:** $$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_{ox}' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} = S \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = S \cdot C_{ox}$$ **Step 3 — Aspect ratio W/L:** $$\frac{W'}{L'} = \frac{W/S}{L/S} = \frac{W}{L} \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$ **Step 4 — Overdrive voltage (V_GS − V_t):** $$(V_{GS} - V_t)' = \frac{V_{GS}}{S} - \frac{V_t}{S} = \frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{S}$$ Squaring: $$(V_{GS} - V_t)'^2 = \frac{(V_{GS} - V_t)^2}{S^2}$$ **Step 5 — Assemble the result:** $$\begin{aligned} I_D' &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_n' \cdot C_{ox}' \cdot \frac{W'}{L'} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t)'^2 \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu_n \cdot (S \cdot C_{ox}) \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_t)^2}{S^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{S} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 \right] \\[4pt] &= \frac{I_D}{S} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,I_D' = \dfrac{I_D}{S}\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: DC Power Dissipation P under CE Scaling For a static CMOS inverter, average dynamic power is: $$P = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$ where α is the switching activity factor, C_L the load capacitance, and f the clock frequency. **Step 1 — Load capacitance C_L:** In a digital circuit, C_L is dominated by gate capacitances of fan-out gates, which scale as: $$C_L' = \frac{C_L}{S}$$ **Step 2 — Supply voltage V_DD:** $$V_{DD}' = \frac{V_{DD}}{S}$$ **Step 3 — Clock frequency f:** Delay is τ = C_L V_DD / I_D, so the maximum clock frequency f_max ∝ 1/τ ∝ S. $$f' = S \cdot f$$ **Step 4 — Combine:** $$\begin{aligned} P' &= \alpha \cdot C_L' \cdot (V_{DD}')^2 \cdot f' \\[4pt] &= \alpha \cdot \left(\frac{C_L}{S}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{S}\right)^2 \cdot (S \cdot f) \\[4pt] &= \alpha \cdot \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}{S^2} \cdot S \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{S} \cdot P \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,P_{\text{dynamic}}' = \dfrac{P}{S}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Common student mistake:** Many textbooks (and hence the Section 2 cheat sheet) report $P' = P/S^2$ because they treat the *per-gate static* power $P = V_{DD} I_D$, not the system-level dynamic power. Both expressions are correct in their respective contexts. **For Module 2 derivations, KTU expects $P' = P/S^2$ from $P = V_{DD} I_D$**, and $P' = P/S$ when the clock frequency is also scaled. **Proof of the per-gate $P' = P/S^2$ formula:** $$\begin{aligned} P &= V_{DD} \cdot I_D \\[4pt] P' &= V_{DD}' \cdot I_D' = \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{S}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_D}{S}\right) = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot I_D}{S^2} = \frac{P}{S^2} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Intrinsic Gate Delay τ under CE Scaling The delay of a single inverter driving an identical inverter is: $$\tau = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_D}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute scaled quantities:** $$\begin{aligned} \tau' &= \frac{C_g' \cdot V_{DD}'}{I_D'} = \frac{(C_g / S) \cdot (V_{DD} / S)}{I_D / S} \\[4pt] &= \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD} / S^2}{I_D / S} = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{S^2} \cdot \frac{S}{I_D} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{S} \cdot \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_D} = \frac{\tau}{S} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,\tau' = \dfrac{\tau}{S}\,}$$ **Step 2 — Consequence: Frequency scales linearly:** $$f_{max}' = \frac{1}{\tau'} = S \cdot f_{max}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power-Delay Product (PDP) under CE Scaling PDP is the *switching energy* of a gate: $$PDP = P \cdot \tau$$ **Substituting the CE results:** $$\begin{aligned} PDP' &= P' \cdot \tau' = \left(\frac{P}{S^2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\tau}{S}\right) = \frac{P \cdot \tau}{S^3} = \frac{PDP}{S^3} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,PDP' = \dfrac{PDP}{S^3}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the killer result of CE scaling.** With every dimension scaled by S, the *energy* required to perform one switching event shrinks by a *cubic* factor. This is what enables billions of transistors to operate on a smartphone battery. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Body-Effect Coefficient γ under CE Scaling The classical expression is: $$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_a}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 1 — Numerator scaling:** $N_a' = S \cdot N_a$, so the square-root term becomes $\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} \cdot S \cdot N_a} = \sqrt{S} \cdot \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_a}$. **Step 2 — Denominator scaling:** $C_{ox}' = S \cdot C_{ox}$. **Step 3 — Combine:** $$\begin{aligned} \gamma' &= \frac{\sqrt{S} \cdot \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_a}}{S \cdot C_{ox}} = \frac{\sqrt{S}}{S} \cdot \gamma = \frac{\gamma}{\sqrt{S}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,\gamma' = \dfrac{\gamma}{\sqrt{S}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > A common KTU textbook error is to write $\gamma' = \gamma \cdot \sqrt{S}$ (an *increase*). Always re-derive from the formula — doping increases, but Cₒₓ increases *faster*, so the net effect is a **decrease by $\sqrt{S}$**. Verify against the formula sheet in Section 2 (Row 19). --- ## 3.6 Derivation 6: CV Scaling — Why Current *Increases* by S Under **Constant Voltage** scaling, $V_{DD}$ is held constant. Therefore the lateral electric field at the drain end of the channel is: $$E_{lat} = \frac{V_{DS}}{L} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E_{lat}' = \frac{V_{DS}}{L/S} = S \cdot E_{lat}$$ **Step 1 — Drain current expression (velocity-saturated regime):** For short-channel devices, $I_D \approx C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{GS} - V_t)$. **Step 2 — Substituting scaled quantities:** $$C_{ox}' = S \cdot C_{ox}, \quad W' = W/S, \quad (V_{GS} - V_t)' = V_{GS} - V_t \text{ (unchanged)}$$ **Step 3 — Result:** $$I_D' = (S \cdot C_{ox}) \cdot (W/S) \cdot v_{sat} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t) = C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{GS} - V_t) = I_D \cdot \frac{S \cdot W}{S \cdot W}$$ Wait — re-evaluating for CV scaling where V_t is **not** scaled: $$I_D' = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n (S \cdot C_{ox}) \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = S \cdot I_D$$ $$\boxed{\,I_D' = S \cdot I_D \quad (\text{CV scaling})\,}$$ **Step 4 — Power under CV scaling:** $$P' = V_{DD} \cdot I_D' = V_{DD} \cdot (S \cdot I_D) = S \cdot P$$ --- ## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-mark Variant) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A MOSFET has $L = 2\ \mu m$, $W = 10\ \mu m$, $t_{ox} = 40\ nm$, $N_a = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $V_{DD} = 5\ V$, $V_t = 0.7\ V$. If the device is scaled by $S = 5$ under **CE rules**, compute the new: (a) $L'$, $W'$, $t_{ox}'$, $N_a'$, $V_{DD}'$, $V_t'$. (b) $I_D'$, $P'$, $\tau'$, $C_g'$, $\gamma'$. **Solution:** **(a) Geometric & electrical parameters:** $$\begin{aligned} L' &= \frac{2\ \mu m}{5} = 0.4\ \mu m \\[4pt] W' &= \frac{10\ \mu m}{5} = 2\ \mu m \\[4pt] t_{ox}' &= \frac{40\ \text{nm}}{5} = 8\ \text{nm} \\[4pt] N_a' &= 5 \times 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3} \\[4pt] V_{DD}' &= \frac{5\ V}{5} = 1\ V \\[4pt] V_t' &= \frac{0.7\ V}{5} = 0.14\ V \end{aligned}$$ **(b) Derived parameters (ratios):** $$I_D' = I_D / 5, \quad P' = P/25, \quad \tau' = \tau/5, \quad C_g' = C_g/5, \quad \gamma' = \gamma/\sqrt{5}$$ $$\text{Transistor density} = 25 \times \text{original}$$ # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Conceptual Map of Scaling Theory ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original MOSFET
L, W, tox, VDD, Na] --> B{Scaling Model?} B -->|CE: Dennard 1974| C[Constant Electric Field] B -->|CV: Voltage fixed| D[Constant Voltage] B -->|Quasi-CV: VDD / sqrt S| E[Quasi-Constant Voltage] C --> F[L' = L/S] C --> G[W' = W/S] C --> H[tox' = tox/S] C --> I[VDD' = VDD/S] C --> J[Vt' = Vt/S] C --> K[Na' = S * Na] D --> F D --> G D --> H D --> L[VDD' = VDD] D --> M[Vt' = Vt] D --> K F --> N[Derived Quantities] G --> N H --> N I --> N J --> N K --> N L --> N M --> N N --> O[Cox' = S * Cox] N --> P[Cg' = Cg / S] N --> Q[ID' = ID / S for CE] N --> R[P' = P / S2 for CE] N --> S[tau' = tau / S] N --> T[PDP' = PDP / S3] N --> U[Density' = S2 * Density] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#713f12,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style N fill:#581c87,stroke:#a855f7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style O fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style P fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style R fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style S fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style T fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style U fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` ## [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2023]** Define the term *scaling factor* in the context of MOS transistor design. List any four device parameters that are scaled and their corresponding scaling relationships under **Constant Electric Field (CE) scaling**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **scaling factor S** is a dimensionless constant greater than 1 that quantifies the ratio by which the physical dimensions of a MOSFET are reduced. It is defined as: $$S = \frac{L_{\text{original}}}{L_{\text{scaled}}}$$ Under CE scaling, the four key parameters are: 1. **Channel length:** $L' = L/S$ ✅ 2. **Channel width:** $W' = W/S$ ✅ 3. **Gate oxide thickness:** $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/S$ ✅ 4. **Supply voltage:** $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}/S$ ✅ Additionally, substrate doping is scaled up: $N_a' = S \cdot N_a$. *(Award full 3 marks only if at least 4 relations with correct notation are listed.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Constant Electric Field (CE) scaling** and **Constant Voltage (CV) scaling** with respect to power dissipation per unit area. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | CE Scaling | CV Scaling | |---|---|---| | Power per device | $P' = P/S^2$ | $P' = S \cdot P$ | | Area per device | $A' = A/S^2$ | $A' = A/S^2$ | | **Power density** | $P'/A' = P/A$ (**constant**) | $P'/A' = S^3 \cdot (P/A)$ (**increases cubically**) | > In **CE scaling**, power density remains constant → no thermal runaway. In **CV scaling**, power density rises by $S^3$ → severe heating problem, which is why CV scaling is not used in practice for bulk CMOS. ✅✅✅ --- ## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to **forget the $\sqrt{S}$ factor in body-effect coefficient γ** and to **mis-apply the $P = V_{DD}I_D$ formula** without remembering that CE scaling assumes $V_{DD}$ *also* scales. Marks are typically lost for: (1) Writing $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$ in a CE scaling problem, (2) Using the wrong sign on the doping scaling, (3) Skipping the derivation step of mobility preservation, and (4) Computing the *per-gate* power $P/S^2$ when the question asks for *system-level* dynamic power $P/S$. Read the question carefully. --- ### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Constant Electric Field (CE) scaling** of a MOSFET. Derive the scaling relationships for **(i)** drain current $I_D$, **(ii)** power dissipation $P$, and **(iii)** intrinsic gate delay $\tau$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A MOSFET is fabricated with the following original parameters: $L = 1\ \mu m$, $W = 5\ \mu m$, $t_{ox} = 20\ \text{nm}$, $N_a = 5 \times 10^{14}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $V_{DD} = 3.3\ V$, $V_t = 0.6\ V$. The drain current measured at the original device is $I_D = 200\ \mu A$ and the gate capacitance is $C_g = 50\ \text{fF}$. If the device is scaled by **S = 10** using CE rules, calculate: 1. The scaled supply voltage $V_{DD}'$ and threshold voltage $V_t'$. 2. The new drain current $I_D'$ and new gate capacitance $C_g'$. 3. The intrinsic delay $\tau$ of the original device and $\tau'$ of the scaled device. 4. The PDP of both devices and the percentage reduction in PDP. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (3 marks) + Derivations (4 marks) **Concept:** CE scaling (Dennard, 1974) preserves the **electric field pattern** inside the MOSFET. The geometry shrinks by S, the voltages shrink by S, and the doping rises by S, so that the dimensionless electric-field profile of the original device is reproduced in the scaled device at every point. **(i) Derivation of $I_D' = I_D/S$:** $$\begin{aligned} I_D &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t)^2 \\[4pt] I_D' &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_n' \cdot C_{ox}' \cdot \frac{W'}{L'} \cdot (V_{GS}' - V_t')^2 \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_n \cdot (S \cdot C_{ox}) \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_t)^2}{S^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{I_D}{S} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Derivation of $P' = P/S^2$:** $$\begin{aligned} P &= V_{DD} \cdot I_D \\[4pt] P' &= V_{DD}' \cdot I_D' = \frac{V_{DD}}{S} \cdot \frac{I_D}{S} = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot I_D}{S^2} = \frac{P}{S^2} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Derivation of $\tau' = \tau/S$:** $$\begin{aligned} \tau &= \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_D} \\[4pt] \tau' &= \frac{C_g' \cdot V_{DD}'}{I_D'} = \frac{(C_g/S)(V_{DD}/S)}{I_D/S} = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{S^2} \cdot \frac{S}{I_D} = \frac{\tau}{S} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **1. Scaled voltages:** $$V_{DD}' = \frac{3.3\ V}{10} = 0.33\ V$$ $$V_t' = \frac{0.6\ V}{10} = 0.06\ V$$ **2. Scaled current and capacitance:** $$I_D' = \frac{200\ \mu A}{10} = 20\ \mu A$$ $$C_g' = \frac{50\ \text{fF}}{10} = 5\ \text{fF}$$ **3. Intrinsic delays:** $$\tau = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_D} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-15}\ F)(3.3\ V)}{200 \times 10^{-6}\ A} = 8.25 \times 10^{-10}\ s = 825\ \text{ps}$$ $$\tau' = \frac{C_g' \cdot V_{DD}'}{I_D'} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-15}\ F)(0.33\ V)}{20 \times 10^{-6}\ A} = 8.25 \times 10^{-11}\ s = 82.5\ \text{ps}$$ $$\text{Verification: } \tau' = \tau/S = 825\ \text{ps}/10 = 82.5\ \text{ps} \quad \checkmark$$ **4. Power-delay products:** $$PDP = P \cdot \tau = (V_{DD} \cdot I_D) \cdot \tau = (3.3)(200\ \mu A)(825\ \text{ps}) = 5.445 \times 10^{-13}\ J = 0.5445\ \text{pJ}$$ $$PDP' = P' \cdot \tau' = (0.33\ V)(20\ \mu A)(82.5\ \text{ps}) = 5.445 \times 10^{-15}\ J = 0.005445\ \text{pJ}$$ $$\text{Verification: } PDP' = PDP/S^3 = 0.5445/1000 = 0.0005445\ \text{pJ} \quad \checkmark$$ $$\text{Percentage reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{S^3}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{1}{1000}\right) \times 100\% = 99.9\%$$ --- ### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a comparative table, explain the differences between **CE scaling** and **CV scaling** for a MOSFET. Derive the scaling relationship for **channel resistance $R_{ch}$** and **transconductance $g_m$** under CE scaling. **(b) [7 Marks]** Under CV scaling with $S = 4$, an NMOS transistor originally operating at $V_{DD} = 3\ V$ with $I_D = 1\ \text{mA}$ and $C_g = 80\ \text{fF}$ is reduced. Determine: 1. The scaled supply voltage $V_{DD}'$ and drain current $I_D'$. 2. The new power dissipation $P'$ and the new power density ratio $(P'/A')/(P/A)$. 3. The intrinsic delay $\tau'$ of the scaled device. 4. Comment on the **practical limitation** of CV scaling that led the industry to adopt quasi-constant voltage scaling. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (3 marks) + Derivations (4 marks) **Comparative Table (any 6 rows for full 3 marks):** | Parameter | CE Scaling | CV Scaling | |---|---|---| | $L, W, t_{ox}$ | $/S$ | $/S$ | | $V_{DD}$ | $/S$ | Constant | | $V_t$ | $/S$ | Constant | | $N_a$ | $\times S$ | $\times S$ | | $I_D$ | $/S$ | $\times S$ | | $P$ | $/S^2$ | $\times S$ | | $E$ field | Constant | $\times S$ | | Power density | Constant | $\times S^3$ | | $\tau$ | $/S$ | $/S$ | | PDP | $/S^3$ | Constant | **Derivation of $R_{ch}$:** $$\begin{aligned} R_{ch} &= \frac{L}{\mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_t)} \\[4pt] R_{ch}' &= \frac{L'}{\mu_n' C_{ox}' W' (V_{GS}' - V_t')} = \frac{L/S}{\mu_n \cdot S \cdot C_{ox} \cdot (W/S) \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t)/S} \\[4pt] &= \frac{L/S}{\mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_t)/S^2} = \frac{L}{\mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_t)} = R_{ch} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **Derivation of $g_m$:** $$\begin{aligned} g_m &= \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t) \\[4pt] g_m' &= \mu_n \cdot S \cdot C_{ox} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_t)}{S} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t) = g_m \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ Both $R_{ch}$ and $g_m$ remain **invariant** under CE scaling. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **1. Scaled $V_{DD}$ and $I_D$:** $$V_{DD}' = V_{DD} = 3\ V \quad \text{(held constant under CV)}$$ $$I_D' = S \cdot I_D = 4 \times 1\ \text{mA} = 4\ \text{mA}$$ **2. Scaled power and power-density ratio:** $$P = V_{DD} \cdot I_D = (3)(1\ \text{mA}) = 3\ \text{mW}$$ $$P' = V_{DD}' \cdot I_D' = (3)(4\ \text{mA}) = 12\ \text{mW}$$ $$\frac{P'}{A'} \Big/ \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P'/P}{A'/A} = \frac{S}{1/S^2} = S^3 = 64$$ The power density increases by a factor of **64**! This is the killer drawback of CV scaling. ⚠️ **3. Intrinsic delay of scaled device:** $$C_g' = C_g / S = 80\ \text{fF} / 4 = 20\ \text{fF}$$ $$\tau' = \frac{C_g' \cdot V_{DD}'}{I_D'} = \frac{(20\ \text{fF})(3\ V)}{4\ \text{mA}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}\ s = 15\ \text{ps}$$ **4. Practical Limitation:** CV scaling produces an **electric field that increases linearly with S** ($E' = S \cdot E$). This leads to three catastrophic effects: - **Hot-carrier injection (HCI):** High-energy electrons injected into the gate oxide, permanently degrading $V_t$ and $I_D$ over time. - **Oxide breakdown:** $E_{ox}$ exceeds the ~10 MV/cm SiO₂ limit, causing irreversible dielectric failure. - **Velocity saturation & mobility degradation:** The transconductance does not scale linearly as predicted, so the speed gains plateau. For these reasons, the semiconductor industry adopted **quasi-constant voltage scaling** (typically $V_{DD}$ scaled by $1/\sqrt{S}$ or $1/\sqrt[3]{S}$), which is a pragmatic compromise between CE (ideal field) and CV (backward compatibility). ✅ --- # 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > ### 🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Scaling Factors for Device Parameters **🔑 Master Definitions:** - ☐ **Scaling factor S:** ratio of original to new dimension; S > 1. - ☐ **CE (Dennard) scaling:** preserves electric field — ideal but requires new low-voltage design. - ☐ **CV scaling:** preserves supply voltage — backward compatible but field grows by S. - ☐ **Quasi-CV scaling:** practical compromise used in industry. **🔑 Memorize the 5 Golden CE Scaling Rules:** - ☐ $L, W, t_{ox}, V_{DD}, V_t \to$ all divided by **S**. - ☐ $N_a, C_{ox} \to$ multiplied by **S**. - ☐ $I_D, C_g, \tau \to$ divided by **S**. - ☐ $P \to$ divided by **$S^2$**. - ☐ $PDP \to$ divided by **$S^3$** (the *most important* result). **🔑 Invariants Under CE Scaling (these do NOT change):** - ☐ Electric field **E**. - ☐ Power density **P/Area**. - ☐ Channel resistance **$R_{ch}$**. - ☐ Transconductance **$g_m$**. - ☐ Current density **J**. - ☐ Mobility **μ_n**. **🔑 CV Scaling — Special Memory Hook:** - ☐ $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$ and $V_t' = V_t$ — voltages **do not scale**. - ☐ Power density scales by **$S^3$** (the *worst* consequence). - ☐ Delay still scales by **$1/S$** (speed still improves). - ☐ $R_{ch}$ decreases by S (not constant like in CE). **🔑 Body-Effect Coefficient γ:** - ☐ $\gamma' = \gamma / \sqrt{S}$ (decreases — verify by derivation, not memorization). **🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam:** - ☐ Don't write $\gamma' = \gamma \cdot \sqrt{S}$ (wrong sign of scaling). - ☐ Don't confuse per-gate static power $P = V_{DD} I_D$ with system dynamic power $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ — they scale by $1/S^2$ and $1/S$ respectively. - ☐ Remember that $C_{ox}$ (per unit area) *increases* by S, but the *total* gate capacitance $C_g$ *decreases* by S. - ☐ Always state the assumption (CE vs CV) at the start of every numerical answer. **🔑 Engineering Significance:** - ☐ CE scaling is the **theoretical foundation of Moore's Law** and Dennard's 1974 paper. - ☐ The **"Dennard limit"** broke down around 2005 (90 nm node) due to **leakage currents** (subthreshold, gate-oxide tunneling) that Dennard did not model. - ☐ The industry responded with **multi-core architectures**, **FinFETs** (3D tri-gate), and **GAAFETs** (Gate-All-Around) to continue density scaling without the energy-efficiency benefits of pure CE scaling. - ☐ Modern nodes (3 nm, 2 nm) use a *hybrid* approach: CE-like scaling of the *channel* combined with CV-like scaling of the *system supply rail*.

topics.title: Analogy of MOSFET Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analogy of MOSFET Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor (MOS) structure, the **MOSFET capacitance** refers to the total small-signal capacitance observed between the gate electrode and the semiconductor body when a small AC signal is superimposed on a slowly varying DC gate bias. Because the MOS system behaves as a series combination of the oxide capacitance ($C_{ox}$) and the semiconductor depletion-layer capacitance ($C_s$), the effective gate capacitance ($C_g$) is a **bias-dependent** quantity. The term **"Analogy of MOSFET Capacitance"** refers to the conceptual equivalence drawn between the MOS structure and a **voltage-controlled variable parallel-plate capacitor**, where the gate voltage ($V_{GB}$) modulates the charge distribution at the Si–SiO₂ interface, thereby changing the effective dielectric thickness of the depletion region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners treat the **C–V (Capacitance–Voltage) characteristics** of a MOS capacitor as the diagnostic fingerprint for identifying the three operating regions: **Accumulation**, **Depletion**, and **Inversion**. Mastering this analogy is essential for Module-2 derivations and Part-B problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **leaky water tank** connected to a pump:
- When the pump pushes water **in heavily** (large negative gate voltage) → water accumulates on one side of the tank wall → this is the **Accumulation region** (capacitance is maximum, $C = C_{ox}$).
- When the pump is **off or low pressure** (small negative gate voltage) → the water recedes, exposing a dry "depletion" zone in the middle → this is the **Depletion region** (capacitance drops because the effective separation between the plates increases).
- When the pump pulls water **from the other side** (positive gate voltage) → water is drawn in from a deep well beneath, forming a thin sheet of "minority carriers" at the surface → this is the **Inversion region** (capacitance either returns to $C_{ox}$ at low frequency or stays at $C_{min}$ at high frequency).

The **oxide layer** acts as a fixed, rigid wall, while the **semiconductor depletion region** acts as a soft, voltage-controlled spacer. Together, they form the two series "plates" of a variable capacitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Numerical Problems):**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
> - Relative permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9$
> - Relative permittivity of Silicon: $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7$
> - Electronic charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ (at 300 K)
> - Thermal voltage: $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV (at 300 K)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MOS Capacitor — Bias-Dependent Behavior

The MOS structure consists of a metal gate, a thin SiO₂ dielectric, and a p-type silicon substrate. Applying a gate-to-body voltage $V_{GB}$ produces three distinct regimes:

**Step 1 — Accumulation ($V_{GB} \ll 0$):**
- Negative gate voltage repels electrons from the Si surface and attracts holes.
- A dense sheet of majority-carrier (hole) charge piles up at the Si–SiO₂ interface.
- The semiconductor surface behaves like a perfect conductor.
- Effective capacitance: $C = C_{ox}$ (maximum).

**Step 2 — Depletion ($-V_{FB} < V_{GB} < V_{th}$):**
- Negative gate voltage is moderate; holes are pushed away, leaving behind a region depleted of mobile carriers.
- Only fixed, negatively charged acceptor ions ($N_A^-$) remain in the depletion zone.
- The depletion width $W$ grows with $|V_{GB}|$, increasing the effective dielectric thickness.
- Effective capacitance: $C < C_{ox}$ and decreases as $V_{GB}$ becomes more negative.

**Step 3 — Inversion ($V_{GB} > V_{th}$):**
- Positive gate voltage attracts minority carriers (electrons) to the surface.
- A thin inversion layer of electrons forms at the Si–SiO₂ interface.
- **Two sub-regimes exist:**
  - **Low-frequency (LF) C–V:** Inversion charge can track the AC signal → $C$ returns to $C_{ox}$.
  - **High-frequency (HF) C–V:** Inversion charge cannot respond (generation–recombination is too slow) → $C$ saturates at $C_{min}$.

### 2.2 The Capacitive Analogy (Why is the Effective Capacitance a Series Combination?)

Because the **oxide layer** and the **semiconductor depletion region** are stacked in series between the gate and the neutral bulk, the equivalent circuit is:

$$C_{total}^{-1} = C_{ox}^{-1} + C_s^{-1}$$

where $C_s = \varepsilon_{si}/W$ is the depletion-layer capacitance per unit area and $W$ is the depletion-region width. As $W$ widens, $C_s$ shrinks, and the series combination drops below $C_{ox}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This C–V analogy is the operating principle behind:
> 1. **Charge-Coupled Devices (CCDs)** in digital cameras.
> 2. **CMOS Image Sensors** in smartphones.
