Higher K_R ⇒ lower V_OL. - ☐ **Body effect** on the load transistor degrades V_OH because V_SB,L = V_out. - ☐ The VTC has **four regions**: (I) driver off / load sat, (II) both sat, (III) driver triode / load sat, (IV) both triode. - ☐ **V_IL** is found where dV_out/dV_in = −1, typically in Region III for K_R > 1. - ☐ **Noise margins**: N_ML = V_IL − V_OL, N_MH = V_OH − V_IH. - ☐ **t_PLH >> t_PHL** in NMOS-load inverters because the load is intrinsically weaker than the driver. - ☐ **Depletion-load NMOS** was the workhorse of the 1980s microprocessors (Intel 8086, 80286). - ☐ **Static power** in linear-enhancement-load inverters is high because the load draws continuous DC current. - ☐ The **pseudo-NMOS** logic family of modern VLSI is a direct descendant of the depletion-load NMOS concept. - ☐ Body-effect-corrected threshold: V_T = V_T0 + γ(√(2ϕ_F + V_SB) − √(2ϕ_F)). - ☐ All current equations are derived from the **Level-1 Shichman-Hodges model** in the KTU syllabus. - ☐ The **load-line method** is the universal tool for plotting the VTC graphically. - ☐ **K_R = 4 : 1** is the typical ratio for a saturated-enhancement-load NMOS inverter. --- > **End of Module 2 — Topic: Inverters with n-Type MOSFET Load** > *PEEBT526 — VLSI Design | KTU 2024 Scheme | Premium Engineered Notes v10*

topics.title: CMOS Inverter - Voltage Transfer Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CMOS Inverter - Voltage Transfer Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **CMOS Inverter** (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Inverter) is the fundamental logic gate of digital VLSI design. It consists of a **pMOS transistor** (pull-up network) and an **nMOS transistor** (pull-down network) connected in series between the power supply **V_DD** and ground **GND**, with their gates tied together to form the input node and the common drain connection forming the output node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A CMOS Inverter is a complementary push-pull structure where the pMOS load transistor (PMOS) and the nMOS driver transistor (NMOS) are mutually exclusive in their conduction. When the input is HIGH, the NMOS turns ON and PMOS turns OFF, pulling the output to **V_OL = 0 V**. When the input is LOW, the PMOS turns ON and NMOS turns OFF, pulling the output to **V_OH = V_DD**.

The **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** is the plot of the output voltage $V_{out}$ as a function of the input voltage $V_{in}$ for a CMOS inverter. It is a **monotonically decreasing** function that defines the static (DC) behavior of the gate. The VTC is the single most important characterization curve in digital CMOS design because it determines **noise margins, switching threshold, gain, and logic levels**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Two-Person See-Saw (Tilt-Point Logic)**

Imagine a see-saw in a playground with two people sitting on opposite ends:
- **Person A (PMOS, Top):** Pushing UP to **V_DD** (the sky/high voltage).
- **Person B (NMOS, Bottom):** Pushing DOWN to **GND** (the ground/zero voltage).
- The **input** $V_{in}$ decides *how hard* each person pushes.

When $V_{in}$ is **near 0 V** (LOW), Person A pushes strongly UP, Person B barely pushes, and the see-saw tilts all the way to the top → **Output = V_DD (HIGH)**.

When $V_{in}$ is **near V_DD** (HIGH), Person B pushes strongly DOWN, Person A barely pushes, and the see-saw tilts all the way to the bottom → **Output = 0 V (LOW)**.

In the **middle region**, both people push with comparable force, and the see-saw is in a delicate balance — the **switching threshold** $V_M$ is precisely the *balance point*. If one person (PMOS) is stronger than the other (NMOS), the balance point shifts. This strength ratio is called the **β-ratio** or **k-ratio** in CMOS design.

This analogy makes it intuitive: the VTC is just a smooth, continuous see-saw motion as input goes from low to high.

## 1.3 Significance in VLSI Design

The VTC of a CMOS inverter is the cornerstone for:
- **Static Noise Margin (SNM) analysis** — robustness against noise.
- **Switching threshold tuning** — for symmetric delay.
- **Cascaded gate design** — ensuring logic level compatibility.
- **Process corner analysis** — Fast/Fast, Slow/Slow, Typical/Typical.
- **Power-supply scaling** — sub-threshold and low-voltage operation.

The VTC is essentially a **sigmoid (S-shaped) curve** mapped from the $V_{in}$ axis (x-axis) to the $V_{out}$ axis (y-axis), with five distinct operating regions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CMOS Inverter Structure

The CMOS inverter schematic is built as:

- **PMOS transistor (MP):** Source tied to **V_DD**, drain tied to output node, gate tied to input.
- **NMOS transistor (MN):** Source tied to **GND**, drain tied to output node, gate tied to input.
- **Output node $V_{out}$:** Common drain connection of MP and MN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Body Effect Note (KTU Favourite):** In a standard CMOS inverter, the body (bulk) of the PMOS is tied to V_DD and the body of the NMOS is tied to GND. Hence, the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB} = 0$ for both devices, which **eliminates the body effect** during static analysis. This is a key advantage of the CMOS topology over pseudo-nMOS.

## 2.2 The Five Operating Regions of the VTC

As $V_{in}$ sweeps from **0 V to V_DD**, the CMOS inverter passes through **five distinct regions**. Each region is defined by the relative terminal voltages of the two transistors.

### Region 1: $0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ — Output HIGH (Stable HIGH)
- **NMOS (MN):** $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ → **OFF (Cutoff)**. No current flows.
- **PMOS (MP):** $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD} < V_{Tp}$ (where $V_{Tp} < 0$) → **ON (Linear/Triode)**. Source-to-drain voltage $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{out} > 0$ (small).
- **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (no current → no voltage drop across PMOS).

### Region 2: $V_{Tn} \le V_{in} < V_{out} + V_{Tp}$ — NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode
- **NMOS:** $V_{DS,n} = V_{out} > V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn} = V_{in} - V_{Tn}$ → **Saturation**.
- **PMOS:** $V_{DS,p} = V_{DD} - V_{out} < V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp} = (V_{in} - V_{DD}) - V_{Tp}$ → **Triode (Linear)**.
- **Output:** $V_{out}$ starts to drop slowly from $V_{DD}$.

### Region 3: $V_{out} + V_{Tp} \le V_{in} \le V_{out} + V_{Tn}$ — Both Transistors in Saturation
- **NMOS:** $V_{DS,n} = V_{out} \ge V_{in} - V_{Tn}$ → **Saturation**.
- **PMOS:** $V_{DS,p} = V_{DD} - V_{out} \ge V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp}$ → **Saturation**.
- **Output:** This is the **high-gain transition region**. A small change in $V_{in}$ causes a large change in $V_{out}$.
- **Switching point** $V_M$ lies in this region. At $V_{in} = V_M$, $V_{out} = V_M$.

### Region 4: $V_{out} + V_{Tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} + V_{Tp}$ — NMOS Triode, PMOS Saturation
- **NMOS:** $V_{DS,n} = V_{out} < V_{in} - V_{Tn}$ → **Triode (Linear)**.
- **PMOS:** $V_{DS,p} = V_{DD} - V_{out} > V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{Tp}$ → **Saturation**.
- **Output:** $V_{out}$ approaches 0 V.

### Region 5: $V_{in} \ge V_{DD} + V_{Tp}$ — Output LOW (Stable LOW)
- **NMOS:** $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} = V_{DD} > V_{Tn}$ and $V_{DS,n} \approx 0$ → **Triode**.
- **PMOS:** $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD} = 0 > V_{Tp}$ → **OFF (Cutoff)**.
- **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$ V (no current → no voltage drop across NMOS).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"Cutoff-Sat-Triode"* for NMOS as $V_{in}$ rises: **OFF → Saturation → Triode → ON**. The PMOS does the exact mirror image.

## 2.3 Critical VTC Parameters

### Switching Threshold $V_M$ (The Inversion Point)
The point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. It is found by equating the NMOS and PMOS drain currents at $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. Both devices are in **saturation** at this point (for the symmetric or near-symmetric case).

$$I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}$$

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2$$

Solving:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric Inverter Condition:** When $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, the inverter is **symmetric** and the noise margins are maximized. This requires:
> $$k_n = k_p \quad \text{and} \quad V_{Tn} = -V_{Tp}$$
> Since $|V_{Tp}| \neq V_{Tn}$ in real processes, designers adjust the **W/L ratios** so that $\frac{k_p}{k_n} = 1$, typically $\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} \approx 2\text{–}3$ (because hole mobility $\mu_p < \mu_n$).

### Noise Margins
- **$V_{IL}$:** Input voltage at which $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ (high-gain boundary, low-side).
- **$V_{IH}$:** Input voltage at which $\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ (high-gain boundary, high-side).
- **$V_{OH} = V_{DD}$** and **$V_{OL} = 0$** for ideal CMOS.
- **Noise Margin High:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$
- **Noise Margin Low:** $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite 14-Mark Question:** Derive the noise margins in terms of the β-ratio and threshold voltages. This is a guaranteed KTU exam topic.

### Logic Levels and Operating Points Summary

| Parameter | Definition | Ideal Symmetric Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{OH}$ | Output HIGH | $V_{DD}$ |
| $V_{OL}$ | Output LOW | $0$ V |
| $V_{IH}$ | Minimum input recognised as HIGH | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ for symmetric |
| $V_{IL}$ | Maximum input recognised as LOW | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ for symmetric |
| $V_M$ | Switching threshold | $0.5 V_{DD}$ for symmetric |
| $NM_H$ | Noise margin high | $\approx 0.4 V_{DD}$ |
| $NM_L$ | Noise margin low | $\approx 0.4 V_{DD}$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Region / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Ideal HIGH output | Region 1 |
| $V_{OL} = 0$ | Ideal LOW output | Region 5 |
| $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD}+V_{Tp})}{1+\sqrt{r}}$ | Switching threshold, where $r = k_p/k_n$ | Region 3 |
| $r_{sym} = \left(\dfrac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}/2 + V_{Tp}}\right)^2$ | Required β-ratio for $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ | Symmetric design |
| $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Noise margin HIGH | Static robustness |
| $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Noise margin LOW | Static robustness |
| $\dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \dfrac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2$ | KCL at $V_M$ (both in saturation) | Region 3 analysis |
| $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter | KTU standard notation |
| $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter | KTU standard notation |
| $\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}$ | Required ratio for symmetric inverter | KTU standard result |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** The threshold voltage $V_{Tp}$ is **negative** by convention. When substituting into formulas, students often forget the negative sign. Always write $V_{Tp} < 0$ and $V_{Tp} = -\vert V_{Tp}\vert$ explicitly. Many KTU students lose 2-3 marks per question on this sign error.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMOS inverter VTC is foundational in:
- **Standard cell library design** — every digital standard cell's behavior traces back to the inverter VTC.
- **SRAM bit-cell read/write margin** — depends directly on inverter noise margins.
- **I/O pad design** — input voltage tolerance and output drive swing.
- **Process variation analysis** — corner simulations of VTC predict yield.
- **Sub-threshold / Near-threshold computing** — VTC degrades severely and must be re-characterized for IoT applications.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$

We derive the switching threshold $V_M$ at which $V_{in} = V_{out}$, with the assumption that both transistors are in **saturation** (valid for symmetric and near-symmetric designs).

### Step 1: Apply KCL at the Output Node
At the switching point, no DC current can flow into the gate, so the drain currents must be equal:
$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

### Step 2: Write the Saturation Current Equations
For the NMOS in saturation:
$$I_{Dn,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$
For the PMOS in saturation (using $|V_{GS,p}|$):
$$I_{Dp,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2$$

### Step 3: Equate and Cancel
$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2$$

$$\frac{k_n}{k_p}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})^2$$

Taking the positive square root (both factors are positive at $V_M$ since $V_M < V_{DD} + V_{Tp}$ when $V_{Tp} < 0$):

$$\sqrt{\frac{k_n}{k_p}}(V_M - V_{Tn}) = (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})$$

Let $r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n}$ (this is the **β-ratio**). Then $\sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$:

$$\frac{(V_M - V_{Tn})}{\sqrt{r}} = V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp}$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically for $V_M$

$$V_M - V_{Tn} = \sqrt{r}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{Tp})$$

$$V_M - V_{Tn} = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})$$

$$V_M - \sqrt{r} \cdot V_M = V_{Tn} - \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})$$

$$V_M(1 - \sqrt{r}) = V_{Tn} - \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})$$

$$\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical CMOS switching threshold formula** that KTU expects students to derive from scratch. Memorize the boxed result and understand each algebraic step.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Symmetric Inverter Condition

A symmetric inverter is one in which $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, which maximizes both noise margins equally. Set $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ in the switching threshold equation.

### Step 1: Substitute $V_M = V_{DD}/2$

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

### Step 2: Cross-Multiply

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2}(1 + \sqrt{r}) = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})$$

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \frac{V_{DD}\sqrt{r}}{2} = V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r} V_{DD} + \sqrt{r} V_{Tp}$$

### Step 3: Collect $\sqrt{r}$ Terms

$$\frac{V_{DD}\sqrt{r}}{2} - \sqrt{r} V_{DD} = V_{Tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \sqrt{r} V_{Tp}$$

$$\sqrt{r}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{DD}\right) = V_{Tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \sqrt{r} V_{Tp}$$

$$-\frac{V_{DD}\sqrt{r}}{2} = V_{Tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \sqrt{r} V_{Tp}$$

### Step 4: Isolate $\sqrt{r}$

$$-\frac{V_{DD}\sqrt{r}}{2} - \sqrt{r} V_{Tp} = V_{Tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

$$\sqrt{r}\left(-\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{Tp}\right) = V_{Tn} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

$$\sqrt{r}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} + V_{Tp}\right) = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{Tn}$$

### Step 5: Final Symmetric Inverter Condition

$$\boxed{\sqrt{r} = \frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}/2 + V_{Tp}}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{r = \left(\frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}/2 + V_{Tp}}\right)^2 = \frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n}}$$

This gives the required **W/L ratio** for the PMOS to make the inverter symmetric:

$$\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}/2 + V_{Tp}}\right)^2$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Full KTU 14-Mark Solution Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a 0.18 μm process with $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.4$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.5$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250$ μA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 100$ μA/V², $(W/L)_n = 1$, $(W/L)_p = 2.5$. Find: (a) the switching threshold $V_M$, (b) the W/L ratio required for a symmetric inverter, (c) the noise margins (assume ideal CMOS).

### Part (a): Switching Threshold $V_M$

Compute the β-ratio $r$:

$$r = \frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n} = \frac{100 \times 2.5}{250 \times 1} = 1.0$$

Apply the switching threshold formula:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{r}(V_{DD} + V_{Tp})}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.4 + \sqrt{1.0}(1.8 + (-0.5))}{1 + \sqrt{1.0}}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.4 + (1.3)}{1 + 1} = \frac{1.7}{2} = 0.85 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V, the inverter is **almost symmetric** but $V_M$ is slightly below $V_{DD}/2$, indicating a marginally stronger NMOS.

### Part (b): Required W/L Ratio for Symmetric Inverter

$$\sqrt{r_{sym}} = \frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}/2 + V_{Tp}} = \frac{0.9 - 0.4}{0.9 + (-0.5)} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25$$

$$r_{sym} = 1.5625$$

$$\frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} = 1.5625$$

$$\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = 1.5625 \times \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} = 1.5625 \times \frac{250}{100} = 3.906$$

So the required W/L ratio is **$(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n \approx 3.9$** for a perfectly symmetric inverter.

### Part (c): Noise Margins

For the ideal symmetric case ($V_M = V_{DD}/2$), KTU accepts the standard approximation:
- $V_{IL} \approx V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V
- $V_{IH} \approx V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V
- $V_{OH} = V_{DD} = 1.8$ V
- $V_{OL} = 0$ V

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 1.8 - 0.9 = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.9 - 0 = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** In actual KTU valuations, if the question says "compute the noise margins", you must define $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ as the input points where the slope of the VTC equals $-1$, and solve the transcendental equation by substituting the saturation and triode current equations. Marks are awarded for the *method* (setting up the slope equation) and not just numerical answer.

## 3.4 Effect of β-Ratio on VTC — Analytical Matrix

| Condition | Effect on $V_M$ | Effect on VTC Shape | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k_n = k_p$ (ideal symmetric) | $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ | Symmetric S-curve, equal $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ | Standard symmetric design |
| $k_n > k_p$ (strong NMOS) | $V_M < V_{DD}/2$ | Curve shifts right; output takes longer to go LOW, but pulls LOW strongly | High-drive LOW output needed |
| $k_p > k_n$ (strong PMOS) | $V_M > V_{DD}/2$ | Curve shifts left; output rises quickly but falls slowly | High-drive HIGH output needed |
| $V_{DD}$ decreases (scaling) | $V_M$ shifts, NM degrades | Curve becomes less steep, gain drops | Sub-threshold / IoT design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The VTC gain in Region 3 is approximately:
> $$A_v = \frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \approx -(k_n + k_p) \cdot \text{function}(V_M, V_{Tn}, V_{Tp})$$
> A higher β-ratio or larger $(W/L)$ increases the gain, but also increases capacitance and area.
