# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is a CMOS transmission gate? Explain its operation with a circuit diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
A CMOS transmission gate (TG) is a parallel combination of one NMOS and one PMOS transistor whose gates are driven by complementary control signals $C$ and $\bar{C}$. When $C = 1$ (HIGH), the NMOS passes a strong '0' and the PMOS passes a strong '1', so the full voltage swing (0 to $V_{DD}$) appears at the output. When $C = 0$ (LOW), both transistors are OFF, isolating the input from the output, producing a **high-impedance (Hi-Z)** state. The TG is therefore a **bidirectional analog switch** widely used in multiplexers, XOR gates, and master–slave flip-flops. The schematic is a 2-transistor cell with NMOS gate on top and PMOS gate on bottom driven by inverted clocks.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a CMOS D-latch and a CMOS D flip-flop. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | D-Latch | D Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Level-sensitive | Edge-triggered |
| Transparency | Transparent when CLK = 1 | Never transparent |
| Structure | 2 TGs + 2 inverters (6T) | 2 latches in series (12T+) |
| Race-around | Possible if $t_{pulse} > t_{pd}$ | Eliminated by master–slave |
| Used in | Asynchronous buffers, line drivers | Synchronous registers, counters |

**Application:** Latches are used in **sense amplifiers in SRAM**; flip-flops are used in **pipeline registers of CPUs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a 2:1 multiplexer using CMOS transmission gates. Draw the transistor-level schematic and explain its operation for both values of the select line. Show that it uses only 6 transistors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a master–slave D flip-flop using CMOS transmission gates. Discuss how it eliminates the race-around condition present in a level-sensitive D-latch.

#### **Model Solution for B1(a):**

**Step 1 — Boolean equation**
$$Y = \bar{S} \cdot A + S \cdot B$$

**Step 2 — Identify control logic**
- TG-1 (passes A) requires: NMOS gate = $\bar{S}$, PMOS gate = $S$.
- TG-2 (passes B) requires: NMOS gate = $S$, PMOS gate = $\bar{S}$.

**Step 3 — Invert S**
One CMOS inverter produces $\bar{S}$ from $S$ (2 transistors: 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS).

**Step 4 — Connect the TGs**
- TG-1: N1 (gate = $\bar{S}$) ∥ P1 (gate = $S$); input = $A$, output = $Y$.
- TG-2: N2 (gate = $S$) ∥ P2 (gate = $\bar{S}$); input = $B$, output = $Y$.

**Step 5 — Operation**
- **Case S = 0:** N1 and P1 ON (since $\bar{S} = 1$ and $S = 0$... wait, N1 needs HIGH, so N1 OFF; P1 needs LOW, so P1 ON). Combined with $\bar{S}$ driving the PMOS, **TG-1 conducts**, passing $A$ to $Y$. TG-2 is OFF because N2 gate is LOW and P2 gate is HIGH. Hence $Y = A$. ✅
- **Case S = 1:** TG-1 OFF, TG-2 ON, hence $Y = B$. ✅

**Step 6 — Transistor count**
2 TGs × 2 transistors + 1 INV × 2 transistors = **6 transistors**.

#### **Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Step 1 — Recall D-latch structure**
A single D-latch uses two transmission gates (TG1, TG2) and two cross-coupled inverters (INV1, INV2). TG1 is active when CLK = 1 (transparent), TG2 holds the value when CLK = 0.

**Step 2 — Master–slave topology**
Two such latches are cascaded: the **master** is clocked by CLK, and the **slave** is clocked by $\bar{CLK}$. The output of the master feeds the input of the slave.

**Step 3 — Operation**
- **When CLK = 1:** Master is *transparent* (TG1 ON, TG2 OFF) → master node $M$ tracks $D$. Slave is *holding* (TG1 OFF, TG2 ON) → $Q$ is preserved.
- **When CLK = 0:** Master is *holding* (last $D$ is latched). Slave becomes *transparent* → $Q$ follows $M$.

**Step 4 — Edge-triggered behavior**
$Q$ changes only on the **falling edge of CLK** (or rising edge, depending on convention), giving **edge-triggered** operation.

**Step 5 — Race-around elimination**
In a level-sensitive latch, if $t_{pulse} > t_{pd}$ of the feedback loop, the output oscillates (race-around). In the master–slave flip-flop, the master and slave are *never simultaneously transparent* — so there is no continuous feedback path during a clock pulse. The output $Q$ can only update once per clock edge, **eliminating race-around**.

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a CMOS XOR gate using 10 transistors. Draw the schematic and derive its logical effort and parasitic delay.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a 1-bit full adder using static CMOS compound gates. Show the transistor count and explain how the sum and carry are generated.

#### **Model Solution for B2(a):**

**Step 1 — XOR function**
$$X = A \oplus B = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B$$

**Step 2 — 10T XOR architecture**
The classic **10-transistor XOR** consists of:
- 1 inverter to produce $\bar{A}$ (2T).
- 1 inverter to produce $\bar{B}$ (2T).
- 1 pass-transistor network: 2 NMOS (one gated by A passing $\bar{B}$, one gated by $\bar{A}$ passing B) → 2T.
- 1 pull-up/pull-down output stage (2 PMOS + 2 NMOS) → 4T.

**Total: 2 + 2 + 2 + 4 = 10 transistors** ✅

**Step 3 — Logical effort**
Reference: inverter input cap = $C_{inv}$. For an XOR driving the same output cap, the input capacitance is asymmetric: $C_{in,A} = 3C_{inv}$, $C_{in,B} = 2C_{inv}$. Using the standard method:

$$g = \frac{C_{in}}{C_{inv}} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{(path effort ratio)}}$$

For balanced design, $g_{XOR} \approx 2.67$ (per KTU textbook by Weste & Harris).

**Step 4 — Parasitic delay**
$$p_{XOR} \approx 2 \cdot p_{inv} = 2$$

(Approximately twice the inverter's intrinsic parasitic delay of 1.)

**Step 5 — Stage delay**
$$t_{p,XOR} = 5\tau \cdot (g + p) = 5\tau \cdot (2.67 + 2) \approx 23.35\tau$$

#### **Model Solution for B2(b):**

**Step 1 — Sum signal**
The sum $S$ requires XOR of three inputs: $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$.

Cascade two XOR gates:
- $X_1 = A \oplus B$ (10T)
- $S = X_1 \oplus C_{in}$ (10T)

**Step 2 — Carry signal using AOI**
The carry equation:
$$C_{out} = A \cdot B + (A \oplus B) \cdot C_{in}$$

AOI implementation:
- PDN (NMOS): A∥B in series with ($X_1$ in series with $C_{in}$).
- PUN (PMOS): dual network = A in series with B, in parallel with ($X_1$ in series with $C_{in}$).
- Plus one output inverter.

Transistor count for AOI: ~**8 transistors**.

**Step 3 — Total transistor count**

| Block | Transistors |
|---|---|
| 1st XOR ($A \oplus B$) | 10 |
| 2nd XOR ($X_1 \oplus C_{in}$) | 10 |
| AOI for $C_{out}$ | 8 |
| **Total** | **28 transistors** |

**Step 4 — Power & delay advantage**
Compared to a fully NAND-based adder (48T+), this is **~40% smaller** with **lower dynamic power** due to fewer switching nodes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inverter for $\bar{S}$** in a TG-based MUX — many students draw only 4 transistors and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Inverting the NMOS/PMOS gate polarity** — the NMOS in a TG must be driven by the *opposite* signal to the PMOS. Drawing both with the same gate loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing LATCH (level) with FLIP-FLOP (edge)** — a frequent conceptual error. Master–slave = edge-triggered; single-latch = level-sensitive.
> 4. **Ignoring clock inversion in master–slave design** — the slave MUST be clocked by $\bar{CLK}$, not $CLK$. The inverter at the clock input is mandatory.
> 5. **Drawing NMOS for pull-up in a "transmission gate"** — only TGs use NMOS+PMOS in parallel. Static CMOS always uses PMOS for pull-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compound CMOS Circuits**

- ✅ A **CMOS Transmission Gate (TG)** = NMOS ∥ PMOS with complementary clock inputs → bidirectional analog switch with **no $V_{TH}$ drop**.
- ✅ A **2:1 MUX using TG** = 6 transistors (2 TGs + 1 INV).
- ✅ A **4:1 MUX using TG** = 12 transistors.
- ✅ A **D-Latch** = 2 TGs + 2 INVs in feedback = 6 transistors (level-sensitive).
- ✅ A **D Flip-Flop** = 2 D-Latches cascaded in master–slave = ~12–14 transistors (edge-triggered).
- ✅ A **10T XOR** uses 4 pass transistors + 2 output drivers + 2 input inverters.
- ✅ A **1-bit Full Adder** ≈ 28 transistors (2 XOR + 1 AOI + 1 INV).
- ✅ **Master–slave flip-flop** eliminates the **race-around condition**.
- ✅ **PUN** and **PDN** in static CMOS are **duals** of each other.
- ✅ **Logical effort**: NAND-2 = $4/3$, NOR-2 = $5/3$, XOR = $\approx 2.67$, INV = $1$.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** $\propto V_{DD}^2$ — reducing $V_{DD}$ is the most effective low-power lever.
- ✅ **Noise margin** must be **$\geq 0.1 V_{DD}$** for robust design.
- ✅ For KTU ESE, always **draw the schematic**, **tabulate transistor count**, and **derive the boolean equation** — this secures full 14 marks.

> **High-Yield 1-Liners for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"TG = NMOS ∥ PMOS, dual-gated, full-swing, bidirectional."*
> *"Latch is level, Flip-Flop is edge — master–slave gives edge."*
> *"PUN–PDN are duals: series ↔ parallel."*
> *"Lower $V_{DD}$ = quadratic power reduction."*
> *"2:1 MUX = 6T, Full Adder ≈ 28T, D-FF ≈ 12T."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Circuit Concepts: Sheet Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Circuit Concepts: Sheet Resistance # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT526 – VLSI Design, Module 3)**, **Sheet Resistance** is formally defined as the resistance offered by a thin conducting film (such as a diffusion layer, polysilicon layer, or metal interconnect) of uniform thickness, measured across a **square geometry** of the film. It is denoted by the symbol $R_s$ (or $R_{\square}$) and is expressed in units of **Ohms per square ($\Omega/\square$)**. Mathematically, the sheet resistance of a uniform thin film is given by: $$R_s = \frac{\rho}{t}$$ where: - $\rho$ is the **bulk resistivity** of the material (in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$) - $t$ is the **thickness** of the conducting film (in cm or $\mu$m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Basic Circuit Concepts):** > Sheet Resistance is a foundational parameter that allows VLSI designers to **estimate parasitic resistances** of on-chip interconnects and doped regions **without needing to know the absolute cross-sectional area** of the conductor. The parameter is **process-dependent** and is typically provided in the foundry design rules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Slice" Intuition Imagine you have a **thin, flat sheet of conductive dough** (like aluminum foil). Now, suppose you cut a **perfect square** of side length $L = W$ from this sheet. The resistance measured across the two opposite edges of this square depends only on the **material** and the **thickness** of the sheet — **not on the actual size** of the square. If you make a *larger* square (say, $L = 2W$), the resistance doesn't change, because the longer current path is exactly compensated by the wider cross-section available for current flow. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Intuition:** > Sheet resistance answers the question — *"If I had a piece of this conducting film shaped like any square, what would be its edge-to-edge resistance?"* The answer is a single number, $R_s$, that lets you compute the resistance of **any rectangle** of the same film using a simple ratio $L/W$. The reason it is called "**per square**" is that $L/W$ is a **dimensionless ratio** — the unit of sheet resistance is therefore *Ohms*, and the "per square" is a geometric descriptor, not a true SI unit. ## 1.3 Standard Sheet Resistance Values in VLSI Processes | Material Layer | Typical $R_s$ Range ($\Omega/\square$) | |---|---| | Aluminum (Metal 1) | 0.05 – 0.1 | | Copper (Metal 1) | 0.03 – 0.08 | | n$^+$ / p$^+$ Diffusion | 50 – 200 | | Polysilicon (unsalicided) | 20 – 100 | | Polysilicon (silicided) | 2 – 10 | | n-well / p-well | 1000 – 5000 | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember:** > - **Unit of Sheet Resistance:** $\Omega/\square$ (Ohms per square) > - **Dimension:** Ohms (resistance), with implicit "per square" geometric qualifier > - **Typical process thickness range:** 0.1 $\mu$m – 2 $\mu$m for on-chip layers > - **Standard formula for rectangular resistance:** $R = R_s \cdot \dfrac{L}{W}$ # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Sheet Resistance In VLSI layout, every conducting layer (polysilicon gate, diffusion region, metal interconnect) is a **thin, planar film** deposited on the substrate. The **absolute thickness** $t$ of these films is fixed by the fabrication process and is not under the designer's control. The **length** $L$ and **width** $W$, however, *are* under the designer's control (they come from the layout). This creates a need for a **resistance parameter** that: 1. Encodes the fixed process information ($\rho$ and $t$). 2. Allows the designer to compute the resistance of *any* shape using only the *ratio* $L/W$. Sheet resistance $R_s = \rho / t$ perfectly satisfies this need. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Rectangular Resistance Formula **Step 1 — Start from the basic resistance equation:** For a uniform conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and bulk resistivity $\rho$: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$ **Step 2 — Apply to a thin film of thickness $t$ and width $W$:** The cross-sectional area perpendicular to current flow is $A = W \cdot t$. Substituting: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t}$$ **Step 3 — Group the process-dependent terms:** Factor out $\rho / t$, which depends only on the fabrication process: $$R = \left(\frac{\rho}{t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Recognize the process-dependent term as Sheet Resistance:** Define $R_s \triangleq \rho / t$. Then: $$\boxed{R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W}}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret:** - $R_s$ has units of $\Omega$. - $L/W$ is a dimensionless number-of-squares. - $R$ is in Ohms. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | Sheet resistance (definition) | $\Omega/\square$ | | 2 | $R = R_s \cdot \dfrac{L}{W}$ | Resistance of rectangular film section | $\Omega$ | | 3 | $N = \dfrac{L}{W}$ | Number of "squares" in the rectangle | dimensionless | | 4 | $R = R_s \cdot N$ | Resistance expressed as squares count | $\Omega$ | | 5 | $G_s = \dfrac{1}{R_s} = \dfrac{t}{\rho}$ | Sheet conductance (inverse) | $\mho/\square$ (Siemens/sq) | | 6 | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t} = \dfrac{1}{q \cdot \mu \cdot N \cdot t}$ | Sheet resistance in terms of doping $N$ and mobility $\mu$ | $\Omega/\square$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary / Limiting Cases:** > - If $L = W$ (perfect square): $R = R_s$, regardless of absolute size. > - If $L \ll W$ (wide, short): $R \to 0$ (low-resistance interconnect). > - If $L \gg W$ (narrow, long): $R \to \infty$ (high-resistance region, e.g., poly resistor). > - If $L < W$ (wide region): $R < R_s$ (less than one square's resistance). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Sheet resistance is the **workhorse parameter** for VLSI designers in the following practical applications: - **Estimating parasitic resistance** of polysilicon gate and diffusion interconnects during pre-layout analysis. - **Sizing resistors in analog/mixed-signal design** — designers draw polysilicon or diffusion strips of specific $L/W$ ratios to realize target resistance values. - **IR drop analysis** in power distribution networks (e.g., $R_s$ of the top metal layer is used to compute voltage drop from the power pad to internal blocks). - **Process monitoring** — wafer-level test structures ("van der Pauw" and Greek cross patterns) measure $R_s$ to characterize the fabrication process. - **Antenna rule** and **electromigration checks** in physical verification, where $R_s$ determines the effective resistance seen by ESD/antenna current paths. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Context:** > In Module 3 (Basic Circuit Concepts), sheet resistance bridges the gap between *layout geometry* and *electrical performance*. It is the parameter that lets you translate a drawn rectangle in your layout editor into a numerical resistance value that affects circuit delay, power, and signal integrity. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Bulk Resistivity to Sheet Resistance We begin with the fundamental expression for the resistance of a uniform conductor of length $L$, uniform cross-sectional area $A$, and bulk resistivity $\rho$: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$ ### Step A — Specialize to a Thin-Film Geometry In VLSI, the conducting layers are *planar thin films* deposited on the silicon substrate. For such a film: - The **length** of the conducting path (along the direction of current flow) is $L$. - The **width** of the film (perpendicular to current flow, in the plane of the wafer) is $W$. - The **thickness** of the film (perpendicular to the wafer surface) is $t$. The cross-sectional area perpendicular to the current flow is therefore: $$A = W \cdot t$$ ### Step B — Substitute into the Resistance Equation Substituting $A = W \cdot t$ into the fundamental equation: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t}$$ ### Step C — Algebraic Rearrangement We can rearrange the right-hand side by grouping the geometric terms ($L$ and $W$) separately from the material/process terms ($\rho$ and $t$): $$R = \left(\frac{\rho}{t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right)$$ The grouping is justified by simple algebra: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t} = \frac{\rho}{t} \cdot \frac{L}{W}$$ ### Step D — Define Sheet Resistance By definition, the **Sheet Resistance** $R_s$ is the material/process-dependent factor: $$R_s \triangleq \frac{\rho}{t}$$ Substituting back: $$R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W}$$ ### Step E — Unit Analysis Let us verify the units carefully: - $\rho$ has units of $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$. - $t$ has units of cm. - Therefore, $R_s = \rho / t$ has units of $\dfrac{\Omega \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}} = \Omega$. - The ratio $L/W$ is dimensionless (length / length). - Therefore, $R = R_s \cdot (L/W)$ has units of $\Omega \cdot 1 = \Omega$. ✓ ### Step F — Interpretation of "Per Square" Consider a square geometry where $L = W$. Then: $$R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = R_s \cdot 1 = R_s$$ This shows that **the resistance of a square of any size** of the same film is exactly $R_s$. The unit "per square" ($\Omega/\square$) is therefore a *geometric descriptor* indicating that the value applies on a per-square basis — the dimensionless "square" count $N = L/W$ then tells you how many such squares are in series in your layout rectangle. > [!NOTE] > **End of Derivation — Result:** > $$\boxed{R_s = \frac{\rho}{t} \quad \text{and} \quad R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem **Problem (Worked-out):** A polysilicon layer in a 0.5 $\mu$m CMOS process has a sheet resistance of $R_s = 30 \, \Omega/\square$. A polysilicon resistor is laid out as a rectangle of length $L = 60 \, \mu$m and width $W = 6 \, \mu$m. The layer thickness is $t = 0.5 \, \mu$m. **(a)** Compute the total resistance of the resistor. **(b)** Compute the bulk resistivity $\rho$ of the polysilicon layer. ### Solution (a) — Total Resistance **Given:** - $R_s = 30 \, \Omega/\square$ - $L = 60 \, \mu$m - $W = 6 \, \mu$m **Step 1:** Compute the number of squares $N$: $$N = \frac{L}{W} = \frac{60 \, \mu\text{m}}{6 \, \mu\text{m}} = 10$$ **Step 2:** Apply the rectangular resistance formula: $$\begin{aligned} R &= R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} \\ &= R_s \cdot N \\ &= 30 \, \Omega/\square \times 10 \\ &= 300 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (a):** $R = 300 \, \Omega$ ### Solution (b) — Bulk Resistivity **Given:** - $R_s = 30 \, \Omega/\square$ - $t = 0.5 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}$ **Step 1:** Apply the sheet resistance definition $R_s = \rho / t$: $$R_s = \frac{\rho}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = R_s \cdot t$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values: $$\begin{aligned} \rho &= 30 \, \Omega \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm} \\ &= 150 \times 10^{-5} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \\ &= 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (b):** $\rho = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \;=\; 1.5 \, \text{m}\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Layout Shapes vs. Resistance | Layout Shape | Geometry | Resistance Formula | Number of Squares | |---|---|---|---| | Square | $L = W$ | $R = R_s$ | 1 | | Rectangle (long) | $L > W$ | $R = R_s \cdot (L/W)$ | $L/W$ | | Rectangle (wide) | $L < W$ | $R = R_s \cdot (L/W) < R_s$ | $L/W < 1$ | | Series of $n$ squares | $L = nW$ | $R = n \cdot R_s$ | $n$ | | Parallel pair (each $L \times W$) | 2 parallel paths | $R = (R_s \cdot L/W) / 2$ | $(L/W) / 2$ | | Corner square (L-bend) | 2 squares, each $W \times W$ | $R \approx 0.5 \cdot R_s$ | ~0.56 (empirical) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important Note on Corners and Bends:** > In practice, a 90° corner in a layout does *not* contribute exactly 1 square. Empirical corrections show that an L-bend contributes approximately **0.56 squares** of resistance. KTU exam questions sometimes include such corrections — read the question carefully to see if a corner correction factor is specified. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sheet Resistance Concept Flow The following block diagram illustrates how a VLSI layout rectangle is mapped to its electrical resistance using the sheet resistance concept. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["LAYOUT INPUT
Drawn Rectangle L x W"] --> B["PROCESS INFO
Bulk Resistivity rho
Film Thickness t"] B --> C["SHEET RESISTANCE COMPUTATION
R sub s equals rho divided by t"] A --> D["GEOMETRY COMPUTATION
Number of squares N equals L divided by W"] C --> E["RECTANGULAR RESISTANCE FORMULA
R equals R sub s times L divided by W"] D --> E E --> F["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
Total Resistance R in Ohms"] F --> G["APPLICATIONS
IR drop analysis
Resistor sizing
Parasitic estimation
Process monitoring"] style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2d5f3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#5f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#5f2d4a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#2d3f5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style G fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Square Counting in Layout Rectangles This diagram explains the "counting squares" mental model used by VLSI designers to compute resistance from layout. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Original Layout
Long rectangle L = 60 um, W = 6 um"] --> B1["Divide into unit squares
Each square is 6 um x 6 um"] B1 --> C1["Count squares in series
N equals 60 divided by 6 equals 10 squares"] C1 --> D1["Each square contributes R sub s Ohms
10 squares in series means 10 times R sub s"] D1 --> E1["Total resistance R equals 10 times R sub s"] style A1 fill:#2d4a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B1 fill:#3f5f2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C1 fill:#5f3f2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style D1 fill:#5f2d3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style E1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — Different Layout Geometries ```mermaid flowchart TD A["GEOMETRY INPUT"] --> B1["SQUARE L equals W"] A --> B2["LONG RECTANGLE L greater than W"] A --> B3["WIDE RECTANGLE L less than W"] A --> B4["SERIES OF N SQUARES"] B1 --> C1["N equals 1
R equals R sub s"] B2 --> C2["N equals L divided by W greater than 1
R equals R sub s times L divided by W"] B3 --> C3["N less than 1
R less than R sub s"] B4 --> C4["R equals N times R sub s
R proportional to N"] style A fill:#1f2d3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B1 fill:#3f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B2 fill:#3f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B3 fill:#3f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style B4 fill:#3f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C1 fill:#5f2d2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C2 fill:#5f2d2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C3 fill:#5f2d2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style C4 fill:#5f2d2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.4 Concept Map — Sheet Resistance in the VLSI Design Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["FABRICATION PROCESS
Material and Thickness"] --> S1["SHEET RESISTANCE R sub s"] L1["LAYOUT GEOMETRY
Length L and Width W"] --> S1 S1 --> S2["PARASITIC RESISTANCE R equals R sub s times L divided by W"] S2 --> A1["TIMING ANALYSIS
RC delay"] S2 --> A2["POWER ANALYSIS
IR drop"] S2 --> A3["ANALOG DESIGN
Resistor sizing"] S2 --> A4["PHYSICAL VERIFICATION
EM and antenna"] style P1 fill:#2d3f5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style L1 fill:#2d3f5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#5f2d4a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#5f4a2d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style A1 fill:#1f3a3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style A2 fill:#1f3a3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#1f3a3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#1f3a3f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Sheet Resistance. Mention its unit and significance in VLSI design.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Sheet Resistance $R_s$ is the resistance of a square-shaped thin conducting film, measured edge-to-edge across the square, with the film having uniform thickness $t$ and bulk resistivity $\rho$. **Formula:** $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ **Unit:** $\Omega/\square$ (Ohms per square). The "per square" is a geometric descriptor, indicating that the value applies to any square of the film regardless of absolute size. **Significance in VLSI Design:** 1. It allows designers to compute the parasitic resistance of on-chip interconnects and doped regions using only the layout geometry ($L/W$ ratio). 2. It abstracts the process parameters ($\rho$, $t$) into a single foundry-provided constant. 3. It is used in IR drop analysis, resistor sizing, RC delay estimation, and electromigration checks. > **Word count for 3-mark answer:** 3–4 sentences covering definition + unit + 2–3 significance points. --- ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A polysilicon interconnect of length 50 $\mu$m and width 5 $\mu$m has a sheet resistance of 40 $\Omega/\square$. Calculate its resistance.** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $L = 50 \, \mu$m, $W = 5 \, \mu$m - $R_s = 40 \, \Omega/\square$ **Step 1:** Compute the number of squares: $$N = \frac{L}{W} = \frac{50}{5} = 10$$ **Step 2:** Compute the resistance: $$R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 40 \times 10 = 400 \, \Omega$$ > **Answer:** $R = 400 \, \Omega$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### CHOICE A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Question A:** With a neat derivation, obtain the expression for the resistance of a rectangular thin-film section in terms of sheet resistance. A metal interconnect of length 200 $\mu$m and width 4 $\mu$m is fabricated with a sheet resistance of 0.08 $\Omega/\square$. **(a)** Calculate the resistance of this interconnect. **(b)** If the same interconnect is folded into a serpentine of 5 parallel legs, each of effective length 40 $\mu$m and width 4 $\mu$m, calculate the new equivalent resistance. Comment on the design trade-off. #### Model Solution **Derivation (3 marks for setup + 4 marks for derivation):** Starting from the basic resistance equation for a uniform conductor: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$ For a thin film of width $W$ and thickness $t$, the cross-sectional area is $A = W \cdot t$. Substituting: $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot t} = \left(\frac{\rho}{t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right)$$ Define $R_s = \rho / t$ as the **Sheet Resistance** of the film. Then: $$\boxed{R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W}}$$ The factor $L/W$ is dimensionless and is called the **number of squares** $N$. Therefore, $R = R_s \cdot N$, meaning the resistance is just the sheet resistance multiplied by the number of squares. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** $L = 200 \, \mu$m, $W = 4 \, \mu$m, $R_s = 0.08 \, \Omega/\square$ **Step 1:** Compute number of squares: $$N_1 = \frac{L}{W} = \frac{200}{4} = 50$$ **Step 2:** Compute resistance: $$R_1 = R_s \cdot N_1 = 0.08 \times 50 = 4 \, \Omega$$ > **Answer (a):** $R_1 = 4 \, \Omega$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** 5 parallel legs, each of $L = 40 \, \mu$m and $W = 4 \, \mu$m. **Step 1:** Resistance of one leg: $$R_{\text{leg}} = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 0.08 \times \frac{40}{4} = 0.08 \times 10 = 0.8 \, \Omega$$ **Step 2:** For 5 legs in **parallel** (parallel current paths): $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{eq}} &= \frac{R_{\text{leg}}}{5} \\ &= \frac{0.8}{5} \\ &= 0.16 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Design Trade-off Comment:** The serpentine layout reduces the resistance by a factor of 5 (from $4 \, \Omega$ to $0.16 \, \Omega$). However, this comes at the cost of: - **Increased layout area** (5 parallel legs require more silicon real estate). - **Increased parasitic capacitance** to substrate (more perimeter, more coupling). - **Reduced electromigration reliability** per leg (current splits but each branch still must carry its share). > **Answer (b):** $R_{\text{eq}} = 0.16 \, \Omega$. Trade-off: lower resistance at the cost of higher area and capacitance. --- ### CHOICE B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Question B:** Explain the concept of sheet resistance with a suitable example. An n$^+$ diffusion region in a CMOS process has a sheet resistance of 80 $\Omega/\square$ and a thickness of 0.4 $\mu$m. **(a)** Calculate the bulk resistivity of the n$^+$ diffusion layer. **(b)** A resistor is to be designed using this layer with a target resistance of 2.4 k$\Omega$. If the minimum design width is 2 $\mu$m, find the required length of the resistor. State any two design considerations for resistor layout in VLSI. #### Model Solution **Concept Explanation (3 marks):** Sheet resistance $R_s$ is the resistance of a square of thin conducting film, defined as $R_s = \rho / t$. For a rectangular film of length $L$ and width $W$, the resistance is $R = R_s \cdot (L/W)$. Sheet resistance is process-dependent and is used to estimate on-chip parasitic resistances and to size layout resistors. **Example:** A polysilicon resistor drawn with $L = 20 \, \mu$m and $W = 2 \, \mu$m in a process with $R_s = 50 \, \Omega/\square$ has a resistance of $R = 50 \times (20/2) = 500 \, \Omega$. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** $R_s = 80 \, \Omega/\square$, $t = 0.4 \, \mu$m **Step 1:** Convert thickness to cm (since $\rho$ is typically in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$): $$t = 0.4 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}$$ **Step 2:** Apply $R_s = \rho / t$: $$\rho = R_s \cdot t = 80 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$$ $$\rho = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} = 3.2 \, \text{m}\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$$ > **Answer (a):** $\rho = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $R = 2.4 \, \text{k}\Omega = 2400 \, \Omega$, $W_{\min} = 2 \, \mu$m, $R_s = 80 \, \Omega/\square$ **Step 1:** Apply the resistance formula $R = R_s \cdot (L/W)$ and solve for $L$: $$L = \frac{R \cdot W}{R_s}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values: $$\begin{aligned} L &= \frac{2400 \, \Omega \times 2 \, \mu\text{m}}{80 \, \Omega/\square} \\ &= \frac{4800}{80} \, \mu\text{m} \\ &= 60 \, \mu\text{m} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (b):** Required length $L = 60 \, \mu$m **Two Design Considerations for Resistor Layout in VLSI:** 1. **Use of Dummy Polysilicon / Dummy Diffusion Strips:** Place dummy structures alongside the active resistor to ensure uniform doping and etching, preventing process variation at the edges (lithographic bias). This improves resistor matching in analog designs. 2. **Avoiding High-Current Density Regions:** Use adequate width to keep current density below electromigration limits. For very long resistors, folding (serpentine layout) is preferred to reduce die area, but care must be taken to maintain matched current paths and avoid hot spots at corners. 3. **(Bonus) Contact Placement:** Place multiple contacts at the resistor ends to minimize contact resistance, which would otherwise add in series with the designed resistance and degrade accuracy. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Forgetting units:** Always carry units in numerical problems; the answer in $\Omega$ (not $k\Omega$ or m$\Omega$ unless asked) often decides between full and partial credit. > - **Mixing up $L$ and $W$:** In a layout, $L$ is *along* the current flow; $W$ is *across*. Reversing them gives an answer that is off by $(L/W)^2$ — a major error. > - **Treating sheet resistance as Ohms per length:** $R_s$ is in $\Omega/\square$, **NOT** $\Omega/\mu$m. The "per square" is dimensionless. > - **Ignoring corner effects:** In serpentine resistors, each 90° corner contributes ~0.56 squares of additional resistance, which can be significant for high-precision analog resistors. KTU exam questions sometimes explicitly mention this — read carefully. > - **Forgetting to convert $\mu$m to cm** when computing $\rho$ from $R_s$ and $t$ — a very common mark-loser. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Sheet Resistance (Module 3, PEEBT526):** > > - **Definition:** $R_s = \rho / t$ — resistance of a square of thin film, units $\Omega/\square$. > - **Rectangular Resistance:** $R = R_s \cdot (L/W) = R_s \cdot N$, where $N$ is the number of squares. > - **Sheet resistance is independent of absolute size** — only depends on material and thickness. > - **Process-dependent:** Provided in foundry design rule manuals; varies between n$^+$, p$^+$, poly, poly (silicided), and metal layers. > - **Typical values:** Metal $\sim 0.05 \, \Omega/\square$, poly $\sim 30$–$100 \, \Omega/\square$, diffusion $\sim 50$–$200 \, \Omega/\square$. > - **Resistor sizing:** To realize a target $R$, choose $L = (R \cdot W) / R_s$ for a chosen width $W$. > - **Higher $R_s$ → smaller area for given $R$** (used in compact poly resistors in analog design). > - **Lower $R_s$ → better for power routing** (used in top thick metal layers). > - **Corner effect:** A 90° bend adds ~0.56 squares of resistance (memorize for KTU problems that mention it). > - **Parasitic resistance applications:** IR drop, RC delay estimation, electromigration, antenna rule checks. > - **Master conversions:** $1 \, \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}$. $R_s$ in $\Omega/\square$, $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$, $t$ in cm. > - **Common exam trick:** When asked for "number of squares" — answer is $L/W$ (dimensionless), not $R/W$ or any other combination. > - **Key intuition:** Sheet resistance is the *translator* between layout geometry and electrical resistance in VLSI design.

topics.title: Area Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Area Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In CMOS VLSI Design, **Area Capacitance** is defined as the capacitive component of a MOS device or interconnect whose magnitude is directly **proportional to the physical planar area** over which the conducting terminals (gate, source, drain diffusions, or polysilicon-metal overlap) lie. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$C_{area} = C_{ox} \cdot A = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \cdot (W \cdot L)$$

where $C_{ox}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area (F/m²), $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $\varepsilon_{ox}$ is the permittivity of silicon dioxide, and $t_{ox}$ is the gate-oxide thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Area capacitance is the *intrinsic, layout-defined* capacitance that does **not** depend on the sidewall/perimeter of the device. It forms the foundation of delay, power, and noise calculations in static CMOS logic gates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tank Model

Imagine a large, flat, rectangular water tank placed on the ground. The amount of water it can hold is determined by:
- **How big its base is** (area) — this is your **area capacitance**.
- **How tall its walls are** (depth/thickness) — this is your effective thickness $t_{ox}$.

If you double the base area, you double the water stored. If you halve the wall thickness, you also double the storage. This perfectly mirrors the MOS capacitor: the gate-oxide capacitor's ability to hold charge scales with the **gate area** $W \times L$ and inversely with the **oxide thickness** $t_{ox}$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m}$
- **Permittivity of Silicon:** $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-10}\ F/m}$
- **Vacuum Permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m}$
- **Typical $C_{ox}$ for 0.18 µm process:** $\approx \mathbf{8.5\ fF/\mu m^2}$
- **Typical $C_{ox}$ for 90 nm process:** $\approx \mathbf{14\ fF/\mu m^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> As technology scales down, $t_{ox}$ decreases, causing $C_{ox}$ per unit area to **increase** — this is a major driver of leakage and power-density issues in modern nanometer CMOS.

### 1.4 Where Area Capacitance Originates in a CMOS Inverter

In a static CMOS inverter, the **area capacitance** specifically refers to the gate-to-channel capacitance of the MOS transistors, i.e., the capacitance between the **gate (polysilicon)** and the **channel region** formed underneath the thin gate oxide.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Structural Sources of Capacitance in a MOS Transistor

A single MOSFET has three distinct capacitive regions, all of which are *area-dependent* in their dominant component:

1. **Gate-to-Channel (Oxide) Capacitance — $C_{gc}$**
   Formed across the thin gate oxide. This is the **primary area capacitance** of the transistor.
2. **Gate-to-Source/Drain Overlap Capacitance — $C_{ov}$**
   Caused by lateral diffusion of source/drain under the gate. Treated as a separate (overlap) capacitance.
3. **Source/Drain Junction (Bottom-Wall) Capacitance — $C_{j,area}$**
   Capacitance from the bottom plate of the source/drain diffusion to the body (substrate).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Charge Behavior

- **Step 1 — Oxide Field Formation:** When a voltage $V_{gs}$ is applied to the gate, an electric field forms across the thin SiO₂ layer, attracting minority carriers (electrons in NMOS) to form the inversion channel.
- **Step 2 — Charge Accumulation:** The amount of channel charge $Q_{ch} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L \cdot V_{gs}$ depends linearly on the gate area.
- **Step 3 — Discharge Pathway:** When the input transitions, this stored charge must be supplied/removed through the transistor channel. The time required is the **intrinsic delay** of the gate, given by $t_{delay} \propto C_{area}/I_{drive}$.
- **Step 4 — Scaling Consequence:** Doubling the width $W$ doubles the area capacitance **and** the drive current. Hence intrinsic delay is **independent of $W$** in first order — a key KTU concept.

### 2.3 Why Area Capacitance Matters in VLSI

| Engineering Concern | Role of Area Capacitance |
|---|---|
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd} \propto C_{load} \cdot V_{dd} / I_{drive}$ — directly affects speed |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_{area} V_{dd}^2 f$ — switching energy stored in $C_{area}$ |
| **Short-Circuit Power** | During input transitions, both NMOS and PMOS conduct through $C_{area}$ |
| **Noise Margins** | Charge-sharing and coupling noise scale with $C_{area}$ |
| **Fan-out Loading** | Each additional gate adds its $C_{area}$ to the load seen by the driver |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 2 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \cdot 10^{4}$ (if $\mu m$ & $fF$) | $C_{ox}$ in fF/$\mu m^2$ | fF/$\mu m^2$ |
| 3 | $C_{gate,area} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ | Total gate area capacitance of one transistor | fF |
| 4 | $C_{ov} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L_D$ | Gate-source/drain overlap capacitance (constant per width) | fF |
| 5 | $C_{j,area} = C_j \cdot A_{diff}$ | Bottom-wall junction capacitance of source/drain | fF |
| 6 | $C_{in,NMOS} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L_n + 2 C_{ox} W_n L_D$ | Input capacitance of minimum-size NMOS | fF |
| 7 | $C_{inv,input} = C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L + 2C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L_D$ | Total input cap of CMOS inverter | fF |
| 8 | $C_{out,area} = C_{drain,NMOS} + C_{drain,PMOS}$ | Output (diffusion) area capacitance | fF |
| 9 | $\gamma = C_{gd}/C_{gs}$ (Miller ratio) | Determines effective Miller multiplier | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{eff,Miller} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Effective input cap due to Miller effect | fF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** When computing $C_{ox}$ in **fF/$\mu m^2$**, remember the unit conversion: $1\ F/m^2 = 10^{-8}\ fF/\mu m^2$. Always check that $W$ and $L$ are in **$\mu m$** before plugging in.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Area capacitance calculations are the **backbone of digital cell library characterization**. In standard-cell design (e.g., a 7nm ASIC), every standard cell (NAND, DFF, MUX) has its delay, power, and area characterized by spice simulation tools that internally use these exact area-capacitance formulas. Without accurate $C_{area}$ models, static timing analysis (STA) would be impossible, and chips would fail timing closure at the foundry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Oxide Capacitance per Unit Area

Starting from the fundamental parallel-plate capacitor equation:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A}{d}
$$

For a MOS structure, the dielectric is SiO₂, the area is $W \times L$, and the distance is $t_{ox}$:

$$
C_{ox,total} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L}{t_{ox}}
$$

**Divide both sides by area** to obtain the **per-unit-area oxide capacitance**:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{C_{ox,total}}{W \cdot L} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}
$$

**Textual logic:** The factor $\varepsilon_{ox}$ is fixed by the material (SiO₂), and $t_{ox}$ is the process parameter. Therefore $C_{ox}$ is a **technology constant**, the same for every transistor in a given process node.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Input Capacitance of a CMOS Inverter (Detailed Method)

Consider a CMOS inverter with NMOS width $W_n$, PMOS width $W_p = \beta \cdot W_n$ (typically $\beta \approx 2$ for equal rise/fall times), and channel length $L$ for both.

**Step 1 — Gate area capacitance of NMOS:**

$$
C_{ox,n} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Gate area capacitance of PMOS:**

$$
C_{ox,p} = C_{ox} \cdot W_p \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Sum to get total gate-to-channel capacitance:**

$$
C_{g,total} = C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L
$$

**Step 4 — Add gate overlap capacitance (2 terminals per transistor):**

$$
C_{ov,total} = 2 \cdot C_{ox} \cdot L_D \cdot (W_n + W_p)
$$

where $L_D$ is the lateral diffusion length (typically $\approx 0.05$ to $0.1 \times L$).

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the full input capacitance of the inverter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in} &= C_{g,total} + C_{ov,total} \\
&= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L + 2 C_{ox} L_D (W_n + W_p) \\
&= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)(L + 2L_D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The first term is the **area capacitance** (true parallel-plate portion), and the second is the **peripheral overlap** contribution. The bracket $(L + 2L_D)$ is sometimes called the *effective electrical length* $L_{eff}$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter in a 0.25 µm process has the following parameters:
- $t_{ox} = 6\ nm$
- $W_n = 1\ \mu m$, $W_p = 2\ \mu m$
- $L = 0.25\ \mu m$, $L_D = 0.05\ \mu m$
- $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m$

**Compute (a) $C_{ox}$ in fF/$\mu m^2$ and (b) total input capacitance $C_{in}$.**

**Part (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ox} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \\
&= \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-11}}{6 \times 10^{-9}}\ F/m^2 \\
&= 5.75 \times 10^{-3}\ F/m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Now convert to fF/$\mu m^2$ using $1\ F/m^2 = 10^{-8}\ fF/\mu m^2$:

$$
C_{ox} = 5.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{8}\ fF/\mu m^2 = 575\ fF/\mu m^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a very high value because $t_{ox} = 6\ nm$ is aggressive; typical 0.25 µm processes use $t_{ox} \approx 5$–$7\ nm$ and yield $C_{ox}$ in the range 500–700 fF/$\mu m^2$.

**Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in} &= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)(L + 2L_D) \\
&= 575 \times (1 + 2) \times (0.25 + 2 \times 0.05) \\
&= 575 \times 3 \times 0.35 \\
&= 603.75\ fF
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the **area-dominated input capacitance** of the inverter. In a 4-input NAND gate, you would simply sum the widths of all 4 NMOS in series (plus the parallel PMOS) into the same formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Scaling on Area Capacitance

| Parameter | Scaling Effect on $C_{area}$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Reduce $L$ by factor $S$ | $C_{area}$ reduces by $S$ | Smaller gate = less charge to switch = faster |
| Reduce $t_{ox}$ by factor $S$ | $C_{area}$ per unit area **increases** by $S$ | Higher $C_{ox}$, more drive but more leakage |
| Increase $W$ by factor $S$ | $C_{area}$ increases by $S$ | More drive current but proportional loading |
| Increase $V_{dd}$ | $C_{area}$ unchanged, but $Q = C \cdot V$ scales up | Dynamic power grows quadratically |
| Use HKMG (high-$\kappa$) | $C_{area}$ equivalent, but $t_{phys}$ larger | Reduces gate leakage drastically |

### 3.5 Miller Multiplication of Area Capacitance

When a CMOS inverter drives another CMOS inverter of the same size, the **gate-to-drain capacitance** $C_{gd}$ of the driving transistor experiences a voltage swing on **both** its terminals. Using the Miller theorem:

$$
C_{Miller,eff} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)
$$

For a CMOS inverter, the low-frequency gain magnitude is the **intrinsic gain** $g_m r_o$. At switching transients, the relevant gain is roughly the **self-gain** $A_v \approx -1$ to $-3$, giving:

$$
C_{Miller,eff} \approx 2\ C_{gd} \quad \text{(during transition)}
$$

This effectively **doubles** the area-capacitance contribution of the gate-drain overlap capacitance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Origin of Area Capacitance in a MOSFET (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MOSPhysicalStructure["MOS Transistor Cross-Section"]
        gate["POLY GATE TERMINAL"]
        oxide["Gate Oxide SiO2 - Area Capacitance Region"]
        channel["Inversion Channel - Si Surface"]
        sd1["Source Diffusion N+"]
        sd2["Drain Diffusion N+"]
        body["P-Substrate Body"]
    end

    Cov1["C_ov : Gate-Source Overlap"]
    Cov2["C_ov : Gate-Drain Overlap"]
    Cgc["C_gc = C_ox * W * L : Gate-Channel Area Capacitance"]
    Cjs["C_j,area : Source-Body Junction"]
    Cjd["C_j,area : Drain-Body Junction"]

    gate --- Cov1
    Cov1 --- sd1
    gate --- Cov2
    Cov2 --- sd2
    gate --- oxide
    oxide --- channel
    channel --- Cjs
    Cjs --- body
    channel --- Cjd
    Cjd --- body
    gate --- Cgc
    Cgc --- channel
```

### 4.2 CMOS Inverter Equivalent Capacitance Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin["Input Voltage Vin"] --> CgateNMOS["C_gate_n : C_ox * Wn * L"]
    Vin --> CoverlapN["C_ov : 2 * C_ox * Wn * LD"]
    Vin --> CgatePMOS["C_gate_p : C_ox * Wp * L"]
    Vin --> CoverlapP["C_ov : 2 * C_ox * Wp * LD"]

    CgateNMOS --> SumNode["Total Input Capacitance Cin"]
    CoverlapN --> SumNode
    CgatePMOS --> SumNode
    CoverlapP --> SumNode

    SumNode --> Result["Cin = C_ox * Wn + Wp * L + 2LD"]

    Result --> DelayBlock["Drives Propagation Delay t_pd"]
    Result --> PowerBlock["Drives Dynamic Power alpha C Vdd^2 f"]

    style Result fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style DelayBlock fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    style PowerBlock fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: From Layout Geometry to Circuit Delay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Layout Specification: W, L, t_ox, LD"] --> B["Compute C_ox per unit area"]
    B --> C["Compute Gate Area Capacitance: C_ox * W * L"]
    A --> D["Compute Overlap Capacitance: C_ox * W * LD"]
    C --> E["Sum Total Cin"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Apply Miller Effect: C_in_eff = C_gd * 1 + |Av|"]
    F --> G["Compute Intrinsic Delay: t_pd proportional to Cin / I_drive"]
    G --> H["STA Output: Cell Delay and Power"]

    style A fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style H fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are rendered as **block-level functional architecture** because the physical cross-section of a MOSFET cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid syntax. Each block represents a real structural region whose area contributes to the total capacitance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define area capacitance in a CMOS transistor. State the formula and explain the role of $t_{ox}$.

**Model Answer:**
Area capacitance is the capacitance that exists between the gate and the channel of a MOS transistor, formed across the thin gate oxide. Its magnitude is directly proportional to the gate area $W \times L$ and inversely proportional to the gate oxide thickness $t_{ox}$. The formula is:

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}
$$

and the total gate area capacitance is $C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$. The role of $t_{ox}$ is critical: as $t_{ox}$ decreases (technology scaling), $C_{ox}$ increases, providing higher drive current but also more leakage current and larger parasitic loading on the previous stage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is area capacitance considered "intrinsic" to a CMOS gate?

**Model Answer:**
Area capacitance is called intrinsic because it cannot be eliminated by circuit techniques — it is set by the **physical layout dimensions** ($W$, $L$, $t_{ox}$) of the transistors themselves, which are mandatory for the transistor to operate. Unlike load or interconnect capacitance (which can be minimized by clever routing or buffering), area capacitance is **built into the gate's switching behavior** and must be charged/discharged every time the gate toggles. This makes it a fundamental component in the propagation delay and dynamic power equations.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the input capacitance of a CMOS inverter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter in a 0.18 µm process, given $W_n = 0.54\ \mu m$, $W_p = 1.62\ \mu m$, $L = 0.18\ \mu m$, $L_D = 0.04\ \mu m$, and $t_{ox} = 4\ nm$, calculate (i) $C_{ox}$ in fF/$\mu m^2$ and (ii) the total input capacitance $C_{in}$ in fF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes. The complete derivation proceeds as follows:

The input capacitance of a CMOS inverter has two parallel components: (1) the **gate-to-channel oxide capacitance** (true area capacitance), and (2) the **gate-to-source/drain overlap capacitance**.

For the NMOS transistor:

$$
C_{ox,n} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L \quad \text{(area term)}
$$

$$
C_{ov,n} = 2 \cdot C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L_D \quad \text{(overlap term, 2 terminals)}
$$

Similarly for the PMOS:

$$
C_{ox,p} = C_{ox} \cdot W_p \cdot L
$$

$$
C_{ov,p} = 2 \cdot C_{ox} \cdot W_p \cdot L_D
$$

Since both gates are tied to the same input node, these capacitances appear in parallel. Summing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in} &= C_{ox,n} + C_{ox,p} + C_{ov,n} + C_{ov,p} \\
&= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L + 2 C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)L_D \\
&= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)(L + 2L_D)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **complete input capacitance** of the CMOS inverter.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Oxide capacitance per unit area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ox} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-11}}{4 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= 8.625 \times 10^{-3}\ F/m^2 \\
&= 8.625 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{8}\ fF/\mu m^2 \\
&= 862.5\ fF/\mu m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total input capacitance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in} &= C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)(L + 2L_D) \\
&= 862.5 \times (0.54 + 1.62) \times (0.18 + 2 \times 0.04) \\
&= 862.5 \times 2.16 \times 0.26 \\
&= 484.38\ fF
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{ox} = 862.5\ fF/\mu m^2$ and $C_{in} \approx 484.4\ fF$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the concept of area capacitance and perimeter capacitance in a MOS transistor. How do they differ? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diffusion region of $2\ \mu m \times 2\ \mu m$ is shared between two transistors. The junction capacitance per unit area is $C_j = 1\ fF/\mu m^2$ and the sidewall capacitance per unit perimeter is $C_{jsw} = 0.25\ fF/\mu m$. Calculate the total junction capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept Explanation:**

- **Area Capacitance ($C_{j,area}$):** The capacitance formed between the **bottom plate** of a source/drain diffusion and the underlying substrate (body). It is proportional to the **bottom area** of the diffusion: $C_{j,area} = C_j \cdot A_{diff}$. This capacitance exists whether the transistor is ON or OFF because the reverse-biased p-n junction always has a depletion region.

- **Perimeter Capacitance ($C_{j,sw}$):** The capacitance formed along the **sidewalls** of the diffusion region — the vertical edges where the diffusion meets the shallow-trench isolation (STI). It is proportional to the **perimeter** of the diffusion: $C_{j,sw} = C_{jsw} \cdot P_{diff}$.

- **Key Differences:**

| Feature | Area Capacitance | Perimeter Capacitance |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric basis | Bottom area $A$ | Perimeter $P$ |
| Dominant in | Large-area, wide transistors | Narrow, minimum-area transistors |
| Process origin | Bottom p-n junction | Sidewall p-n junction under STI |
| Scaling trend | Reduces as devices shrink | Becomes more dominant in nanometer nodes |

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

The diffusion is $2\ \mu m \times 2\ \mu m$ — a square.

**Bottom area:**

$$
A = 2 \times 2 = 4\ \mu m^2
$$

**Perimeter of the square:**

$$
P = 4 \times 2 = 8\ \mu m
$$

**Area junction capacitance:**

$$
C_{j,area} = C_j \cdot A = 1 \times 4 = 4\ fF
$$

**Perimeter junction capacitance:**

$$
C_{j,sw} = C_{jsw} \cdot P = 0.25 \times 8 = 2\ fF
$$

**Total junction capacitance:**

$$
C_{j,total} = C_{j,area} + C_{j,sw} = 4 + 2 = \mathbf{6\ fF}
$$

**Observation:** Even in this modest geometry, perimeter capacitance contributes **33%** of the total. In advanced nanometer nodes (28 nm, 14 nm), perimeter can exceed 50%, making it the dominant component.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Area Capacitance Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often forget to convert $C_{ox}$ from F/m² to fF/$\mu m^2$ using the factor $10^{-8}$. Always state the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in Overlap Capacitance (–2 marks):** Gate-to-source and gate-to-drain overlap each contribute — do not multiply by 1, multiply by **2** for both terminals.
> 3. **Confusing Area and Perimeter Capacitance (–2 to –4 marks):** Junction capacitance has both. Read the problem statement carefully — "junction capacitance per unit area" ($C_j$) versus "sidewall capacitance per unit perimeter" ($C_{jsw}$).
> 4. **Ignoring Miller Effect (–1 to –2 marks):** In delay calculations, $C_{gd}$ is effectively doubled at switching transients. Forgetting this is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up NMOS and PMOS widths (–1 mark):** Always use $W_n + W_p$ for the inverter's total input capacitance, never just $W_n$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Area Capacitance Definition:** Capacitance directly proportional to the planar area of the structure. For a MOS gate: $C_{area} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$.
- **Key Formula:** $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox} / t_{ox}$ — technology constant, increases as $t_{ox}$ scales down.
- **CMOS Inverter Input Cap:** $C_{in} = C_{ox}(W_n + W_p)(L + 2L_D)$ — sum of area + overlap.
- **Junction Capacitance Components:** $C_j = C_{j,area} + C_{j,sw} = C_j \cdot A + C_{jsw} \cdot P$.
- **Miller Effect:** Effective $C_{gd}$ is multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$; at switching, this is roughly $2 C_{gd}$.
- **Scaling Implication:** As $L$ shrinks, area capacitance decreases; as $t_{ox}$ shrinks, $C_{ox}$ per area increases. Net effect: gate area capacitance trend is roughly stable across nodes, but leakage increases.
- **Perimeter Dominance:** At nanometer scales (≤ 28 nm), perimeter/sidewall capacitance becomes comparable to or larger than area capacitance.
- **Permittivity Constants to Memorize:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m$, $\varepsilon_{si} = 1.04 \times 10^{-10}\ F/m$.
- **Engineering Use:** Area capacitance drives propagation delay, dynamic power, and fan-out loading — the three most critical metrics in digital cell design.
- **KTU Board Favorite:** Problems combining unit conversion + total $C_{in}$ calculation appear in nearly every End-Semester Examination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delay Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Delay Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Logical Effort Methodology** (a cornerstone technique in CMOS VLSI design popularized by Sutherland, Sproull, and Harris), a **Delay Unit** (often called a **Unit Inverter** or **Reference Inverter**) is a standardized minimum-sized CMOS inverter used as the *fundamental yardstick* for measuring and comparing the intrinsic delay of every logic gate in a digital design family.

The **Unit Inverter** is a static CMOS inverter constructed using **minimum-geometry** transistors in the target technology, with the following canonical sizing convention:

$$
W_p \;=\; 2 \cdot W_{n,\min} \qquad ; \qquad W_n \;=\; 1 \cdot W_{n,\min}
$$

This **2:1 PMOS-to-NMOS width ratio** is engineered so that the inverter exhibits *symmetric rise and fall times* under matched load conditions, given the lower hole mobility in PMOS devices (μ_n ≈ 2μ_p in a generic bulk CMOS process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The Delay Unit is the **quantitative backbone** of Module 3. It is the reference element against which the *logical effort*, *electrical effort*, *parasitic delay*, and *path delay* of all other CMOS gates (NAND, NOR, XOR, AOI, OAI, mux, latch) are normalized. Without a stable delay unit, no meaningful delay comparison is possible in a technology-mapped netlist.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are an architect measuring building heights. Before you can say "this skyscraper is 6 units tall," you must first agree on **what a "unit" is** — historically, that was the height of a standard brick. In VLSI, the **unit inverter is the "standard brick"** of delay analysis:

- It is the **simplest CMOS gate** (only 2 transistors, 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS).
- It is the **smallest realizable logic block** that performs a Boolean inversion.
- It is **independently characterized** in the Process Design Kit (PDK) for rise resistance (R_p), fall resistance (R_n), input gate capacitance (C_in), and output diffusion capacitance (C_out).
- Every other gate's delay is expressed as a **multiple** of this unit inverter's self-loaded delay, denoted **τ (tau)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the unit inverter?**
> 1. **Simplicity** – 2 transistors, easy to model analytically.
> 2. **Symmetry** – 2:1 sizing gives balanced drive strength and balanced rise/fall delays.
> 3. **Self-reference** – When a unit inverter drives an identical unit inverter, the resulting delay **defines** the unit of time τ used in all subsequent gate-delay calculations.

## 1.3 The Three Canonical Capacitances of the Delay Unit

A unit inverter is completely characterized by three capacitance numbers (expressed in units of `C`, where `C` = gate capacitance of a minimum-width NMOS):

| Capacitance | Symbol | Value (in units of C) | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input gate capacitance | C_in | **3** | 1 from NMOS gate + 2 from PMOS gate (PMOS is 2× wider) |
| Output diffusion capacitance | C_out | **3** | Drain diffusions of NMOS + PMOS |
| Intrinsic output node cap (no load) | C_int | **3** | Same as C_out (self-only) |

> [!TIP]
> The magic number **3C** appears everywhere. Memorize it. It is the seed from which all CMOS delay calculations in KTU Module 3 grow.

## 1.4 Definition of the Reference Delay τ

The **reference delay τ** is the propagation delay of a unit inverter when it drives an **identical** unit inverter (the so-called *self-loaded* or *fan-out-of-1* condition):

$$
\tau \;\triangleq\; t_{pd}\bigl(\text{INV driving identical INV}\bigr) \;=\; 3RC
$$

where R is the effective pull-down (or pull-up, due to symmetry) output resistance of the unit inverter, and C is the gate capacitance of a minimum-width NMOS. This value **3RC** becomes the *universal unit* in which all gate delays in the design are expressed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Delay Unit — From Transistor to Delay Number

The construction of a delay value from a unit inverter proceeds through **four logical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Physical Transistor Sizing
- **NMOS**: minimum width W_n, length L (minimum feature size).
- **PMOS**: double width W_p = 2·W_n to compensate for μ_p < μ_n.
- **Result**: t_pHL ≈ t_pLH, the inverter is *symmetric*.

### Layer 2 — Resistance & Capacitance Extraction
- Each transistor has an *effective on-resistance* (R_n, R_p) and a *parasitic capacitance* (gate cap, diffusion cap, overlap cap).
- For the unit inverter: R = R_n (NMOS pull-down), C_gate = C (per min-width NMOS).

### Layer 3 — Input/Output Capacitance Normalization
- Input cap C_in = 3C (because PMOS is 2× wider, contributing 2C, plus NMOS 1C).
- Output cap C_out = 3C (sum of NMOS + PMOS drain diffusions).

### Layer 4 — Delay Computation
- Under self-loading, total output capacitance C_L = C_out (driven inv) + C_out (driver inv) = **6C**.
- Intrinsic RC delay: t_pd ∝ R · C_L = R · 6C = 6RC.
- Define τ = 3RC (i.e., half of the self-loaded RC, or equivalently, the parasitic delay of the unit inverter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The reason τ is defined as 3RC (not 6RC) is that the *parasitic* component of the self-loaded delay — i.e., the part that is *unavoidable* even with zero external load — equals 3RC. This 3RC becomes the **parasitic delay p = 1** (in units of τ) for the unit inverter, leaving exactly **1 τ of effort-driven delay** at fan-out = 1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **complete mathematical toolkit** for Delay Unit problems. Every quantity below is expressed in *normalized units* (capacitances in units of C, delays in units of τ), unless stated otherwise.

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| τ | Reference delay (unit delay) | τ = 3 R C | Self-loaded delay of unit inverter |
| R | Effective pull resistance | Process-dependent | NMOS on-resistance per unit width |
| C | Gate cap per min-width NMOS | C = C_ox · L · W_min | Reference capacitance unit |
| C_in | Input capacitance of unit INV | C_in = 3 C | 1C (NMOS) + 2C (PMOS) |
| C_out | Output diffusion cap of unit INV | C_out = 3 C | Sum of drain diffusions |
| g | Logical effort | g = C_in,gate / 3 C | Ratio of gate input cap to unit-inverter cap delivering same current |
| g_inv | Logical effort of inverter | g_inv = 1 | By definition |
| g_NAND2 | Logical effort of 2-input NAND | g_NAND2 = 4/3 | From parallel-PMOS / series-NMOS topology |
| g_NOR2 | Logical effort of 2-input NOR | g_NOR2 = 5/3 | From series-PMOS / parallel-NMOS topology |
| h | Electrical effort (fan-out) | h = C_out / C_in | Ratio of load cap to input cap |
| p | Parasitic delay | p (in units of τ) | Delay of gate driving zero external load |
| p_inv | Parasitic delay of inverter | p_inv = 1 | 3C output diffusion cap ÷ 3C unit |
| p_NAND2 | Parasitic delay of 2-input NAND | p_NAND2 = 2 | Two series NMOS + two parallel PMOS diffusions |
| p_NOR2 | Parasitic delay of 2-input NOR | p_NOR2 = 2 | Two series PMOS + two parallel NMOS diffusions |
| b | Branching effort | b = (C_on_path + C_off_path) / C_on_path | Accounts for off-path capacitance |
| F | Path effort | F = G · B · H | Total path effort |
| G | Path logical effort | G = Π g_i | Product of stage logical efforts |
| B | Path branching effort | B = Π b_i | Product of stage branching efforts |
| H | Path electrical effort | H = C_out,path / C_in,path | Output to input cap ratio of path |
| N | Number of stages on path | (variable) | Optimized via N_opt = log_4(F) |
| ρ | Best per-stage effort | ρ = F^(1/N) | Equalizes delay across stages |
| D | Path delay (normalized) | D = N · ρ + Σ p_i | Sum of stage delays |
| D_min | Minimum path delay | D_min = N · F^(1/N) + Σ p_i | Achieved with optimal buffer sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute-Value Notation in LaTeX:** When writing a magnitude or absolute value in exam scripts or this formula sheet, **always** use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never the bare pipe `|x|`. A raw `|` will break Markdown table parsing and also looks unprofessional in KTU answer sheets. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why Does This Matter?

| Domain | Application of Delay Unit |
|---|---|
| **Standard Cell Library Design** | Every cell (NAND, NOR, DFF, mux) is characterized with delay = k·τ where k is technology-specific |
| **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** | Tools like Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Tempus use the unit-inverter delay as a calibration anchor |
| **Technology Mapping** | Synthesis tools (Design Compiler, Genus) pick gates that minimize total τ-weighted delay on critical paths |
| **Pipeline Optimization** | Number of buffer stages and their sizes are chosen using the ρ = F^(1/N) rule derived from τ |
| **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)** | PVT corners are characterized by varying τ; corner-to-corner τ variation drives setup/hold budgets |
| **Process Migration** | When porting a design from 28 nm → 7 nm, the ratio of gate delay to τ remains roughly constant, allowing design reuse |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete mathematical derivation** of every delay quantity related to the unit inverter. Each algebraic transition is explicitly written.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Input Capacitance of the Unit Inverter

**Starting point:** Gate capacitance is given by C_g = C_ox · W · L. Since L is minimum for all transistors in the unit inverter, gate capacitance scales linearly with width.

**Step 1:** Compute NMOS gate capacitance (1 unit of width).

$$
C_{g,n} \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot W_{\min} \cdot L_{\min} \;\triangleq\; C
$$

This is our **reference capacitance unit C**.

**Step 2:** Compute PMOS gate capacitance (2 units of width, due to 2:1 sizing for mobility compensation).

$$
C_{g,p} \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot (2W_{\min}) \cdot L_{\min} \;=\; 2C_{ox} \cdot W_{\min} \cdot L_{\min} \;=\; 2C
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the two gate capacitances (they appear in parallel at the input node).

$$
C_{in} \;=\; C_{g,n} + C_{g,p} \;=\; C + 2C \;=\; 3C
$$

**Result:** C_in = 3C. This number is the foundation of every logical-effort calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Capacitance of the Unit Inverter

**Starting point:** The output node of the inverter is the shared drain diffusion region of the NMOS and PMOS. Each drain contributes a *junction diffusion capacitance* C_db (drain-to-body).

**Step 1:** Assume the minimum-width NMOS drain diffusion has a capacitance equal to its gate capacitance C (a common, design-reasonable assumption for short-channel devices where C_db ≈ C_g).

$$
C_{db,n} \;\approx\; C_{ox} \cdot W_{\min} \cdot L_{\min} \;=\; C
$$

**Step 2:** The PMOS drain is 2× wider, so its diffusion capacitance is 2× larger.

$$
C_{db,p} \;\approx\; 2C
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the two diffusion capacitances (they are in parallel at the output node).

$$
C_{out} \;=\; C_{db,n} + C_{db,p} \;=\; C + 2C \;=\; 3C
$$

**Result:** C_out = 3C. Notice the beautiful symmetry: C_in = C_out = 3C for the unit inverter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Self-Loaded Delay (Defining τ)

**Scenario:** A unit inverter drives an identical unit inverter (fan-out = 1).

**Step 1:** Identify all capacitances present at the output node of the *driving* inverter.

The output node of the driving inverter sees:
- Its own drain diffusion capacitance: C_out = 3C.
- The input gate capacitance of the *next* inverter: C_in,next = 3C.

**Step 2:** Sum to get total load capacitance.

$$
C_L \;=\; C_{out,driver} + C_{in,load} \;=\; 3C + 3C \;=\; 6C
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the RC delay approximation t_pd ≈ 0.69·R·C_L (or simply t_pd = R·C_L for first-order KTU analysis).

$$
t_{pd,self} \;\approx\; R \cdot C_L \;=\; R \cdot 6C \;=\; 6RC
$$

**Step 4:** Define τ to be the parasitic component of this delay (the part attributable to the driver's own output diffusion cap).

$$
\tau \;\triangleq\; R \cdot C_{out} \;=\; R \cdot 3C \;=\; 3RC
$$

**Step 5:** Express the self-loaded delay in units of τ.

$$
d_{self} \;=\; \frac{t_{pd,self}}{\tau} \;=\; \frac{6RC}{3RC} \;=\; 2
$$

So the self-loaded unit-inverter delay is **2τ** in the Weste-Harris convention (parasitic 1τ + effort 1τ). Some textbooks (Sutherland) define τ as the self-loaded delay itself (i.e., τ = 6RC, and parasitic delay = 1τ + effort 1τ = 2τ... hmm, no, they define τ = 3RC and the inverter's self-loaded delay = 1τ... let me recheck).

**Reconciliation of Conventions:**

- **Weste-Harris (CMOS VLSI Design textbook):** τ = RC (nMOS), and unit-inverter self-loaded delay = **3τ** (because output diffusion cap is 3C and 3C·R = 3RC = 3τ). Under this convention, **inverter parasitic delay = 3τ = p·τ, with p = 3**. To match Sutherland's p = 1, the unit delay is rescaled: τ_new = 3RC. We use **τ = 3RC** throughout this module to align with KTU's logical-effort treatment.

- **Sutherland (Logical Effort book):** τ = delay of inverter with input cap 3 driving an identical inverter. With output cap 3 + 3 = 6, the delay is R·6C. But Sutherland defines τ such that the unit inverter has a self-loaded delay of 1τ and parasitic delay of 1τ, achieved by a normalization choice τ = 6RC/2 = 3RC. Same result.

**Canonical Definition Used in This Module:**

$$
\boxed{\; \tau \;=\; 3RC \;}
$$

The unit-inverter self-loaded delay is **2τ** (parasitic 1τ + effort 1τ), and its **parasitic delay is p_inv = 1**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — General CMOS Gate Delay Using the Delay Unit

**Step 1:** For a general CMOS gate of logical effort g and parasitic delay p, the delay in units of τ is:

$$
d \;=\; g \cdot h + p
$$

**Step 2:** To find the **actual** delay in seconds, multiply by τ.

$$
t_{pd} \;=\; \tau \cdot (g \cdot h + p) \;=\; 3RC \cdot (g \cdot h + p)
$$

**Step 3:** For a unit inverter with fan-out h = C_load / C_in = C_load / 3C:

$$
d_{inv} \;=\; 1 \cdot h + 1 \;=\; h + 1
$$

**Step 4:** For a 2-input NAND with fan-out h:

$$
d_{NAND2} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \cdot h + 2
$$

**Step 5:** For a 2-input NOR with fan-out h:

$$
d_{NOR2} \;=\; \frac{5}{3} \cdot h + 2
$$

These three formulas are the **workhorse equations** of KTU delay problems.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Optimum Number of Buffer Stages (Logical Effort Path Optimization)

**Given:** A path of overall electrical effort H = C_out / C_in, total logical effort G, total branching B, total parasitic Σp.

**Step 1:** Compute the path effort F.

$$
F \;=\; G \cdot B \cdot H
$$

**Step 2:** The minimum path delay occurs when each stage on the path has the same stage effort ρ. With N stages:

$$
D \;=\; N \cdot \rho + \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i
$$

**Step 3:** Forcing ρ = F^(1/N):

$$
D(N) \;=\; N \cdot F^{1/N} + \sum p_i
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate w.r.t. N to find the optimal N:

$$
\frac{dD}{dN} \;=\; F^{1/N} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\ln F}{N}\right) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for N_opt:

$$
\boxed{\; N_{opt} \;=\; \log_4 F \;}
$$

This is the famous **"4x fan-out per stage"** rule: each buffer should drive a load ~4× its own input capacitance for minimum delay.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Sizing of an Inverter Chain

**Scenario:** A minimum-size inverter (input cap 3C) must drive a load C_L = 1000 C through a chain of N inverters.

**Step 1:** Compute path electrical effort.

$$
H \;=\; \frac{C_L}{C_{in,first}} \;=\; \frac{1000C}{3C} \;\approx\; 333.3
$$

**Step 2:** With G = B = 1 (all inverters, no branching), F = H ≈ 333.

**Step 3:** Find optimal number of stages.

$$
N_{opt} \;=\; \log_4(333) \;\approx\; 4.15 \;\Rightarrow\; N \;=\; 4 \text{ stages}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute per-stage effort.

$$
\rho \;=\; F^{1/N} \;=\; 333^{1/4} \;\approx\; 4.27
$$

**Step 5:** Size each stage by multiplying its input cap by ρ:

- Stage 1: C_in,1 = 3C → C_in,2 = 3C · ρ ≈ 12.8 C
- Stage 2: C_in,2 ≈ 12.8 C → C_in,3 ≈ 54.7 C
- Stage 3: C_in,3 ≈ 54.7 C → C_in,4 ≈ 233.5 C
- Stage 4: C_in,4 ≈ 233.5 C → C_L ≈ 998 C ≈ 1000 C ✓

**Step 6:** Compute minimum delay.

$$
D_{min} \;=\; N \cdot \rho + N \cdot p_{inv} \;=\; 4 \cdot 4.27 + 4 \cdot 1 \;\approx\; 21.1 \,\tau
$$

This single delay number **characterizes the entire buffer chain** in terms of the delay unit.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Unit Inverter & Its Delay Extraction Flow

This block diagram captures the complete signal-flow view of how a single unit inverter is decomposed into its constituent capacitances and how the reference delay τ is extracted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT VOLTAGE VIN] --> B[NMOS GATE W MIN]
    A --> C[PMOS GATE W 2X MIN]
    B --> D[GATE CAPACITANCE CN = 1C]
    C --> E[GATE CAPACITANCE CP = 2C]
    D --> F[INPUT NODE TOTAL CAPACITANCE]
    E --> F
    F --> G[CIN = 1C PLUS 2C = 3C]
    H[VDD] --> C
    I[GND] --> B
    J[OUTPUT NODE VOUT] --> K[NMOS DRAIN DIFFUSION]
    J --> L[PMOS DRAIN DIFFUSION]
    K --> M[CDB N = 1C]
    L --> N[CDB P = 2C]
    M --> O[OUTPUT CAPACITANCE COUT]
    N --> O
    O --> P[COUT = 1C PLUS 2C = 3C]
    Q[EXTERNAL LOAD CL] --> J
    P --> R[TOTAL OUTPUT CAPACITANCE]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[CTOT = COUT PLUS CIN LOAD = 3C PLUS CL]
    S --> T[RC DELAY TPD = R CTOT]
    T --> U[DEFINE TAU = R 3C]
    T --> V[NORMALIZED DELAY D = TPD DIVIDED BY TAU]
    V --> W[D = 1 PLUS H WHERE H = CL DIVIDED BY 3C]
```

## 4.2 Logical Effort Methodology — Decision and Computation Flow

This diagram shows the **end-to-end logical effort calculation pipeline** that starts from a gate-level netlist and ends with a path-delay number expressed in τ.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START WITH CMOS GATE NETLIST] --> B[IDENTIFY CRITICAL PATH]
    B --> C[EXTRACT STAGES ON PATH]
    C --> D[STAGE 1: COMPUTE G1, P1, H1]
    D --> E[STAGE 2: COMPUTE G2, P2, H2]
    E --> F[STAGE N: COMPUTE GN, PN, HN]
    F --> G[COMPUTE PATH LOGICAL EFFORT G = G1 G2 ... GN]
    G --> H[COMPUTE PATH BRANCHING B = B1 B2 ... BN]
    H --> I[COMPUTE PATH ELECTRICAL EFFORT H = COUT PATH / CIN PATH]
    I --> J[PATH EFFORT F = G B H]
    J --> K{OPTIMAL N KNOWN?}
    K -- YES --> L[STAGE EFFORT RHO = F RAISED TO 1/N]
    K -- NO --> M[COMPUTE N OPT = LOG4 F]
    M --> L
    L --> N[MINIMUM PATH DELAY D = N RHO PLUS SUM OF PI]
    N --> O[ACTUAL DELAY TPD = TAU TIMES D]
    O --> P[COMPARE TPD TO TIMING BUDGET]
    P --> Q{MEETS BUDGET?}
    Q -- YES --> R[ACCEPT PATH]
    Q -- NO --> S[RESIZE STAGES / ADD BUFFERS]
    S --> B
```

## 4.3 Delay Unit as the Universal Yardstick — Hierarchy Diagram

This diagram illustrates **how the unit inverter sits at the foundation** of all other delay metrics in a hierarchical fashion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UNIT INVERTER DELAY UNIT TAU] --> B[PRIMITIVE GATES: INV NAND NOR]
    A --> C[COMPLEX GATES: AOI OAI XOR]
    A --> D[SEQUENTIAL ELEMENTS: DFF LATCH]
    A --> E[WIRE DELAY: RC PI ELMORE]
    A --> F[BUFFER CHAINS: INVERTER CHAIN REPEATER]
    B --> G[STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[SLACK COMPUTATION: T_REQUIRED MINUS T_ACTUAL]
    H --> I[TIMING CLOSURE: SIGN-OFF]
```
