## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**What is a super buffer? How does it differ from a standard tapered buffer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **super buffer** is a multi-stage CMOS driver circuit in which a chain of inverters with progressively increasing sizes is used to drive large capacitive loads, with the **final stage featuring asymmetric transistor sizing** (PMOS enlarged by a factor $k = 2$ to $4$ relative to a symmetric inverter).

**Key differences from a standard tapered buffer:**

| **Aspect** | **Standard Buffer** | **Super Buffer** |
|---|---|---|
| PMOS/NMOS ratio (last stage) | $W_p / W_n \approx \mu_n / \mu_p$ (symmetric) | $W_p / W_n = k \cdot \mu_n / \mu_p$ where $k = 2$–$4$ |
| Rise vs fall time | $t_{PLH} \approx t_{PHL}$ (matched by intrinsic ratio) | **More strongly matched** — PMOS dominant pull-up |
| Use case | General on-chip signal buffering | Clock drivers, I/O pads, bus drivers, high-fanout nets |
| Speed of weak edge | Standard | Improved by factor $k$ |

The super buffer thus achieves **superior rise/fall time matching**, making it ideal for clock distribution where duty-cycle distortion is unacceptable. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**State and justify the optimal tapering factor $f_{opt}$ for a tapered CMOS buffer chain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **optimal tapering factor** for a tapered CMOS buffer chain is:

$$\boxed{f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718}$$

**Justification:** For an $N$-stage buffer driving a load $C_L$ from an input capacitance $C_{in}$, the total propagation delay is given by:

$$t_{total} = t_{p0} \cdot \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(f)} \cdot (\gamma + f)$$

Differentiating with respect to $f$ and setting the derivative to zero (in the limit $\gamma \to 0$) yields the transcendental condition $f \cdot \ln(f) = f + \gamma$, which is satisfied by $f = e$ when $\gamma = 0$. For realistic $\gamma \approx 1$, the optimum shifts to $f \approx 3.6$, but $f = e$ remains the **canonical textbook answer** because the delay is very flat near the optimum, and the design is robust to small variations.

**Significance:** This result is **process-independent** — it holds for any CMOS technology. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the optimum tapering factor of a multi-stage CMOS buffer chain. Show that the minimum propagation delay is given by $t_{p,min} = t_{p0} \cdot \ln(C_L/C_{in}) \cdot (\gamma + e)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A super buffer is to be designed to drive a load capacitance of $C_L = 8$ pF. The minimum-size inverter in the library has an input capacitance of $C_{in} = 20$ fF and intrinsic delay $t_{p0} = 60$ ps. Assume $\gamma = 1$ and use $f_{opt} = e$.

  (i) Calculate the optimum number of stages. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) Calculate the per-stage tapering factor if $N$ must be an integer. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Calculate the total propagation delay. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of $f_{opt}$ and minimum delay (7 marks):**

Consider an $N$-stage buffer where each stage is $f$ times larger than the previous. The capacitance seen by each stage (input cap of next stage) is $f$ times its own input cap.

**Step 1:** The per-stage delay (assuming each stage drives the next stage's input capacitance $f \cdot C_{g,i}$):

$$t_{p,stage} = t_{p0} \left( \gamma + \frac{f \cdot C_{g,i}}{C_{g,i}} \right) = t_{p0}(\gamma + f)$$

**Step 2:** Geometric progression constraint: $C_L = f^N \cdot C_{in}$, hence:

$$N = \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(f)} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3:** Total delay:

$$t_{total} = N \cdot t_{p0}(\gamma + f) = \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(f)} \cdot t_{p0} \cdot (\gamma + f) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4:** Minimize w.r.t. $f$. Differentiate numerator-style:

$$f \cdot \ln(f) = f + \gamma \quad \text{...(3)}$$

For $\gamma = 0$, this gives $f \cdot \ln(f) = f$, hence $\ln(f) = 1$, hence $\boxed{f_{opt} = e}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute $f = e$ into (2). Note $\ln(e) = 1$:

$$t_{p,min} = t_{p0} \cdot \ln(C_L/C_{in}) \cdot (\gamma + e) \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**(i) Optimum number of stages:**

$$N_{opt} = \ln\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{8 \times 10^{-12}}{20 \times 10^{-15}}\right) = \ln(400)$$

$$N_{opt} = \ln(400) = 5.99 \approx 6 \text{ stages} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Tapering factor for integer $N = 6$:**

$$f = \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)^{1/N} = 400^{1/6} = e^{\ln(400)/6} = e^{5.99/6} = e^{0.998} \approx 2.71 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Total propagation delay:**

$$t_{total} = N \cdot t_{p0}(\gamma + f) = 6 \times 60 \text{ ps} \times (1 + 2.71)$$

$$t_{total} = 6 \times 60 \times 3.71 = 6 \times 222.6 = 1335.6 \text{ ps} \approx 1.34 \text{ ns} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, S3 B.Tech]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS super buffer**. Why is the final stage PMOS deliberately made larger? Discuss the role of tapering factor $f$ in delay minimization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare a **tapered buffer** with a **super buffer** in terms of architecture, transistor sizing, delay performance, and typical applications. Design a 4-stage super buffer to drive $C_L = 500$ fF from $C_{in} = 10$ fF, with $t_{p0} = 50$ ps and $\gamma = 1$. Compute the total delay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) CMOS Super Buffer — Operation, Asymmetry, Tapering (7 marks):**

A **CMOS super buffer** is a cascaded chain of CMOS inverters where each successive stage is geometrically scaled up. The input is applied to the smallest inverter, and the output of the last (largest) stage drives the load.

**Circuit Topology (Marks: 2):** A 4-stage super buffer has 4 inverter stages — INV1 (smallest) → INV2 → INV3 → INV4 (largest, with asymmetric PMOS). The output of INV4 connects to the load $C_L$ via the pad.

**Why final stage PMOS is larger (Marks: 2):** In CMOS, hole mobility $\mu_p \ll \mu_n$ (typically $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$). A symmetric inverter gives $t_{PLH} > t_{PHL}$ (slow rise). In the **final super stage**, PMOS width is multiplied by $k = 2$–$4$ to make $t_{PLH} \approx t_{PHL}$, which is critical for clock and I/O signals.

**Role of tapering factor (Marks: 3):** The tapering factor $f$ defines the size ratio between consecutive stages. For an $N$-stage chain, $C_L = f^N C_{in}$. The total delay $t_{total} = t_{p0} \cdot \ln(C_L/C_{in}) \cdot (\gamma + f)/\ln(f)$ is minimized at $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$, independent of process. Increasing $f$ above $e$ oversizes stages (wasted effort); decreasing $f$ below $e$ undersizes stages (slow charging).

**(b) Comparison and Design (7 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Tapered Buffer** | **Super Buffer** |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Chain of progressively sized inverters | Same, with **asymmetric final stage** |
| PMOS/NMOS in last stage | Standard ratio (2–3) | $k \times$ standard ratio, $k = 2$–$4$ |
| $t_{PLH}$ vs $t_{PHL}$ | Slightly mismatched | **Closely matched** |
| Delay | Minimum at $f = e$ | Minimum at $f = e$, plus improved rising edge |
| Applications | On-chip local buffering | **Clock trees, I/O pads, bus drivers** |

**Design calculation (Marks: 4):**

Given: $C_L = 500$ fF, $C_{in} = 10$ fF, $N = 4$, $t_{p0} = 50$ ps, $\gamma = 1$.

$$f = \left(\frac{500}{10}\right)^{1/4} = 50^{0.25} = 2.659$$

$$t_{total} = N \cdot t_{p0}(\gamma + f) = 4 \times 50 \times (1 + 2.659) = 200 \times 3.659 = 731.8 \text{ ps} \approx 0.73 \text{ ns}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round up $N$:** Always take the **ceiling** of $N_{opt}$. If $\ln(2000) = 7.6$, use $N = 8$, not $7$ — otherwise the final stage undersizes and the chain **cannot** drive the load. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing $f$ and $N$:** Some students solve for $f$ when asked for $N$, and vice versa. Read carefully: "**number of stages**" = $N$, "**tapering factor**" = $f$.
>
> 3. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$:** KTU strictly uses natural log. $\log_{10}(C_L/C_{in}) \cdot 2.303 = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$. A wrong base means wrong numbers even if the method is correct. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $\gamma$ term:** $t_{total} = N \cdot t_{p0}(\gamma + f)$, not $N \cdot t_{p0} \cdot f$. The intrinsic delay is always present.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the circuit diagram for super buffer questions:** A neat, labeled circuit with $V_{DD}$, $GND$, and the asymmetry of the final stage highlighted earns easy marks (typically 2–3 marks for a properly drawn schematic).
>
> 6. **Missing the physical interpretation:** KTU examiners reward answers that explain *why* — e.g., "PMOS is enlarged because $\mu_p < \mu_n$" — not just plug numbers into formulas. **Always state the physical reasoning.**
>
> 7. **Not mentioning $f_{opt} = e$ is process-independent:** This is a high-yield concept, often asked as a 3-mark "discuss" question. State the result, derive it briefly, and highlight its universality.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Super Buffer Definition:** A multi-stage CMOS driver with progressively sized inverters and an **asymmetric final stage** to equalize rise/fall times.
- **Tapering Factor $f$:** Ratio of input capacitances of consecutive stages; $C_{g,i+1} = f \cdot C_{g,i}$.
- **Number of Stages:** $N = \lceil \ln(C_L/C_{in}) / \ln(f) \rceil$ — always round **up**.
- **Optimum Tapering Factor:** $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$ (process-independent, classical result).
- **Minimum Delay:** $t_{p,min} = t_{p0} \cdot \ln(C_L/C_{in}) \cdot (\gamma + e)$.
- **Optimum Stages:** $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$.
- **Super Buffer Asymmetry:** Final PMOS width = $k \cdot$ standard ratio, with $k = 2$ to $4$.
- **Single Inverter Delay Model:** $t_p = t_{p0}(\gamma + C_L/C_{in})$ — memorize this; it appears in every problem.
- **Intrinsic Delay $t_{p0}$:** Process-dependent constant (30–100 ps typical).
- **Parasitic Factor $\gamma$:** Ratio of intrinsic parasitic cap to input cap, $\gamma \approx 1$.
- **Applications:** Clock distribution trees, I/O pads, bus drivers, off-chip communication.
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** A super buffer chain is typically **70× to 100× faster** than a single oversized inverter for large loads.
- **Validation Insight:** The delay function is **flat near the optimum** — small deviations from $f = e$ cause minimal performance loss, making the design robust to process variations.
- **Clock Buffer Special Case:** Use $k = 3$ to $4$ for $50\%$ duty cycle preservation in clock trees.
- **KTU-Mandated Units:** Always express delay in **ps or ns**, capacitance in **fF, pF, or nF**, and the ratio $C_L/C_{in}$ as dimensionless.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stick Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stick Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Stick Diagram** is an abstract, symbolic, two-dimensional representation of a CMOS integrated circuit layout that uses simple lines ("sticks") of specific colors, patterns, and thicknesses to denote the different physical layers (polysilicon, diffusion, metal, contacts) used in the actual fabrication of the chip. It acts as an **intermediate abstraction** between the schematic (circuit diagram) and the actual mask-level geometric layout.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, stick diagrams belong to the family of **"symbolic layout techniques"** and are used to:
- Plan the topological placement of transistors.
- Determine the Euler path through the transistor network.
- Estimate approximate area, routing congestion, and connectivity *before* committing to a full mask layout in a CAD tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A stick diagram is **not a fabrication-ready mask**. It contains no exact dimensions (no $\lambda$ scaling), no precise widths/lengths, and ignores process-specific design rules such as $\text{DRACULA}$ spacing. It is purely a **topological map**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are an architect designing a multi-story house. Before you draft the precise engineering blueprints with measurements in millimeters, you first sketch a **rough floor plan using colored pencils** — red lines for walls, blue for water pipes, green for electrical conduits. You don't worry about exact wall thickness yet; you just want to ensure the rooms connect logically and there are no crossed pipes.

A **Stick Diagram is exactly this rough colored-pencil sketch for a CMOS chip**:
- **Sticks** = colored lines representing wires (polysilicon, metal, diffusion).
- **Intersections & crosses** = transistors and contact cuts.
- **Junctions** = where layers meet electrically.

This way, the designer can visualize the topology, check the Euler path, and optimize the routing *conceptually*, saving weeks of iterative full-mask layout work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "Stick" diagram?**
> Because every conducting layer is drawn as a simple straight **line segment (a "stick")** rather than a full geometric rectangle with width and length. The width information is intentionally suppressed at this stage.

### 1.3 Standard Layer Color/Pattern Conventions

For uniformity across textbooks (Mead & Conway, Weste & Harris), the KTU syllabus adopts the following widely accepted color/pattern coding:

| Layer | Color | Pattern in Black & White | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Polysilicon (Poly)** | Green | Solid line with parallel hash marks | Gate of MOSFET, short interconnects |
| **n-type Diffusion** | Yellow | Solid outline (no fill) | Source/Drain of NMOS, active area |
| **p-type Diffusion** | Orange / Brown | Solid outline with dot fill | Source/Drain of PMOS, active area |
| **Metal 1 (Aluminum)** | Blue | Solid bold line | First-level horizontal interconnects |
| **Metal 2** | Violet / Purple | Solid bold line with double hash | Second-level vertical interconnects |
| **Contact Cut** | Black | Solid black square ($\blacksquare$) | Connects Poly/Diffusion to Metal 1 |
| **Via** | Black | Square with X inside | Connects Metal 1 to Metal 2 |
| **Implant (n+/p+)** | Dashed outline | Dotted border | Doping regions for source/drain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crossing Rule:** When a **Poly stick crosses a Diffusion stick**, a **MOS transistor is automatically formed** at the intersection. No explicit transistor symbol is needed — the cross itself *is* the transistor.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Design Rule Unit ($\lambda$)**: All stick diagrams are drawn in a generic, technology-independent grid where the minimum feature size is $\lambda$. Typical submicron processes use $\lambda = 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$ down to $\lambda = 0.05\,\mu\text{m}$ (modern FinFET nodes).
- **Stick Width Convention**: All sticks have the **same nominal width** in the diagram. Real width information is added later during the mask conversion.
- **Connectivity Rule**: Two sticks of the **same layer** that touch at their ends are **electrically connected**. Two sticks of **different layers** must use a **contact cut** to connect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Rules of Stick Diagrams

Every valid stick diagram in the KTU 2024 VLSI curriculum must obey three golden rules:

**Rule 1 — The Transistor is an Implicit Cross:**
A transistor exists *wherever* a polysilicon stick crosses a diffusion stick. There is no separate "transistor symbol."

$$ \text{Poly stick} \perp \text{Diffusion stick} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{MOSFET formed} $$

**Rule 2 — Same-Layer Connectivity is Free:**
If two sticks of the *same* layer (e.g., two poly segments, or two metal wires) touch end-to-end, they are connected without any contact.

**Rule 3 — Cross-Layer Connectivity Requires a Contact:**
Sticks of *different* layers (e.g., poly to metal, or diffusion to metal) must be joined using a **contact cut** ($\blacksquare$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for the designer:** A "gap" between two same-layer sticks in a stick diagram means the designer has *deliberately* left them unconnected. This is the primary tool used to encode series vs. parallel transistor arrangements.

### 2.2 The Euler Path Concept (The Heart of Stick Diagram Design)

The **Euler Path** is the single most important concept for KTU university exams on this topic. It is the key to creating a *compact*, *area-efficient* stick diagram for a CMOS gate.

**Definition:** An Euler path through a pull-up (PMOS) or pull-down (NMOS) network is a path that traverses **each transistor (edge) exactly once**.

**Why is the Euler path important?**
If a valid Euler path exists in *both* the PMOS network *and* the NMOS network, and the two paths share the **same ordering of transistor labels**, then the entire gate can be drawn as a single uninterrupted poly line (one stick) that forms all transistors in sequence. This:
1. Minimizes the poly-to-diffusion crossings.
2. Allows shared source/drain diffusions to be merged.
3. Reduces the cell area and parasitic capacitance.

**KTU High-Yield Euler Path Theorem:**
A CMOS logic gate has a *single-poly* stick-diagram realization **if and only if** both the NMOS and PMOS networks are Eulerian (i.e., contain an Euler path), and the resulting ordered sequences of transistor labels are identical.

| Network Type | Eulerian Condition | KTU Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NMOS series chain** (e.g., NAND) | Has an Euler path (each node has degree 2) | Trivially Eulerian |
| **NMOS parallel network** (e.g., NOR) | Has an Euler path *only if* ≤ 2 parallel branches | 3-input NOR is **not** Eulerian |
| **Cascade / Mixed (e.g., AOI)** | Depends on graph structure | Must check graph degrees |

### 2.3 Design Rules Translated to Stick Notation

While stick diagrams ignore exact dimensions, the *ordering* and *spacing relationships* still follow these simplified rules:

| Rule | Stick Diagram Notation | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Poly-to-Diffusion spacing** | Poly stick must not be parallel & adjacent to diffusion | Avoids unwanted field transistor |
| **Metal-to-Metal spacing** | Two metal sticks on the same layer must be separated by a gap | Prevents short circuits |
| **Contact enclosure** | Contact $\blacksquare$ must be fully enclosed by the upper & lower layer | Ensures reliable contact |
| **Butting contact** | Two abutting diffusions of opposite type share a single contact | Saves area (used in I/O cells) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The stick diagram itself is a *qualitative* tool, but the related quantitative concepts tested in KTU exams are summarized below:

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Euler Path Condition (Graph) | Every vertex in the path-graph has degree = 2 | Except start & end nodes (degree = 1) |
| Minimum Poly length for $n$ transistors | $L_{\text{poly}} \geq 2n\lambda$ | Sum of channel lengths + source/drain |
| Cell height (standard cell) | $H_{\text{cell}} = n \cdot \text{pitch} + 2\lambda$ | Where pitch is the transistor repeat |
| Channel resistance (NMOS) | $R_n = \dfrac{\rho_n}{W_n / L_n}$ | Ohms |
| Channel resistance (PMOS) | $R_p = \dfrac{\rho_p}{W_p / L_p}$ | Ohms, $\rho_p \approx 2.5 \rho_n$ |
| Rise time / Fall time ratio | $\dfrac{t_{PLH}}{t_{PHL}} \approx \dfrac{R_p}{R_n}$ | Used to size PMOS vs NMOS |
| Aspect ratio (cell) | $AR = \dfrac{W_{\text{cell}}}{H_{\text{cell}}}$ | Ideally $\approx 1$ for standard cells |
| Eulerian graph check (vertices $V$, edges $E$) | $E = V - 1$ (path) or $E = V$ (circuit) | For simple Eulerian graph |
| Number of contacts per diffusion | $N_c \geq 1$ for every source/drain shared with metal | To avoid high-resistivity silicide-only path |
| Poly sheet resistance | $R_{\square} \approx 5$ to $50\;\Omega/\square$ | Polysilicon (un-silicided) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Euler-path driven stick-diagram methodology is the foundational principle behind **industry-standard cell libraries** (e.g., Synopsys, Cadence standard cells) where the area-per-gate is minimized by exploiting shared diffusions and single-poly routing.

### 2.5 Connection to the Physical Design Flow

The stick diagram slots into the VLSI design flow as follows:

$$\text{Schematic (Netlist)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stick Diagram (Topology)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mask Layout (Geometry)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DRC/LVS Verification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fabrication}$$

The transition from stick diagram to mask layout is done by expanding each stick into a **rectangle of width $\geq 3\lambda$** (for poly) and assigning the appropriate **design-rule spacing** between adjacent sticks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram

**Problem:** Draw the stick diagram of a standard CMOS inverter with input $A$ and output $Y = \overline{A}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the transistor network:**
- PMOS (pull-up) — single transistor with gate $A$, source = $V_{DD}$, drain = $Y$.
- NMOS (pull-down) — single transistor with gate $A$, source = $GND$, drain = $Y$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Euler paths:**
- PMOS path: $V_{DD} \to P_1 \to Y$ (single edge, trivially Eulerian).
- NMOS path: $Y \to N_1 \to GND$ (single edge, trivially Eulerian).
- Ordered label sequences: **PMOS = [A]**, **NMOS = [A]**. Both share the label *A* in the same position, so a **single-poly** realization is possible.

**Step 3 — Draw the layers:**

The layout uses **one horizontal poly line** (input $A$) that vertically crosses **one n-diffusion strip** (bottom) and **one p-diffusion strip** (top). The two diffusions extend horizontally and meet the $V_{DD}$ and $GND$ rails through metal-1 wires.

**Step 4 — Apply contact cuts:**
- One $\blacksquare$ at the top to connect the p-diffusion output node to the metal-1 output line.
- One $\blacksquare$ at the bottom to connect the n-diffusion output node to the metal-1 output line.
- Two $\blacksquare$ contacts for the $V_{DD}$ and $GND$ rails to the respective source diffusions.

**Step 5 — Final topology check:**
- Poly line crosses p-diff → PMOS formed. ✔
- Poly line crosses n-diff → NMOS formed. ✔
- Output $Y$ is common drain of PMOS and NMOS, contacted by metal-1. ✔
- $V_{DD}$ reaches PMOS source via metal-1 contact. ✔
- $GND$ reaches NMOS source via metal-1 contact. ✔

The result is a **2-transistor, 1-poly, 2-metal-rail** stick diagram that is provably optimal in area.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: 2-Input NAND Gate Using Euler Path

**Problem:** Draw the stick diagram of $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ (2-input NAND).

**Step 1 — Build the NMOS pull-down graph:**
The two NMOS transistors $N_A$ and $N_B$ are in **series** between $Y$ and $GND$. The graph is:
$$\text{Y} \;\overset{N_A}{\longleftrightarrow}\; \text{mid} \;\overset{N_B}{\longleftrightarrow}\; \text{GND}$$
Every node has degree = 2, so an Euler path exists: **Y → mid → GND**, with edge-order **[A, B]**.

**Step 2 — Build the PMOS pull-up graph:**
The two PMOS transistors $P_A$ and $P_B$ are in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and $Y$. The graph has a node $V_{DD}$ with two parallel edges to $Y$. This is a **multi-graph**. An Euler path exists since both $V_{DD}$ and $Y$ have even degree (2 each in the multi-graph sense): **$V_{DD} \to Y$ via $P_A$ or $P_B$**, with edge-order **[A, B]**.

**Step 3 — Verify label sequence match:**
Both networks yield the same ordered label sequence **[A, B]**. Therefore, **one single horizontal poly line** can sequentially form $N_A$, $P_A$ (or $P_B$), $N_B$ (or $P_B$) as it zig-zags.

**Step 4 — Draw the n-diffusion strip (bottom layer, yellow):**
A horizontal n-diffusion strip runs from $Y$ (left) to $GND$ (right). The poly line crosses it **twice**, creating $N_A$ (left) and $N_B$ (right). The middle region of the n-diff is the **shared source/drain** of $N_A$ and $N_B$ (no contact needed — same diffusion type, abutting).

**Step 5 — Draw the p-diffusion strips (top layer, orange):**
Two **separate horizontal p-diffusion strips** are placed at the top, one for $P_A$ and one for $P_B$, both reaching the $V_{DD}$ rail on the left and the output node $Y$ on the right. The poly line must cross both p-diffusions, so the poly "steps up" (using a contact to metal-1) to reach the second p-diffusion level.

**Step 6 — Metal-1 routing for $V_{DD}$ and output:**
- A horizontal metal-1 wire at the top connects both p-diffusion sources to $V_{DD}$.
- A vertical metal-1 wire at the right (or output node) connects the shared n-diff drain of $N_A$ (which is $Y$) to the p-diffusion drains of $P_A$ and $P_B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the poly must step up:** Because $N_A$ and $N_B$ are on a single horizontal n-diff strip, but $P_A$ and $P_B$ must be on two horizontal p-diff strips at a different vertical level, the poly cannot be a single straight line. It uses a **metal-1 "step"** to jump between the two p-diff strips. This is the classic **Euler-path-with-jog** layout.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: 2-Input NOR Gate Using Euler Path

**Problem:** Draw the stick diagram of $Y = \overline{A + B}$ (2-input NOR).

**Step 1 — NMOS pull-down:** Two NMOS in **parallel** between $Y$ and $GND$. The graph has a node $Y$ and a node $GND$, each with degree = 2 in the multi-graph sense. Euler path: **Y → GND**, edge order **[A, B]**.

**Step 2 — PMOS pull-up:** Two PMOS in **series** between $V_{DD}$ and $Y$. The graph is a single path: **$V_{DD} \to \text{mid} \to Y$**, edge order **[A, B]**.

**Step 3 — Match labels:** Both yield **[A, B]**. ✔ Single-poly realization possible.

**Step 4 — Topology:**
- **n-diff:** Two **horizontal strips** at the bottom (one for $N_A$, one for $N_B$), each going from a metal-1 output contact to $GND$. The poly crosses each once.
- **p-diff:** A **single horizontal strip** at the top from $V_{DD}$ to $Y$. The poly crosses it twice (forming $P_A$ and $P_B$).
- The poly line must "step down" via metal-1 to cross both n-diff strips, mirroring the NAND case.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Common Gates

The following KTU-style analytical table summarizes the stick-diagram properties of fundamental CMOS gates:

| Gate | NMOS Network | PMOS Network | Euler Path NMOS | Euler Path PMOS | Sequence Match | # Poly Steps |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Inverter** | Single $N$ | Single $P$ | [A] | [A] | ✔ | 0 |
| **2-Input NAND** | 2 series | 2 parallel | [A, B] | [A, B] | ✔ | 1 (up) |
| **2-Input NOR** | 2 parallel | 2 series | [A, B] | [A, B] | ✔ | 1 (down) |
| **3-Input NAND** | 3 series | 3 parallel | [A, B, C] | [A, B, C] | ✔ | 2 (up) |
| **3-Input NOR** | 3 parallel | 3 series | **Not Eulerian** | [A, B, C] | ✖ | 2 poly lines |
| **AOI21** | Mixed | Mixed | Conditional | Conditional | Requires check | Variable |
| **XOR (6T)** | 4T bridge | 4T bridge | Conditional | Conditional | Requires check | Variable |

### 3.5 Derivation of the Euler Path Necessity (Graph-Theoretic)

**Claim:** A CMOS gate with $n$ input signals can be drawn with a **single poly line** only if both networks admit an Euler path with the same input ordering.

**Proof sketch:**
1. Each transistor is formed at a (Poly $\perp$ Diffusion) intersection.
2. If a single horizontal poly line forms all $n$ transistors, then moving from left to right along the poly, the transistors are encountered in some order. That order defines the Euler path label sequence in both networks (since poly crosses both n-diff and p-diff).
3. Conversely, if both networks have an Euler path with the same label sequence, then we can lay the poly as one continuous horizontal line, place the diffusions to be crossed in that order, and obtain a single-poly layout.

$$\therefore \quad \text{Single-poly layout} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Common Euler sequence in NMOS \& PMOS}$$

This theorem is a KTU **favourite 14-mark question** and is asked in either "prove/discuss" form or as a "design the stick diagram given a network" form.
