## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a stick diagram. Mention the three golden rules used while drawing a stick diagram for a CMOS circuit.*

**Model Answer:**
A stick diagram is a symbolic, topology-only representation of a CMOS layout where different layers (polysilicon, diffusion, metal, contacts) are drawn as colored sticks/lines, and exact dimensions are deliberately omitted at this stage.

**Three golden rules:**
1. **Transistor as a cross:** A transistor is formed implicitly wherever a poly stick crosses a diffusion stick.
2. **Free same-layer connectivity:** Two sticks of the same layer touching end-to-end are electrically connected without any contact.
3. **Contact for cross-layer:** A contact cut is mandatory to connect two different layers (e.g., poly to metal, n-diff to metal).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three rules)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is an Euler path? Why is it important in stick-diagram design?*

**Model Answer:**
An **Euler path** in a transistor network graph is a continuous path that traverses **each edge (transistor) exactly once**, without lifting the pen.

**Importance in stick-diagram design:**
- It guarantees that a **single poly line** can form all the transistors of the network, minimizing area.
- It allows **shared source/drain diffusions** between series transistors, reducing parasitic capacitance.
- It is the **optimality criterion** — only Eulerian networks yield minimum-area stick diagrams.
- For a CMOS gate, the NMOS and PMOS networks must both admit an Euler path with the **same input ordering** to achieve a single-poly layout.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the importance/justification)*

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> **Important Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries ~14 marks and offers an internal choice between two full questions. Both alternatives are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the stick diagram of a **2-input CMOS NAND gate**. Clearly mark all layers (polysilicon, n-diffusion, p-diffusion, metal-1) and all contact cuts. Justify your topology using the **Euler path theorem**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Boolean function and transistor structure:**

$$Y = \overline{A \cdot B} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{NMOS: } N_A, N_B \text{ in series}; \quad \text{PMOS: } P_A, P_B \text{ in parallel}$$

**Step 2 — Euler path check:**

- NMOS series graph: $Y \to \text{mid} \to GND$ traversed by $[A, B]$. ✔ Eulerian.
- PMOS parallel graph: multi-edge between $V_{DD}$ and $Y$, traversed by $[A, B]$. ✔ Eulerian.
- Both yield the same label sequence **[A, B]** ⇒ single-poly realization is possible.

**Step 3 — Layer-by-layer stick construction:**

1. **One horizontal n-diffusion strip** (yellow, bottom): runs from output node $Y$ (left) to $GND$ rail (right). The poly crosses it twice → forms $N_A$ (left) and $N_B$ (right). The middle region of the n-diff is the shared internal diffusion (no contact needed).
2. **Two horizontal p-diffusion strips** (orange, top): each goes from $V_{DD}$ (left) to $Y$ (right). The poly crosses both, forming $P_A$ (left) and $P_B$ (right).
3. **Polysilicon line** (green, with hash marks): enters from the left at the n-diff level, crosses $N_A$, steps up to the p-diff level via a metal-1 contact, crosses $P_A$, continues on p-diff level, steps up/down as needed, crosses $P_B$, then steps down to the n-diff level, crosses $N_B$, exits right.
4. **Metal-1 wire** (blue, bold): horizontal $V_{DD}$ rail connecting both p-diff sources; vertical output wire at the shared drain connecting all three (n-diff shared drain, both p-diff drains) to output $Y$.

**Step 4 — Contacts ($\blacksquare$):**
- One contact at $V_{DD}$ to p-diff.
- One contact at the shared n-diff internal node (if needed for substrate bias, otherwise optional).
- Two contacts at the shared n-diff drain (output $Y$) to metal-1.
- Two contacts at each p-diff drain (output $Y$) to metal-1.
- Two poly-to-metal-1 contacts for the poly step (one "up" via metal-1).

**Step 5 — Validation:**
- Poly crosses n-diff twice → exactly 2 NMOS ✔
- Poly crosses p-diff twice → exactly 2 PMOS ✔
- Series NMOS verified by the single n-diff strip ✔
- Parallel PMOS verified by the two parallel p-diff strips ✔
- Euler path realized with one jog in the poly line ✔

*(7 marks: 2 for Euler justification + 4 for stick diagram + 1 for contact enumeration)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **lambda-based design rules** that govern the transition from a stick diagram to a mask layout. List at least four such rules with their minimum lambda values for a generic $\lambda$-based process. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Lambda ($\lambda$) is the fundamental unit of length in a scalable CMOS design rule. A **$\lambda$-based rule** expresses all minimum dimensions and spacings as integer multiples of $\lambda$, making the same layout portable across process generations by simply rescaling $\lambda$.

**Generic $\lambda$-based design rules:**

| Rule | Minimum Value | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Poly width | $2\lambda$ | Minimum gate width to avoid breakage |
| 2. Diffusion width | $2\lambda$ | Source/drain active area |
| 3. Poly-to-Diffusion spacing (gate) | $2\lambda$ | Channel length $L = 2\lambda$ |
| 4. Poly-to-Diffusion spacing (field) | $1\lambda$ | Outside the transistor (field oxide) |
| 5. Metal width | $3\lambda$ | First-level metal wire |
| 6. Metal-to-Metal spacing | $3\lambda$ | Same-layer metal separation |
| 7. Contact size | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ | Square contact cut |
| 8. Contact enclosure (poly/metal over contact) | $1\lambda$ | Margin on all sides |
| 9. n+ to p+ spacing (same substrate) | $4\lambda$ | To prevent latch-up |
| 10. Substrate/well contact spacing | $2\lambda$ | Tap rule for body connection |

**Why the stick diagram must respect these in spirit:**
Even though exact $\lambda$ values are not drawn, the stick diagram must respect the **ordering and non-overlapping intent** of these rules. For example, a poly stick and a diffusion stick that are *not* meant to form a transistor must be drawn with an **explicit gap** (representing the field-oxide spacing) to avoid creating an unintended MOSFET.

**Significance for KTU exams:** When asked "convert the stick diagram to a mask layout", the student is expected to expand each stick into a rectangle of the correct width and insert the correct $\lambda$-spaced gaps between parallel sticks.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept + 4 for rule table + 1 for stick-diagram-to-mask transition logic)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a stick diagram, explain the design of a **2-input CMOS NOR gate**. Show the Euler path derivation and label all layers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Boolean function and transistor structure:**

$$Y = \overline{A + B} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{NMOS: } N_A, N_B \text{ in parallel}; \quad \text{PMOS: } P_A, P_B \text{ in series}$$

**Step 2 — Euler path check:**

- PMOS series graph: $V_{DD} \to \text{mid} \to Y$ traversed by $[A, B]$. ✔ Eulerian.
- NMOS parallel graph: multi-edge between $Y$ and $GND$, traversed by $[A, B]$. ✔ Eulerian.
- Common sequence **[A, B]** ⇒ single-poly realization is possible. *(This is the *dual* of the NAND case.)*

**Step 3 — Stick diagram construction:**

1. **One horizontal p-diffusion strip** (orange, top): runs from $V_{DD}$ (left) to $Y$ (right). The poly crosses it twice → forms $P_A$ (left) and $P_B$ (right). The middle region of the p-diff is the shared internal diffusion (no contact needed).
2. **Two horizontal n-diffusion strips** (yellow, bottom): each goes from $Y$ (left) to $GND$ (right). The poly crosses both, forming $N_A$ (left) and $N_B$ (right).
3. **Polysilicon line** (green, with hash marks): enters from the left at the p-diff level, crosses $P_A$, steps down to the n-diff level via a metal-1 contact, crosses $N_A$, continues at n-diff level, crosses $N_B$, then steps up to the p-diff level, crosses $P_B$, exits right.
4. **Metal-1 wire** (blue, bold): horizontal $GND$ rail connecting both n-diff sources; vertical output wire at the shared p-diff drain connecting all three (p-diff shared drain, both n-diff drains) to output $Y$.

**Step 4 — Euler path visualization (ordered):**

$$\text{Poly travels: } \underbrace{P_A}_{1} \to \underbrace{N_A}_{2} \to \underbrace{N_B}_{3} \to \underbrace{P_B}_{4} \quad \text{(sequence: A, A, B, B with PMOS/NMOS interleaved)}$$

This means the poly visits input $A$ twice (once for PMOS, once for NMOS) and input $B$ twice (once for PMOS, once for NMOS), which is **physically realizable** in CMOS because $P_A$ and $N_A$ are on different vertical levels.

*(7 marks: 2 for Euler derivation + 4 for stick diagram + 1 for layer labelling)*

---

**(b)** Compare the stick diagrams of **2-input NAND and 2-input NOR gates** in terms of (i) number of diffusion strips required, (ii) number of poly-to-metal steps (jogs), and (iii) shared diffusion usage. Justify which gate is more area-efficient and why. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Parameter | 2-Input NAND | 2-Input NOR |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **(i) n-diffusion strips** | 1 (single horizontal) | 2 (two parallel horizontals) |
| **(i) p-diffusion strips** | 2 (two parallel horizontals) | 1 (single horizontal) |
| **(ii) Poly-to-metal jogs (steps)** | 1 upward jog | 1 downward jog |
| **(iii) Shared internal diffusion** | Yes, on n-diff strip (between $N_A$ and $N_B$) | Yes, on p-diff strip (between $P_A$ and $P_B$) |
| **Total diffusion area** | Lower (1 + 2 = 3 strips) | Higher (2 + 1 = 3 strips) |
| **Parasitic capacitance on internal node** | Lower (n-diff capacitance) | Higher (p-diff capacitance, larger to body) |

**Area efficiency analysis:**
- The **NAND gate is more area-efficient** in a standard-cell design because:
  1. The n-diff strip is at a single horizontal level — **no internal routing** is needed for the series connection.
  2. The p-diff strips are at the top, which is naturally a wider region for parallel routing.
  3. The internal shared n-diff node has **low capacitance to ground**, enabling faster switching.
- The **NOR gate** has the dual advantage, but because the **PMOS devices are typically wider** (to match drive strength), the series connection of two wide PMOS transistors leads to **higher stacking resistance** and **larger area** for equivalent drive current.

**Rule of thumb for KTU:** "**NAND is preferred over NOR** in CMOS static logic for the same fan-in, because series-connected NMOS (smaller, faster) is preferred over series-connected PMOS (larger, slower)."

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table + 3 for analysis + 2 for the NAND-preferred conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Euler path justification (–2 marks):** Many students directly draw the stick diagram without first proving that a single-poly layout is possible. Always state the NMOS Euler sequence, the PMOS Euler sequence, and confirm they match.
> 2. **Missing contact cuts (–1 mark):** Every diffusion-to-metal and poly-to-metal transition must show a $\blacksquare$ contact. The internal shared diffusion (series transistors) does **not** need a contact — this is a common point of confusion.
> 3. **Wrong layer assignment (–1 to 2 marks):** Using the wrong color/pattern for a layer (e.g., drawing PMOS diffusion as n-type) will cost you the layer-marker marks. Stick strictly to the **green-poly, yellow-n-diff, orange-p-diff, blue-metal-1** convention.
> 4. **Drawing the poly as a single straight line through stacked gates (–2 marks):** When NMOS and PMOS are on different vertical levels, the poly **must jog** via a metal-1 step. A straight poly line that "magically" crosses both levels is a topological error.
> 5. **Confusing stick diagram with mask layout (–1 mark):** Do **not** write $\lambda$ dimensions or rectangle widths inside the stick diagram. The stick diagram is dimensionless; the mask layout has dimensions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **stick diagram** is a dimensionless, topological representation of a CMOS circuit using colored sticks for each process layer.
- [ ] **Standard color codes:** Green = Poly, Yellow = n-Diff, Orange/Brown = p-Diff, Blue = Metal 1, Violet = Metal 2, Black square = Contact.
- [ ] **Rule 1:** Poly crossing Diffusion ⇒ a MOSFET is formed (no symbol needed).
- [ ] **Rule 2:** Same-layer sticks touching at ends are connected for free.
- [ ] **Rule 3:** Different-layer sticks need a contact cut ($\blacksquare$) to connect.
- [ ] **Euler Path** = a graph path that traverses every edge (transistor) exactly once.
- [ ] **KTU Theorem:** A CMOS gate has a single-poly stick diagram **iff** the NMOS and PMOS networks are both Eulerian and yield the **same input-label ordering**.
- [ ] **CMOS Inverter** = trivially Eulerian, zero poly steps.
- [ ] **2-input NAND** = 1 n-diff strip + 2 p-diff strips + 1 upward poly jog.
- [ ] **2-input NOR** = 1 p-diff strip + 2 n-diff strips + 1 downward poly jog.
- [ ] **3-input NOR is NOT Eulerian** (NMOS has 3 parallel transistors → node degree 3) ⇒ requires **two separate poly lines**.
- [ ] **Internal shared diffusion** (between series transistors) needs **no contact** — saves area.
- [ ] **NAND is preferred over NOR** in standard-cell design due to lower series resistance of stacked NMOS vs. stacked PMOS.
- [ ] The **$\lambda$-based design rule** system allows the same stick topology to be retargeted to different fabrication processes by simply rescaling $\lambda$.
- [ ] **Stick diagram $\neq$ Mask layout:** Stick diagram is the conceptual blueprint; the mask layout is the geometric, DRC-clean, fabrication-ready output.
- [ ] **Metal-1 is the standard routing layer** in stick diagrams unless multi-level routing is required, in which case Metal 2 (violet) is used with via cuts.
- [ ] The stick diagram slots into the design flow as: **Schematic → Euler Path → Stick Diagram → Mask Layout → DRC/LVS → GDSII → Fabrication**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Design Rules for MOS Circuits - Lamda-Based Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layout Design Rules for MOS Circuits - Lamda-Based Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Layout Design Rules** are a set of geometric constraints and dimensional specifications that govern the physical construction of an integrated circuit (IC) mask layout. They define the **minimum widths, minimum spacings, minimum enclosures, and minimum extensions** permitted for each mask layer (diffusion, polysilicon, metal, contact cut, etc.) so that the fabricated die yields reliably, free from open circuits, short circuits, or process-induced defects.

**Lambda-Based (λ-Based) Design Rules** are a *parameterized*, *scalable* form of layout rules introduced by **Carver Mead and Lynn Conway (1979)**. In this methodology, a single fundamental unit called **lambda (λ)** is used to express every geometric dimension. All lengths, widths, and spacings are integer or half-integer multiples of λ. When the process technology shrinks, only the value of λ changes; the layout itself remains unchanged.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **λ (Lambda)** is the *resolution unit* of the process. It corresponds to **half the minimum feature size** of the technology. For a 1.2 µm CMOS process, the minimum drawn gate length is 2λ = 1.2 µm, so **λ = 0.6 µm**. For a 0.5 µm process, λ = 0.25 µm. For a 180 nm process, λ = 90 nm.

Mathematically, the relationship between the technology's minimum feature dimension $F_{min}$ and lambda is:

$$F_{min} = 2\lambda$$

$$\lambda = \frac{F_{min}}{2}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Lego Brick System:**  
> Think of lambda as the **size of one Lego stud pitch** on a baseplate. Every Lego block, tower, or sculpture you build must align to that stud grid. You cannot cut a brick in half or place it at a weird angle, because it would not "click" properly. The baseplate pitch (λ) is the fundamental grid; all structures are integer multiples of it.  
>  
> Similarly, in VLSI layout, the photolithographic grid and process tolerances force every mask shape to snap to integer multiples of λ. If you obey this grid, your design will "click" perfectly with the fab's process. If you don't, the chip will malfunction due to misalignment, short circuits, or open circuits.

**Another Analogy — Road Traffic Lanes:**  
Imagine a road where each vehicle must stay at least *d* meters from the vehicle ahead. The rule is uniform regardless of whether the car is a small hatchback (2λ wide) or a heavy truck (4λ wide). The rule scales. Lambda-based design rules work the same way — one parameter governs all.

---

## 1.3 Why Lambda-Based Rules? — Motivation

1. **Process Portability:** The same layout can be re-targeted to a new fab by simply scaling λ.
2. **Simple Education Tool:** Students and designers can learn layout using λ and ignore complex process-specific micron values.
3. **DRC Simplification:** Design Rule Checking becomes a single uniform check (e.g., "all separations are integer multiples of λ").
4. **First-Pass Success:** A well-defined rule set ensures high manufacturing yield.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Lambda rules are pedagogically essential and exam-relevant for KTU**, but in modern sub-micron processes they have been **superseded by micron (absolute) design rules** because the scaling is **not perfectly uniform** across layers (a poly gate may scale faster than a metal interconnect, breaking the lambda assumption). However, λ-rules are still taught and examined in KTU 2024 scheme syllabus under PEEBT526 Module 4.

---

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Mapping

| **Syllabus Sub-Topic** | **Coverage in this Note** |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Lamda-based design rules | All 5 sections |
| Layout of CMOS gates | Stick diagram & layout architecture (SECTION 3 & 4) |
| Design rule checking | Discussed in SECTION 2 & 3 |
| Stick diagrams | Covered in SECTION 3 & 4 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lambda Design Rule Philosophy

Lambda-based rules assume that the process is *scalable* and *isotropic* — i.e., every feature in every layer scales by the same factor. The four primary constraint categories are:

1. **Minimum Width (W):** The smallest line/feature a layer can print.
2. **Minimum Spacing (S):** The smallest edge-to-edge gap between two features of the same or different layer.
3. **Minimum Extension (E):** The smallest overlap of one layer over another.
4. **Minimum Enclosure (Enc):** The smallest overlap of a contact or via by the surrounding layer.

Each of these is expressed as an integer multiple of λ.

---

## 2.2 The Master Lambda Rule Set (Mead–Conway)

> [!NOTE]  
> The following is the **canonical λ-rule set** taught in KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize the values; they are high-yield for ESE and module tests.

### A. Diffusion Layer Rules (n+ and p+)

| **Rule** | **Constraint** | **Lambda Value** |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Minimum width of n-diffusion | $W_{n}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum width of p-diffusion | $W_{p}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing, n-diff to n-diff | $S_{nn}$ | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing, p-diff to p-diff | $S_{pp}$ | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing, n-diff to p-diff (same well) | $S_{np}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| p+ to n+ spacing across a well boundary | $S_{pn\text{-}well}$ | $4\lambda$ (or 6λ with substrate/well contact) |
| Active area extension beyond poly gate | $E_{active}$ | $2\lambda$ |

### B. Polysilicon Layer Rules

| **Rule** | **Constraint** | **Lambda Value** |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Minimum poly width (gate) | $W_{poly}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum poly-to-poly spacing | $S_{poly\text{-}poly}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Poly gate extension over thin-oxide (active) | $E_{gate}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Poly extension over field oxide (i.e., poly over isolation) | $E_{field}$ | $1\lambda$ |

### C. Metal Layer Rules (Metal 1)

| **Rule** | **Constraint** | **Lambda Value** |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Minimum metal width | $W_{metal}$ | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum metal-to-metal spacing | $S_{metal}$ | $3\lambda$ |
| Metal overhang over contact (enclosure) | $E_{metal\text{-}contact}$ | $1\lambda$ |

### D. Contact (Cut) Layer Rules

| **Rule** | **Constraint** | **Lambda Value** |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Contact cut size | $W_{contact} \times L_{contact}$ | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |
| Minimum contact-to-contact spacing (same layer) | $S_{contact}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| Contact-to-poly spacing on same active region | $S_{c\text{-}poly}$ | $1\lambda$ |
| Contact-to-diffusion spacing on same poly region | $S_{c\text{-}diff}$ | $1\lambda$ |
| Poly/diff enclosure of contact | $E_{contact}$ | $1\lambda$ |
| Minimum number of contacts per source/drain | $N_{contacts}$ | $\geq 2$ |

### E. Implant (p+ and n+ Select) Rules

| **Rule** | **Constraint** | **Lambda Value** |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| p+ implant overhang over active p-region | $E_{p\text{-}impl}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| n+ implant overhang over active n-region | $E_{n\text{-}impl}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| p+ to n+ spacing (across well) | $S_{p\text{-}n\text{-}impl}$ | $4\lambda$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Relations

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> These formulas are tested directly in KTU university exams.

$$
\boxed{\lambda = \frac{F_{min}}{2} = \frac{L_{min}}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Layout Area} = (W_{trans} + S) \times (L_{poly} + 2E)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Drawn Gate Length } L_g = 2\lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Effective Channel Length } L_{eff} = L_g - 2L_D}
$$

where $L_D$ is the lateral diffusion (typically $0.1\lambda$ to $0.2\lambda$ in λ-rules).

**Pitch of a Poly-Si Gate Array:**

$$
P_{poly} = W_{poly} + S_{poly\text{-}poly} = 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 4\lambda
$$

**Pitch of a Metal-1 Routing Track:**

$$
P_{metal} = W_{metal} + S_{metal} = 3\lambda + 3\lambda = 6\lambda
$$

**Number of Routing Tracks per Micron (for nλ process):**

$$
N_{tracks} = \frac{1\,\mu m}{(W + S)\,\lambda}
$$

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Numbers

| **Rule** | **Physical Reason** |
|----------|---------------------|
| Min width 2λ | Lithographic resolution; below this, line breaks occur. |
| Min n+ to p+ spacing 4λ (across well) | Prevents punch-through and latch-up between adjacent wells. |
| Poly extension 2λ | Ensures complete channel coverage despite mask misalignment. |
| Contact 2λ × 2λ | Ensures reliable step coverage of metal over the cut. |
| Metal enclosure 1λ | Guarantees metal-to-contact overlap under worst-case misalignment. |
| Two contacts per source/drain | Redundancy — a single missing contact won't kill the device. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lambda rules are the **conceptual backbone of academic chip design** and are still used in:

- **MOSIS (fabrication broker) educational runs**
- **Microwind / Electric / Magic VLSI layout tools** (academic EDA)
- **FPGA/ASIC cell library characterization** at introductory level
- **Migration of legacy designs** to newer process nodes

In industry, micron rules (absolute, layer-specific) from foundries like **TSMC, Intel, Samsung, GlobalFoundries** have replaced λ-rules for sub-100 nm processes, but the *mindset* of design rules — minimum width, spacing, enclosure — remains identical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting Technology Node to Lambda

**Given:** A CMOS process has a minimum drawn feature size $F_{min} = 1.0\,\mu m$. Find λ.

**Step 1 — Recall the master relation:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{F_{min}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.0\,\mu m}{2} = 0.5\,\mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to nanometres for clarity:**

$$
\lambda = 0.5\,\mu m = 500\,nm
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the minimum drawn gate length:**

$$
L_{g,\,drawn} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.5\,\mu m = 1.0\,\mu m
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the effective channel length with $L_D = 0.1\lambda$:**

$$
L_{D} = 0.1 \times 0.5\,\mu m = 0.05\,\mu m
$$

$$
L_{eff} = L_{g} - 2L_{D} = 1.0\,\mu m - 2(0.05\,\mu m) = 0.9\,\mu m
$$

**Final Answer:** $\lambda = 0.5\,\mu m$, $L_{g,\,drawn} = 1.0\,\mu m$, $L_{eff} = 0.9\,\mu m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stick Diagram → Layout Dimension Conversion

**Problem:** A CMOS NAND-2 gate stick diagram has the following poly gate widths along the X-direction: $L_{poly,1} = 4\lambda$, $L_{poly,2} = 4\lambda$, separated by spacing $S = 3\lambda$. Compute the total X-extent of the active area.

**Step 1 — Identify the contributing features:**

The X-extent = sum of poly gate lengths + spacing between them.

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
X_{total} = L_{poly,1} + S + L_{poly,2}
$$

$$
X_{total} = 4\lambda + 3\lambda + 4\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
X_{total} = 11\lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Add the minimum n+/p+ overlap on each side (2λ on each end):**

$$
X_{total,\,final} = 11\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 15\lambda
$$

**Final Answer:** The active-area X-extent = $15\lambda$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Area of a CMOS Inverter (Euler / Stick Layout)

**Problem:** Design a minimum-area CMOS inverter in λ-rules. Compute its (W × L) and pitch.

**Step 1 — Place the poly gate:**

The poly line is drawn vertically with width $W_{poly} = 2\lambda$. The nMOS and pMOS are placed side-by-side in the active region.

**Step 2 — Active region X-extent:**

$$
X_{active} = L_{poly} + 2 \times E_{active} = 2\lambda + 2(2\lambda) = 6\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Active region Y-extent (one transistor):**

$$
Y_{active} = W_{transistor} + 2 \times (\text{contact} + \text{enclosure})
$$

$$
Y_{active} = 2\lambda + 2(2\lambda + 2 \times 1\lambda) = 2\lambda + 2(4\lambda) = 10\lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Width of pMOS (typically $2 \times$ nMOS for symmetric drive):**

$$
W_{pMOS} = 2 W_{nMOS}
$$

For minimum area, $W_{nMOS} = 2\lambda$, so $W_{pMOS} = 4\lambda$.

**Step 5 — Total Y-extent of the inverter cell:**

$$
Y_{cell} = Y_{nMOS} + S_{np} + Y_{pMOS}
$$

$$
Y_{cell} = 10\lambda + (2\lambda\text{ to } 4\lambda) + 14\lambda
$$

Using minimum $S_{np} = 2\lambda$ (with no separate well tap):

$$
Y_{cell} = 10\lambda + 2\lambda + 14\lambda = 26\lambda
$$

**Step 6 — Cell pitch in standard-cell row:**

$$
P_{cell} = X_{active} = 6\lambda
$$

**Final Answer:** Inverter cell area $= 6\lambda \times 26\lambda = 156\lambda^2$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Routing Track Density (Metal-1)

**Problem:** How many parallel Metal-1 routing tracks fit in a 100 µm wide block, given λ = 0.5 µm?

**Step 1 — Compute the metal pitch:**

$$
P_{metal} = W_{metal} + S_{metal} = 3\lambda + 3\lambda = 6\lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Express pitch in µm:**

$$
P_{metal} = 6 \times 0.5\,\mu m = 3.0\,\mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Divide block width by pitch:**

$$
N_{tracks} = \frac{100\,\mu m}{3.0\,\mu m} = 33.33
$$

**Step 4 — Round down (only whole tracks fit):**

$$
N_{tracks} = 33
$$

**Final Answer:** **33 Metal-1 tracks** fit in a 100 µm width.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: CMOS NAND-2 Cell Area

**Problem:** Compute the area of a 2-input NAND gate in λ-rules, with $W_n = 2\lambda$ (each nMOS) and $W_p = 4\lambda$ (each pMOS in parallel).

**Step 1 — Active region X-extent (single poly, two diffusions):**

$$
X_{active} = 2\lambda + 2(2\lambda) = 6\lambda
$$

**Step 2 — nMOS network Y-extent (two nMOS in series):**

$$
Y_{nMOS} = 2 \times Y_{nMOS,\,one} + S_{nn}
$$

$$
Y_{nMOS} = 2(2\lambda + 2(2\lambda + 2\lambda)) + 3\lambda = 2(2\lambda + 8\lambda) + 3\lambda = 20\lambda + 3\lambda = 23\lambda
$$

Wait — rechecking using the formula $Y_{active,one} = W_{nMOS} + 2(\text{contact region})$:

For minimum $W_n = 2\lambda$, contact $2\lambda$, enclosure $1\lambda$ on each side, so contact region = $2\lambda + 2 \times 1\lambda = 4\lambda$ per side.

$$
Y_{nMOS,one} = 2\lambda + 2(4\lambda) = 10\lambda
$$

For two nMOS in series:

$$
Y_{nMOS,total} = 2 \times 10\lambda + S_{nn} = 20\lambda + 3\lambda = 23\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — pMOS network Y-extent (two pMOS in parallel):**

$W_p = 4\lambda$, so $Y_{pMOS,one} = 4\lambda + 2(4\lambda) = 12\lambda$.

For two pMOS in parallel (sharing source/drain, but with separate drain contacts — typically drawn as single wider source):

$$
Y_{pMOS,total} \approx Y_{pMOS,one} = 12\lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Total Y-extent of the NAND cell:**

$$
Y_{cell} = Y_{nMOS,total} + S_{np} + Y_{pMOS,total}
$$

$$
Y_{cell} = 23\lambda + 2\lambda + 12\lambda = 37\lambda
$$

**Step 5 — Cell area:**

$$
A_{NAND2} = 6\lambda \times 37\lambda = 222\lambda^2
$$

**Final Answer:** NAND-2 cell area $\approx 222\lambda^2$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Design Rule Violations

| **Violation** | **What Happens Physically** | **Yield Impact** |
|---------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| Poly width < 2λ | Poly line breaks (open circuit) | Catastrophic |
| Metal spacing < 3λ | Short circuit between metal lines | Catastrophic |
| Contact not enclosed by 1λ | Open contact, no metal-via connectivity | Catastrophic |
| p+ to n+ spacing < 4λ across well | Latch-up, parasitic thyristor fires | Functional failure |
| Poly over field < 1λ | Field transistor turns on, leakage | Parametric failure |
| Single contact only | Random contact failure kills device | Yield loss |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lambda Rule Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The lambda-based design system can be visualized as a hierarchical pipeline from technology parameters to final layout:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Technology Node F_min] --> B[Derive Lambda: Lambda equals F_min over 2]
    B --> C[Rule Set Master: 2 lambda widths, 3 lambda spacings, 1 lambda enclosures]
    C --> D[Apply to Layers: Diffusion, Polysilicon, Metal, Contact, Implant]
    D --> E[Construct Stick Diagram: Symbolic representation]
    E --> F[Convert to Mask Layout: Geometric polygons]
    F --> G[DRC Verification: Check against lambda rules]
    G --> H{Pass?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Fab-Ready GDS II Stream]
    H -- No --> J[Fix Violations]
    J --> F
```

---

## 4.2 Stick Diagram Notation Reference

The stick diagram is the symbolic, colour-coded ancestor of the mask layout. Lambda rules are most easily taught using stick diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Color: GREEN = n+ Diffusion]
    B[Color: YELLOW = p+ Diffusion]
    C[Color: RED = Polysilicon]
    D[Color: BLUE = Metal 1]
    E[Color: BLACK SQUARE = Contact Cut]
    F[Color: BLACK X = Buried Contact]
    G[Color: CYAN = n Well Boundary]
```

| **Symbol** | **Layer** | **Physical Meaning** |
|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Green horizontal line | n+ diffusion | Active NMOS regions |
| Yellow horizontal line | p+ diffusion | Active PMOS regions |
| Red vertical/horizontal line | Polysilicon | Gates, poly interconnect |
| Blue line | Metal 1 | First-level metal interconnect |
| Black filled square | Contact cut | Via between poly/diff and Metal 1 |
| Black X | Buried contact | Direct poly-diffusion contact (no cut) |
| Dashed cyan | n-well | pMOS substrate region |

---

## 4.3 CMOS Inverter — Stick Diagram and Layout Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INV["CMOS Inverter Cell"]
        VDD[VDD Rail - Metal 1 - Top]
        OUT[Output Node Y - Metal 1 - Middle]
        GND[VSS Rail - Metal 1 - Bottom]
    end
    
    subgraph PMOS["pMOS Transistor Region"]
        PWL[p+ Source]
        PGL[p+ Drain]
        PG[Poly Gate = Input A]
    end
    
    subgraph NMOS["nMOS Transistor Region"]
        NWL[n+ Source]
        NGL[n+ Drain]
        NG[Poly Gate = Input A]
    end
    
    VDD --- PWL
    PGL --- OUT
    OUT --- NGL
    NWL --- GND
    
    PG -.Controls.-> PGL
    NG -.Controls.-> NGL
    
    style VDD fill:#0000FF,color:#FFFFFF
    style GND fill:#0000FF,color:#FFFFFF
    style OUT fill:#0000FF,color:#FFFFFF
    style PWL fill:#FFFF00,color:#000000
    style PGL fill:#FFFF00,color:#000000
    style NWL fill:#00FF00,color:#000000
    style NGL fill:#00FF00,color:#000000
    style PG fill:#FF0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style NG fill:#FF0000,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.4 Lambda Rule Spacing Visualisation — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R1["Rule Category 1: Minimum Width"]
        W1["n-diff: 2λ wide"]
        W2["poly: 2λ wide"]
        W3["metal: 3λ wide"]
    end
    
    subgraph R2["Rule Category 2: Minimum Spacing"]
        S1["n to n diff: 3λ"]
        S2["poly to poly: 2λ"]
        S3["metal to metal: 3λ"]
        S4["n+ to p+ across well: 4λ"]
    end
    
    subgraph R3["Rule Category 3: Extensions and Enclosures"]
        E1["poly over active: 2λ"]
        E2["poly over field: 1λ"]
        E3["metal over contact: 1λ"]
        E4["active around contact: 1λ"]
    end
    
    subgraph R4["Rule Category 4: Contact Geometry"]
        C1["contact size: 2λ by 2λ"]
        C2["contact to contact: 2λ"]
        C3["contacts per source: 2 minimum"]
    end
    
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
```

---

## 4.5 CMOS NAND-2 Stick Diagram — Architectural View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input A - Vertical Poly Line]:::poly
    B[Input B - Vertical Poly Line]:::poly
    
    subgraph NW["nMOS Network - Series"]
        N1[nMOS A: Source to Internal Node]:::n
        N2[nMOS B: Internal Node to GND]:::n
    end
    
    subgraph PW["pMOS Network - Parallel"]
        P1[pMOS A: VDD to Output]:::p
        P2[pMOS B: VDD to Output]:::p
    end
    
    O[Output Y - Metal 1]:::metal
    
    N1 --- N2
    P1 --- O
    P2 --- O
    N1 --- O
    
    A -.Gate.-> N1
    A -.Gate.-> P1
    B -.Gate.-> N2
    B -.Gate.-> P2
    
    classDef poly fill:#FF0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    classDef n fill:#00FF00,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    classDef p fill:#FFFF00,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    classDef metal fill:#0000FF,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
```

---

## 4.6 Layout Rule Cross-Section (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph XS["Cross-Section Through a MOS Transistor - Y direction"]
        MET["Metal 1 - Blue Layer - Thickness 1 lambda"]
        CT["Contact Cut - 2 lambda by 2 lambda"]
        D1["n+ Diffusion - Active Region"]
        POLY["Polysilicon Gate - 2 lambda wide"]
        CH["Channel Region - Under Poly"]
        FOX["Field Oxide - Thick Oxide Isolation"]
        SUB["p-type Substrate"]
    end
    
    MET --- CT
    CT --- D1
    D1 --- CH
    POLY --- CH
    FOX --- D1
    SUB --- FOX
```

---

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagrammatic Note:**  
> The above diagrams are *block-level architectural schematics* representing the lambda rule system. Actual IC mask layouts are drawn using polygons on multiple GDS-II layers in tools like **Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys Custom Compiler, Microwind, or Electric VLSI**. KTU expects stick-diagram literacy and rule-table knowledge, not pixel-perfect GDS-II drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **Define lambda-based design rules. Why is a single parameter λ used to express all geometric constraints in the Mead-Conway design system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lambda-based design rules are a set of geometric constraints used to design the physical mask layout of MOS integrated circuits, where every dimension (width, spacing, enclosure) is expressed as a multiple of a single resolution parameter called **lambda (λ)**.

λ is defined as **half the minimum feature size** of the process. A single parameter is used because:

1. **Scalability:** The same layout can be retargeted to different fab processes by simply rescaling λ. (1 mark)
2. **Simplicity:** It allows designers to learn and apply one consistent rule set without worrying about process-specific absolute micron values. (1 mark)
3. **DRC uniformity:** A design rule check can be performed with a single resolution grid, simplifying EDA tools and verification. (1 mark)

**Examiner's Marking Tip:** State the formula $\lambda = F_{min}/2$ and the merit of portability to score full marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

> **List any six lambda-based design rules for the polysilicon layer, with their standard λ values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per rule):**

| # | **Polysilicon Rule** | **λ Value** |
|---|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Minimum poly width (gate) | $2\lambda$ |
| 2 | Minimum poly-to-poly spacing | $2\lambda$ |
| 3 | Poly gate extension over thin-oxide (active) | $2\lambda$ |
| 4 | Poly extension over field oxide | $1\lambda$ |
| 5 | Poly enclosure of contact (on either side) | $1\lambda$ |
| 6 | Contact cut size (when cut sits on poly) | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Choice 1 — Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of design rules in VLSI layout. Differentiate between **micron (absolute) rules** and **lambda (scalable) rules**, with their relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Draw the stick diagram and corresponding lambda-rule-based mask layout of a **CMOS 2-input NAND gate**. State the design rules used and compute the approximate cell area. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** Design rules are a set of geometric constraints that specify the minimum dimensions and spacings of various mask layers to ensure that the fabricated chip is functional, with acceptable yield.

**Micron Rules (3 marks):**
- Specify dimensions in **absolute micrometres** (e.g., metal width = 0.5 µm).
- **Process-specific** — each foundry publishes its own rule set.
- **Layer-dependent** — different rules for poly, metal, contact, etc.
- **Accurate** for sub-100 nm processes where scaling is non-uniform.
- **Demerits:** Cannot be scaled; complex; fab-specific.

**Lambda Rules (3 marks):**
- Specify all dimensions as multiples of a single unit **λ = F_min / 2**.
- **Process-portable** — scale λ to migrate to a new node.
- **Pedagogically simple** — one rule set covers all technologies.
- **Demerits:** Non-uniform scaling breaks the assumption in modern nodes; p+ to n+ spacing across well does not scale linearly with gate length, making λ-rules inaccurate below ~0.5 µm.

**Comparison Table (extra for clarity, not mandatory):**

| **Aspect** | **Micron Rules** | **Lambda Rules** |
|------------|------------------|------------------|
| Unit | Micrometres | λ |
| Portability | None | High |
| Accuracy | High | Approximate |
| Use | Industry | Education |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Stick Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the CMOS NAND-2 stick diagram with:
- Two parallel pMOS (yellow diffusions) at the top, sharing VDD and output.
- Two series nMOS (green diffusions) at the bottom, sharing GND and an internal node.
- Two vertical poly lines for inputs A and B (red).
- Output (blue metal) connects the nMOS drain to both pMOS drains.
- A black square (contact) at each poly-diffusion intersection is implicit.

**Mask Layout (3 marks):** Convert stick to a polygon layout obeying:
- Poly width = $2\lambda$, poly spacing = $2\lambda$.
- Active area width = $2\lambda$, extension past poly = $2\lambda$ each side.
- Contact = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ with $1\lambda$ enclosure by poly and by metal.
- n+ to p+ spacing across well = $4\lambda$.
- Metal width = $3\lambda$, metal spacing = $3\lambda$.

**Cell Area (1 mark):**

$$
A_{NAND2} \approx 6\lambda \times 37\lambda = 222\lambda^2
$$

(As derived in SECTION 3.5.)

---

### **Choice 1 — Question B** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **(a)** Discuss the **Mead-Conway scalable design rules**. Explain any eight design rules with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to a **CMOS inverter**, explain the stick-diagram representation of each mask layer using colour codes. Compute the cell area assuming minimum dimensions. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Mead-Conway Rules (1 mark):** Introduced by Carver Mead and Lynn Conway in 1979, these rules use a single parameter λ to make layouts process-independent.

**Eight Rules (6 marks — 0.75 mark each):**

1. **Minimum n+ diffusion width = $2\lambda$** — to avoid line breakage.
2. **Minimum poly width = $2\lambda$** — defines minimum gate length.
3. **Minimum metal width = $3\lambda$** — metal is harder to print than poly.
4. **Minimum n+ to n+ spacing = $3\lambda$** — to avoid short between adjacent source/drain.
5. **Poly gate extension over thin oxide = $2\lambda$** — to ensure channel coverage.
6. **Contact size = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$** — standard contact cut dimension.
7. **Contact enclosure by poly/diff/metal = $1\lambda$** — for step-coverage safety.
8. **n+ to p+ spacing across well = $4\lambda$** — to prevent latch-up.

**Diagrams:** Show each rule as a stick diagram with annotated dimensions. (Use arrows and labels indicating $2\lambda$, $3\lambda$, etc.)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Stick Diagram with Colour Codes (4 marks):**

| **Layer** | **Colour** | **Function in Inverter** |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| n+ diffusion | Green | Source/drain of nMOS, connected to GND |
| p+ diffusion | Yellow | Source/drain of pMOS, connected to VDD |
| Polysilicon | Red | Common gate of nMOS and pMOS, input A |
| Metal 1 | Blue | VDD/GND rails, output Y |
| Contact cut | Black | Connects poly/diff to Metal 1 |

**Cell Area Calculation (3 marks):**

For minimum area CMOS inverter:
- nMOS: $W_n = 2\lambda$, $L = 2\lambda$, with $W_p = 2 W_n = 4\lambda$ for matched drive.
- Active region: $X = 6\lambda$, $Y_{nMOS} = 10\lambda$, $Y_{pMOS} = 14\lambda$.

$$
A_{INV} = 6\lambda \times (10\lambda + 2\lambda + 14\lambda) = 6\lambda \times 26\lambda = 156\lambda^2
$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Lambda-Rule Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting lateral diffusion:** Many students write $L_{eff} = 2\lambda$ when $L_{eff} = 2\lambda - 2L_D$. Always subtract $2L_D$ (or $0.2\lambda$ to $0.4\lambda$ total) for a realistic answer. (–1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Confusing extension and enclosure:** Extension = poly sticking out *beyond* the active region (≥ 2λ). Enclosure = contact cut being *surrounded* by metal/poly (≥ 1λ). Mixing these up is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Not mentioning "two contacts per source/drain"** in layout diagrams. This is a mandatory lambda rule for yield. Skipping it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Wrong colour codes in stick diagrams:** Green = n+ (electrons), Yellow = p+ (holes). Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per swap.
> 
> 5. **Drawing NAND-2 stick with nMOS in parallel:** Common mistake. NAND has nMOS in **series** and pMOS in **parallel**. NOR is the opposite. A wrong transistor arrangement is a –2 mark hit.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting n-well boundary:** Every pMOS must lie inside a dashed n-well rectangle in the stick diagram. Omission costs 0.5 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **λ = F_min / 2.** Minimum feature = 2λ. For 0.5 µm process, λ = 0.25 µm.
- ☐ **Diffusion width = 2λ.** Diffusion-diffusion spacing = 3λ.
- ☐ **Poly width = 2λ.** Poly-poly spacing = 2λ. Poly extension over active = 2λ.
- ☐ **Metal width = 3λ.** Metal-metal spacing = 3λ. Metal overhang over contact = 1λ.
- ☐ **Contact = 2λ × 2λ.** Contact enclosure = 1λ. **Always use ≥ 2 contacts per source/drain.**
- ☐ **p+ to n+ spacing across well = 4λ** (key latch-up rule).
- ☐ **Implant overhang = 2λ** over its respective active region.
- ☐ **Colour codes:** Green = n+, Yellow = p+, Red = poly, Blue = metal, Black square = contact, Black X = buried contact, Dashed cyan = n-well.
- ☐ **Stick diagrams** are colour-coded symbolic layouts; **mask layouts** are geometric polygons on multiple layers.
- ☐ **Mead-Conway rules** = process-portable λ-based rules; replaced by **micron rules** in modern deep-submicron fabs.
- ☐ **Standard-cell area** of an inverter ≈ 156λ²; NAND-2 ≈ 222λ² (numbers vary slightly by source).
- ☐ **Three things that can fail in layout:** open circuit, short circuit, parametric mismatch — design rules target all three.
- ☐ **λ-rules assume isotropic scaling** — broken in real sub-100 nm nodes but excellent for education.
- ☐ **DRC (Design Rule Check)** is automated in tools (Cadence Diva/Assura, Mentor Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator) and verifies every spacing/width/enclosure against the rule set.
- ☐ **Stick diagrams** of CMOS gates: NAND-2 has nMOS in series, pMOS in parallel; NOR-2 has nMOS in parallel, pMOS in series.
- ☐ **Buried contact** saves space but is process-expensive; **butting contact** is used to short poly to diffusion directly.
- ☐ **Examinee mnemonic:** "**2-2-3**" — minimum widths are 2λ (diff, poly), 2λ (gate ext), 3λ (metal); spacings are 2λ, 2λ, 3λ.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Lamda-Based Layout Design Rules for MOS Circuits**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Examples of Inverter, NAND and NOR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layout Examples of Inverter, NAND and NOR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of CMOS VLSI Design, a **layout** is the geometric representation (top-down 2D mask-level drawing) of a circuit, constructed using a set of predefined geometric **design rules** on different fabrication layers (n-diffusion, p-diffusion, polysilicon, metal, contact cuts). It is the final, fabricable blueprint that bridges the gap between a circuit schematic and the silicon die.

For the inverter, NAND, and NOR gates, the layout examples form the canonical pedagogical set used to teach:

- The mapping of a **schematic** (transistor-level) to a **stick diagram** (1D symbolic) to a **mask layout** (2D physical).
- Application of **Lambda (λ) based scalable design rules**.
- The concept of the **Euler path** (a continuous path through the graph of connected nodes that traverses every edge exactly once) used to minimise the number of diffusion breaks.
- Transistor **sizing** (W/L ratios) for symmetric switching thresholds and noise margins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to draw the stick diagram, identify a valid Euler path, and translate it to a mask-correct layout for the basic CMOS gates (Inverter, 2-input NAND, 2-input NOR). Marks are frequently awarded for correct Euler-path identification, layer identification, and applying λ-rules for minimum width and spacing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Intuition

Think of CMOS layout design as **urban city planning on a silicon wafer**:

| City Planning Element | VLSI Layout Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Roads connecting buildings | **Polysilicon & Metal lines** (the wires) |
| Residential vs Industrial zones | **p-diffusion vs n-diffusion** regions (different transistor types) |
| Minimum width of a road | **Minimum feature size** (governed by λ) |
| Building a flyover vs an intersection | **Transistor arrangement** (series vs parallel) |
| Routing city traffic in one continuous flow | **Euler path** (a single unbroken diffusion/poly strip) |

A CMOS **inverter** is the simplest "two-zone" structure — one NMOS zone and one PMOS zone. A **NAND** puts two NMOSes in **series** (like two toll booths in a single lane) and two PMOSes in **parallel** (like a fork in the road). A **NOR** does the opposite. The "Euler path" trick is exactly like a postman finding a single walking route that covers every street exactly once — he has to retrace fewer steps, saving silicon area.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are **board-exam essentials**:

- **Minimum feature size = 2λ** (where λ is the scalable design parameter, typically 0.25 µm → 22 nm processes and beyond).
- **μn ≈ 2.5 to 3 × μp** (electron mobility is roughly **2.5–3 times** hole mobility).
- **Cox (oxide capacitance per unit area)** — process-dependent.
- **Inverter switching threshold V_M = V_DD / 2** when $(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n = \sqrt{\mu_n / \mu_p}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-recommended assumption:** Use $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 3$ unless the question states otherwise. This leads to $(W/L)_p \approx \sqrt{3} \approx 1.7$ to 2 times $(W/L)_n$ for symmetric rise/fall.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Design Methodology

The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **4-stage layout design flow** for any CMOS gate:

1. **Stage 1 — Circuit Schematic:** Identify NMOS (pull-down network, PDN) and PMOS (pull-up network, PUN) transistors. For CMOS logic, PDN and PUN are **duals** of each other.
2. **Stage 2 — Graph / Euler Path Construction:** Treat each net (signal interconnection) as a node and each transistor as an edge. Find an Euler path that traverses every edge exactly once — this minimises the number of diffusion breaks (which are area-expensive).
3. **Stage 3 — Stick Diagram:** Convert the Euler path into a 1D symbolic diagram. Use:
   - **Vertical polysilicon lines** for gate electrodes.
   - **Horizontal diffusion lines** (n+ or p+) for source/drain regions.
   - **Dotted blue (or labelled "ndiff")** for n-diffusion, **green dotted (or "pdiff")** for p-diffusion.
   - **Solid black horizontal lines** for metal1 interconnect.
   - **Crosses (×)** for contact cuts (vias).
4. **Stage 4 — Mask Layout:** Assign 2D dimensions to every layer using λ-rules. Apply minimum width (2λ), minimum spacing (3λ), poly-over-diffusion overlap (2λ), contact size (2λ×2λ), and contact-to-gate spacing (2λ).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Euler path** reduces the number of separate diffusion regions, which directly reduces **parasitic capacitance** and **silicon area**.
- **Stick diagram** abstracts away physical dimensions so the **topology** can be verified before commitment to a geometric layout.
- **λ-rules** make the design **scalable** — the same mask layout works for any process generation by simply shrinking λ.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(W/L)_p = \sqrt{\mu_n / \mu_p} \cdot (W/L)_n$ | PMOS sizing for symmetric V_M | $\approx 1.7\,(W/L)_n$ |
| 2 | $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n / \mu_p) \cdot (W/L)_n$ | PMOS sizing for equal rise/fall resistance | $\approx 3\,(W/L)_n$ |
| 3 | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{GS}-V_T)}$ | On-resistance of MOSFET | Inversely ∝ W/L |
| 4 | Minimum poly width | Poly gate minimum width | **2λ** |
| 5 | Minimum poly spacing | Poly-to-poly pitch | **2λ** (gate-to-gate) / **3λ** (same diffusion) |
| 6 | Minimum diffusion width | Source/drain width | **3λ** (or 2λ for some rules) |
| 7 | Poly over diffusion overlap | Gate overlap of active area | **2λ** each side |
| 8 | Contact cut size | Minimum contact dimension | **2λ × 2λ** |
| 9 | Contact enclosure by poly/diff | Metal overlap over contact | **1λ** (top/bottom) |
| 10 | Metal width / spacing | Metal1 minimum geometry | **3λ** width, **2λ** spacing (Mead-Conway) |
| 11 | n+ to p+ spacing | Substrate/well tie boundary | **6λ** to **10λ** |
| 12 | $t_{pd} = 0.69\,R_{on} C_L$ | Inverter propagation delay | First-order RC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation safeguard:** In LaTeX, the absolute value or "such that" is written as `\vert x \vert` or `x \mid` — never use the bare vertical pipe character `|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table syntax. KTU examiners often use such notation in their model solutions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EDA Tool Calibration:** These layout primitives form the benchmark test-cases for place-and-route algorithms in tools like Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC, and open-source OpenROAD.
- **DRC (Design Rule Check) Verification:** Industrial foundries (TSMC, Samsung) scale λ-rules into absolute nanometre rules — the underlying topology remains identical.
- **Standard Cell Library Design:** Every gate in a real ASIC library (NAND2_X1, NOR2_X1, INV_X1 in Liberty format) is a refined, optimised version of these textbook layouts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PMOS-to-NMOS Sizing Ratio for Symmetric Inverter

The DC switching threshold of a CMOS inverter is defined as the input voltage $V_{in}$ at which $V_{out} = V_{in}$. Setting the NMOS and PMOS drain currents equal at $V_{in} = V_M$:

$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

Using the long-channel square-law model (in saturation at the switching point):

$$I_{Dn} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

$$I_{Dp} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

**Equating and simplifying** (assuming $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ for simplicity, and setting $V_M = V_{DD}/2$):

$$\mu_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_T\right)^2 = \mu_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_T\right)^2$$

**Cancel common terms:**

$$\mu_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = \mu_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p$$

**Solve for the sizing ratio:**

$$\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}$$

Substituting the standard KTU-recommended ratio $\mu_n / \mu_p = 3$:

$$\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = 3$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Because holes are 3× slower than electrons, the PMOS must be **3× wider** than the NMOS to deliver equal drive strength. This is why CMOS inverters appear visibly asymmetric in the layout — the PMOS occupies a larger diffusion rectangle than the NMOS.

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU questions use the **square-root rule** $(W/L)_p = \sqrt{\mu_n / \mu_p} \cdot (W/L)_n$ to size for equal **resistance** under average operating conditions. Read the question carefully: "equal noise margin" → square-root rule; "equal drive current at V_M" → linear ratio rule.

## 3.2 Derivation: NAND2 Sizing for Equal Rise/Fall Delay

For a **2-input NAND** gate, consider the worst-case paths:

- **Worst-case pull-down (NMOS series):** Both NMOS transistors are ON in series → effective resistance is $2 R_n$ because resistance adds in series.
- **Worst-case pull-up (PMOS parallel):** Both PMOS transistors may be ON in parallel → effective resistance is $R_p / 2$.

To balance the rise and fall delays, equate worst-case pull-up and pull-down resistance:

$$\frac{R_p}{2} = 2 R_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_p = 4 R_n$$

Since $R \propto 1 / (W/L)$:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$

With $\mu_n / \mu_p = 3$:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{3}{4} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$

This is the **design optimisation** that explains why in a NAND2 layout, the **PMOS width can be smaller** than in a standalone inverter — but most textbook layouts use the **unit-inverter sizing** (PMOS = 3× NMOS) for simplicity.

## 3.3 Derivation: NOR2 Sizing for Symmetric Delay

For a **2-input NOR** gate, the topology is the dual of NAND2:

- **Worst-case pull-down (NMOS parallel):** $R_n / 2$.
- **Worst-case pull-up (PMOS series):** $2 R_p$.

Equating for balance:

$$\frac{R_n}{2} = 2 R_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_n = 4 R_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 4 \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n$$

This means in a **NOR2**, the PMOS must be made **much larger** than in a NAND2 (because the PMOSes are in the series path, which degrades pull-up strength more). This area penalty is one reason NOR gates are less popular than NAND gates in high-density standard cell libraries.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layout Topology Comparison

| Parameter | CMOS Inverter | 2-input NAND | 2-input NOR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NMOS arrangement** | Single transistor | **Series** chain of 2 | **Parallel** pair |
| **PMOS arrangement** | Single transistor | **Parallel** pair | **Series** chain of 2 |
| **Number of Euler paths** | 1 (forced ordering) | 4 (A,B,ab,ba orderings) | 1 (forced: A,ab,b) |
| **PMOS sizing for equal delay** | $(W/L)_p = 3\,(W/L)_n$ | $(W/L)_p = 0.75\,(W/L)_n$ (optimised) or 3× (unit inverter) | $(W/L)_p = 12\,(W/L)_n$ (optimised) or 3× (unit inverter) |
| **Worst-case pull-up resistance** | $R_p$ | $R_p$ (one PMOS ON) | $2 R_p$ (both ON in series) |
| **Worst-case pull-down resistance** | $R_n$ | $2 R_n$ (both ON in series) | $R_n$ (one NMOS ON) |
| **Diffusion breaks required** | 0 (single n+ and single p+) | 0 with Euler path A,B,ab | 0 with Euler path A,ab,b |
| **Stuck-at-fault coverage** | N/A | Detects all s-a-0/1 on inputs | Detects all s-a-0/1 on inputs |
| **Logic function** | $Y = \overline{A}$ | $Y = \overline{AB}$ | $Y = \overline{A+B}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram (Mermaid Functional Representation)

Because Mermaid cannot render true physical mask geometries, we represent the **stick diagram as a functional block-level schematic** showing the topological ordering along the Euler path.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[VDD Metal Rail] --> B0[PMOS Source p+ node]
    B0 --> C0[PMOS Gate polysilicon A]
    C0 --> D0[PMOS Drain p+ output node]
    D0 --> E0[Shared n+/p+ junction OUTPUT Y]
    E0 --> F0[NMOS Drain n+]
    F0 --> G0[NMOS Gate polysilicon A]
    G0 --> H0[NMOS Source n+]
    H0 --> I0[GND Metal Rail]

    style A0 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style I0 fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#000099
    style E0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#996600
```

**Reading the diagram:** Reading top-to-bottom is the **Euler path** itself — VDD → PMOS source → gate (input A) → drain → OUTPUT → NMOS drain → gate (input A) → source → GND. The single continuous metal/diffusion strip minimises breaks.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Euler Path Search for 2-Input NAND Gate

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PMOS_Network["PMOS PUN - Parallel"]
        VDD[(VDD)] --- P1A((PMOS-A))
        VDD --- P1B((PMOS-B))
        P1A --- OUTN((OUT))
        P1B --- OUTN
    end

    subgraph NMOS_Network["NMOS PDN - Series"]
        OUTN --- N1A((NMOS-A))
        N1A --- N1B((NMOS-B))
        N1B --- GNDN[(GND)]
    end

    style VDD fill:#ffcccc
    style GNDN fill:#ccccff
    style OUTN fill:#ffff99
```

**Euler path for NAND2** (one of four valid orderings): `VDD → PMOS-A source → PMOS-A gate (A) → PMOS-A drain → OUT → NMOS-A drain → NMOS-A gate (A) → NMOS-A source → NMOS-B drain → NMOS-B gate (B) → NMOS-B source → GND`. Reading the gates in order **A, B** along the vertical line of polysilicon columns is one canonical Euler-path choice.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Layout Design Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Circuit Schematic] --> S2[Step 2: Build Transistor Graph]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Find Euler Path]
    S3 --> S4{Valid Euler Path?}
    S4 -- No --> S2
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Step 4: Construct Stick Diagram]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Apply Lambda Design Rules]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Generate Mask Layout]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Run DRC + LVS Verification]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Final GDSII Stream-out]

    style S4 fill:#ffeecc,stroke:#cc6600
    style S9 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — CMOS Inverter Cross-Section Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CrossSection["Inverter Cross-Section Layer Stack (Top-to-Bottom)"]
        L1[Layer 1: Metal1 - VDD GND Output Routing]
        L2[Layer 2: Contact Cuts - 2λ x 2λ]
        L3[Layer 3: n+ and p+ Diffusion - Source or Drain]
        L4[Layer 4: Thin Gate Oxide - 50 Angstrom to 2 nm]
        L5[Layer 5: Polysilicon Gate - Minimum Width 2λ]
        L6[Layer 6: Field Oxide - LOCOS or STI Isolation]
        L7[Layer 7: p-type Substrate and n-well]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7

    style L5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style L3 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L7 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NAND2_Topology["NAND2 - PUN Parallel, PDN Series"]
        VDD1[(VDD)] --> P1((PMOS-A)) --> OUT1((OUT))
        VDD1 --> P2((PMOS-B)) --> OUT1
        OUT1 --> N1((NMOS-A)) --> N2((NMOS-B)) --> GND1[(GND)]
    end

    subgraph NOR2_Topology["NOR2 - PUN Series, PDN Parallel"]
        VDD2[(VDD)] --> P3((PMOS-A)) --> P4((PMOS-B)) --> OUT2((OUT))
        OUT2 --> N3((NMOS-A)) --> GND2[(GND)]
        OUT2 --> N4((NMOS-B)) --> GND2
    end

    NAND2_Topology ~~~ NOR2_Topology
```
