## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the dynamic power dissipation equation for a CMOS inverter and identify the parameter that has the strongest influence on reducing it.*

**Model Answer:**
The dynamic power dissipated in a CMOS gate is given by:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$$

where α is the switching activity factor, C_L is the load capacitance, V_DD is the supply voltage, and f is the clock frequency. The parameter with the strongest influence is the **supply voltage V_DD**, because dynamic power is **proportional to V_DD²** (quadratic dependence). Reducing V_DD by a factor of k reduces power by a factor of k². **Verdict: V_DD is the most effective knob.** *(2 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Energy per Operation" (E_op) for a digital CMOS circuit. Why is it considered a more fundamental metric than dynamic power?*

**Model Answer:**
Energy per operation is the total energy consumed by the circuit to perform one complete logic operation (e.g., one clock cycle's worth of switching):

$$E_{op} = P_{dynamic} \cdot T_{clk} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2}$$

It is considered more fundamental than dynamic power because it is **independent of the operating frequency f** and reflects the true thermodynamic cost of computation. A designer can lower P by simply lowering f, but this does *not* save energy — it only spreads the same energy over a longer time. Reducing E_op requires reducing V_DD, C_L, or α. **For battery-operated devices, E_op (not P) determines battery life.** *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS circuit. Explain why scaling the supply voltage V_DD provides a quadratic reduction in power, while scaling the frequency f provides only a linear reduction. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):**
Consider an n-MOS transistor charging a load capacitance C_L from 0 to V_DD. The energy drawn from the supply during one 0→1 transition is:

$$E_{VDD} = \int_0^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i_{VDD}(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \cdot \int_0^{\infty} C_L \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} \, dt$$

$$E_{VDD} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

Half of this energy (½ C_L V_DD²) is stored on C_L; the other half is dissipated as heat in the p-MOS transistor during charging. On the subsequent 1→0 transition, the stored energy ½ C_L V_DD² is dissipated in the n-MOS transistor. Hence the **average energy dissipated per switching cycle is C_L V_DD²**.

For a system with switching activity factor α and clock frequency f, the average dynamic power is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$$

**Step 2 — V_DD scaling gives quadratic reduction (2 marks):**
From the equation, P ∝ V_DD² (with other parameters held constant). Halving V_DD (k = 2) reduces power by a factor of k² = 4. This is because the energy stored on the capacitor scales as V_DD², and the power is energy per unit time.

**Step 3 — Frequency scaling gives only linear reduction (2 marks):**
P ∝ f (linearly). Halving f reduces power by only a factor of 2. Furthermore, since E_op = α C_L V_DD² is **independent of f**, lowering the clock does *not* reduce total energy per operation — it merely stretches execution time. Therefore, V_DD scaling is far more effective than frequency scaling for low-power design.

---

**(b)** *A digital signal processor operates at V_DD = 1.0 V, f = 200 MHz, with α = 0.2 and C_total = 5 nF. The design team wants to reduce power by 4× while maintaining the same throughput. Propose a design using a combination of voltage scaling and parallelism. Compute the new V_DD, the required number of parallel units, and the new operating frequency. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Baseline power (1 mark):**

$$P_0 = 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 200 \times 10^{6} = 0.2 \text{ W} = 200 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Power reduction target (1 mark):**
Target power P_target = P_0 / 4 = 50 mW. Power ratio = 1/4 = 1/k², so k = 2.

**Step 3 — New V_DD (1 mark):**

$$V_{DD,new} = \dfrac{V_{DD,0}}{k} = \dfrac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Required parallelism N (1 mark):**
Since delay increases by factor k = 2 (in long-channel approximation), we need 2 parallel units running at half the frequency to maintain the same aggregate throughput:

$$N = k = 2 \text{ parallel units}$$

**Step 5 — New per-unit frequency (1 mark):**

$$f_{new} = \dfrac{f_0}{N} = \dfrac{200 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 100 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (2 marks):**

$$P_{new} = 2 \times 0.2 \times \left(\dfrac{5 \text{ nF}}{2}\right) \times (0.5)^{2} \times 100 \text{ MHz}$$

(Assuming workload is partitioned: C_unit = C_total/2.)

$$P_{new} = 2 \times 0.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.25 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{new} = 2 \times 0.2 \times 2.5 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-1} = 2 \times 0.0125 = 0.025 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ mW}$$

Power ratio: 25/200 = **1/8 = 1/k³** (because workload is also partitioned). This *exceeds* the target of 1/4. ✅ The design comfortably meets the 4× power-reduction target with a comfortable margin, using 2× area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that the **V_DD scaling enables parallelism**, not the other way around. The correct sequence is: **(1)** Decide how much you want to scale V_DD (k), **(2)** Compute the new delay, **(3)** Determine N needed to keep throughput constant, **(4)** Verify the new frequency. Reversing this order (e.g., "we'll use 4-way parallelism, so we need ¼ the voltage") is **WRONG** unless you also have algorithmic partitioning. Also, students frequently omit the **C_unit = C_total/N** term in the verification step and arrive at 1/k² instead of 1/k³, which is not wrong but misses the bonus from algorithmic partitioning. **Read the question carefully!**

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** *Explain with a suitable block diagram how parallelism can be combined with voltage scaling to achieve constant throughput at reduced power consumption. Show the derivation of the k² power-reduction rule. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept and block diagram description (3 marks):**
Refer to the schematic in Section 4.4 (Parallel FIR filter). The block diagram shows an input data stream being demultiplexed into **N** parallel sub-filter units. Each sub-filter operates at a **slower clock (f/N)** and a **lower supply voltage (V_DD/k)**. The outputs of the N units are combined by an adder tree to produce the final output. The aggregate throughput of the system equals the original single-unit throughput, but the total power is reduced by a factor of k² (or k³ with workload partitioning).

**Step 2 — Derivation of k² rule (4 marks):**
Starting from the baseline power:

$$P_0 = \alpha \, C_{total} \, V_{DD,0}^{2} \, f_0$$

Apply k-way parallelism: N = k, V_DD,new = V_DD,0/k, f_new = f_0/k. With **constant C_total** (workload duplicated, not partitioned):

$$P_{new} = k \cdot \alpha \, C_{total} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{DD,0}}{k}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{f_0}{k}\right)$$

$$P_{new} = k \cdot \alpha \, C_{total} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD,0}^{2}}{k^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{f_0}{k} = \dfrac{\alpha \, C_{total} \, V_{DD,0}^{2} \, f_0}{k^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_0} = \dfrac{1}{k^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering tradeoff comment (1 mark):**
The price paid is **k× silicon area** and additional routing/interconnect complexity. This is justified only when the energy savings outweigh the area and design-cost penalties (e.g., in low-power DSP and battery-operated systems).

---

**(b)** *Compare the three approaches: (i) voltage scaling alone, (ii) parallelism alone, and (iii) voltage scaling combined with parallelism. Tabulate the comparison in terms of throughput, power, energy per operation, and area overhead. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Metric | Baseline | (i) V-Scaling Only (V_DD/k, f fixed) | (ii) Parallelism Only (N = k, V_DD, f fixed) | (iii) V-Scaling + Parallelism (V_DD/k, f/k, N = k) |
|--------|----------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| Supply Voltage | V_DD | V_DD / k | V_DD | V_DD / k |
| Clock Frequency | f | f | f / k | f / k |
| Number of Units | 1 | 1 | k | k |
| Throughput | R₀ | R₀ / k | R₀ | R₀ |
| Dynamic Power | P₀ | P₀ / k² | P₀ | **P₀ / k² (or k³ with workload partition)** |
| Energy per Operation | E₀ | E₀ / k² | E₀ | **E₀ / k² (or k³ with workload partition)** |
| Area | A | A | k · A | k · A |
| Design Complexity | Low | Medium (timing closure) | Medium-High (interconnect) | High (routing, clock tree, skew) |
| Suitable Application | — | Non-real-time, low throughput | High throughput, no energy budget | **Low-power real-time DSP / IoT** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often **forget to mention throughput recovery** when comparing techniques — KTU specifically tests this in the 7-mark sub-parts.
> 2. When deriving the k² rule, **show all intermediate cancellations** of the factor k. Skipping algebraic steps costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. For the comparison table, include **all six rows** (voltage, frequency, units, throughput, power, area). Incomplete tables lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Common unit error:** Confusing **pJ (10⁻¹² J)** with **nJ (10⁻⁹ J)**. Always re-check powers of ten in numerical answers. A wrong prefix is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 4, Dynamic Power Reduction**

- **Dynamic Power Equation:** $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — the four parameters and their roles must be memorized.
- **Quadratic vs Linear:** $V_{DD}$ gives **quadratic (V²)** savings — the strongest lever. $f$ and $C_L$ and $\alpha$ give **linear** savings.
- **Energy per Operation:** $E_{op} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ — **frequency-independent**. This is the most tested conceptual point in KTU exams.
- **Pure Parallelism:** Adds area, **does NOT save power** (P_total = P_0 if V_DD and total C are unchanged).
- **Voltage Scaling + Parallelism:** The combined technique yields **k² power reduction** (or **k³ with workload partitioning**) at constant throughput, at the cost of **k× area**.
- **k² Rule Derivation:** $P_{new} = k \cdot \alpha C_{total} \cdot (V_{DD}/k)^2 \cdot (f/k) = P_0 / k^2$. **Memorize the three k-factors:** one in the numerator (for N replicas) and two in the denominator (V² scaling) and one in the denominator (f scaling) — net k².
- **k³ Rule (Algorithmic Parallelism):** Achieved only when workload is genuinely partitioned (e.g., a 1024-tap FIR split into 4 × 256-tap filters), so that **C_unit = C_total / N**. KTU sometimes asks this distinction.
- **Delay Constraint:** $t_d \propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}$. As $V_{DD} \to V_{th}$, delay → ∞. This is why **voltage scaling has a lower bound**.
- **Tradeoff:** Lower power ⇔ Lower throughput (V-scaling alone) ⇔ Higher area (parallelism) ⇔ Higher design complexity (clock tree, interconnect).
- **Engineering Metrics:** EDP (Energy-Delay Product) and PDP (Power-Delay Product) — both used in KTU problems to compare designs.
- **Real-World Examples:** Apple big.LITTLE, Qualcomm Snapdragon heterogeneous cores, MediaTek Dimensity, TPU systolic arrays — all combine parallelism + voltage/frequency scaling.
- **Memorize the Numerical Example:** A 2-V, 500-MHz, 0.2-α, 2-nF design gives 200 mW (or whatever the question states) — practice computing power with V_DD halved and frequency halved to reinforce the k² rule.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers: W, mW, μW for power; J, pJ, fJ for energy; Hz, MHz, GHz for frequency.
- **Diagram requirement:** For 7-mark sub-parts involving parallelism, **draw a block diagram** (demux → N parallel units → adder tree) to fetch full marks.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Saying "parallelism reduces power" — **NO!** Parallelism *enables* voltage scaling; the actual power reduction comes from the squared V term.
- **KTU 2024 Specific:** Questions often combine this topic with **clock gating, multi-V_th, and pipelining** — be ready to compare and choose the best technique for a given scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining (Basic Concepts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pipelining (Basic Concepts) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **PIPELINING (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)** > *Pipelining* is a VLSI design technique in which a sequential computational process is decomposed into a series of independent, overlapping sub-operations called **stages**, with intermediate **latch/flip-flop registers** placed between consecutive stages. Each stage performs a partial operation on the data, and the registers synchronize the partial results to a global clock, enabling multiple data items to be processed concurrently across the stages. In the KTU 2024 VLSI Design syllabus, pipelining is classified as a fundamental **throughput-enhancement architectural technique**, sitting alongside parallelism, retiming, and clocking strategies under the broader umbrella of *high-performance digital design*. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Laundry Pipeline" Imagine **4 students** — Alice, Bob, Chen, and Dave — each with a basket of dirty clothes. The laundry process has **4 stages**: *Wash → Dry → Fold → Store*. Each stage takes **30 minutes**. **Without Pipelining (Sequential Execution):** | Time (min) | 0–30 | 30–60 | 60–90 | 90–120 | 120–150 | 150–180 | 180–210 | 210–240 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Alice | Wash | Dry | Fold | Store | – | – | – | – | | Bob | – | – | – | – | Wash | Dry | Fold | Store | | Chen | – | – | – | – | – | – | – | – | | Dave | – | – | – | – | – | – | – | – | **With Pipelining (Overlapped Execution):** | Time (min) | 0–30 | 30–60 | 60–90 | 90–120 | |---|---|---|---|---| | Alice | Wash | Dry | Fold | Store | | Bob | – | Wash | Dry | Fold | | Chen | – | – | Wash | Dry | | Dave | – | – | – | Wash | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** The *first* basket still takes 4 stages × 30 min = **120 minutes** to complete (this is the **latency**). However, *every subsequent* basket finishes every **30 minutes** (this is the **throughput**). The pipeline increases **throughput**, not the speed of any single operation. This is exactly how a VLSI adder, multiplier, or processor pipeline works — multiple operands flow through fixed hardware stages simultaneously. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted) The following metrics are **bold-weighted** in KTU valuation keys for Module 4: - **Throughput (TP):** Number of tasks completed per unit time. - **Latency (L):** Time required for *one single task* to traverse from input to output. - **Speedup (S):** Ratio of execution time without pipelining to execution time with pipelining. - **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of actual speedup to the theoretical maximum speedup. - **Clock Period ($T_c$):** Time interval of one clock cycle, governed by the slowest stage. - **Pipeline Depth (k):** Number of stages in the pipeline. - **Pipeline Fill Time:** The initial cycles required to keep all stages busy. ## 1.4 Why Pipelining Matters in VLSI > [!TIP] > Modern VLSI chips (CPUs, GPUs, DSPs) rely on pipelining to achieve **GHz-level operating frequencies**. Without pipelining, the critical path of an N-bit adder would be O(N) gate delays; with pipelining, it is reduced to O(N/k) per stage, allowing the chip to clock much faster. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Components of a Pipeline A generic synchronous VLSI pipeline contains three repeating elements: 1. **Combinational Logic Block (CLB):** Performs the partial operation of that stage (e.g., a partial addition, shifting, or logic reduction). 2. **Pipeline Register (Latch/Flip-Flop):** Captures the CLB output at the active clock edge and holds it for the next stage. 3. **Clock Distribution Network:** A balanced clock tree delivering a low-skew clock signal to every register simultaneously. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Terminology Note:** The terms *pipeline register*, *inter-stage register*, *latch*, and *boundary register* are used interchangeably in Module 4. ## 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Logic The execution of $n$ tasks through a $k$-stage pipeline proceeds as follows: - **Step 1 — Pipeline Fill (Cycles 1 to $k$):** The first task enters Stage 1, then the second clock cycle enters Stage 2, and so on. By the $k^{th}$ cycle, all $k$ stages are processing different tasks simultaneously. - **Step 2 — Steady State (Cycles $k+1$ to $n$):** For every new clock cycle, a *new* completed task exits the pipeline, while a new task enters Stage 1. The pipeline operates at peak throughput. - **Step 3 — Pipeline Drain (Cycles $n+1$ to $n+k-1$):** The last task finishes Stage 1, then progressively moves through Stages 2, 3, ..., $k$ until it exits Stage $k$. After this, the pipeline becomes idle. > [!IMPORTANT] > The total number of clock cycles required to process $n$ tasks in a $k$-stage pipeline is **$n + k - 1$**. This single expression is the most-tested formula in KTU Module 4 problems. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below assume an **ideal pipeline** with no hazards, no stalls, no branching, and no register setup/hold violations. Bold text indicates KTU high-yield entries. | # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Description | Engineering Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | **Speedup** for $n$ tasks in $k$ stages | Dimensionless | | 2 | $S_{max} = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$ | **Maximum Speedup** (ideal) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{n + k - 1}$ | **Pipeline Efficiency** | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 4 | $TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{n}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c}$ | **Throughput** in pipelined mode | tasks/second | | 5 | $TP_{seq} = \dfrac{1}{k \cdot T_c}$ | **Throughput** of non-pipelined equivalent | tasks/second | | 6 | $L = k \cdot T_c$ | **Latency** of one task in ideal pipeline | seconds | | 7 | $T_c = \max(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k) + t_{reg}$ | **Clock Period** (slowest stage + register delay) | seconds | | 8 | $T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_c$ | **Total Pipelined Execution Time** | seconds | | 9 | $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T_c$ | **Total Sequential Execution Time** | seconds | | 10 | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | **Maximum Clock Frequency** | Hz | > [!WARNING] > In Formula 7, $t_{reg}$ is the **clock-to-Q delay + setup time** of the pipeline register. Many KTU students forget this term, leading to **2-mark deduction** in numerical problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs):** Texas Instruments' C6000 series and Analog Devices' SHARC use **deep pipelines** (8–14 stages) to enable real-time FIR/FFT computation at GHz rates. - **Microprocessors:** Intel's *Pentium 4 (Prescott)* used a 31-stage pipeline (Hyper-Pipelined Architecture) to push clock speeds above 3.8 GHz. - **Image/Video Processing Pipelines:** Camera ISPs and H.264/HEVC codecs use pipelined hardware to sustain 4K@60fps throughput. - **AI Accelerators:** Google's TPU and NVIDIA's Tensor Cores use **systolic pipelined arrays** for matrix multiplication. > The underlying design trade-off always remains the same: **more stages → higher throughput, but also higher latency and increased hazard sensitivity**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives every key formula from first principles, so you can reproduce them in the KTU exam with full marks. ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Pipelined Execution Time $T_{pipe}$ **Given:** A $k$-stage pipeline with stage delays $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$ and pipeline register delay $t_{reg}$. Let the clock period be $T_c$ and the number of tasks be $n$. **Step 1 — Determine the Clock Period.** The clock period must accommodate the slowest stage plus the register's clock-to-Q delay and setup time. $$T_c = \max_{i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}} (t_i) + t_{reg}$$ **Logic:** If the clock period were shorter than the slowest stage, that stage's output would be unstable before the next register captures it — a setup-time violation. **Step 2 — Count the Total Number of Clock Cycles.** The pipeline needs $k$ cycles to *fill* (the first task traverses Stage 1 → Stage 2 → ... → Stage $k$). After that, one new task completes every cycle. The last task then needs an additional $k-1$ cycles to *drain* (it leaves Stage 1 but must still pass through Stages 2, ..., $k$). $$\text{Total Cycles} = k + (n - 1) = n + k - 1$$ **Step 3 — Multiply by Clock Period.** $$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_c$$ **Step 4 — Special Case (Large $n$).** When $n \gg k$, the term $k - 1$ becomes negligible, and the pipeline approaches its **steady-state throughput**: $$TP_{steady} = \frac{1}{T_c}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Speedup $S$ **Given:** Compare the pipelined execution time to the non-pipelined (sequential) execution time. **Step 1 — Define Sequential Execution Time.** In a non-pipelined design, every task must complete all $k$ stages sequentially before the next task begins. The delay of one task is the sum of all stage delays plus the register overhead. $$T_{task} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + k \cdot t_{reg}$$ For an *ideal* pipeline where all stages are balanced ($t_i = T_c - t_{reg}$ for all $i$), this simplifies to: $$T_{task} = k \cdot T_c$$ **Step 2 — Sequential Time for $n$ Tasks.** $$T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T_c$$ **Step 3 — Speedup Ratio.** $$S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Both Expressions.** $$S = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_c}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $T_c$ (Assumption: same clock period in both cases).** $$\boxed{S = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting Case.** Apply L'Hôpital's rule or simple intuition: as $n \to \infty$: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{1 + \frac{k-1}{n}} = k$$ Thus $S_{max} = k$, the *number of pipeline stages*. This is the **theoretical upper bound** of speedup, achievable only in the limit of infinite tasks. ## 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency $\eta$ **Step 1 — Define Efficiency.** Efficiency is the *fraction of the theoretical maximum speedup* actually achieved. $$\eta = \frac{S}{S_{max}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $S = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ and $S_{max} = k$.** $$\eta = \frac{\frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}}{k}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{n}{n + k - 1}}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting Case.** As $n \to \infty$: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n}{n + k - 1} = 1 = 100\%$$ This is the *ideal* efficiency — every stage is busy every cycle. In real designs, hazards and stalls reduce this to **60%–85%** typically. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A **4-stage pipelined RISC processor** executes **500 instructions**. Stage delays are 25 ns, 30 ns, 20 ns, and 35 ns. Pipeline register delay is **5 ns**. Compute: (i) Clock period, (ii) Total execution time, (iii) Speedup over non-pipelined execution, (iv) Efficiency, (v) Throughput. **Solution:** **Step (i) — Clock Period.** The slowest stage is Stage 4 at 35 ns. $$T_c = \max(25, 30, 20, 35) + 5 = 35 + 5 = 40 \text{ ns}$$ **Step (ii) — Total Pipelined Execution Time.** Here $n = 500$ tasks and $k = 4$ stages. $$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_c = (500 + 4 - 1) \cdot 40 = 503 \cdot 40 = 20{,}120 \text{ ns} = 20.12 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step (iii) — Speedup.** First, the non-pipelined time per task: $$T_{task} = 25 + 30 + 20 + 35 + (4 \cdot 5) = 110 + 20 = 130 \text{ ns}$$ Sequential total time: $$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_{task} = 500 \cdot 130 = 65{,}000 \text{ ns} = 65 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ Speedup: $$S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{65{,}000}{20{,}120} \approx 3.23$$ > [!NOTE] > Note that the speedup is **less than 4** (the maximum). This is because $n$ is finite; the fill + drain overhead of $k-1 = 3$ cycles hurts the average. If $n = 1{,}000{,}000$, $S$ would approach **3.999**. **Step (iv) — Efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.23}{4} \approx 0.808 = 80.8\%$$ **Step (v) — Throughput.** $$TP = \frac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{500}{20{,}120 \text{ ns}} = \frac{500}{20.12 \text{ }\mu\text{s}} \approx 24.85 \text{ MIPS}$$ (Million Instructions Per Second.) ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sequential vs. Pipelined | Performance Parameter | Sequential (Non-Pipelined) | Ideal Pipelined ($n \to \infty$) | Finite $n$ Pipelined | |---|---|---|---| | Time per task | $k \cdot T_c$ | $k \cdot T_c$ (latency) | $k \cdot T_c$ (latency) | | Total time for $n$ tasks | $n \cdot k \cdot T_c$ | $\approx n \cdot T_c$ (steady state) | $(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c$ | | Throughput | $\frac{1}{k \cdot T_c}$ | $\frac{1}{T_c}$ | $\frac{n}{(n+k-1) \cdot T_c}$ | | Speedup | $1$ | $k$ | $\frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | | Efficiency | $100\%$ | $100\%$ | $\frac{n}{n+k-1}$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section provides a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a generic VLSI pipeline, mapped through Mermaid diagrams. These diagrams are designed to be directly reproducible in the KTU answer sheet (using a hand-drawn equivalent if Mermaid is not permitted). ## 4.1 Generic k-Stage Pipelined Architecture The following diagram shows the canonical structure of a $k$-stage synchronous pipeline. Note the strict alternation between **Combinational Logic** and **Pipeline Registers**, both clocked by a global clock tree. ```mermaid graph LR inputA["Input Operand A"] --> regA["Register R1"] regA --> stage1["Stage 1 Logic
Delay t1"] stage1 --> regB["Register R2"] regB --> stage2["Stage 2 Logic
Delay t2"] stage2 --> regC["Register R3"] regC --> stage3["Stage 3 Logic
Delay t3"] stage3 --> regD["Register R4"] regD --> stage4["Stage 4 Logic
Delay t4"] stage4 --> outputY["Output Result Y"] clkSrc["Global Clock
f_clk"] -.-> regA clkSrc -.-> regB clkSrc -.-> regC clkSrc -.-> regD style regA fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000 style regB fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000 style regC fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000 style regD fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000 style stage1 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763 style stage2 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763 style stage3 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763 style stage4 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763 style clkSrc fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Blue blocks** = Combinational logic stages (perform partial operations). - **Yellow blocks** = Pipeline registers (capture data on the active clock edge). - **Dotted green arrows** = Clock distribution lines (must have low skew). - **Solid black arrows** = Data path (unidirectional flow). ## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram — Multi-Task Execution This diagram illustrates the *temporal overlap* of multiple tasks across pipeline stages over successive clock cycles. ```mermaid gantt title Pipelined Execution of 5 Tasks across 4 Stages dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Task T1 T1 Stage 1 :a1, 0, 1 T1 Stage 2 :a2, 1, 1 T1 Stage 3 :a3, 2, 1 T1 Stage 4 :a4, 3, 1 section Task T2 T2 Stage 1 :b1, 1, 1 T2 Stage 2 :b2, 2, 1 T2 Stage 3 :b3, 3, 1 T2 Stage 4 :b4, 4, 1 section Task T3 T3 Stage 1 :c1, 2, 1 T3 Stage 2 :c2, 3, 1 T3 Stage 3 :c3, 4, 1 T3 Stage 4 :c4, 5, 1 section Task T4 T4 Stage 1 :d1, 3, 1 T4 Stage 2 :d2, 4, 1 T4 Stage 3 :d3, 5, 1 T4 Stage 4 :d4, 6, 1 section Task T5 T5 Stage 1 :e1, 4, 1 T5 Stage 2 :e2, 5, 1 T5 Stage 3 :e3, 6, 1 T5 Stage 4 :e4, 7, 1 ``` **Reading the Gantt chart:** - In **cycle 3** (X = 3), the pipeline is *fully filled*: T1 is in Stage 4, T2 in Stage 3, T3 in Stage 2, T4 in Stage 1. - From **cycle 4** onwards, the pipeline is in *steady state* — one new task completes every cycle. - The **last task T5** finishes at cycle 7. Total cycles = $n + k - 1 = 5 + 4 - 1 = 8$? Actually, T5 *enters* at cycle 4 and *exits* at cycle 7, so the total *clock cycles from first enter to last exit* is 8, but the *active processing cycles* for the batch is $n + k - 1 = 5 + 4 - 1 = 8$. ✓ ## 4.3 Pipeline Stage Delay and Clock Period Determination The following flowchart shows how the **clock period** is determined from individual stage delays. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Start: Stage Delays t1...tk"] --> findmax["Compute T_stage_max = max of all ti"] findmax --> addr["Add Register Overhead t_reg"] addr --> clockperiod["T_c = T_stage_max + t_reg"] clockperiod --> freq["f_clk = 1 / T_c"] freq --> checkb["Is f_clk within design target?"] checkb -->|No| balance["Re-balance stages
Insert retiming registers"] balance --> findmax checkb -->|Yes| final["Clock Period Finalized"] style startA fill:#fce5cd style final fill:#d9ead3 style balance fill:#fff2cc ``` **Reading the flowchart:** - If the design's target clock frequency cannot be met, the designer must **re-balance** the stages (move logic between stages) and **insert retiming registers** to shorten the critical path. This is an iterative VLSI design optimization technique taught later in Module 5. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap The following questions strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks, short answer) and Part B (14 marks, with internal choice and sub-parts of 7+7 marks). --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define pipelining in the context of VLSI design. List any three advantages and one limitation of pipelining.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pipelining is a design technique in which a sequential operation is partitioned into $k$ independent sub-operations called *stages*, separated by clocked registers. Multiple data operands are processed simultaneously across the stages, with each stage handling a different operand at any given clock cycle. **Three Advantages (1 mark, 0.33 each):** 1. Increased **throughput** (one result per clock cycle in steady state). 2. Reduced **critical path delay** per stage, enabling higher clock frequency. 3. Better **hardware utilization** — all stages are busy concurrently. **One Limitation (0.5 marks):** - Increased **latency** for the first result (pipeline fill time of $k$ cycles). Also, pipeline hazards can reduce actual throughput. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between *throughput* and *latency* in a pipelined data path. A 6-stage pipeline has a clock period of 10 ns. Find its latency and peak throughput.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Differentiation (2 marks):** | Aspect | Throughput | Latency | |---|---|---| | Definition | Number of tasks completed per unit time | Time for one task to traverse the entire pipeline | | Symbol | $TP$ | $L$ | | Unit | tasks/second | seconds | | Effect of more stages | Increases (in steady state) | Increases (linearly with $k$) | | Optimization Goal | Maximize | Often minimize, but secondary to throughput | **Calculation (1 mark):** $$L = k \cdot T_c = 6 \cdot 10 \text{ ns} = 60 \text{ ns}$$ $$TP_{peak} = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{10 \text{ ns}} = 100 \text{ MHz (i.e., } 10^8 \text{ tasks/sec)}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a neat block diagram, explain the basic concept of pipelining in VLSI. Discuss a real-world analogy to justify the design trade-off between latency and throughput.** **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A pipeline consists of $k$ combinational logic blocks (stages) interleaved with $k-1$ pipeline registers. Each stage performs a partial operation, and the registers synchronize the data flow at each active clock edge. A global clock signal feeds all registers simultaneously through a low-skew clock distribution tree. ``` INPUT → [S1] → [R1] → [S2] → [R2] → [S3] → [R3] → [S4] → OUTPUT ↑ ↑ ↑ CLK CLK CLK ``` **Real-World Analogy — The Laundry Pipeline (2 marks):** Consider 4 students with laundry tasks, each requiring 4 stages: *Wash → Dry → Fold → Store*. If executed sequentially, one student's laundry takes 4 × 30 min = 120 min. With pipelining, while Alice's clothes are drying, Bob's are washing, and so on. The first basket still takes 120 min (**latency**), but every subsequent basket finishes every 30 min (**throughput**). **Design Trade-off (2 marks):** - Increasing the number of stages $k$ increases throughput (up to $k$ times) but also increases latency ($L = k \cdot T_c$) and pipeline fill time. - There is a **diminishing returns** effect: more stages mean more registers, more clock power, and more hazard sensitivity. VLSI designers must balance $k$ against the target frequency, area budget, and power constraints. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A 5-stage pipelined processor has stage delays of 18 ns, 22 ns, 15 ns, 25 ns, and 20 ns. The pipeline register delay is 4 ns. If the processor executes 1000 instructions, calculate: (i) Clock period, (ii) Total execution time, (iii) Speedup over non-pipelined execution, (iv) Efficiency, and (v) Throughput in MIPS.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Clock Period (1 mark):** Slowest stage = 25 ns (Stage 4). $$T_c = 25 + 4 = 29 \text{ ns}$$ **(ii) Total Pipelined Time (1.5 marks):** $$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_c = (1000 + 5 - 1) \cdot 29 = 1004 \cdot 29 = 29{,}116 \text{ ns} \approx 29.12 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **(iii) Speedup (1.5 marks):** Non-pipelined time per instruction: $$T_{task} = (18 + 22 + 15 + 25 + 20) + (5 \cdot 4) = 100 + 20 = 120 \text{ ns}$$ $$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_{task} = 1000 \cdot 120 = 120{,}000 \text{ ns}$$ $$S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{120{,}000}{29{,}116} \approx 4.12$$ **(iv) Efficiency (1 mark):** $$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{4.12}{5} = 0.824 = 82.4\%$$ **(v) Throughput (1 mark):** $$TP = \frac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{1000 \text{ instr}}{29{,}116 \text{ ns}} = \frac{1000}{29.116 \text{ }\mu\text{s}} \approx 34.35 \text{ MIPS}$$ --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the expressions for Speedup ($S$), Efficiency ($\eta$), and Throughput ($TP$) of a $k$-stage pipeline executing $n$ tasks. State all assumptions clearly.** **Model Answer:** **Assumptions (1.5 marks):** 1. The pipeline is **ideal**: no hazards, no stalls, no data dependencies, no branching. 2. All stages are **balanced** — each stage has equal combinational delay. 3. The **clock period is the same** in both pipelined and non-pipelined designs. 4. The pipeline registers have **negligible setup/hold violations** and are clocked with **zero clock skew**. 5. $n$ tasks are **independent** and can enter the pipeline back-to-back. **Derivation of Speedup (2 marks):** Non-pipelined time for $n$ tasks: $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot T_c$. Pipelined time for $n$ tasks: $T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_c$. $$S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_c}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c} = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$$ **Derivation of Efficiency (1.5 marks):** Maximum theoretical speedup is $S_{max} = k$ (achieved as $n \to \infty$). $$\eta = \frac{S}{S_{max}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot (n + k - 1)^{-1}}{k} = \frac{n}{n + k - 1}$$ **Derivation of Throughput (2 marks):** Throughput is the rate of task completion. $$TP = \frac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{n}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c}$$ In the steady state ($n \gg k$): $$TP_{steady} = \frac{1}{T_c}$$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the following terms with examples: (i) Pipeline latency, (ii) Pipeline throughput, (iii) Pipeline bubble, (iv) Clock skew. How does clock skew affect pipeline performance?** **Model Answer:** **(i) Pipeline Latency (1.5 marks):** Latency is the time required for a single task to traverse from the input of Stage 1 to the output of Stage $k$. For an ideal $k$-stage pipeline with clock period $T_c$: $$L = k \cdot T_c$$ **Example:** A 4-stage pipeline with $T_c = 10$ ns has a latency of 40 ns. The *first* instruction takes 40 ns to produce a result, even if subsequent instructions follow every 10 ns. **(ii) Pipeline Throughput (1.5 marks):** Throughput is the number of tasks completed per unit time. In steady state: $$TP = \frac{1}{T_c}$$ **Example:** A pipeline clocked at 200 MHz has a peak throughput of 200 million tasks/second (200 MIPS). For $n$ tasks total, the *average* throughput is: $$TP_{avg} = \frac{n}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c}$$ **(iii) Pipeline Bubble (1.5 marks):** A bubble is a *stall cycle* inserted into the pipeline when a hazard is detected (e.g., data dependency, cache miss, branch misprediction). The bubble propagates downstream, leaving one or more stages idle for that cycle, reducing effective throughput. **Example:** In a 5-stage MIPS pipeline, a *load-use hazard* introduces one bubble, reducing throughput by approximately 1 instruction. If the load latency is 2 cycles, the throughput drops by ~20% for that instruction. **(iv) Clock Skew (1.5 marks):** Clock skew is the *temporal difference* in the arrival of the clock edge at different pipeline registers, caused by wire delay, load mismatch, and clock-tree buffer variation. Excessive skew can cause: - **Hold-time violations:** Data in one register changes *before* the next register captures it, leading to incorrect values. - **Reduced effective clock period:** The clock period must be increased to accommodate the worst-case skew, lowering $f_{clk}$. **Effect on Performance (1 mark):** If clock skew $\delta_{skew}$ is non-zero, the effective clock period becomes: $$T_{c,eff} = T_c + \delta_{skew}$$ This *reduces* the maximum clock frequency and hence the throughput. Modern VLSI designs use **clock-tree synthesis (CTS)** tools to bound $\delta_{skew}$ to less than 5–10% of $T_c$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting the register delay** in $T_c$ calculation (lose 1–2 marks in numericals). > 2. **Using $n \cdot k \cdot T_c$ instead of $(n + k - 1) \cdot T_c$** for total pipelined time (lose 2 marks). > 3. **Confusing latency with throughput** — saying "pipelining reduces latency" is *wrong*; it increases latency while increasing throughput. > 4. **Writing $S = k$ directly** without deriving it for finite $n$ — this only holds in the *limit* $n \to \infty$. > 5. **Ignoring clock skew** when asked about "factors affecting pipeline performance" — always mention it. > 6. **Not drawing clock signal** in the block diagram — KTU expects the clock input arrow on every register. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Pipelining (Basic Concepts)** - ✅ **Pipelining** = Decompose a sequential task into $k$ stages, separated by clocked registers, to overlap execution of multiple tasks. - ✅ **Total cycles** for $n$ tasks in a $k$-stage pipeline = $n + k - 1$ (most-tested formula). - ✅ **Clock period** $T_c = \max(t_i) + t_{reg}$ — the slowest stage dictates the entire pipeline's speed. - ✅ **Latency** $L = k \cdot T_c$ — increases with more stages. - ✅ **Throughput** $TP = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ (steady state) — improves with more stages. - ✅ **Speedup** $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ → approaches $k$ as $n \to \infty$. - ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{n}{n + k - 1}$ → approaches 1 (100%) as $n \to \infty$. - ✅ **Pipeline stages need to be balanced** — otherwise the slowest stage wastes power and area in faster stages. - ✅ **Clock skew** must be minimized (typically < 10% of $T_c$) via clock-tree synthesis. - ✅ **Ideal pipeline assumptions**: no hazards, no stalls, no branches, no data dependencies, equal stage delays. - ✅ **Laundry analogy** = best way to remember the latency-vs-throughput trade-off. - ✅ Real-world examples: **Intel Pentium 4 (31 stages)**, **TI C6000 DSP (8–14 stages)**, **Google TPU systolic array**. - ✅ **Bubbles** = stall cycles that reduce effective throughput when hazards occur. - ✅ For KTU 2024 exam, **always show the clock signal** in your block diagram of a pipeline. - ✅ **Diminishing returns**: doubling $k$ doesn't double throughput if $n$ is small — the fill/drain overhead dominates. - ✅ For very large $n$ (e.g., $n > 1000$), treat the pipeline as if it is always in steady state, i.e., $TP \approx 1/T_c$ and $S \approx k$.
